topics.title: PEEK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PEEK**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of PEEK

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PEEK (Polyether Ether Ketone)** is a linear, **semi-crystalline thermoplastic aromatic polymer** belonging to the **Polyaryletherketone (PAEK)** family, characterized by repeating ether–ether–ketone linkages in its backbone. Its IUPAC systematic name is **poly(oxy-1,4-phenylene-oxy-1,4-phenylene-carbonyl-1,4-phenylene)**.

The repeat unit of PEEK has the molecular structure shown below:

$$\text{Repeat Unit: } -\left[\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\right]-\$$

With molecular formula of the repeat unit: $\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{14}\text{O}_3$, giving a **molar mass of the repeat unit ≈ 288.3 g/mol**.

| Property | Value (Typical) | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Density (ρ) | **1.32** | g/cm³ |
| Glass Transition (T_g) | **143** | °C |
| Melting Point (T_m) | **343** | °C |
| Continuous Use Temperature | **260** | °C |
| Tensile Strength | **100** | MPa |
| Young's Modulus | **3.6** | GPa |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Titanium of Plastics"**
> Imagine a rope made by alternating two kinds of knots — one **stretchy knot** (ether linkage, like a flexible hinge) and one **stiff, locked knot** (ketone/aryl group, like a rigid plate). This combination gives PEEK its paradox: it is **tough like metal yet light like plastic**. In spacecraft, where every gram counts but failure is catastrophic, PEEK replaces small aluminium or titanium parts to reduce mass by up to **40–70%** without losing structural strength.

## 1.3 Why PEEK? — The Space-Grade Premium

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** PEEK is specifically highlighted in KTU Module 1 because it is the *gold-standard* high-performance thermoplastic for **Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and Geostationary Orbit (GEO)** hardware, where it must survive:
> - **Vacuum outgassing** (NASA ASTM E595 compliance)
> - **Atomic oxygen erosion** (with proper coatings)
> - **Thermal cycling** between −150 °C and +200 °C
> - **Cosmic radiation** (γ-rays, electrons, protons)

It is sold commercially as **Victrex PEEK™**, **Solvay Ketaspire™**, **Evonik VESTAKEEP™**, and **RTP Compounds**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Molecular Architecture — The "Why" Behind Performance

The PEEK backbone contains three functional motifs arranged in a **1:1 alternating pattern**:

1. **Arylene (Phenylene) Rings** $\text{C}_6\text{H}_4$ — Provide **rigidity, thermal stability, and radiation resistance** because aromatic π-electron clouds absorb high-energy photons non-destructively.
2. **Ether Linkages** $-\text{O}-$ — Provide **chain flexibility** (rotational freedom around the C–O bond) and **processability** (lower melt viscosity).
3. **Carbonyl (Ketone) Linkages** $-\text{CO}-$ — Provide **dipole interaction, polarity, and hydrogen-bond acceptance**, contributing to exceptional **adhesion with carbon fibres** in composites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ether:ketone ratio is **2:1**, hence the name **P**oly**e**ther **e**ther **k**etone. PEEK is the **most flexible** member of the PAEK family because ether content is maximised. Reducing ethers (e.g., PEK, PEKK) increases T_g and stiffness but reduces melt processability.

## 2.2 Synthesis Route — Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution

PEEK is industrially produced via the **nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) polycondensation** route:

$$\text{HO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{OH} \;(\text{hydroquinone}) + \text{F}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{F} \;(\text{4,4'-difluorobenzophenone}) \xrightarrow{\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{DMSO/DMAc, } 300\,°\text{C}} \text{PEEK} + 2\text{NaF} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Mechanism steps:
- Step A: Hydroquinone is deprotonated by sodium carbonate to form the **phenolate nucleophile**.
- Step B: The phenolate attacks the **ipso-carbon** of the C–F bond on 4,4'-difluorobenzophenone via a **Meisenheimer complex**.
- Step C: Fluoride leaves (F⁻ is the leaving group, stabilised by sodium), forming a new C–O bond.
- Step D: The reaction is **step-growth** in nature; achieving high molecular weight requires **exact stoichiometric balance** and removal of water.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Point:** The polymerisation is **step-growth** (condensation), so the **Carothers' equation** applies:
> $$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$
> where $r$ = stoichiometric imbalance ratio of functional groups and $p$ = extent of reaction. To achieve $\overline{X}_n \geq 100$, both $r \to 1$ AND $p \to 1$ must hold.

## 2.3 Crystallinity & Morphology

PEEK is **semi-crystalline**, with crystallinity between **30%–35%** in virgin material, which can be increased up to **48%** by controlled thermal annealing near T_g.

Crystallisation kinetics follow the **Avrami equation** (critical for KTU derivations):

$$X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K t^n)$$

where $X_c(t)$ = relative crystallinity at time $t$, $K$ = crystallisation rate constant, and $n$ = Avrami exponent (typically 2.0–3.0 for PEEK indicating **3-D spherulitic growth with heterogeneous nucleation**).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The Master Cheat Sheet — Memorise Before Exam**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Carothers' Number-Average DP | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | $r \leq 1$, $p \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Weight-Average DP (equal reactivity) | $\overline{X}_w = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r(1-2p)}$ | $\overline{X}_w \geq \overline{X}_n$ |
| 3 | Polydispersity Index | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\overline{X}_w}{\overline{X}_n}$ | For ideal step-growth PDI → 2.0 |
| 4 | Avrami Crystallisation | $X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K t^n)$ | $n = 2\text{–}3$ for PEEK |
| 5 | Crystallisation Half-Time | $t_{1/2} = \left(\dfrac{\ln 2}{K}\right)^{1/n}$ | minutes |
| 6 | Fox–Flory T_g Relation | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$ | High M_w → higher T_g |
| 7 | Voids Volume Fraction (Composite) | $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_m$ | For CF/PEEK, target $V_v \leq 1\%$ |
| 8 | Rule of Mixtures (Modulus) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Parallel loading |
| 9 | CTE (Composite) | $\alpha_c = \alpha_f V_f + \alpha_m V_m$ | Linear CTE approximation |
| 10 | Density of Composite | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Mass balance |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of PEEK in Space

- **Satellite structural brackets** — replaces aluminium 7075 with **CF/PEEK** composite (Toray Cetex®).
- **Bushings and bearings** — self-lubricating, no need for liquid grease (which outgasses).
- **Wire and cable insulation** — radiation-cross-linked PEEK (e.g., Gore® Space Cables).
- **Pump impellers** — chemical resistance to rocket-grade propellants (MMH, NTO).
- **Cubesat chassis panels** — low mass, dimensionally stable under thermal cycling.
- **Man-rated space-suit interfaces** — high strength-to-weight with biocompatibility.

> [!TIP]
> **NASA Outgassing Rule:** PEEK's TML (Total Mass Loss) ≤ **0.4%** and CVCM (Collected Volatile Condensable Materials) ≤ **0.05%** per ASTM E595, satisfying NASA's baseline criteria for spacecraft hardware.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Weight from Stoichiometry (Carothers' Equation)

**Problem Statement:** Synthesise PEEK with number-average degree of polymerisation $\overline{X}_n = 200$. What stoichiometric imbalance $r$ is required at extent of reaction $p = 0.995$?

**Step 1 — Write the Carothers' number-average equation:**

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values $\overline{X}_n = 200$ and $p = 0.995$:**

$$200 = \frac{1+r}{1+r - 2r(0.995)}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$200 = \frac{1+r}{1+r - 1.990\,r}$$

$$200 = \frac{1+r}{1+r(1 - 1.990)}$$

$$200 = \frac{1+r}{1 - 0.990\,r}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply:**

$$200\,(1 - 0.990\,r) = 1 + r$$

$$200 - 198\,r = 1 + r$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $r$:**

$$200 - 1 = 198\,r + r$$

$$199 = 199\,r$$

$$\boxed{\,r = 1.000\,}$$

This implies **perfect stoichiometric balance** is required. In industrial practice, the difluorobenzophenone is added in slight stoichiometric **excess** (typically 1% – 2% mol) to control the molecular weight precisely and cap chain ends, avoiding runaway viscosity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Students often forget that $r$ is defined as the **ratio of the lesser functional group to the greater**, so $r \leq 1$. Writing $r > 1$ is a common mistake that costs 2 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Avrami Half-Time of PEEK Crystallisation

**Problem:** PEEK is isothermally crystallised at $T_c = 300\,°\text{C}$. Isothermal DSC data yields Avrami parameters $K = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-n}$ and $n = 2.8$. Calculate the crystallisation half-time $t_{1/2}$.

**Step 1 — Avrami equation:**

$$X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K t^n)$$

**Step 2 — Half-time condition $X_c(t_{1/2}) = 0.5$:**

$$0.5 = 1 - \exp(-K t_{1/2}^n)$$

$$\exp(-K t_{1/2}^n) = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$-K t_{1/2}^n = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$

$$K t_{1/2}^n = 0.6931$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_{1/2}$:**

$$t_{1/2}^n = \frac{0.6931}{K}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \left(\frac{0.6931}{K}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values:**

$$t_{1/2} = \left(\frac{0.6931}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{1/2.8}$$

$$t_{1/2} = (57758.3)^{0.3571}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the power:**

$$\ln(57758.3) = 10.964$$

$$10.964 \times 0.3571 = 3.915$$

$$t_{1/2} = \exp(3.915) \approx 50.0 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{1/2} \approx 50 \text{ seconds}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Rule-of-Mixtures for CF/PEEK Composite Modulus

**Problem:** A CF/PEEK composite plate (Toray Cetex) has fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.60$. Carbon fibre modulus $E_f = 230 \text{ GPa}$; PEEK matrix modulus $E_m = 3.6 \text{ GPa}$. Calculate the longitudinal composite modulus.

**Step 1 — Apply the iso-strain (Voigt) rule of mixtures:**

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$E_c = (230)(0.60) + (3.6)(0.40)$$

$$E_c = 138 + 1.44$$

$$\boxed{\,E_c = 139.44 \text{ GPa}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The matrix contribution (1.44 GPa) is less than **1%** of the total. This is why even minor voids ($V_v > 2\%$) drastically reduce mechanical performance in space-grade laminates — voids behave as $E_v = 0$ regions in the load path.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property Comparison Among PAEK Family

| Property | **PEEK** | **PEK** | **PEKK** | **PEEKK** | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Ether:Ketone Ratio | **2 : 1** | 1 : 1 | 0 : 2 | 1 : 2 | — |
| T_g | 143 | 157 | 165 | 158 | °C |
| T_m | 343 | 373 | 388 | 367 | °C |
| Tensile Strength | 100 | 105 | 110 | 102 | MPa |
| Dielectric Strength | 24 | 23 | 28 | 25 | kV/mm |
| NASA TML (ASTM E595) | **0.20** | 0.30 | 0.25 | 0.22 | % |
| Cost (per kg) | **Medium** | High | High | High | USD |
| Processability | **Easy** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | — |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define PEEK. List any four salient properties that make it suitable for space applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Count ~80):**

PEEK (Polyether Ether Ketone) is a **semi-crystalline thermoplastic aromatic polymer** belonging to the polyaryletherketone (PAEK) family, having ether–ether–ketone linkages in its repeat unit. Four salient properties for space use:

1. **High continuous use temperature (260 °C)** — survives LEO/GEO thermal extremes.
2. **Excellent radiation resistance** — withstands γ, e⁻, and proton fluxes without embrittlement.
3. **Low outgassing** — TML ≤ 0.4%, CVCM ≤ 0.05% per ASTM E595.
4. **High specific strength** — replaces aluminium with ~40–70% mass saving.

*(Award 0.5 mark for definition, 0.5 mark for each property = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Explain the role of ether linkages in the PEEK repeat unit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The ether linkages ($-\text{O}-$) in PEEK are bivalent oxygen bridges connecting adjacent phenylene rings. They serve three critical functions:

- **Flexibility:** The C–O–C bond angle allows ~360° of free rotation, lowering the glass transition temperature and providing chain mobility.
- **Processability:** Ether units reduce inter-chain dipole interactions, lowering melt viscosity and enabling injection moulding at 370–400 °C.
- **Toughness:** The flexible ether segments absorb impact energy, giving PEEK a notched Izod impact strength of ~85 J/m.

*(Award 1 mark per role)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Always **draw the repeat unit** before writing properties — students lose 1–2 marks by describing the structure vaguely.
> 2. Always quote **numerical values with units** — "high T_g" is unacceptable; write "T_g = 143 °C".
> 3. For composite questions, **state the rule of mixtures assumption** (iso-strain / iso-stress) — failure to do so leads to a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. Avoid confusing PEK, PEEK, and PEKK — examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Synthesis, Structure, and Thermal Behaviour

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **industrial synthesis of PEEK** via nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Include the monomers, reaction conditions, mechanism steps, and the role of sodium carbonate.

**Model Answer:**

**Monomers (2 marks):**
- **Hydroquinone** $\text{HO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{OH}$ (provides phenolate nucleophile after deprotonation).
- **4,4′-Difluorobenzophenone** $\text{F}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{F}$ (provides electrophilic C–F bond for substitution).

**Reaction Conditions (1 mark):**
- Solvent: Diphenyl sulfone (DPS) or DMAc/DMSO mixture.
- Temperature: 280–320 °C (above T_m for one-step melt polymerisation).
- Atmosphere: Inert N₂ to prevent hydroquinone oxidation.
- Base: Na₂CO₃ (or K₂CO₃) to neutralise HF generated.

**Mechanism (3 marks — 1 mark each step):**
- **Step 1 — Phenolate Generation:** Na₂CO₃ deprotonates hydroquinone → phenolate ion (nucleophile) + NaHCO₃.
- **Step 2 — Meisenheimer Complex:** Phenolate attacks the ipso-carbon bearing fluorine on difluorobenzophenone, forming a stable anionic σ-complex.
- **Step 3 — Fluoride Elimination:** The complex collapses, expelling F⁻ (captured by Na⁺ as NaF) and forming the new C–O–C aryl ether bond. This is **step-growth** polycondensation.

**Role of Na₂CO₃ (1 mark):**
It acts as the **HCl/HF scavenger**, removing the acidic by-product that would otherwise protonate phenolate and terminate chain growth. It also drives equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **thermal properties** of PEEK with reference to T_g, T_m, crystallinity, and the Avrami crystallisation kinetics. A PEEK sample is crystallised at $T_c = 310\,°\text{C}$ with Avrami parameters $n = 3.0$ and $K = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}^{-n}$. Calculate the crystallisation half-time.

**Model Answer:**

**Thermal Properties (4 marks):**
- **T_g = 143 °C** — onset of segmental motion (amorphous regions).
- **T_m = 343 °C** — melting of crystalline lamellae.
- **Degree of crystallinity:** 30–35% in moulded parts; up to 48% after annealing at 200–280 °C. Higher crystallinity improves solvent resistance and modulus but reduces ductility.
- **Continuous use temperature:** 260 °C (highest among melt-processable thermoplastics).
- **Heat deflection temperature (HDT):** 162 °C at 1.82 MPa.

**Avrami Kinetics (1 mark — theory):**
PEEK follows 3-D spherulitic growth with heterogeneous nucleation. Avrami exponent $n \approx 2\text{–}3$ indicates nucleation-and-growth morphology.

**Calculation (2 marks):**
Using $X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K t^n)$ and $X_c(t_{1/2}) = 0.5$:

$$t_{1/2} = \left(\frac{\ln 2}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{0.6931}{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}\right)^{1/3.0}$$

$$t_{1/2} = (1.3862 \times 10^{5})^{0.3333}$$

$$t_{1/2} = (138620)^{1/3} = 51.81 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{1/2} \approx 51.8 \text{ s}\,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Composite Properties & Space Applications

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **structure of CF/PEEK composite**. Derive the longitudinal modulus using the **rule of mixtures** for a composite with $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 240 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 3.6 \text{ GPa}$. Comment on the role of voids.

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of CF/PEEK (3 marks — see SECTION 4 diagrams):**
CF/PEEK is a **fibre-reinforced thermoplastic composite** where continuous carbon fibres (Toray T800 or IM7) are embedded in a PEEK matrix. The composite is typically manufactured by:
1. **Film stacking** — alternating layers of PEEK film and carbon fibre tow.
2. **Consolidation** — hot pressing at 380–400 °C under 5–10 bar pressure in vacuum.
3. The result is a **laminate** with fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.55\text{–}0.65$.

The PEEK matrix protects fibres from atomic oxygen, transfers load via shear, and prevents galvanic corrosion with aluminium fittings. Carbon fibres carry the primary tensile/compressive load along the fibre direction.

**Rule of Mixtures Derivation (3 marks):**
Under **iso-strain** condition (load parallel to fibres), both phases experience the same strain $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$.

Total stress:
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

Since $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ and strain is equal:
$$E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon (1 - V_f)$$

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

**Calculation:**
$$E_c = (240)(0.55) + (3.6)(0.45) = 132 + 1.62 = 133.62 \text{ GPa}$$

**Role of Voids (1 mark):**
Voids have $E_v = 0$ and act as **stress concentrators** and **crack initiation sites**. Aerospace specifications mandate void content $V_v \leq 1\%$ (per ASTM D2734). For every 1% increase in voids, ILSS (inter-laminar shear strength) drops by ~5–7%.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **space-grade qualification tests** that PEEK and CF/PEEK composites must undergo before flight. Explain ASTM E595, thermal cycling, atomic oxygen exposure, and outgassing tests in detail.

**Model Answer:**

| Test | Standard | Pass Criterion | Why It Matters in Space |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mass Loss & CVCM | **ASTM E595** | TML ≤ 1.0%, CVCM ≤ 0.10% | Prevents contamination of optics, sensors, and thermal control surfaces |
| Thermal Cycling | **NASA-STD-7001** | 1000 cycles, −120 °C to +120 °C | LEO components experience ~16 cycles/day |
| Outgassing under Vacuum | **ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C** | TML < 1%, RML < 1% | Quantifies volatile release at 10⁻⁶ Pa |
| Atomic Oxygen | **ASTM E2089** | Mass loss < 5% after AO flux | LEO contains AO that erodes polymers (Kapton, Mylar); PEEK inherently resistant |
| UV / Radiation | **ISO 15856** | No cracking at 1000 ESH | Sun-facing surfaces degrade via chain scission |
| Off-gassing / Flammability | **NASA-STD-6001B** | Self-extinguishing | Crew safety on manned missions |

**Detailed Discussion (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **ASTM E595 (Outgassing Test):** Specimen held at 125 °C for 24 h under 10⁻⁵ Pa. TML = (m_initial − m_final)/m_initial × 100%. PEEK typically yields **TML ≈ 0.2%**, well below the 1% limit.

2. **Thermal Cycling:** Specimens subjected to ±120 °C cycles (some missions use −180 °C to +150 °C) to simulate orbital eclipses. CF/PEEK shows excellent dimensional stability due to low CTE of carbon fibre matching the matrix.

3. **Atomic Oxygen Exposure (LEO specific):** At ~400 km altitude, AO flux ≈ 10¹⁴ atoms/cm²/s with impact energy 4–5 eV. PEEK forms a protective **passivating carbonaceous layer** that limits further erosion — this is why PEEK outperforms PDMS and PTFE in LEO.

4. **Radiation / UV:** PEEK's aromatic rings absorb UV non-destructively. After 1000 ESH (equivalent sun hours), tensile strength retention > 90%. Electron doses up to 10⁷ Gy cause minimal embrittlement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Glance Summary — Read This Before Walking Into the Exam Hall**

- ✅ **PEEK** = **P**oly**e**ther **e**ther **k**etone, repeat unit $\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{14}\text{O}_3$, M = 288.3 g/mol.
- ✅ **Backbone:** 2 ether : 1 ketone ratio; aryl rings give rigidity, ethers give flexibility, ketones give polarity.
- ✅ **Synthesis:** Step-growth polycondensation via SNAr of hydroquinone + 4,4′-difluorobenzophenone, base = Na₂CO₃.
- ✅ **Carothers' Equation:** $\overline{X}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$; **need $r = 1$ AND $p = 1$ for high M_w**.
- ✅ **Thermal:** T_g = 143 °C; T_m = 343 °C; Continuous service = 260 °C.
- ✅ **Semi-crystalline:** 30–35% crystallinity; up to 48% on annealing.
- ✅ **Avrami:** $n = 2\text{–}3$ (3-D spherulitic), $K$ depends on T_c (max around 300 °C).
- ✅ **NASA outgassing:** TML < 0.4%, CVCM < 0.05% — passes ASTM E595.
- ✅ **Radiation:** Aromatic backbone gives inherent γ/e⁻/proton resistance.
- ✅ **CF/PEEK Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ — **iso-strain** model.
- ✅ **Composite CTE:** $\alpha_c = \alpha_f V_f + \alpha_m V_m$.
- ✅ **Voids:** Target $V_v \leq 1\%$ per ASTM D2734; voids = $E = 0$ regions.
- ✅ **Density:** PEEK = 1.32 g/cm³; CF/PEEK ≈ 1.55 g/cm³ — still **40% lighter than Al 7075** (ρ = 2.81).
- ✅ **Key commercial products:** Victrex PEEK 450G, Toray Cetex®, Solvay Ketaspire®, Evonik VESTAKEEP®, Ensinger TECAPEEK.
- ✅ **Space heritage:** Airbus A350 satellite brackets, ISS cable harnesses, Mars helicopter blade hinges, SpaceX Starlink chassis components, James Webb Space Telescope motor housings.
- ✅ **Difference:** PEEK (2:1) vs PEK (1:1) vs PEKK (0:2) — fewer ethers = higher T_g but harder to process.
- ✅ **Limitation:** High cost (~USD 60–100/kg); attacked by concentrated H₂SO₄ and acetone above 100 °C.
- ✅ **Always quote T_g with units** in exams; "high T_g" alone is **not** an accepted answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polycarbonates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polycarbonates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polycarbonates (PC)** are a class of linear, amorphous, thermoplastic engineering polymers whose backbones are characterized by the repetitive presence of the **carbonate functional group (–O–(C=O)–O–)** chemically linking dihydroxy aromatic (or aliphatic) monomers.

The most industrially significant polycarbonate is **Bisphenol A Polycarbonate (BPA-PC)**, synthesized from **Bisphenol A (2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)propane)** and a carbonate precursor (phosgene or diphenyl carbonate). The polymer belongs to the family of **step-growth polymers** formed via condensation reactions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT746 / Module 1):** Polycarbonates are classified as **high-performance amorphous thermoplastics** owing to their unique combination of optical transparency, dimensional stability, and exceptional impact strength, making them indispensable for aerospace transparent enclosures.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Invisible Aircraft Window"

Imagine sitting inside a commercial aircraft or a spacecraft cockpit. The window in front of you must be simultaneously:
- **Crystal clear** like glass so the pilots can see outside,
- **Bulletproof / bird-strike resistant** so a collision at high velocity does not shatter it,
- **Lightweight** because every gram in space costs thousands of dollars to launch,
- **Tough at –150 °C and stable at +135 °C** because orbital thermal swings are brutal.

Glass fails on toughness and weight. Polycarbonates deliver all four. **The window material that lets a pilot "see through" a jet cockpit while protecting him/her is essentially a polycarbonate sheet**, optionally coated with abrasion-resistant hardcoats.

In simple words:
> **Polycarbonate = "Transparent Steel-like Plastic"** — it behaves like a metal under impact but transmits light like glass.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics of Bisphenol A Polycarbonate

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|----------|:------:|:-------------:|:-----:|
| Density | ρ | **1.20** | g/cm³ |
| Glass Transition Temperature | T_g | **145 – 150** | °C |
| Tensile Strength | σ_t | **55 – 75** | MPa |
| Young's Modulus | E | **2.3 – 2.6** | GPa |
| Izod Notched Impact Strength | I | **600 – 850** | J/m |
| Refractive Index | n | **1.586** | — |
| Light Transmission (visible) | T | **≈ 90** | % |
| Heat Deflection Temperature (1.82 MPa) | HDT | **135 – 140** | °C |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | CTE | **65 × 10⁻⁶** | K⁻¹ |
| Limiting Oxygen Index | LOI | **≈ 25 – 27** | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LOI = 25?** A material requires a LOI of the surrounding atmosphere greater than its own LOI to sustain combustion. Since atmospheric oxygen is only **≈ 21 %**, BPA-PC is **self-extinguishing in air** — a critical property for spacecraft interiors under NASA flammability requirements (NASA-STD-6001).

---

## 1.4 KTU Board Examiner's "One-Line" Definition to Memorize

> *"Polycarbonates are amorphous engineering thermoplastics containing –O–CO–O– linkages in the main chain, derived typically from Bisphenol A, exhibiting optical clarity, high T_g (≈ 147 °C), and exceptional impact resistance suitable for aerospace transparent components."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the Repeat Unit

The repeat unit of BPA-polycarbonate is:

**– [–O–C(=O)–O–C₆H₄–C(CH₃)₂–C₆H₄–]–**

Let us decode this chain:
- **Aromatic Rings (–C₆H₄–)** → provide rigidity and π-conjugation (responsible for high T_g).
- **Gem-Dimethyl Bridge (–C(CH₃)₂–)** → disrupts crystallinity, ensuring **amorphous** morphology → optical transparency.
- **Carbonate Linkage (–O–CO–O–)** → the characteristic functional group; polar but hydrolytically vulnerable.
- **Isopropylidene Group** → contributes to chain stiffness and thermal stability.

## 2.2 Polymerization Chemistry — The Two Industrial Routes

### Route A — Interfacial Polycondensation (Phosgene Process)

This is the historical, dominant industrial route.

**Step 1: Deprotonation of BPA**

$$\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–C(CH}_3)_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} + 2\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{NaO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–C(CH}_3)_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–ONa} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The phenoxide salt is **nucleophilically activated** to attack the carbonyl of phosgene.

**Step 2: Reaction at the Organic–Aqueous Interface**

$$\text{n NaO–Ar–ONa} + \text{n COCl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{–[–O–Ar–O–CO–]}_n\text{–} + 2\text{n NaCl}$$

**Reaction Medium:** Dichloromethane (CH₂Cl₂) as organic phase + aqueous NaOH.
**Catalyst:** Quaternary ammonium salts (e.g., benzyltriethylammonium chloride — BTEAC) act as **phase-transfer catalysts**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Worthy Point for KTU:** The reaction is **Schotten–Baumann type** — an interfacial technique where the diol (BPA-dianion) sits in aqueous phase and acid chloride (phosgene) sits in organic phase; product precipitates at the interface.

### Route B — Melt Transesterification (Non-Phosgene, "Green" Route)

$$n\,\text{HO–Ar–OH} + n\,(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O})_2\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{–[–O–Ar–O–CO–]}_n\text{–} + 2n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

- Catalyst: **Lithium hydroxide (LiOH)** or **sodium stearate**.
- By-product **phenol** is removed under vacuum (0.1 – 1 mbar) at 280 – 300 °C to drive equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier's principle).
- This is the modern, environmentally preferred route (Asahi Kasei, SABIC, Covestro).

## 2.3 Molecular Weight Control — Carothers' Equation

For step-growth polymerization, the **Carothers' equation** governs degree of polymerization:

$$X_n = \frac{1}{(1 - p)}$$

Where:
- $X_n$ = number-average degree of polymerization.
- $p$ = extent of reaction (fraction of functional groups reacted).

| Conversion $p$ | $X_n$ |
|:--:|:--:|
| 0.90 | 10 |
| 0.99 | 100 |
| 0.999 | 1000 |
| 0.9999 | 10000 |

> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Industrial BPA-PC targets $\overline{M_w} =$ **30,000 – 60,000 g/mol**, requiring $p \geq 0.999$. Therefore *exacting stoichiometric balance is non-negotiable*.

## 2.4 Free Volume Theory & Glass Transition

The glass transition of PC is explained via the **Free Volume Theory (Fox–Flory)**:

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$$

Where:
- $T_{g,\infty}$ = T_g at infinite molecular weight (≈ **150 °C** for BPA-PC).
- $K$ = Fox–Flory constant (related to free volume per chain end).
- $\overline{M}_n$ = number-average molecular weight.

**Physical interpretation:** Chain ends possess excess free volume. As $\overline{M}_n$ increases, chain-end concentration drops, free volume decreases, and T_g rises asymptotically to $T_{g,\infty}$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1 - p}$ | Carothers' DP | Predict molecular weight from conversion |
| 2 | $\overline{X}_w = \dfrac{1 + p}{1 - p}$ | Weight-average DP | Dispersity index $\;\mathcal{D} = 1 + p$ |
| 3 | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$ | Fox–Flory | Predict T_g shift on molecular weight |
| 4 | $\eta_0 = K \overline{M}_w^{3.4}$ | Mark–Houwink–Sakaruda scaling | Melt viscosity for injection moulding |
| 5 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Stress–strain | Aerospace mechanical design |
| 6 | $\text{CTE} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0 \cdot \Delta T}$ | Thermal expansion | Predict dimensional change in orbit |
| 7 | $n = \dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$ | Snell's law (optics) | Visor optical clarity |

> **Note on notation:** All delimiters using `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values within any future formula to avoid markdown table breakage.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real Aerospace Field

| Component | Application |
|-----------|-------------|
| Crew vehicle windows (Crew Dragon, Soyuz upgrades) | Transparent enclosure |
| Astronaut helmet visors / bubble helmets | High-impact face protection |
| Satellite lens housings | Optical + mechanical hybrid |
| Aircraft cockpit canopies | Bird-strike resistant glazing |
| Mars rover structural shrouds | Dust-impact resistant shells |
| LED light covers on ISS | UV-stabilized, lightweight diffusers |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Molecular Weight vs. Conversion (Carothers' Equation)

Let $N_0$ = initial number of bifunctional monomer molecules.
Let $N$ = number of unreacted functional groups at time $t$.

**Step 1: Define extent of reaction**

$$
p = \frac{\text{Number of functional groups reacted}}{\text{Initial number of functional groups}} = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0}
$$

**Step 2: Express the remaining molecules**

$$
N_{\text{left}} = N_0 - (N_0 - N) = N
$$

But each **unreacted molecule still has at least one unreacted functional group**, hence the count of *distinct polymer molecules* surviving is equal to the number of *unreacted ends*.

**Step 3: Form the average degree of polymerization**

By definition, the number-average degree of polymerization is the ratio of initial monomer molecules to remaining molecules:

$$
X_n = \frac{N_0}{N_{\text{remaining}}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the expression for $N_{\text{remaining}}$**

$$
X_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1 - p)} = \frac{1}{1 - p}
$$

$$
\boxed{X_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}
$$

**Step 5: Convert DP to molecular weight**

$$
\overline{M}_n = M_0 \cdot X_n = \frac{M_0}{1 - p}
$$

Where $M_0$ = molecular weight of the repeat unit of BPA-PC = **254.28 g/mol**.

**Step 6: Worked numerical example**

> Suppose we want $\overline{M}_n = 50{,}000$ g/mol.

$$
50{,}000 = \frac{254.28}{1 - p}
$$

$$
1 - p = \frac{254.28}{50{,}000} = 5.0856 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
p = 1 - 5.0856 \times 10^{-3} = 0.99491
$$

> **Conclusion:** A conversion of **99.49 %** is mandatory — emphasizing why industrial PC reactors use precise stoichiometric control and continuous removal of byproduct.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Mechanistic Derivation — Interfacial Polymerization Step-by-Step

The interfacial polymerization of BPA with phosgene proceeds through four well-defined elementary steps.

**Step (i): Aqueous-phase deprotonation**

$$
\text{BPA–OH} + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{BPA–O}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

*Bisphenol A is weakly acidic (pKₐ ≈ 10). Aqueous NaOH converts the –OH groups into nucleophilic phenoxide ions.*

**Step (ii): Phase transfer to the organic interface**

$$
\text{BPA–O}^-_{(aq)} \xrightarrow{\text{BTEAC catalyst}} \text{BPA–O}^-_{(org)}
$$

*The quaternary ammonium salt BTEAC forms an ion pair with the phenoxide, ferrying it across the aqueous–organic (CH₂Cl₂) interface.*

**Step (iii): Nucleophilic acyl substitution at carbonyl of phosgene**

$$
\text{BPA–O}^- + \text{Cl–CO–Cl} \longrightarrow \text{BPA–O–CO–Cl} + \text{Cl}^-
$$

*The phenoxide attacks the electrophilic carbon of phosgene, displacing one chloride.*

**Step (iv): Second substitution and propagation**

$$
\text{BPA–O–CO–Cl} + \text{BPA–O}^- \longrightarrow \text{BPA–O–CO–O–BPA} + \text{Cl}^-
$$

*The intermediate chloroformate reacts with another phenoxide to form the **dicarbonate bridge** that constitutes the polycarbonate repeat unit.*

**Step (v): Chain propagation and byproduct removal**

Byproduct NaCl is washed out with water. Excess phosgene and CH₂Cl₂ are stripped and recycled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Reaction:**
>
> $$\text{n HO–Ar–OH} + \text{n COCl}_2 + 2\text{n NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{–[–O–Ar–O–CO–]}_n\text{–} + 2\text{n NaCl} + 2\text{n H}_2\text{O}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Interfacial vs. Melt Process

| Parameter | Interfacial (Phosgene) | Melt (Transesterification) |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Phosgene usage | **Yes — extremely toxic** | **No** |
| Solvent | CH₂Cl₂ (toxic, regulated) | None (bulk melt) |
| Temperature | 25 – 35 °C | 280 – 310 °C |
| Catalyst | Phase-transfer (BTEAC) | LiOH / Na stearate |
| M_w achievable | 30,000 – 60,000 | 15,000 – 35,000 |
| Optical quality | Excellent (medical, optical) | Good (general-purpose) |
| Environmental footprint | High | Low |
| Commercial leaders | SABIC (Lexan®), Covestro (Makrolon®) | Asahi Kasei (Wonderlite®) |

---

## 3.4 Quantitative Reasoning — Why PC is Optically Transparent

Crystalline polymers scatter light at crystallite boundaries, appearing opaque. The gem-dimethyl bridge (–C(CH₃)₂–) between the two phenyl rings creates an irregular geometry that prevents close chain packing, **suppressing crystallization** completely. Thus BPA-PC is **amorphous** → no crystallites → no scattering → **> 90 % light transmission**.

Quantitative expression of transparency via refractive index:

$$
n_{PC} = 1.586, \quad \text{Loss}_{\text{Fresnel}} = 2 \left(\frac{n - 1}{n + 1}\right)^2 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Loss}_{\text{Fresnel}} = 2\left(\frac{1.586 - 1}{1.586 + 1}\right)^2 = 2\left(\frac{0.586}{2.586}\right)^2 \approx 5.15\%
$$

Hence maximum theoretical transmission ≈ **94.85 %** per surface; with anti-reflective (AR) hardcoats, **> 99 %** is achievable.

---

## 3.5 Predicting T_g Shift on Molecular Weight — Numerical Worked Example

Given $T_{g,\infty} = 150\,^\circ\text{C}$, $K = 1.0 \times 10^5$ (°C·g/mol) for BPA-PC, and $\overline{M}_n = 25{,}000$ g/mol:

$$
T_g = 150 - \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{25{,}000} = 150 - 4 = 146\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

If $\overline{M}_n$ increases to **100,000 g/mol**:

$$
T_g = 150 - \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{100{,}000} = 150 - 1 = 149\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** High-M_w grades used in aerospace (e.g., spacecraft visors) have T_g approaching 149 °C, ensuring dimensional stability across the harsh thermal cycles of LEO (Low Earth Orbit).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Polymerization Process Flow — Interfacial Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["BPA Dissolved in Aqueous NaOH"] --> B["Formation of Sodium Phenoxide"]
    B --> C["Phase Transfer via BTEAC Catalyst"]
    C --> D["Migration to Organic DCM Phase"]
    D --> E["Reaction with Liquid Phosgene"]
    E --> F["Chloroformate Intermediate Formed"]
    F --> G["Nucleophilic Attack by Second Phenoxide"]
    G --> H["Dicarbonate Linkage Created"]
    H --> I["Chain Propagation to High MW Polymer"]
    I --> J["Washing to Remove NaCl"]
    J --> K["Precipitation in Methanol"]
    K --> L["Drying and Pelletization"]
    L --> M["Final BPA Polycarbonate Resin"]
    M --> N["Injection Molding or Extrusion"]
    N --> O["Aerospace Component Window or Visor"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Spacecraft Transparent Enclosure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OuterLayer["OUTER SPACE-FACING LAYER"]
        OL1["Hardcoat: SiO2 Plasma Layer"]
        OL2["Anti-Static ITO Coating"]
    end
    subgraph CoreStack["PRIMARY STRUCTURAL STACK"]
        PC1["Polycarbonate Substrate 3 mm"]
        PC2["Polycarbonate Substrate 3 mm"]
        PC3["Polycarbonate Substrate 3 mm"]
        AD1["Adhesive Interlayer PVB 0.5 mm"]
        AD2["Adhesive Interlayer PVB 0.5 mm"]
    end
    subgraph InnerLayer["CREW-FACING LAYER"]
        IL1["Scratch-Resistant Acrylic"]
        IL2["Anti-Fog Hydrophilic Coating"]
    end
    OL1 --> PC1
    PC1 --> AD1
    AD1 --> PC2
    PC2 --> AD2
    AD2 --> PC3
    PC3 --> IL1
    IL2 --> PC3
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Multi-layer laminated polycarbonate structures resist hypervelocity micrometeoroid impact because each PC layer decelerates fragments sequentially, and the **PVB interlayer absorbs kinetic energy** through plastic deformation — preventing catastrophic penetration.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Synthesis Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{"Target Application?"} -->|Optical Medical| R1["Interfacial Phosgene Route"]
    S -->|General Industrial| R2["Melt Transesterification"]
    S -->|Aerospace Transparent| R3["Interfacial with Ultra-Pure BPA"]
    R1 --> Q1["Lexan Grade HP"]
    R2 --> Q2["Makrolon GP Grade"]
    R3 --> Q3["Lexan OQ Aerospace Grade"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define polycarbonates. Write the structure of Bisphenol A polycarbonate repeat unit and identify the characteristic functional group.**

**Model Answer:**

Polycarbonates are a class of thermoplastic, amorphous, step-growth polymers characterized by the presence of the **carbonate group (–O–CO–O–)** in the polymer backbone. They are typically derived from aromatic dihydroxy compounds, most commonly **Bisphenol A**, reacted with phosgene or diphenyl carbonate.

**Repeat Unit of BPA Polycarbonate:**

$$\text{–[–O–C(=O)–O–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–C(CH}_3)_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–]}_n\text{–}$$

The characteristic functional group is the **carbonate ester linkage (–O–CO–O–)** which links two aromatic rings through an oxygen-bridged carbonyl, granting the polymer its high thermal and mechanical properties.

*(3 marks: 1 — definition, 1 — structure, 1 — functional group identification)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State any three characteristic properties of polycarbonates that make them suitable for aerospace transparent applications.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Optical Transparency (> 90 %):** The amorphous structure (due to the bulky isopropylidene bridge) eliminates crystallite light scattering, providing glass-like clarity — essential for cockpit canopies, helmet visors, and spacecraft windows.

2. **High Impact Strength (Izod ≈ 600 – 850 J/m):** Polycarbonate absorbs energy through ductile deformation at room and cryogenic temperatures, withstanding bird strikes and micrometeoroid impacts.

3. **High Glass Transition Temperature (T_g ≈ 147 °C):** Ensures dimensional stability across the thermal extremes of LEO orbits (–150 °C to +120 °C), preventing warping or loss of optical tolerance.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per property with aerospace justification)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the interfacial polycondensation process for the synthesis of Bisphenol A polycarbonate with phosgene. Include the role of the phase-transfer catalyst.**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Compare and contrast the phosgene and non-phosgene (melt transesterification) routes for polycarbonate manufacture, highlighting environmental and product-quality implications.**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**1. Reaction Setup (2 marks):**
Bisphenol A is dissolved in aqueous sodium hydroxide (NaOH), forming the **sodium salt of BPA (disodium phenoxide)**. Phosgene (COCl₂) is dissolved in an immiscible organic solvent, typically **dichloromethane (DCM)**. The two immiscible phases form a liquid–liquid interface.

$$
\text{BPA + 2 NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{BPA} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**2. Phase Transfer Catalyst (2 marks):**
A **phase-transfer catalyst**, typically a quaternary ammonium salt such as **benzyltriethylammonium chloride (BTEAC)** or **tetra-n-butylammonium chloride**, facilitates the migration of the hydrophilic BPA-dianion across the aqueous–organic interface. The catalyst forms a lipophilic ion-pair:

$$
[\text{NR}_4]^+ \cdot [\text{BPA-O}^-]_{(aq)} \longrightarrow [\text{NR}_4]^+ \cdot [\text{BPA-O}^-]_{(org)}
$$

**3. Interfacial Reaction (2 marks):**
At the interface, the phenoxide ion attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of phosgene in a nucleophilic acyl substitution, eliminating NaCl. Stepwise, a chloroformate intermediate forms, which reacts with another phenoxide to yield the **dicarbonate linkage**:

$$
\text{n Na}_2\text{BPA} + \text{n COCl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{–[–O–BPA–O–CO–]}_n\text{–} + 2\text{n NaCl}
$$

**4. Product Recovery (1 mark):**
The polymer precipitates at the interface. It is washed free of NaCl, re-precipitated in methanol, dried, and pelletized for extrusion.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

| Criterion | Interfacial Phosgene Route | Melt Transesterification Route |
|-----------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Carbonyl Source** | Phosgene (COCl₂) — toxic gas | Diphenyl carbonate — safer solid |
| **Solvent** | Dichloromethane (toxic, VOC) | None — bulk melt |
| **Temperature** | 25 – 35 °C | 280 – 310 °C |
| **By-products** | NaCl, water | Phenol (recyclable) |
| **Molecular Weight Achievable** | Up to 60,000 g/mol (high) | Up to 35,000 g/mol (moderate) |
| **Environmental Impact** | High — phosgene & VOC emissions | Low — green chemistry compliant |
| **Optical Purity** | Excellent (medical / aerospace grade) | Good (general purpose) |
| **Energy Demand** | Low temperature, high solvent cost | High temperature, low solvent cost |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The **melt transesterification route** is preferred for environmental and regulatory reasons, but the **interfacial route** still dominates for ultra-high-M_w optical-grade aerospace polycarbonate because of superior chain-length control and minimal color body formation.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the structure–property relationships of Bisphenol A polycarbonate. Explain why it is amorphous yet possesses a high T_g.**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the aerospace applications of polycarbonates. Mention at least four applications and explain the property that justifies each use.**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**1. Molecular Architecture (2 marks):**
The BPA-PC backbone contains three structural motifs:
- Two **phenylene rings** per repeat unit.
- A central **isopropylidene (–C(CH₃)₂–) bridge**.
- A **carbonate (–O–CO–O–) linkage**.

**2. Why Amorphous? (2.5 marks):**
The **gem-dimethyl (–C(CH₃)₂–)** bridge introduces a **kink** of about **110°** in the chain axis. The two methyl groups on the same carbon sterically hinder close chain packing, preventing three-dimensional crystalline order. Since amorphous domains do not have grain boundaries that scatter visible light, the polymer is **optically transparent**.

**3. Why High T_g Despite Amorphous? (2.5 marks):**
- The **aromatic phenylene rings** restrict rotational freedom around chain bonds, raising the energy barrier for segmental motion.
- The **polar carbonate group** introduces strong dipole–dipole interactions, increasing cohesive energy density.
- The combined steric hindrance from phenylene and gem-dimethyl groups reduces chain mobility, requiring higher temperature for free-volume activation.

Therefore, BPA-PC has T_g ≈ **147 °C**, much higher than that of amorphous polyethylene (T_g ≈ –120 °C) despite both being amorphous.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

| Application | Property Justifying Use |
|-------------|-------------------------|
| **1. Spacecraft Windows (e.g., Crew Dragon)** | Optical transparency (> 90 %) + impact resistance against micrometeoroids |
| **2. Astronaut Helmet Visors (EVA suits)** | High T_g (≈ 147 °C) + low density (1.2 g/cm³) for head-borne protection in vacuum |
| **3. Aircraft Cockpit Canopies (F-22, Eurofighter)** | Bird-strike resistance + thermoformability into compound curves |
| **4. Satellite Antenna Domes / Radomes** | Low dielectric loss + RF transparency + thermal stability |
| **5. Mars Rover Sensor Housings** | Dust-impact toughness + thermal cycling endurance (–130 °C to +70 °C on Mars) |
| **6. ISS Interior Light Diffusers** | Flame retardancy (LOI ≈ 26) + UV transparency |

**Key Statement (1 mark):** The combination of **transparency + toughness + thermal stability + low density** makes polycarbonate uniquely irreplaceable among aerospace polymers; glass is heavier, acrylic is more brittle, and PES/PEI are opaque.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Polycarbonate Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "polycarbonate" with "polycarbon"** (carbon allotrope) — read the question carefully; in Module 1 it is the polymer.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify "Bisphenol A"** when writing the structure — generic polycarbonates exist (e.g., aliphatic), but the syllabus focuses on BPA-PC.
> 3. **Writing phosgene as "phosgene gas" without noting its toxicity** — examiners award 1 mark for recognizing the safety/environmental issue.
> 4. **Missing the role of BTEAC** in interfacial polymerization — a recurring 2-mark question.
> 5. **Stating T_g of PC as "100 °C" or "200 °C"** — the correct value is **≈ 147 °C**; ± 5 °C is acceptable.
> 6. **Failing to differentiate amorphous vs. semi-crystalline** — students often write "PC is crystalline"; in fact, BPA-PC is **strictly amorphous**.
> 7. **Not applying Carothers' equation** in numerical problems — marks are awarded for the formula, not just the answer.
> 8. **Listing applications without justifying the property** — Part B requires a **property ↔ application** pairing for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Polycarbonates**

- ☐ Polycarbonates contain the **carbonate group (–O–CO–O–)** in the backbone.
- ☐ The most common type is **Bisphenol A polycarbonate (BPA-PC)**.
- ☐ Repeat unit molecular weight $M_0$ = **254.28 g/mol**.
- ☐ Density = **1.20 g/cm³**, Refractive Index = **1.586**.
- ☐ **Glass transition temperature T_g ≈ 147 °C** (amorphous).
- ☐ **Optical transparency > 90 %** (due to amorphous morphology).
- ☐ **Izod impact strength ≈ 600 – 850 J/m** (one of the highest among transparent polymers).
- ☐ Two industrial synthesis routes: **Interfacial Phosgene Process** and **Melt Transesterification**.
- ☐ Interfacial route uses **BTEAC as phase-transfer catalyst** and CH₂Cl₂ as organic phase.
- ☐ Melt route uses **diphenyl carbonate + LiOH catalyst**, byproduct is **phenol** (recyclable).
- ☐ Step-growth kinetics follow **Carothers' equation**: $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1 - p}$.
- ☐ For $\overline{M}_n = 50{,}000$ g/mol, conversion $p \approx 0.9949$.
- ☐ T_g shift with molecular weight: **Fox–Flory** relation $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K / \overline{M}_n$.
- ☐ LOI ≈ 25 → **self-extinguishing in air** (good for spacecraft interiors).
- ☐ Aerospace uses: **windows, visors, canopies, radomes, rover housings**.
- ☐ Multi-layer laminated PC + PVB is standard for **hypervelocity impact resistance**.
- ☐ Amorphous structure arises from **gem-dimethyl kink** disrupting crystalline packing.
- ☐ High T_g arises from **rigid aromatic rings + polar carbonate dipoles**.
- ☐ Always pair an **application with its justifying property** in descriptive answers.
- ☐ Remember NASA's flammability standard: **NASA-STD-6001 Test 1** for PC interiors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyethers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polyethers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Polyethers** are a major family of addition/step polymers in which the repeating backbone unit contains the characteristic **ether linkage (–C–O–C–)**, formed most commonly through the **ring-opening polymerisation (ROP)** of cyclic ethers (epoxides, oxetanes, tetrahydrofuran) or via the polycondensation of diols. The general structural motif is:

$$\text{–}[\text{–O–R–}]_n\text{–}$$

where **R** is an aliphatic, cycloaliphatic or aromatic segment. The oxygen atoms in the backbone impart high chain flexibility, polarity, and hydrogen-bond accepting capability without introducing bulky side groups that would stiffen the chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Polyethers are the *foundation* of polyol chemistry that ultimately feeds into polyurethanes, epoxies, and propellant binders — three of the most space-critical polymer families.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a chain of paper clips linked end-to-end. Now imagine inserting a small, freely pivoting **rubber washer** between every two clips. That washer represents the ether oxygen: it lets the clips swing freely in any direction (chain flexibility), it does not get tired under repeated motion (low hysteresis), and it attracts water molecules loosely (hydrophilicity). Polyether chains behave exactly like this — *flexible spine with freely rotating oxygen hinges*.

### 1.3 Classification of Polyethers (KTU 2024 Scheme Taxonomy)

| Family | Repeating Unit | Tg (°C) | Tm (°C) | Space Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO/PEG) | –(CH₂–CH₂–O)– | **–67** | **66** | Binder precursor, sublimation coolant |
| Poly(propylene oxide) (PPO/PPG) | –(CH₂–CH(CH₃)–O)– | **–75** | amorphous | Polyurethane foams, propellant binder |
| Poly(tetrahydrofuran) (PTHF/PTMG) | –(CH₂–CH₂–CH₂–CH₂–O)– | **–75** | **43** | Elastic fibres, elastomers |
| Poly(epichlorohydrin) (PECH) | –(CH₂–CH(CH₂Cl)–O)– | **–22** | amorphous | Fuel-resistant elastomers |
| Phenoxy resins (bisphenol-A polyethers) | –(Ar–O–CH₂–CH(OH)–CH₂–Ar–O)– | **80–95** | amorphous | Matrix resin in composites |

> [!NOTE]
> The absence of **π-bonds or rigid rings in the main chain** is what makes most aliphatic polyethers flexible (low Tg) — a property directly exploited in low-temperature space environments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Origin of Polyether Properties

The ether oxygen (sp³-hybridised, two lone pairs) inserts three physical effects into the chain:

- **Bond angle accommodation:** The C–O–C angle (~112°) is wider than the C–C–C angle (~109.5°), reducing steric hindrance between adjacent repeat units.
- **Low rotational barrier:** Rotation about C–O bonds requires only **~4.5 kJ/mol** versus **~12 kJ/mol** for C–C bonds → high segmental mobility.
- **Hydrogen-bond accepting:** The lone pairs coordinate with water, NH, OH groups → drives compatibility with isocyanates (polyurethane formation).

### 2.2 Synthesis Routes

**(a) Ring-Opening Polymerisation (ROP) — Anionic:**
Initiation by alkoxide (RO⁻) on ethylene oxide:

$$\text{RO}^- + \text{CH}_2\text{–CH}_2 \,(\text{epoxide}) \longrightarrow \text{RO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O}^-$$

The active alkoxide chain-end propagates by repeated nucleophilic attack on monomer epoxide rings.

**(b) Cationic ROP — for THF:**

$$\text{THF} + \text{BF}_3 \cdot \text{OEt}_2 + \text{epoxide initiator} \longrightarrow \text{PTHF}$$

**(c) Step-Growth (for phenoxy/epoxy resins):** Bisphenol-A + epichlorohydrin → diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A (DGEBA).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DP_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | Number-average degree of polymerisation |
| 2 | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Carothers DP for linear step-growth (p = conversion) |
| 3 | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Mark-Houwink-Sakurada (intrinsic viscosity vs MW) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | Fox equation for copolymer/Blend Tg |
| 5 | $R_p = k_p \,[\text{M}] \,[\text{C}^\ominus]$ | Anionic ROP rate (first-order in monomer) |
| 6 | $\Delta G_{ROP} = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Thermodynamic driving force; negative when ring strain relieved |
| 7 | $\tau = \eta_0 \, \dot{\gamma}^{-1}$ | Shear-thinning relevance for propellant binder rheology |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** Use **\vert** not **\pipe** when rendering absolute-value bars inside any table row to preserve markdown column separation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In space engineering, polyethers are not used directly as structural plastics — they are **precursors and binders**. PEO/PPG polyols react with diisocyanates (TDI, MDI, IPDI) to form **polyurethane foams** that insulate cryogenic tanks (LH₂, LOX). PTHF diols give elastic fibres (Spandex-class) used in astronaut pressure suits. PEG itself, due to high latent heat of sublimation, was investigated as a **passive thermal buffer** for satellite electronics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Weight of PEO from Hydroxyl Number

Polyether polyols are commercially characterised by the **Hydroxyl Number (OH#)**, defined as the milligrams of KOH equivalent to the hydroxyl content of 1 g of polyol.

**Step 1:** Write the definition.

$$\text{OH\#} = \frac{56.1 \times 1000}{M_n} \times f$$

where 56.1 g/mol is the equivalent weight of KOH, and **f** is the functionality (number of –OH per molecule, f = 2 for diols).

**Step 2:** Rearrange for $M_n$.

$$M_n = \frac{56.1 \times 1000 \times f}{\text{OH\#}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute f = 2 (typical difunctional PEO/PPG).

$$M_n = \frac{1.122 \times 10^{5}}{\text{OH\#}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation — example: a PEG with OH# = 187 mg KOH/g.

$$M_n = \frac{1.122 \times 10^{5}}{187} \approx 600 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 5:** Compute degree of polymerisation (M₀ of ethylene oxide = 44 g/mol).

$$DP_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{600}{44} \approx 13.6$$

Therefore the oligomer has roughly **13–14 ethylene oxide repeat units** (PEG-600).

### 3.2 Derivation: Mark-Houwink Parameters for PEO in Water (25 °C)

For PEO the empirical constants are **K = 1.25 × 10⁻⁴ dL/g** and **a = 0.78**. Compute intrinsic viscosity for $M_w = 10^5$:

**Step 1:** Substitute.

$$[\eta] = (1.25 \times 10^{-4})\,(10^5)^{0.78}$$

**Step 2:** Resolve the exponent.

$$10^{5 \times 0.78} = 10^{3.90} \approx 7943$$

**Step 3:** Multiply.

$$[\eta] \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 7943 \approx 0.99 \;\text{dL/g}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret — PEO of 100 kDa in water behaves as a swollen coil of intrinsic viscosity ~1 dL/g, explaining its use as a **thickener** in aqueous propellant simulants and space-suit coolant loops.

### 3.3 Derivation: Glass Transition via Fox Equation for a PEG–PPG Blend

Suppose a 60:40 (w/w) PEG (Tg₁ = 213 K) / PPG (Tg₂ = 198 K) blend.

**Step 1:** Apply Fox equation with weight fractions $w_1 = 0.60$, $w_2 = 0.40$.

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.60}{213} + \frac{0.40}{198}$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term.

$$\frac{0.60}{213} = 2.817 \times 10^{-3}, \quad \frac{0.40}{198} = 2.020 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Sum.

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 4.837 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Invert.

$$T_g = \frac{1}{4.837 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 206.7 \;\text{K} \approx -66.5^{\circ}\text{C}$$

This ultra-low Tg is why PEG/PPG blends remain **rubbery at cryogenic orbital temperatures**, vital for soft-seal applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture: Polyether Role in a Space Propulsion System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ethylene Oxide Monomer] --> B[Anionic ROP Reactor]
    B --> C[PEO Polyol diol]
    D[Propylene Oxide Monomer] --> E[Cationic ROP Reactor]
    E --> F[PPG Polyol diol]
    C --> G[PU Foam Reactor with diisocyanate]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Cryogenic Tank Insulation Foam]
    C --> I[Propellant Binder Mixer]
    F --> I
    I --> J[Solid Rocket Propellant Grain]
    K[THF Monomer] --> L[Cationic ROP]
    L --> M[PTHF diol]
    M --> N[Spandex-class Elastic Fibre]
    N --> O[Astronaut Pressure Suit Layer]
```

### 4.2 Reaction Schematic: Anionic ROP of Ethylene Oxide

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Initiator RO negative ion] --> stepB[Nucleophilic attack on epoxide ring]
    stepB --> stepC[Alkoxide chain end formed]
    stepC --> stepD[Next EO insertion propagation]
    stepD --> stepE[Growing PEO alkoxide chain]
    stepE --> stepF[Termination by proton donor methanol]
    stepF --> stepG[Neutral PEO diol HO-PEO-OH]
```

### 4.3 Property–Structure Map for Polyethers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[C-O-C ether linkage] --> nodeB[Wide bond angle 112 deg]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Low rotational barrier 4.5 kJ per mol]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Hydrogen bond acceptor]
    nodeB --> nodeE[High chain flexibility]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Very low Tg -60 to -80 deg C]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Polyurethane reactivity]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Aerospace foam and elastomer]
    nodeF --> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above depict **block-level functional architecture** rather than physical drawings, ensuring Mermaid renders reliably without syntax conflicts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define polyethers. Write the general structure and name two important members used in space applications.

**Model Answer:**
Polyethers are polymers whose repeating unit contains the ether linkage **–C–O–C–** along the main chain. General structure: **–[–O–R–]ₙ–** where R is an aliphatic or aromatic residue.
Two space-relevant members:
1. **Poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO/PEG)** – binder precursor for polyurethane cryogenic foam insulation.
2. **Poly(propylene oxide) (PPG)** – binder polyol for solid propellant grains and flexible PU foams.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Explain why polyethers exhibit very low glass transition temperatures.

**Model Answer:**
The ether oxygen (sp³ hybridised) in the backbone widens the C–O–C bond angle and drastically lowers the rotational energy barrier (~4.5 kJ/mol vs ~12 kJ/mol for C–C). The chain therefore undergoes segmental motion at very low temperatures, giving Tg values between **–60 °C and –80 °C** for PEO/PPG/PTHF. This low Tg ensures rubbery behaviour in cryogenic space environments.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Module Choice Option

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the ring-opening polymerisation mechanism of ethylene oxide using an anionic initiator. Derive the rate expression and indicate how molecular weight is controlled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the use of polyether polyols in the manufacture of polyurethane foams for cryogenic tank insulation in launch vehicles. List four property requirements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

*Mechanism* — Initiation by an alkoxide (RO⁻, typically KOH or NaOMe activated) attacks the strained three-membered epoxide ring of ethylene oxide. The ring opens to generate a new primary alkoxide at the chain end. Propagation proceeds by repeated nucleophilic attack of the chain-end alkoxide on fresh EO monomer. Termination occurs upon protonation (e.g., with methanol) yielding a terminal –OH.

*Rate expression* — For anionic ROP with constant active centre concentration [C⁻]:

$$R_p = -\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M][C^{\ominus}]$$

Because [C⁻] is constant (no termination in living polymerisation), kinetics are pseudo-first-order in monomer.

$$\ln \frac{[M]_0}{[M]} = k_p [C^{\ominus}] \, t$$

*Molecular weight control* — Since the polymerisation is "living," $DP_n$ is set by the monomer-to-initiator molar ratio:

$$DP_n = \frac{[M]_0}{[I]_0}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

Polyether polyols (PEG or PPG diols/triols, MW 500–6000) react with aromatic diisocyanates (MDI, TDI) or aliphatic ones (IPDI, HDI) in the presence of a blowing agent (water → CO₂ or pentane), surfactant, and amine catalyst. The resulting rigid/flexible PU foam is sprayed or poured into cryogenic tank walls.

Four property requirements:
1. **Closed-cell content > 90 %** to block convective gas flow.
2. **Thermal conductivity ≤ 0.022 W/m·K** at 20 °C (NASA-grade).
3. **Density 30–50 kg/m³** for low-mass penalty.
4. **Cryogenic flexibility** — Tg below –180 °C to survive LOX/LH₂ temperatures without microcracking.
5. (Bonus) Low outgassing per **ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C** (TML < 1 %, CVCM < 0.1 %).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice Option

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare the structures, glass transition temperatures, and space applications of PEO, PPG, and PTHF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEG sample has a hydroxyl number of 56 mg KOH/g. Calculate its number-average molecular weight and degree of polymerisation. Given K = 1.25 × 10⁻⁴ dL/g and a = 0.78 for PEO in water at 25 °C, find the intrinsic viscosity for this polymer assuming $M_w \approx M_n$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Property | PEO | PPG | PTHF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeating unit | –CH₂CH₂O– | –CH₂CH(CH₃)O– | –(CH₂)₄O– |
| Tg (°C) | –67 | –75 | –75 |
| Tm (°C) | 66 | amorphous | 43 |
| Space application | Sublimation coolant precursor, hydrogel, binder | Polyurethane foam polyol, propellant binder | Elastic fibre for space-suit layer |

The methyl side group in PPG disrupts crystallinity, yielding a fully amorphous, low-Tg rubber. PEO is semicrystalline due to chain symmetry. PTHF bridges the two — semicrystalline but with even lower Tg.

**Model Solution (b):**

*Step 1:* For a difunctional polyol (f = 2):

$$M_n = \frac{56.1 \times 1000 \times 2}{\text{OH\#}} = \frac{1.122 \times 10^{5}}{56} \approx 2004 \;\text{g/mol}$$

*Step 2:* Degree of polymerisation (M₀ of EO = 44 g/mol):

$$DP_n = \frac{2004}{44} \approx 45.5$$

*Step 3:* Mark-Houwink intrinsic viscosity (M = 2004):

$$[\eta] = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times (2004)^{0.78}$$

$$2004^{0.78} = 10^{0.78 \times \log_{10} 2004} = 10^{0.78 \times 3.302} = 10^{2.576} \approx 376.6$$

$$[\eta] \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 376.6 \approx 0.0471 \;\text{dL/g}$$

This corresponds to a moderately low-viscosity liquid polyol, ideal for foam processing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Writing the ether linkage as **–C–O–H** (alcohol) instead of **–C–O–C–** → −1 mark.
> 2. Forgetting to use **f (functionality)** in the OH-number equation → 7-mark derivation zeroed.
> 3. Confusing **DP_n** (number-average) with **DP_w** (weight-average) when the question specifies number-average.
> 4. Using vertical pipes \vert inside formula tables — always render absolute values using \vert or \mid in LaTeX.
> 5. Skipping units in the final numerical answer → −0.5 mark per occurrence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Polyethers = polymers with **–C–O–C–** in the backbone; high flexibility; very low Tg.
- Major members: **PEO, PPG, PTHF, PECH, phenoxy resins**.
- Main synthesis: **ring-opening polymerisation** (anionic for EO/PO, cationic for THF).
- $M_n$ of polyether polyols is determined by **hydroxyl number** using $M_n = 56.1 \times 1000 \times f / \text{OH\#}$.
- Intrinsic viscosity follows **Mark-Houwink**: $[\eta] = K M^a$.
- Tg of blends obeys the **Fox equation** in reciprocal form.
- Space roles: **polyurethane foam insulation, propellant binders, pressure-suit elastic fibres, hydrogel coolants**.
- Crystallinity follows chain symmetry: PEO and PTHF crystallise; PPG is amorphous due to pendant CH₃.
- Oxygen lone pairs drive **hydrogen-bond acceptance**, the chemical origin of polyurethane reactivity.
- Always use **\vert** (not \pipe) for absolute value inside markdown tables.
- Remember **f = 2 for diols, 3 for triols** — functionality controls network architecture in final PU.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyether Sulphones
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Polyether Sulphones # Polyether Sulphones (PES) — A High-Performance Aerospace Thermoplastic ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Polyether Sulphone (PES)** is an amorphous, linear, aromatic high-performance thermoplastic belonging to the polyarylethersulphone (PAES) family. Its macromolecular backbone is composed of alternating **aryl ether (-Ar-O-Ar-)** linkages and **aryl sulphone (-Ar-SO₂-Ar-)** linkages connected through *para*-substituted phenylene rings, giving the generic repeat unit: $$\bigl[-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{SO}_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\bigr]_n$$ In strict KTU 2024 terminology, PES is classified as a **semi-rigid chain aromatic heterocyclic thermoplastic** with a **glass transition temperature (T_g) of approximately 220–230 °C**, a continuous service temperature up to **180 °C**, and an oxygen index greater than **38 %**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PES belongs to the "**Polysulphones**" sub-class of high-performance thermoplastics, alongside Polysulphone (PSU) and Polyphenylsulphone (PPSU). The presence of the **isopropylidene group [-C(CH₃)₂-]** derived from Bisphenol-A distinguishes PES from PSU. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **chain-link fence** where every alternate "twist" in the wire is a super-strong sulphone bridge and the connecting rods are flexible ether linkages. The sulphone group acts like a **rigid, heat-proof clasp** that holds the structure together at extreme temperatures, while the ether oxygen acts like a **flexible hinge** that allows the chain to bend without breaking. Together, they produce a plastic that is simultaneously **tough, transparent, thermally stable, and flame-resistant** — exactly what satellite interiors and rocket insulation demand. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Meaning:** PES is the engineering plastic you choose when you need the clarity of glass, the toughness of metal, and the heat-resistance of a ceramic, all in one lightweight, mouldable sheet. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Value | Significance for Space Use | |---|---|---| | Density (ρ) | **1.37 g cm⁻³** | Lightweight — minimises launch mass | | Glass Transition (T_g) | **225 °C** | Retains shape near re-entry heating zones | | Tensile Strength | **83 MPa** | Load-bearing structural capability | | Oxygen Index (LOI) | **38 %** | Self-extinguishing in pure O₂ atmosphere | | Dielectric Strength | **16 kV mm⁻¹** | Excellent insulation for avionics | | Refractive Index | **1.65** | Optical-grade transparency | | Water Absorption (24 h) | **0.43 %** | Stable in humid/space vacuum cycling | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the PES Repeat Unit The PES repeat unit is a **copolymer of three aromatic blocks** joined by two functional linkages: 1. **Bisphenol-A block** — supplies the *isopropylidene* bridge `C(CH₃)₂` which gives chain flexibility. 2. **Diphenyl Sulphone block** — supplies the `SO₂` group, the *backbone of thermal stability*. 3. **Ether linkages (-O-)** — connect the two aromatic blocks and provide rotational freedom. $$\text{Block A (BPA unit)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Ether oxygen} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Block B (DPS unit)}$$ ### 2.2 Why the Backbone Geometry Works in Space - The **SO₂ group** withdraws electron density from the adjacent phenylene rings (strong −M and −I effects). This **deactivates the ring against oxidative degradation** under UV/atomic-oxygen attack in LEO. - The **C–S bond** has high bond dissociation energy (~**272 kJ mol⁻¹**), resisting thermal scission up to ~**400 °C**. - The **ether linkage** has a low rotational barrier (~**8 kJ mol⁻¹**) but is *conjugated* with the aromatic system, giving a partial double-bond character that raises the chain rigidity. - The combined effect: an amorphous polymer with **no melting point** (only T_g) but with dimensional stability far superior to commodity plastics. ### 2.3 Mechanism of Property Generation | Functional Group | Property Delivered | Reason | |---|---|---| | Aromatic rings | High T_g, UV resistance | π-conjugation, resonance stabilisation | | Sulphone (-SO₂-) | Thermal stability, flame retardancy | Electron-withdrawing, high S=O bond energy (~535 kJ/mol) | | Ether (-O-) | Chain flexibility, processability | Low torsional barrier | | Isopropylidene | Solubility, melt flow | Disrupts crystallinity, increases free volume | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The same sulphone group that gives PES its high T_g also makes it transparent. Because the chain is **amorphous** (no crystalline regions to scatter light), PES films are used as **thermal-control windows and viewports** in spacecraft. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | g mol⁻¹ | | 2 | $\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | g mol⁻¹ | | 3 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$ | Polydispersity index (≈ 2 for condensation) | dimensionless | | 4 | $\eta_{\text{inh}} = \dfrac{\ln(t/t_0)}{c}$ | Inherent viscosity (used to grade PES: 0.36, 0.45, 0.58) | dL g⁻¹ | | 5 | $D_P = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Carothers' degree of polymerisation (p = conversion) | dimensionless | | 6 | $T_g \approx \dfrac{2}{3}\,T_m\,(\text{K})$ | Empirical rule (Boyer–Beaman) for semi-crystalline polymers | K | | 7 | $\tau_c = \tau_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius thermal endurance life | s | | 8 | $\rho_{\text{composite}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_f}{\rho_f}+\dfrac{w_m}{\rho_m}}$ | Rule of mixtures for fibre/matrix density (for PES-CF composites) | g cm⁻³ | | 9 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures for composite modulus (parallel loading) | MPa | | 10 | $\Delta H_{\text{decomp}} \geq 25\,\text{kJ g}^{-1}$ | Criterion for char-forming, self-extinguishing polymer | kJ g⁻¹ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse PES (Poly**ether** Sulphone) with PPSU (Poly**phenyl** Sulphone) or PSU (Poly**sulphone**). The presence of the Bisphenol-A `C(CH₃)₂` bridge is the *defining* feature of PES. PSU uses Bisphenol-A *plus* Bisphenol-S; PPSU replaces BPA entirely with *p*-phenylene. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Synthesis of PES via Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution (NAS) The industrial route to PES is the **step-growth polycondensation** of the **disodium salt of Bisphenol-A** with **4,4′-dichlorodiphenyl sulphone (DCDPS)** in a high-boiling polar aprotic solvent (e.g., *N*-methyl-2-pyrrolidone, NMP) at **180–220 °C**. ### Step 1 — Deprotonation of Bisphenol-A Bisphenol-A reacts with two equivalents of sodium hydroxide to form the **disodium phenolate** (nucleophile). $$\text{HO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{NaOH}$$ $$\longrightarrow\;\text{NaO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{ONa} \;+\; 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The phenoxide anion (ArO⁻) is the **active nucleophile** in the next step. ### Step 2 — Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution (S_NAr) The phenoxide attacks the *ipso*-carbon of the C–Cl bond on DCDPS, forming a *Meisenheimer complex*, which then eliminates NaCl. $$\text{ArO}^{-}\,+\,\text{Cl}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{SO}_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{Cl}$$ $$\longrightarrow\;\text{ArO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{SO}_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{Cl}\;+\;\text{Cl}^{-}$$ The newly formed ether linkage creates a new *para*-chloro end-group, ready for chain extension. ### Step 3 — Chain Propagation (Alternating Coupling) The reaction proceeds alternately from both sides of the DCDPS unit, building the high-molar-mass polymer. $$\begin{aligned} \text{DCDPS} &+ \text{NaO-BPA-NaO} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cl}-\text{Ar}-\text{SO}_2-\text{Ar}-\text{O}-\text{BPA}-\text{O}-\text{Ar}-\text{SO}_2-\text{Ar}-\text{Cl} \\ &\quad\downarrow\;\text{+ NaO-BPA-NaO}\\ &\text{Cl}-[\text{Ar-SO}_2\text{-Ar-O-BPA-O}]_2\text{-Ar-SO}_2\text{-Ar-Cl}\;+\;2\,\text{NaCl} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — By-product Removal and Recovery The released NaCl is filtered off; the polymer is precipitated in methanol/water, washed, and dried under vacuum at **120 °C** to constant mass. > [!NOTE] > **Stoichiometric Balance is Critical:** Since this is an **A–B step-growth polymerisation**, an equimolar ratio of `ArO⁻ : ArCl` groups is essential. Even a **0.1 mol % stoichiometric imbalance** can halve the achievable molecular weight. ## 3.2 Derivation of Carothers' Equation Applied to PES For a strictly equimolar **AA + BB** system, let `p` be the *fraction of functional groups* that have reacted. Each reaction consumes **two** functional groups and forms **one** polymer bond. The number-average degree of polymerisation is: $$\begin{aligned} D_P &= \dfrac{\text{Initial molecules}}{\text{Remaining molecules}} \\ &= \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{1-p} \end{aligned}$$ For PES at **p = 0.99** (99 % conversion): $$D_P = \dfrac{1}{1-0.99} = \dfrac{1}{0.01} = 100$$ So the repeat-unit count per chain is **100**, giving a typical commercial PES $\overline{M}_n \approx 22{,}000$–$30{,}000$ g mol⁻¹. For **p = 0.999**: $$D_P = \dfrac{1}{1-0.999} = \dfrac{1}{0.001} = 1000$$ which corresponds to $\overline{M}_n \approx 2.3\times10^5$ g mol⁻¹ — the engineering-grade "high-viscosity" PES grades. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PES vs. PSU vs. PPSU | Property | PSU (Polysulphone) | PES (Polyethersulphone) | PPSU (Polyphenylsulphone) | |---|---|---|---| | Repeat unit bridge | `C(CH₃)₂` + SO₂ | `C(CH₃)₂` (BPA only) | direct *p*-phenylene | | T_g (°C) | 190 | **225** | 220 | | Continuous service (°C) | 160 | **180** | 180 | | Tensile strength (MPa) | 70 | **83** | 70 | | Impact strength (kJ m⁻²) | 6.9 | **8.4** | 12 (highest) | | Transparency | yes | **yes (highest)** | slightly amber | | Sterilisable (steam, 134 °C) | limited | limited | **yes** | | Space ranking | good | **best overall** | best impact | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PES Repeat Unit Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["BPA unit
C6H4 C(CH3)2 C6H4"] nodeB["Ether oxygen O"] nodeC["DPS unit
C6H4 SO2 C6H4"] nodeD["Repeat bond n"] nodeE["Sulphone SO2 group"] nodeF["Isopropylidene
C(CH3)2"] nodeG["Aromatic ring C6H4"] nodeA -- ether linkage --> nodeB nodeB -- to DPS --> nodeC nodeC -- repeat --> nodeD nodeD -- back to --> nodeA nodeE --> nodeC nodeF --> nodeA nodeG --> nodeA nodeG --> nodeC ``` ## 4.2 PES Synthesis Flow — Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Bisphenol A
HO C6H4 C(CH3)2 C6H4 OH"] stepB["NaOH 2 equivalents
180 C in NMP"] stepC["Disodium phenolate
NaO Ar C(CH3)2 Ar ONa"] stepD["4,4 Dichlorodiphenyl
sulphone DCDPS"] stepE["SNAr attack
Meisenheimer complex"] stepF["Eliminate NaCl
chain extension"] stepG["Polyethersulphone PES
Mn 22000 to 30000"] stepH["By product NaCl
filtered off"] stepI["Precipitate in
methanol water"] stepJ["Dry at 120 C
vacuum"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepF --> stepH stepG --> stepI stepI --> stepJ ``` ## 4.3 Space-Application Block Diagram of PES ```mermaid flowchart TD appA["PES Polymer
Raw Resin"] appB["Injection
Moulding"] appC["Extrusion
Film and Sheet"] appD["Solution
Casting"] appE["Composite Layup
CF or GF reinforced"] appF["Satellite
PCB substrate"] appG["Spacecraft
thermal blanket"] appH["Rocket
insulation layer"] appI["Window and
viewport panel"] appJ["Aerospace
structural bracket"] appA --> appB appA --> appC appA --> appD appA --> appE appB --> appF appB --> appJ appC --> appG appC --> appH appD --> appI appE --> appJ ``` ## 4.4 Property-Origin Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR origin1["Sulphone SO2"] origin2["Aromatic rings"] origin3["Ether O linkage"] origin4["Isopropylidene
C(CH3)2"] propA["Thermal stability
up to 400 C"] propB["Flame retardancy
LOI 38"] propC["High Tg 225 C"] propD["Amorphous
transparent"] propE["Chemical
resistance"] propF["Processability
melt flow"] origin1 --> propA origin1 --> propB origin2 --> propC origin2 --> propD origin3 --> propD origin3 --> propF origin4 --> propF origin4 --> propE ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Polyether Sulphone. Write its repeat unit and report its glass transition temperature.** **Model Answer:** Polyether Sulphone (PES) is an amorphous, aromatic, high-performance thermoplastic of the polyarylethersulphone family, characterised by alternating aryl-ether and aryl-sulphone linkages in its backbone, derived from Bisphenol-A and 4,4′-dichlorodiphenyl sulphone. $$\bigl[-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{SO}_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\bigr]_n$$ **T_g ≈ 225 °C** (continuous service temperature ≈ 180 °C). --- ### Q2.
