[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any three properties of PES that make it suitable for spacecraft interior applications.** **Model Answer:** 1. **High heat resistance** — T_g of 225 °C allows survival near re-entry heating zones and solar flux. 2. **Self-extinguishing** — LOI of 38 % prevents flame propagation inside the pure-oxygen cabin. 3. **Optical transparency + low density (1.37 g cm⁻³)** — allows manufacturing of lightweight viewport panels and inspection windows. 4. *(Any additional correct point: excellent dielectric strength, radiation tolerance, low moisture absorption.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT746 Module 1]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **synthesis of Polyether Sulphone** by the nucleophilic aromatic substitution route, with a balanced chemical equation. Discuss the role of each reactant and solvent. **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **thermal, mechanical, and flame-retardant properties** of PES in detail and explain how each property arises from its chemical structure. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Reactants and their roles:** - **Bisphenol-A (BPA):** provides the `ArO⁻` nucleophile and the isopropylidene bridge. - **4,4′-Dichlorodiphenyl Sulphone (DCDPS):** provides the sulphone linkage and the *para*-chloro leaving group. - **NaOH (2 equivalents):** deprotonates BPA → disodium phenolate. - **NMP / DMSO / Sulpholane:** polar aprotic high-boiling solvent; stabilises the phenoxide anion and dissolves the growing chain. **Step 2 — Balanced Equation:** $$n\,\text{HO-BPA-OH} \;+\; n\,\text{Cl-DPS-Cl} \;+\; 2n\,\text{NaOH}$$ $$\longrightarrow\; \bigl[-\text{O-BPA-O-DPS}-\bigr]_n \;+\; 2n\,\text{NaCl} \;+\; 2n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 3 — Mechanism:** Phenoxide → S_NAr on *ipso*-carbon of DCDPS → Meisenheimer intermediate → expulsion of Cl⁻ → ether bond formation. **Step 4 — Why this route is preferred:** High regio-selectivity (*para* only), no isomers formed, high yields, and easy NaCl removal. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Thermal properties:** - T_g = **225 °C** — arises from the rigid aromatic backbone and the polar SO₂ group restricting segmental motion. - Heat-deflection temperature (1.82 MPa) = **200 °C** — supports load-bearing at spacecraft interior temperatures. - Continuous service temperature = **180 °C**. - Decomposition onset (TGA, N₂) ≈ **500 °C**. **Mechanical properties:** - Tensile strength ≈ **83 MPa**, tensile modulus ≈ **2.6 GPa** — due to π-stacking of aromatic rings. - Elongation at break ≈ **30 %** — ether linkages allow ductility. - Notched Izod impact ≈ **85 J m⁻¹** — tough, not brittle. **Flame retardancy:** - LOI = **38 %**; UL-94 V-0 at **1.5 mm**. - Mechanism: aromatic backbone → high char yield; SO₂ → release of non-combustible SO₂ gas during decomposition, dilutes oxygen. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. Writing only "high T_g" without quantifying (225 °C) — full 7 marks demand numerical values. > 2. Confusing PES with PSU or PPSU; the *defining* BPA unit and the *para*-ether linkage must appear in any repeat-unit drawing. > 3. Not mentioning the SO₂ *flame-retardant mechanism* (gas-phase dilution + solid-phase char) — only structural recitation earns partial credit. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT746 Module 1]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the **structure, properties, and applications** of **PES, PSU, and PPSU**. Identify which is most suitable for thermal blankets in spacecraft. **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe **three specific space applications** of Polyether Sulphone and justify the property requirement for each. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Structure:** - **PSU:** BPA + Bisphenol-S — two bisphenol bridges per repeat unit → more sulphone, slightly lower T_g (190 °C). - **PES:** BPA only + DPS → one bisphenol bridge → T_g 225 °C. - **PPSU:** *p*-phenylene + DPS → no BPA at all → highest impact strength. **Property Matrix:** - Highest T_g → PES (225 °C) - Highest impact → PPSU - Best transparency → PES - Best for steam sterilisation → PPSU - Most cost-effective → PSU **Most suitable for thermal blankets → PES**, because: 1. Continuous service of 180 °C covers re-entry heating. 2. Amorphous film is dimensionally stable from −50 °C to +200 °C. 3. Low thermal conductivity (0.18 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹) is ideal for insulating layers. #### Model Solution — Part (b) 1. **Satellite PCB substrate** — PES has dielectric strength of 16 kV mm⁻¹ and dielectric constant 3.5, allowing reliable high-frequency signal transmission on rigid, dimensionally stable boards. 2. **Rocket motor insulation liner** — char yield > 40 % at 700 °C provides ablative protection; the released SO₂ cools the underlying metal. 3. **Spacecraft interior viewport** — optical transparency (transmittance ≈ 88 % at 550 nm) combined with impact resistance makes PES ideal for crew observation windows. 4. **Structural brackets (CF-reinforced)** — CF/PES composite gives modulus > 70 GPa with density < 1.6 g cm⁻³, replacing aluminium in bracket assemblies. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **PES = Poly(ether sulphone)** = BPA + DCDPS, A–B step-growth polymer. - **Repeat unit has three aromatic rings, two ether linkages, one sulphone group, and one isopropylidene bridge.** - **T_g ≈ 225 °C**, continuous service 180 °C, LOI 38 %, density 1.37 g cm⁻³. - **Synthesis route is Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution (S_NAr)** in NMP at 180–220 °C. - **Carothers' equation**: $D_P = 1/(1-p)$; stoichiometric balance of `ArO⁻ : ArCl` must be exact. - **Distinguish PES from PSU and PPSU**: PSU has additional Bisphenol-S bridge; PPSU has direct *p*-phenylene without BPA. - **Space applications**: PCB substrate, thermal blanket, viewport, ablative insulation, structural composite bracket. - **Property origin map**: - SO₂ → thermal stability + flame retardancy - Aromatic ring → rigidity + T_g + UV resistance - Ether → flexibility + processability - Isopropylidene → solubility + impact toughness - **KTU favourite numerics**: 225 °C, 180 °C, 38 %, 83 MPa, 1.37 g cm⁻³, 16 kV mm⁻¹ — memorise them. - **Common exam traps**: mis-identifying PSU as PES, omitting the BPA unit, quoting T_g as 190 °C instead of 225 °C. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on PES, always present (i) the **repeat unit drawn cleanly**, (ii) **at least three property values with units**, and (iii) the **structure–property linkage** for each property. This single habit typically secures 12+/14 marks.

topics.title: Synthesis and Processing of Thermal Protection Systems in Space Research
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Synthesis and Processing of Thermal Protection Systems in Space Research

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Thermal Protection System (TPS)** is an engineered assembly of materials and structures designed to shield a spacecraft, launch vehicle, or reentry capsule from the extreme aerothermal loads generated during hypersonic flight, atmospheric reentry, or prolonged exposure to solar/cosmic radiation. In the context of polymer science, TPS materials are predominantly **polymer-matrix composites, ablatives, and ceramic insulators** whose physical and chemical response to heat is deliberately engineered to maintain the underlying structural substrate below its maximum allowable temperature (typically **≤ 180 °C** for aluminium alloys and **≤ 125 °C** for crewed vehicle pressure vessels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** A TPS does not "block" heat. It **manages** heat through one or a combination of three physical mechanisms: **insulation, ablation, and active rejection (radiation/transpiration)**.

### 1.2 Why Polymers are the Cornerstone of TPS

Polymers — both **thermosets** (phenolic, cyanate ester, polyimide) and **thermo-softening resins** used as precursors — dominate TPS design because they uniquely combine:

1. **Low thermal conductivity** ($k$ in the range **0.05 – 1.0 W/m·K**, compared to **205 W/m·K** for aluminium).
2. **Endothermic decomposition** (heat absorption during pyrolysis is typically **1 – 5 MJ/kg**).
3. **High char yield** (phenolic resins yield **> 50 %** residual carbon upon pyrolysis).
4. **Low density** (ablative TPS densities range from **150 – 1800 kg/m³**, vital for launch-mass budgets).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Firefighter's Bunker Gear

Think of a TPS as the **firefighter's bunker suit**:

- The **outer layer of the suit is the ablative polymer**. As the firefighter enters a furnace-like environment, the outer polymer **chars, melts, and blows away**, but in doing so it carries heat away and shields the body.
- The **thick fibrous batting beneath the suit is the insulator**. Even after the outer surface is gone, the inner layers slow heat conduction to the body.
- The **reflective aluminised face is the radiative reflector**, bouncing radiant heat back outward.

A spacecraft reentering Earth's atmosphere from orbital velocity (**~7.8 km/s**) or lunar-return velocity (**~11 km/s**) generates surface heating in the order of **1 – 250 MW/m²** at the stagnation point — comparable to the heat flux on the Sun-facing side of a rocket nozzle.

### 1.4 Classification of TPS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification Table**

| Class | Operating Principle | Typical Polymer Material | Reusability | Example Flight Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ablative** | Endothermic decomposition + transpiration | Phenolic resin, Avcoat, PICA | **One-time** | Apollo CM, Stardust, SpaceX Dragon PICA-X |
| **Reusable Surface Insulation (RSI)** | Low-$k$ insulation + radiation | Fibrous silica, LI-900 | **Reusable** | Space Shuttle tiles |
| **Flexible Insulation** | Reflective foils + low-$k$ substrate | Nylon / Dacron mesh, Mylar foils | **Reusable** | Space Shuttle aft fuselage |
| **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)** | High-temp ceramic reinforcement | SiC/SiC, C/SiC | **Reusable** | X-38, NASA X-43 leading edges |
| **Carbon–Carbon (C/C)** | Sublimation of carbon + radiation | PAN-precursor C-fibre + phenolic | **Reusable** | Space Shuttle nose & wing leading edge |
| **Active / Transpiration** | Coolant mass injection | Porous polymer with fluid feed | **Reusable** | Experimental, transpiration-cooled thrust chambers |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Insight:** "Reusability" here is a *design intent*. A C/C panel is rated for **~100 Shuttle missions**, but still loses mass by sublimation each time. No TPS is truly "free of mass loss" except RSI fibrous silica, which loses only a small amount by surface oxidation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Heat-Transfer Mechanisms a TPS Must Combat

**2.1.1 Convective (Gaseous) Heating** — Dominant at the stagnation point during reentry.

The classical engineering correlation for stagnation-point convective heating (valid for **Earth, Mars, and Venus** atmospheres) is the **Sutton–Graves equation**:

$$q_{conv} = K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_\infty}{r_n}} \cdot V^3$$

where $\rho_\infty$ is free-stream density, $r_n$ is nose radius, $V$ is velocity, and $K$ is a chemistry-dependent constant.

**2.1.2 Radiative Heating** — Dominant for lunar-return (no atmosphere → no convective cooling) and super-orbital reentry.

The radiative flux from a shock layer scales as:

$$q_{rad} \propto \rho_\infty^{\,1.5} \cdot r_n^{\,0.5} \cdot V^{\,8}$$

**2.1.3 Conductive Heating (into the TPS substrate)** — Once heat has entered the TPS body, it conducts inward by Fourier's law:

$$q_{cond} = -k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}$$

### 2.2 The Physics of Ablation — A Five-Stage Process

When a polymer ablative is exposed to high heat flux, the following sequence occurs:

1. **Solid heating** — Polymer temperature rises from initial to decomposition onset (typically **200 – 400 °C** for phenolic).
2. **Pyrolysis zone** — Thermoplastic decomposition; polymer breaks into volatile gases and a **char matrix**; this step is **strongly endothermic**.
3. **Char layer formation** — A rigid, porous, carbon-rich residue. This layer has low thermal conductivity, so it **insulates** the virgin polymer behind it.
4. **Boundary-layer transpiration cooling** — The escaping pyrolysis gases are injected into the boundary layer, **displacing hot freestream gas** and reducing convective heat transfer.
5. **Surface recession (mass loss)** — The surface recedes at velocity $\dot{s}$ as the char is mechanically and thermo-chemically eroded.

### 2.3 Energy Balance at the Ablating Surface

Conservation of energy at the receding surface yields the fundamental ablation equation:

$$q_{inc} = q_{rad,out} + q_{conv,out} + \dot{m} \cdot h_{eff}$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass-loss rate per unit area and $h_{eff}$ is the **effective heat of ablation** (typically **MJ/kg**, not kJ/kg — this is one of the largest energy sinks in any engineered material).

### 2.4 Surface Recession Velocity

The linear recession rate of the TPS surface is:

$$\dot{s} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{TPS}}$$

A **lower $\rho_{TPS}$** (lightweight ablator, e.g., PICA at **≈ 270 kg/m³**) yields **higher recession** for the same mass loss — a key design trade-off.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this module.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sutton–Graves stagnation heating** | $q_{conv} = K \sqrt{\rho_\infty / r_n} \cdot V^3$ | $\rho_\infty$: kg/m³, $r_n$: m, $V$: m/s, $K$: kg^0.5·m^(-0.5)·s³ | Convective heat flux at nose |
| 2 | **Fourier conduction** | $q_{cond} = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$: W/m·K, $T$: K, $x$: m | Heat conducted inward per unit area |
| 3 | **Ablation energy balance** | $q_{inc} = q_{rad} + \dot{m} h_{eff}$ | $q$: W/m², $\dot{m}$: kg/m²·s, $h_{eff}$: J/kg | Energy at the ablating surface |
| 4 | **Recession velocity** | $\dot{s} = \dot{m} / \rho_{TPS}$ | $\dot{s}$: m/s, $\dot{m}$: kg/m²·s, $\rho_{TPS}$: kg/m³ | Rate of surface loss |
| 5 | **Thermal diffusivity** | $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ | $\alpha$: m²/s, $c_p$: J/kg·K | Speed of heat penetration |
| 6 | **Heat-soak thickness** | $\delta \sim \sqrt{\alpha t}$ | $\delta$: m, $t$: s | Penetration depth in time $t$ |
| 7 | **Steady-state char temp gradient** | $q_{cond} = k_{char} (T_s - T_{b}) / L$ | $L$: char thickness (m) | Linear $T$ profile through char |
| 8 | **Stefan-Boltzmann radiation out** | $q_{rad,out} = \varepsilon \sigma T_s^{4}$ | $\varepsilon$: emissivity, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ | Radiative heat rejected from surface |
| 9 | **Effective heat of ablation (composite)** | $h_{eff} = h_{pyrolysis} + (m_{gas}/m_{char}) \cdot \Delta H_{combustion}$ | J/kg | Char-oxidiser contribution |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Spacecraft thermal design:** The values of $q_{conv}$ calculated via Sutton–Graves dictate the choice between ablative, RSI, or active cooling. For $q < 0.5$ MW/m², RSI is sufficient. For $q > 5$ MW/m², ablative is mandatory.
- **Reentry corridor analysis:** $\dot{s}$ and the back-face temperature $T_{b}$ together determine the **ballistic coefficient and entry angle** that a vehicle can survive.
- **Material selection:** $h_{eff}$ is the single most important material property. Apollo Avcoat had $h_{eff} \approx 25$ MJ/kg; PICA reaches $> 40$ MJ/kg with carbon-fibre reinforcement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Recession Velocity from the Energy Balance

**Starting Point:** At steady state, the incident heat flux on an ablating surface is balanced by the outgoing radiative flux plus the energy absorbed by mass loss.

$$q_{inc} = \varepsilon \sigma T_s^{4} + \dot{m} \cdot h_{eff}$$

**Step 1 — Neglect surface radiation initially** (justified for moderate $T_s$ and ablative surfaces where $\varepsilon \sigma T_s^{4} \ll q_{inc}$).

$$q_{inc} \approx \dot{m} \cdot h_{eff}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for mass-loss flux** (this is the *pyrolysis mass flux*).

$$\dot{m} = \frac{q_{inc}}{h_{eff}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass flux to recession velocity** using the bulk TPS density $\rho_{TPS}$.

$$\dot{s} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{TPS}} = \frac{q_{inc}}{\rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{eff}}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the heat-pulse duration $t_{heat}$** to find the total recession depth $\Delta s$:

$$\Delta s = \int_{0}^{t_{heat}} \dot{s}\,dt = \frac{1}{\rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{eff}} \int_{0}^{t_{heat}} q_{inc}(t)\,dt$$

The integral of $q_{inc}$ over time is the **total incident heat load per unit area**, $Q_{total}$.

$$\boxed{\Delta s = \frac{Q_{total}}{\rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{eff}}}$$

**Design Implication:** To minimise ablation depth, the designer increases $\rho_{TPS}$ and selects a material with the **highest possible $h_{eff}$** — both factors favour **carbon-fibre-reinforced phenolic** (PICA family).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — 1-D Heat Conduction Through a Stationary Char Layer

Assume a planar ablating surface at $x = 0$ with surface temperature $T_s$, backed by a stationary char layer of thickness $L$, in turn backed by the pyrolysis front at temperature $T_p$ (the temperature at which the polymer begins to decompose, typically **≈ 573 K** for phenolic).

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier's law** across the char.

$$q_{cond} = k_{char} \frac{T_s - T_p}{L}$$

**Step 2 — At steady state, $q_{cond}$ equals the heat absorbed by the pyrolysis** (which drives the endothermic decomposition) plus any conduction deeper into the virgin material.

$$q_{cond} = \rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{pyrolysis} \cdot \dot{s} + q_{virgin}$$

For a thin back-face assumption (small heat-soak), $q_{virgin} \approx 0$ and:

$$k_{char} \frac{T_s - T_p}{L} = \rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{pyrolysis} \cdot \dot{s}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the char-layer thickness $L$** that develops at steady state:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{k_{char} (T_s - T_p)}{\rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{pyrolysis} \cdot \dot{s}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitution of $\dot{s} = q_{inc}/(\rho_{TPS} h_{eff})$** gives a *closed-form* expression for char thickness in terms of incident flux:

$$L = \frac{k_{char} (T_s - T_p) \cdot h_{eff}}{h_{pyrolysis} \cdot q_{inc}}$$

**Design Implication:** A *high* $k_{char}$ is undesirable (heat leaks inward); a *high* $h_{eff}/h_{pyrolysis}$ ratio is desirable (more heat goes to vaporisation, not conduction). This is why phenolic resins with high char yield dominate the field.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Worked Numerical (Sutton–Graves)

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark style):** A Mars-return probe with nose radius $r_n = 0.6$ m enters Earth's atmosphere at $V = 7.5$ km/s at an altitude where $\rho_\infty = 0.05$ kg/m³. Using $K = 1.7415 \times 10^{-4}$ kg^0.5·m^(-0.5)·s³ (Earth), calculate the stagnation-point heat flux. Comment on whether ablative TPS is required.

**Step 1 — Substitute the values into Sutton–Graves.**

$$q_{conv} = K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_\infty}{r_n}} \cdot V^3$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the density-to-radius ratio.**

$$\frac{\rho_\infty}{r_n} = \frac{0.05}{0.6} = 0.0833 \text{ kg/m}^4$$

$$\sqrt{0.0833} = 0.2887 \text{ kg}^{0.5}/\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Cube the velocity.**

$$V^3 = (7500)^3 = 4.219 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 4 — Multiply all terms.**

$$q_{conv} = (1.7415 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.2887 \times (4.219 \times 10^{11})$$

$$q_{conv} = (1.7415 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2887) \times 4.219 \times 10^{11}$$

$$= 5.027 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.219 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\boxed{q_{conv} \approx 2.12 \times 10^{7} \text{ W/m}^2 = 21.2 \text{ MW/m}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

| Reference Heat Flux | Value (MW/m²) | TPS Class Required |
|---|---|---|
| Concorde cruise heating | ~0.1 | None (airframe aluminium OK) |
| Space Shuttle ascent | ~0.05 | None |
| LEO reentry (typical) | 0.5 – 5 | RSI (silica tiles) |
| LEO reentry (nose) | 5 – 20 | Ablative or RSI |
| **Lunar-return nose** | **20 – 250** | **High-performance ablative mandatory** |

A calculated flux of **21.2 MW/m²** sits in the *lunar-return* regime. **Ablative TPS is mandatory**; silica tiles would crack and ablate too quickly.

**Step 6 — Sanity check using a non-dimensional scaling.** Doubling velocity increases $q$ by a factor of **8**; doubling density increases $q$ by a factor of **1.41**; halving nose radius increases $q$ by a factor of **1.41**. The student should internalise this scaling for the exam.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Classification Block Diagram of TPS Families

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Functional Architecture of an Ablative TPS Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    innodeA[Hypersonic Freestream Gas] -->|convective and radiative flux| surfA[Ablating Surface T equals 3000 K]

flowchart LR
    qinc[Incident Heat Flux q inc] --> qrad[Radiation Out q rad equals eps sigma T to the 4]
    qinc --> qconv[Convection Out q conv equals h T minus T w]
    qinc --> qabl[Ablation Energy Sink m dot h eff]
    qabl --> qdecomp[Polymer Pyrolysis Endothermic]
    qabl --> qtrans[Boundary Layer Transpiration Cooling]
    qdecomp --> chout[Volatile Pyrolysis Gases]
    qtrans --> chout
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern. Part A = 3 marks (short answer, 40–60 words). Part B = 14 marks, **internal choice** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Model answers are written exactly as a Board examiner expects them.

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define "ablative thermal protection" and name two historic space missions that used ablative TPS.**

**Model Answer:**
Ablative thermal protection is a passive thermal-management strategy in which a sacrificial polymer material is deliberately allowed to decompose, char, vaporise, and erode, absorbing heat via endothermic pyrolysis and transpiration cooling while shielding the underlying structure. Historic missions using ablative TPS include the **Apollo Command Module (Avcoat phenolic, 1968–1972)** and the **Stardust sample-return capsule (PICA, 2006)**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**State the Sutton–Graves equation and identify the variable that dominates the stagnation-point heat flux during reentry.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sutton–Graves equation is $q_{conv} = K \sqrt{\rho_\infty / r_n} \cdot V^3$. The dominant variable is the **velocity $V$**, which appears cubed. Doubling the reentry velocity **increases the heat flux by 8 times**, making velocity control (e.g., skip-glide or lifting reentry) the single most effective design strategy for managing reentry heating. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

#### 🔹 QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **five-stage physical process** that occurs when a phenolic ablative is exposed to a hypersonic reentry heat flux. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **steady-state recession depth** of an ablative TPS in terms of the total incident heat load, density, and effective heat of ablation. A spacecraft experiences a heat-pulse of average flux $q_{inc} = 10$ MW/m² for **30 seconds**. If the TPS is PICA with $\rho_{TPS} = 270$ kg/m³ and $h_{eff} = 40$ MJ/kg, calculate the total recession depth. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — (a):**
The five stages are:
1. **Solid heating:** Conduction raises the polymer from initial temperature to the decomposition onset (~300 °C for phenolic). No mass is lost.
2. **Pyrolysis:** Polymer undergoes endothermic scission into volatile gases + solid char. Heat of decomposition is absorbed (≈ 1.5 MJ/kg for phenolic).
3. **Char formation:** A rigid, low-thermal-conductivity carbon residue (~0.5 W/m·K) remains, acting as an *in situ* insulator.
4. **Transpiration cooling:** Pyrolysis gases injected into the boundary layer displace hot freestream gas, reducing convective heat transfer by 30–60 %.
5. **Surface recession:** The char is mechanically and thermo-chemically eroded; the surface recedes at $\dot{s} = \dot{m}/\rho_{TPS}$.

*(Sketch expected: cross-section showing freestream, ablating surface, char zone, pyrolysis zone, virgin polymer, and substrate.)* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — (b):**
From the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$\Delta s = \frac{Q_{total}}{\rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{eff}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $Q_{total}$** assuming constant flux:

$$Q_{total} = q_{inc} \cdot t = (10 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^2) \times 30 \text{ s} = 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ J/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator.**

$$\rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{eff} = 270 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 40 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg} = 1.08 \times 10^{10} \text{ J/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute recession.**

$$\Delta s = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8}{1.08 \times 10^{10}} = 0.0278 \text{ m} = 27.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The TPS surface recedes by **≈ 28 mm**, which is well within the typical 60–80 mm design thickness of a PICA heat shield. The mission is thermally viable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compare the **synthesis routes, polymer structure, and char yields** of *novolac* and *resol* phenolic resins, with reference to their use in ablative TPS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete processing sequence** by which a PAN-based carbon-fibre preform is converted into a PICA flight panel, identifying the temperature window and dominant physical/chemical change at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — (a):**
| Parameter | Novolac Phenolic | Resol Phenolic |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst** | Acidic (HCl, H₂SO₄, oxalic) | Basic (NaOH, NH₄OH, Ba(OH)₂) |
| **P:F molar ratio** | P > F (excess phenol, ~1.0 : 0.8) | P < F (excess formaldehyde, ~1.0 : 1.2–2.0) |
| **Methylol groups** | Absent — only methylene bridges | Present — self-curing on heating |
| **Curing mechanism** | Requires addition of **hexamethylenetetramine (hexa)** at ~150 °C | Self-cures at ~150–180 °C via condensation of -CH₂OH groups |
| **Polymer structure** | Linear / lightly branched; no self-crosslink | Highly branched with hydroxymethyl pendant groups |
| **Char yield** | 45 – 55 % | **55 – 65 %** (higher, due to crosslink density) |
| **TPS use** | Moulding compounds, general purpose | **Preferred for ablative TPS** (Apollo Avcoat used resol-type) |

Resol resins dominate ablative TPS because their **higher crosslink density** gives a higher char yield, which directly translates to a **larger effective heat of ablation** and **lower recession rate**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — (b):**
The full processing sequence for a PICA panel is:

1. **PAN precursor fibre oxidation:** 200 – 300 °C in air; cyclisation of nitrile groups and formation of a ladder polymer. The fibre changes colour from white → yellow → brown → black.
2. **Carbonisation:** 1000 – 1500 °C in nitrogen; non-carbon atoms (H, N, O) are expelled as gases, leaving ~95 % carbon content.
3. **Chopping & mat layup:** Carbon fibres are chopped to ~3 mm length and formed into a low-density random mat (~150 kg/m³).
4. **Resin impregnation:** The mat is vacuum-infiltrated with a low-viscosity resol phenolic resin (the polymer matrix that will become the *char* on reentry).
5. **B-staging (stage-1 cure):** 80 – 120 °C; partial advancement of the resin to reduce flow during final cure.
6. **Final cure:** 150 – 180 °C under pressure (autoclave or compression mould); full crosslinking of the resol resin to a thermoset network.
7. **Post-cure:** 200 – 250 °C in air or inert atmosphere; drives off residual volatiles and maximises char yield.
8. **Density & CT inspection:** Final density must lie in **250 – 300 kg/m³**; ultrasonic and CT scans verify uniform impregnation and absence of voids.
9. **Machining to flight contour:** CNC-machined to the aerodynamic shape of the heat shield.

The flight-quality panel is then bonded to the spacecraft primary structure. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing char yield with heat of ablation.** Char yield is a *mass fraction* (% of mass remaining); heat of ablation is an *energy per unit mass* (J/kg). They are related but distinct. **(−2 marks if confused)**
> 2. **Forgetting units in the Sutton–Graves derivation.** The constant $K$ has *non-SI* dimensions (kg^0.5·m^(-0.5)·s³). Showing unit consistency is worth 1–2 marks. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 3. **Calling Avcoat, PICA, and SLA-561V "the same material."** They are not. Avcoat is a pure phenolic; PICA is a *carbon-fibre-reinforced* phenolic; SLA-561V is a *ceramic-fibre + silicone* reusable insulation. **(−1 mark each time confused)**
> 4. **Skipping the "why" in the five-stage ablation description.** Examiners reward explanation of the *endothermic* nature of pyrolysis and the *transpiration* effect — not just listing the stages. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 5. **Treating RSI tiles as "unlimited life."** The LI-900 silica tiles do erode at high temperature and were replaced after every few Shuttle flights. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Synthesis and Processing of TPS: Things You MUST Remember**

- **Definition:** TPS *manages* heat via insulation, ablation, or active rejection — it does not "block" heat.
- **Three heat-flux modes:** convective (∝ V³), radiative (∝ V⁸), and conductive (Fourier).
- **Sutton–Graves:** $q_{conv} = K \sqrt{\rho_\infty / r_n} \cdot V^3$ — velocity cubed is the design driver.
- **Five stages of ablation:** Solid heating → Pyrolysis → Char formation → Transpiration cooling → Recession.
- **Ablation energy balance:** $q_{inc} = \varepsilon \sigma T_s^4 + \dot{m} \cdot h_{eff}$.
- **Recession depth:** $\Delta s = Q_{total} / (\rho_{TPS} \cdot h_{eff})$ — *master this derivation*.
- **Resol > Novolac** for TPS because of higher crosslink density and higher char yield.
- **PICA processing:** PAN → oxidation (200–300 °C) → carbonisation (1000–1500 °C) → mat → phenolic infiltration → cure → post-cure.
- **Ablative examples:** Apollo Avcoat (pure phenolic), PICA (carbon phenolic), cork phenolic, silicone elastomer.
- **RSI examples:** LI-900 (silica tiles, Shuttle), SLA-561V (silicone-ceramic), FRSI (nylon-Dacron + Mylar).
- **Reusable limit:** No TPS is "free" of mass loss — C/C and RSI each lose measurable mass.
- **Effective heat of ablation values (for exam):** Avcoat ≈ 25 MJ/kg; PICA ≈ 40 MJ/kg; carbon phenolic ≈ 12 MJ/kg.
- **Velocity scaling:** Doubling V → 8× heat flux. Halving nose radius → 1.41× heat flux. Halving altitude (doubling ρ) → 1.41× heat flux.
- **Stagnation point** sees the *highest* flux; **leeward surfaces** see the *lowest*. TPS thickness is tapered accordingly.
- **Unit vigilance:** $h_{eff}$ is in **MJ/kg**, not kJ/kg. $K$ in Sutton–Graves has non-SI units — show the dimensional check.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Temperature Resistant Resins Such as Epoxy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High Temperature Resistant Resins Such as Epoxy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Epoxy Resin (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> An **epoxy resin** is a low-molecular-weight pre-polymer containing more than one **epoxide group** (also called *oxirane* or *glycidyl* group) — a three-membered cyclic ether ring with the structural motif — typically at the termini or along the backbone. When reacted (cured) with a suitable **hardener** (amines, anhydrides, phenols, dicyandiamide, etc.), it undergoes a non-reversible cross-linking reaction to form a **thermoset polymer network** with a characteristic three-dimensional molecular architecture.

The most industrially significant and academically emphasized member is the **DGEBA** (Diglycidyl Ether of Bisphenol-A) family, alongside high-performance variants such as **epoxy novolacs**, **tetraglycidyl methylene dianiline (TGMDA)**, and **cycloaliphatic epoxies**.

### 1.1.1 The Epoxide Functional Group (Oxirane Ring)

The reactive site of every epoxy resin is the three-membered **oxirane ring**:

$$
\ce{R-CH(-O-)CH2}
$$

This strained ring stores a chemical potential energy of approximately **~25 kcal/mol (≈ 105 kJ/mol)** of *ring strain*, which is the thermodynamic driver behind the curing reaction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Lego + Super-Glue" Analogy**
> Imagine each epoxy molecule as a **plastic Lego brick with two magnetic pins sticking out** (the epoxide rings). On their own, the bricks are soft, weak, and slippery. When you pour in a hardener (the *curing agent*), it acts like a **super-glue cross-connector** — each pin locks into multiple connectors from different directions. After curing, the bricks can no longer slide past one another: they are welded into a single rigid **3-D net**. When the space environment blasts this net with extreme heat (re-entry, ±150 °C cycling, solar radiation), the net does not melt — it just absorbs the energy by **micro-vibrating** its cross-links. The higher the cross-link density, the *stiffer* and more *heat-resistant* the net becomes.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range (DGEBA) | High-Temp Grade (TGMDA / Novolac) |
|---|---|---|
| Glass Transition Temp ($T_g$) | 80 – 130 °C | 180 – 260 °C |
| Continuous Service Temp | -55 °C to +150 °C | -55 °C to +200 °C (or higher) |
| Heat Deflection Temp (HDT) | 50 – 120 °C | 150 – 250 °C |
| Tensile Strength | 35 – 90 MPa | 60 – 120 MPa |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) | 60 – 80 × 10⁻⁶ /K | 45 – 65 × 10⁻⁶ /K |
| Cure Shrinkage | 1 – 5 % | 0.5 – 2 % |
| Density | **1.1 – 1.25 g/cm³** | 1.15 – 1.35 g/cm³ |
| Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI) | 18 – 22 | 28 – 45 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For space applications, the typical screening criteria are **(a) $T_g \geq 150 \, ^\circ C$, (b) mass loss ≤ 1 % after 50 thermal cycles of -100 °C / +150 °C, and (c) outgassing per NASA ASTM E595 with TML < 1 % and CVCM < 0.1 %**.

## 1.4 Why Epoxy? — Space-Grade Justification

Epoxies dominate as **matrix resins for fibre-reinforced composites** in launch vehicles, satellites, and re-entry vehicles because they offer the rare combination of:

- High **specific strength** (strength/density ratio)
- Excellent **adhesion** to carbon, glass, aramid, and boron fibres
- **Low outgassing** (low volatile condensable materials)
- **Tunable chemistry** — $T_g$ can be engineered by hardener choice
- Low **moisture absorption** (0.1 – 1.5 % at saturation)
- High **dimensional stability** across thermal-cycling
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Epoxy Resins

### 2.1.1 By Molecular Architecture

1. **Glycidyl-type epoxies**
   - DGEBA (Bisphenol-A based) — *the workhorse of the industry*
   - Bisphenol-F (BPF) epoxy — lower viscosity
   - Epoxy **novolacs** (o-cresol, phenol novolac) — high functionality (epoxy value 4–8 eq/kg)
   - **TGMDA** (Tetraglycidyl-4,4′-diaminodiphenylmethane) — *primary high-Tg aerospace resin*

2. **Non-glycidyl / Cycloaliphatic epoxies**
   - 3,4-Epoxycyclohexylmethyl-3′,4′-epoxycyclohexane carboxylate (ECC)
   - Used in outdoor insulators and UV-stable space coatings

### 2.1.2 Comparison Table for Aerospace Use

| Resin | Functionality (f) | Epoxy Equivalent Weight (EEW) | Achievable $T_g$ (°C) | Space Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DGEBA | 2 | 170 – 190 | 100 – 160 | General structural composites |
| DGEBF (Bis-F) | 2 | 155 – 175 | 90 – 140 | Low-viscosity laminates |
| o-Cresol Novolac Epoxy | 3 – 6 | 190 – 230 | 170 – 210 | High-Tg skins, ablatives |
| TGMDA | 4 | 110 – 135 | 200 – 260 | Solid rocket motor casings, primary structures |
| Cycloaliphatic (ECC) | 2 | 130 – 145 | 150 – 200 | UV-stable coatings, insulators |

## 2.2 The Curing Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "cure"?** Because polymerization converts the liquid resin into an infusible, insoluble, three-dimensional network. The reaction is *exothermic* and *irreversible*.

### Step 1: Ring-Opening Nucleophilic Attack
A primary amine ($\ce{R-NH2}$) attacks the **less substituted carbon** of the oxirane ring (SN2-type), opening the ring and forming an **alkyl-amine** with a secondary hydroxyl ($\ce{-OH}$).

### Step 2: Chain Propagation
The newly formed secondary amine attacks another epoxide, creating a **tertiary amine** and another secondary -OH group.

### Step 3: Cross-link Formation
The generated -OH group, in the presence of tertiary amine catalysts or at high temperature, can catalyze the **homopolymerization** of additional epoxide rings, generating polyether cross-links.

### Step 4: Gelation → Vitrification → Full Cure
- **Gelation** — first appearance of infinite network; viscosity diverges.
- **Vitrification** — $T_g$ of the reacting system overtakes the cure temperature; reaction becomes diffusion-limited.
- **Post-cure** — re-heating above $T_g$ drives the reaction to completion (pushes degree of cure $\alpha \to 1$).

### 2.2.1 Stoichiometry of Epoxy-Amine Cure

For an ideal epoxy-primary-amine system, the **stoichiometric ratio (r)** is:

$$
r = \frac{n_{H}}{n_{E}}
$$

where $n_H$ = moles of active hydrogen (amine) and $n_E$ = moles of epoxide.

- $r = 1$ → ideal cross-link density
- $r > 1$ → flexible (amine-rich) network
- $r < 1$ → rigid, brittle (epoxy-rich) network

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{\lambda \cdot X_n}{1 - \lambda \cdot X_n}$ | **DiBenedetto equation** — $T_g$ rises with cross-link density | °C, K |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ | **Fox equation** — $T_g$ of cured blend | K, mass fraction $w$ |
| 3 | $\alpha(t) = 1 - e^{-k t^n}$ | **Kamal autocatalytic cure kinetics** | dimensionless, $n$ ≈ 1–2 |
| 4 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | **Arrhenius** rate constant | $E_a$ in kJ/mol |
| 5 | $\tau(T) = \tau_0 \cdot e^{E_a / RT}$ | **Relaxation time** (WLF/Arrhenius) | s |
| 6 | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_{cured}}{\Delta H_{total}}$ | Degree of cure (DSC) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E(t) = E_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Modulus build-up | MPa, GPa |
| 8 | $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K$ | Boltzmann constant (CODATA) | J/K |
| 9 | $R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$ | Universal gas constant | J/(mol·K) |
| 10 | $E_{ring} = 25 \, kcal/mol$ | Epoxide ring strain | kcal/mol |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Caveat:** All absolute-value and "such-that" symbols are written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve table formatting. Never use the bare pipe `|`.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The TGMDA + DDS (4,4′-diaminodiphenylsulfone) system is the **gold standard** for space-grade carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) primary structures — e.g., solid rocket motor casings, satellite bus panels, antenna reflectors. Its $T_g \approx 240 \, ^\circ C$ allows operation in **shadowed + sunlit** temperature regimes of LEO/MEO without micro-cracking.

## 2.5 Thermal Degradation Pathway (High-Temp Failure Mode)

Above **~ 350 °C** in inert atmosphere, epoxy networks degrade in three stages:

1. **Stage I (300 – 400 °C):** Scission of **C–N** and **C–O** bonds in amine-cured networks.
2. **Stage II (400 – 500 °C):** Dehydration, dehydrogenation, formation of **polycyclic aromatic char**.
3. **Stage III (>500 °C):** Char oxidation (in air); mass stabilizes at 15 – 35 % residue (carbon char).

> [!TIP]
> The presence of **aromatic backbones** (Bisphenol-A, DDS) is what allows epoxies to char rather than volatilize — the char acts as a thermal barrier, extending survivability in **re-entry** heating transients.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kamal-Sourour Cure Kinetics

The most widely used cure-kinetic model for aerospace-grade epoxies is the **Kamal autocatalytic** equation:

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = \left(k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m\right) (1 - \alpha)^n
$$

We will derive its integrated form used for **isothermal DSC analysis**.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define Degree of Cure ($\alpha$)

$$
\alpha = \frac{H(t)}{H_{total}}
$$

where $H(t)$ is the cumulative heat released up to time $t$, and $H_{total}$ is the ultimate heat of cure obtained by scanning a fully cured sample.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Apply Autocatalytic Assumption

The autocatalytic term $k_2 \alpha^m$ models the fact that hydroxyl groups (formed early in cure) catalyze further ring-opening.

For a simplified **single-order case** with $m = 1$ and $n = 1$:

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k \, \alpha \, (1 - \alpha)
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Separate Variables

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{\alpha (1 - \alpha)} = k \, dt
$$

Apply partial-fraction decomposition:

$$
\frac{1}{\alpha (1 - \alpha)} = \frac{1}{\alpha} + \frac{1}{1 - \alpha}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\left( \frac{1}{\alpha} + \frac{1}{1 - \alpha} \right) d\alpha = k \, dt
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Integrate

$$
\int \left( \frac{1}{\alpha} + \frac{1}{1 - \alpha} \right) d\alpha = \int k \, dt
$$

$$
\ln \alpha - \ln(1 - \alpha) = k t + C
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \right) = k t + C
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Apply Initial Condition

At $t = 0$, $\alpha = 0$. The LHS diverges, so we apply the **regularized limit** $\alpha(t) = \alpha_0$ (a small initial conversion from thermal initiation):

$$
C = \ln \left( \frac{\alpha_0}{1 - \alpha_0} \right)
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Solve for $\alpha(t)$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{\alpha / (1 - \alpha)}{\alpha_0 / (1 - \alpha_0)} \right) = k t
$$

$$
\frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{\alpha_0}{1 - \alpha_0} e^{k t}
$$

For most practical cures, $\alpha_0 \to 0$, giving the canonical form:

$$
\alpha(t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-k t}}
$$

This is a **sigmoidal (logistic) curve**, capturing the autocatalytic acceleration. The constant $k$ follows Arrhenius:

$$
k = A \, e^{-E_a / R T}
$$

Hence the **half-life of cure** is:

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(1/\alpha_0 - 1)}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: DiBenedetto Equation for $T_g$ vs Cross-link Density

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Empirical Observation
Cross-link density $\nu$ is proportional to the elastic modulus in the rubbery plateau. As $\nu$ increases, $T_g$ rises.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Lattice Model
DiBenedetto (1987) proposed a lattice-based expression:

$$
\frac{T_g - T_{g,0}}{T_{g,\infty} - T_{g,0}} = \frac{\lambda \nu}{1 + (1 - \lambda) \nu}
$$

where:
- $T_{g,0}$ = $T_g$ of the uncured resin
- $T_{g,\infty}$ = $T_g$ at infinite cross-link density
- $\lambda$ = an adjustable constant ($\approx$ 0.4 – 0.6 for amine epoxies)

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Practical Engineering Form

Solving for $T_g$ explicitly:

$$
T_g = T_{g,0} + \frac{(T_{g,\infty} - T_{g,0}) \, \lambda \nu}{1 + (1 - \lambda) \nu}
$$

This is the equation most often quoted in literature (e.g., Pascault & Williams 1990, reviewed by Ellis 2000).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical TGMDA-DDS system achieves $\nu \approx 6 \times 10^{-3} \, mol/cm^3$, giving $T_g$ near 240 °C. Pushing cross-link density beyond $\sim 10^{-2} \, mol/cm^3$ provides diminishing returns because chain-segment mobility becomes diffusion-limited and the network becomes brittle.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property Dependence on Cure Schedule

| Cure Schedule | $T_g$ Achieved | Degree of Cure $\alpha$ | Residual Stress | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RT cure + RT post-cure | 80 – 110 °C | 0.7 – 0.85 | High | Adhesives |
| 80 °C × 2 h + 120 °C × 2 h | 120 – 150 °C | 0.90 | Moderate | Secondary structures |
| 120 °C × 2 h + 175 °C × 4 h | 170 – 200 °C | 0.95 – 0.98 | Low | Aircraft interior panels |
| 175 °C × 2 h + 220 °C × 6 h | 220 – 260 °C | ≥ 0.99 | Very low | **Solid rocket motor casings** |

> [!TIP]
> The relationship between post-cure temperature and $T_g$ is **non-linear** and asymptotic. Each additional 10 °C of post-cure gives progressively smaller $T_g$ gains above ~ 80 % of $T_{g,\infty}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Epoxy Network Formation — Block-Level Functional Flow

This flow diagram traces the engineering process from raw resin to a fully cured, space-qualified composite matrix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw DGEBA Resin + Hardener DDS] --> B[Mixing and Degassing under Vacuum 60 C]
    B --> C[Resin Film Impregnation on Carbon Fibre Tow]
    C --> D[Lay-up of Prepreg Plies on Tooling]
    D --> E[Vacuum Bagging and Autoclave Cure 175 C 4 h 0.7 MPa]
    E --> F[Free-cure Step at 220 C 6 h]
    F --> G{Quality Check via DSC and DMA}
    G -- Pass --> H[Cured CFRP Laminate Tg 240 C]
    G -- Fail --> I[Re-Post-cure or Reject]
    H --> J[Space-Qualified Structural Panel]
```

## 4.2 TGMDA + DDS Network Architecture

The following block-flow schematic maps how a **tetra-functional** epoxy cross-links with a **di-functional** aromatic amine to form a 3-D thermoset.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[TGMDA Tetraglycidyl Methylenedianiline 4 epoxide groups] --> nodeB[Primary Amine Attack on Epoxide Ring 1]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Primary Amine Attack on Epoxide Ring 2]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Primary Amine Attack on Epoxide Ring 3]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Primary Amine Attack on Epoxide Ring 4]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Secondary Hydroxyl OH]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG[Tertiary Amine Cross-link Node]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[3-D Aromatic-Rich Cross-linked Network]
    nodeH --> nodeI[High Tg High Char High Modulus]
```

## 4.3 Thermal Degradation Cascade of Cured Epoxy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Cured TGMDA-DDS Network Tg 240 C] --> step1[Stage I 300-400 C C-N and C-O Scission]
    step1 --> step2[Stage II 400-500 C Aromatization and Char Formation]
    step2 --> step3[Stage III 500 C plus Char Oxidation in Air]
    step3 --> step4[Final Residue 15-35 percent Carbon Char]
    step1 --> loss1[Volatiles Amines Phenols]
    step2 --> loss2[Light Gases H2O CO2 CH4]
    step3 --> loss3[CO and CO2 Dominant]
```

## 4.4 Curing Reaction — Step-by-Step Chemical Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rA[Epoxide Ring 3-membered cyclic ether] --> rB[Amine N-H Attack]
    rB --> rC[Ring Opens to form Secondary Amine and OH]
    rC --> rD[Secondary Amine Attacks another Epoxide]
    rD --> rE[Tertiary Amine formed Cross-link site]
    rE --> rF[OH Group Ethers additional Epoxide Polyether Branch]
    rF --> rG[Network Propagation until Gelation]
    rG --> rH[Vitrification Tg exceeds Cure T Reaction Slows]
    rH --> rI[Post-cure above Tg drives alpha to 1]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define epoxy resin. List any two high-temperature curing agents used with it.**

**Model Answer:**
An **epoxy resin** is a low-molecular-weight pre-polymer containing two or more terminal **oxirane (epoxide) rings** that undergo a cross-linking reaction with a hardener to form a rigid 3-D thermoset network.

**Two high-temperature curing agents (hardeners):**
1. **4,4′-Diaminodiphenylsulfone (DDS)** — aromatic di-amine offering $T_g$ up to 240 °C with TGMDA.
2. **Dicyandiamide (DICY)** — latent hardener activated at 150 – 180 °C, used in prepregs for shelf-life stability.
3. (Optional extra) **Methylenedianiline (MDA)**, **Novolac phenolics**, **anhydrides like Nadic methyl anhydride (NMA)**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the significance of glass transition temperature ($T_g$) for epoxy resins in space applications.**

**Model Answer:**
The **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** is the temperature at which the cured epoxy transitions from a rigid glassy state to a soft rubbery state, marked by a sharp drop in modulus and a rise in CTE. For space applications, $T_g$ defines the **upper service temperature** of structural composites. Designers require $T_g$ to be **at least 25 – 30 °C above the maximum in-orbit operational temperature** to avoid micro-cracking, loss of dimensional stability, and resin softening under thermal-cycling loads.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a chemical equation, explain the **ring-opening cure mechanism** of a DGEBA epoxy resin using a primary amine hardener. Discuss the role of the hydroxyl group generated in autocatalysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **DGEBA, epoxy novolac, and TGMDA** with respect to molecular functionality, achievable $T_g$, and one specific space application each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DGEBA + Primary Amine Ring-Opening Cure**

The structural formula of **DGEBA** is:

$$
\ce{CH2-CH(O)-CH2-O-C6H4-C(CH3)2-C6H4-O-CH2-CH(O)-CH2}
$$

The terminal oxirane ring is opened by the **nitrogen lone pair** of a primary amine in an **SN2-type nucleophilic attack** on the less hindered carbon:

$$
\ce{R-NH2 + H2C(-O-)CH-R' -> R-NH-CH2-CH(OH)-R'}
$$

A **secondary amine** and a **secondary hydroxyl group** are produced. The secondary amine can attack a *second* epoxide on a different chain, creating a **cross-link** and a **tertiary amine** node:

$$
\ce{R-NH-CH2-CH(OH)-R' + H2C(-O-)CH-R'' -> R-N(CH2-CH(OH)-R')(CH2-CH(OH)-R'')}
$$

The generated **-OH groups** play a critical autocatalytic role. They form a **tri-molecular transition state** with the incoming epoxide and amine, lowering the activation energy for further ring-opening. This is the molecular basis of the **$k_2 \alpha^m$ term** in the Kamal-Sourour cure kinetics model. As cure proceeds, -OH concentration rises, accelerating the reaction until diffusion limits set in at vitrification.

**(b) Comparative Table of DGEBA, Epoxy Novolac, and TGMDA**

| Property | DGEBA | Epoxy Novolac (o-Cresol) | TGMDA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Functionality (epoxy groups / molecule) | 2 | 3 – 6 | 4 |
| Typical EEW (g/eq) | 170 – 190 | 190 – 230 | 110 – 135 |
| Achievable $T_g$ with DDS | 130 – 175 °C | 180 – 220 °C | 220 – 260 °C |
| Viscosity at 50 °C | Low (2 – 5 Pa·s) | Medium-High | Medium |
| Char yield at 700 °C (TGA, N₂) | 10 – 15 % | 25 – 35 % | 30 – 40 % |
| Space application | Satellite antenna brackets, secondary structures | Ablative liners, re-entry vehicle heat shields | Solid rocket motor casings, primary satellite bus panels |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **Fox equation** for predicting the glass transition temperature of a polymer blend. A TGMDA-DDS system has a fully-cured $T_{g,\infty} = 260 \, ^\circ C$ and uncured $T_{g,0} = 10 \, ^\circ C$. Using the DiBenedetto equation with $\lambda = 0.45$, find the $T_g$ at a cross-link density of $\nu = 6 \times 10^{-3} \, mol/cm^3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three-stage thermal degradation mechanism** of a cured high-$T_g$ epoxy, and state two material-design strategies to improve the char yield at 700 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Fox Equation**

The Fox equation is a **weight-fraction mixing rule** for $T_g$ of polymer blends. It assumes additivity of free volume.

For two polymers 1 and 2 with weight fractions $w_1$ and $w_2$:

$$
\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}
$$

Derivation outline:
- Free volume theory (Doolittle): polymer $T_g$ corresponds to a fixed fractional free volume $f_g \approx 0.025$.
- For a blend, free volume is the *weight-fraction-weighted average* of component free volumes.
- Combining with the Doolittle relation and assuming constant $T_g$-free-volume yields the Fox equation.

**Numerical Problem — DiBenedetto Equation:**

$$
T_g = T_{g,0} + \frac{(T_{g,\infty} - T_{g,0}) \, \lambda \nu}{1 + (1 - \lambda) \nu}
$$

Substitute:
- $T_{g,0} = 10 \, ^\circ C$
- $T_{g,\infty} = 260 \, ^\circ C$
- $\lambda = 0.45$
- $\nu = 6 \times 10^{-3} \, mol/cm^3$

First compute numerator:

$$
(T_{g,\infty} - T_{g,0}) \, \lambda \nu = (260 - 10) \times 0.45 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
= 250 \times 0.45 \times 0.006 = 250 \times 0.0027 = 0.675 \, ^\circ C
$$

Then denominator:

$$
1 + (1 - 0.45) \times 6 \times 10^{-3} = 1 + 0.55 \times 0.006 = 1 + 0.0033 = 1.0033
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_g = 10 + \frac{0.675}{1.0033} = 10 + 0.6728 \approx 10.67 \, ^\circ C
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Numerical Disaster Zone:**
> A common student mistake is to leave $T_{g,0}$ and $T_{g,\infty}$ in **°C** while the DiBenedetto equation is most rigorously valid in **K (Kelvin)**. Always convert: 10 °C = 283 K, 260 °C = 533 K. Re-doing the problem in Kelvin gives $T_g = 284.7 \, K = 11.6 \, ^\circ C$ — same order but a 1 °C offset that can be penalized. **Always state which unit system you are using.** A 2-mark penalty is typical for unit-inconsistency in this KTU problem.
> Also, the very small result ($T_g$ of 10–11 °C at low cross-link density) is *physically reasonable* — at $\nu \to 0$, $T_g \to T_{g,0}$ — confirming the calculation.

**(b) Three-Stage Thermal Degradation Mechanism**

**Stage I (300 – 400 °C):** Initial breakdown of the **C–N bonds** of the amine-epoxy linkages and the **C–O ether bonds** of the bisphenol linkages. Low-MW volatiles released: amines, phenols, water.

**Stage II (400 – 500 °C):** **Aromatization** of the residual network — conjugated polyaromatic structures grow. The material darkens, mass loss slows, and a **carbonaceous char** begins to form. Light gases ($\ce{H2O}$, $\ce{CO2}$, $\ce{CH4}$) evolve.

**Stage III (> 500 °C, in air):** The char is **oxidized** to $\ce{CO}$ and $\ce{CO2}$, and only a stable inorganic residue remains (often 15 – 35 % of initial mass). In inert atmosphere, this stage manifests as slow graphitization of the char.

**Two Design Strategies to Increase Char Yield:**

1. **Incorporate aromatic / heterocyclic backbones:** Replace aliphatic hardeners with **aromatic amines (DDS, MDA)** or blend with **novolac phenolics**. Aromatic rings form stable polyaromatic chars, raising residue to 35 – 45 %.

2. **Add nano-fillers / char-promoters:** Disperse **organically modified montmorillonite (oMMT) clay**, **POSS (polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane)** cages, or **phosphorus-containing additives (DOPO derivatives)**. These act as catalytic char-formers and produce a tortuous path for volatile escape, also providing self-extinguishing (flame-retardant) behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common 1–2 Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing $T_g$ with melting temperature ($T_m$):** Epoxies are **thermosets** — they have NO $T_m$. They have a $T_g$ and then a *decomposition temperature*. Mentioning $T_m$ is a guaranteed -2 marks.
> 2. **Stating that epoxies "melt at $T_g$":** They soften — that is the *physical* meaning of $T_g$, not melting. The covalent network does not break until thermal degradation.
> 3. **Skipping units in the numerical problem:** As warned above, the unit-inconsistency penalty is real.
> 4. **Forgetting autocatalysis in part (a):** The role of -OH in catalysing further ring-opening is the *core* of part (a)'s second half — students often stop at "ring opens, cross-link forms" and lose 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Epoxy Resins for Space — High-Density Revision Cheat Sheet**

- [x] **Definition:** Epoxy = pre-polymer with terminal **oxirane** rings; cured with hardener to form 3-D thermoset.
- [x] **Strain energy** of oxirane ring ≈ **25 kcal/mol** — drives cure thermodynamically.
- [x] **DGEBA** is the workhorse; **TGMDA + DDS** is the *gold standard* for high-Tg aerospace use ($T_g \approx 240 \, ^\circ C$).
- [x] **Functionality** = number of epoxide groups per molecule; higher functionality → higher cross-link density → higher $T_g$ and char yield, but also higher brittleness.
- [x] **Cure mechanism** for amines: SN2 attack → secondary amine → tertiary amine; -OH groups catalyse further ring-opening (**autocatalysis**).
- [x] **Kamal-Sourour equation:** $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$.
- [x] **Fox equation** for blends: $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \sum \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$.
- [x] **DiBenedetto equation** links $T_g$ to cross-link density $\nu$.
- [x] **Thermal degradation** is a 3-stage process; char yield is enhanced by **aromaticity** and **nano-fillers (POSS, oMMT, DOPO)**.
- [x] **Space-grade screening** criteria: $T_g \geq 150 \, ^\circ C$, TML < 1 %, CVCM < 0.1 % (per **NASA ASTM E595**).
- [x] **Cure schedule** must include **post-cure above final $T_g$** to drive $\alpha \to 1$.
- [x] **Outgassing**, **moisture absorption**, and **CTE mismatch** with fibres are the three dominant reliability concerns for in-orbit composites.
- [x] **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K$ (CODATA exact value — keep on your formula sheet).
- [x] **Gas constant** $R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$ — required for all Arrhenius calculations.
- [x] Always state **units explicitly** in numerical answers; convert °C ↔ K for DiBenedetto/Fox problems.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — High Temperature Resistant Resins Such as Epoxy**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phenolic and Polyimides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phenolic and Polyimides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Phenolic Resins

### Formal Academic Definition
**Phenolic resins** are a class of **thermosetting synthetic polymers** obtained by the condensation polymerization reaction between **phenol (C₆H₅OH)** and **formaldehyde (HCHO)** in the presence of an acid or base catalyst. They are considered the **first fully synthetic commercial plastic** (Leo Baekeland, 1907, "Bakelite") and belong to the family of high-performance thermosets characterized by a highly **cross-linked aromatic network** that delivers exceptional **thermal stability, flame retardance, char yield, and ablative performance** in space environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Phenolic resins are specifically studied under Polymers for Space Applications because of their use as **ablative thermal protection systems (TPS)** on re-entry vehicles, rocket nozzles, and heat shields (e.g., Pioneer, Viking, Galileo probe heat shields).

### Two Principal Sub-Families
1. **Novolac Resins** — Linear/branched thermoplastic-like prepolymers formed under **acidic conditions** with **excess phenol** (P:F > 1). Require a hardener (typically **hexamethylenetetramine, HMTA**) for curing.
2. **Resole Resins** — Self-curing, fully reactive prepolymers prepared under **basic conditions** with **excess formaldehyde** (F:P > 1). Contain reactive **hydroxymethyl (-CH₂OH)** and **benzylic ether (-CH₂-O-CH₂-)** groups that cross-link on heating.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Charcoal Skeleton":** Imagine building a 3D jungle-gym out of benzene rings connected by short -CH₂- bridges. When you heat this jungle-gym in a blowtorch, the weak bridges burn away, but the **aromatic rings fuse into a glassy carbon char**. That carbon char is what protects a spacecraft from the 3000 °C plasma of atmospheric re-entry. The phenolic resin is essentially a **pre-organized carbon scaffold** — it is designed to fail gracefully into a thermally insulative carbon layer.

### Key Physical Metrics for Space Applications
- **Density (ρ):** ~1.25 – 1.30 g/cm³ (unfilled) ; up to ~1.85 g/cm³ (carbon-fiber phenolic)
- **Glass transition (Tg):** Not measurable in the classical sense; phenolic is fully **thermosetting** (cross-linked) and decomposes rather than softens
- **Char yield at 1000 °C (N₂):** **55 – 65 %** (one of the highest among unfilled organic polymers)
- **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI):** **> 40 %** (self-extinguishing)
- **Heat of ablation:** ~ 10 – 25 MJ/kg
- **Service temperature range:** – 150 °C to + 250 °C (continuous); transient > 2000 °C in ablative mode

---

## 1.2 Polyimides

### Formal Academic Definition
**Polyimides (PI)** are a class of high-performance step-growth polymers containing the **imide functional group (–CO–N–CO–)** as a repeating unit in the polymer backbone, typically fused to **aromatic (benzene) rings** to yield the **aromatic polyimide** subclass. The most commercially iconic member is **Kapton® (DuPont, poly(4,4'-oxydiphenylene pyromellitimide))**, a fully aromatic, linear polyimide film possessing the rare combination of outstanding **thermal stability (– 269 °C to + 400 °C), mechanical toughness, radiation resistance, and dielectric properties**, making it indispensable in space systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Polyimides are emphasized in space applications as **flexible printed circuit (FPC) substrates in solar arrays**, **multi-layer insulation (MLI) thermal blankets**, **wire insulation**, and **structural adhesives for composite honeycomb panels** in satellite and launch-vehicle structures.

### Two Synthesis Routes
1. **Two-Step (Most Common):** A **dianhydride** (e.g., PMDA) reacts with a **diamine** (e.g., ODA) at room temperature in a polar aprotic solvent (DMF, DMAc, NMP) to form a soluble **poly(amic acid) (PAA) precursor**, which is then thermally or chemically **imidized** (cyclodehydrated) at 300–350 °C to yield the final polyimide.
2. **One-Step (High-Temperature):** The monomers are reacted directly at high temperature (>180 °C) in a high-boiling solvent (e.g., m-cresol) — preferred for insoluble rigid polyimides.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Origami":** Polyimides are made in two stages. In Stage 1, you fold a long, flexible, soluble "rope" (the polyamic acid precursor) and lay it down as a thin film on a substrate. In Stage 2, you heat the film, and each link of the rope "clicks shut" into a rigid, insoluble, ultra-stable ring (the imide group). Once clicked shut, the polymer is essentially a chain of interconnected five-membered molecular rings — extremely stiff, extremely tough, and extremely heat-resistant, like a closed zipper of tiny molecular seatbelts.

### Key Physical Metrics for Space Applications
- **Density (ρ):** ~1.30 – 1.42 g/cm³
- **Glass transition (Tg):** **> 400 °C** for fully aromatic PIs
- **Tensile strength:** ~ 70 – 230 MPa (film, depending on grade)
- **Elongation at break:** 5 – 80 %
- **Dielongic strength:** ~ 100 – 300 kV/mm
- **Outgassing (TML / CVCM per NASA ASTM E-595):** TML < 1 %, CVCM < 0.1 % (Kapton HN passes)
- **CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion):** ~ 20 – 30 ppm/K
- **Service temperature range:** – 269 °C to + 400 °C
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phenolic Resin — Chemistry, Curing & Engineering Logic

### 2.1.1 Novolac (Acid-Catalyzed, Phenol-Deficient)
- Reaction medium: **HCl, H₂SO₄, or oxalic acid** (pH < 3)
- Initial electrophilic aromatic substitution: formaldehyde protonated to form **⁺CH₂OH** (or ⁺CH₂O⁻ in modified mechanism), which attacks the *ortho* and *para* positions of phenol to form **hydroxymethylphenols**.
- Hydroxymethylphenols are unstable under acid conditions and react further with phenol to yield **methylene bridges (–CH₂–)**.
- Net result: **phenolic nuclei joined by –CH₂– bridges**, with terminal phenol groups. No reactive –CH₂OH groups remain (F was deficient), so Novolac is **inert at room temperature**.
- **Curing:** A hardener, **hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA, (CH₂)₆N₄)**, decomposes at 120–180 °C to liberate formaldehyde and ammonia, which cross-link the Novolac chains into a 3D network through additional –CH₂– and **benzylamine (–CH₂–NH–CH₂–)** bridges.

### 2.1.2 Resole (Base-Catalyzed, Formaldehyde-Excess)
- Catalyst: **NaOH, NH₄OH, Ba(OH)₂** (pH > 7)
- Phenol is deprotonated to **phenoxide (PhO⁻)**, which is more nucleophilic, attacking formaldehyde to form mono-, di-, and tri-hydroxymethylphenols.
- Excess formaldehyde ensures that the polymer chains bear pendant **–CH₂OH** groups (and ether bridges –CH₂–O–CH₂– upon self-condensation).
- On heating to 120–200 °C, these reactive groups condense further to form a **3D cross-linked network** without any external hardener (self-curing).

### 2.1.3 Why Phenolic Resins Excel in Space Ablation
- Ablation is **sacrificial mass loss** that absorbs heat via (a) **convective blowing** of degradation gases into the boundary layer, (b) **re-radiation** from the hot char surface, and (c) **endothermic thermal decomposition** of the polymer.
- The high **char yield (55–65 %)** means most of the polymer mass is converted to a solid carbonaceous layer that remains intact and continues to insulate the underlying structure. This is the **"transpiration cooling + char insulation"** synergy.
- Decomposition gases (H₂O, CO, CO₂, CH₄, phenol derivatives) are low-molecular-weight, so they are easily entrained away in the re-entry flow.

### 2.1.4 Phenolic Ablation Engineering Equation
The **steady-state surface recession rate (ṡ)** of an ablative material under a heat flux is governed by the energy balance at the moving char-ablative interface:

$$\dot{s} = \frac{\dot{q}_{inc} - \epsilon \sigma T_s^{4}}{\rho_{abl} \cdot \Delta H_{eff}}$$

where:
- $\dot{s}$ = surface recession rate (m/s)
- $\dot{q}_{inc}$ = incident convective + radiative heat flux (W/m²)
- $\epsilon$ = surface emissivity (≈ 0.85 – 0.95 for phenolic char)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan–Boltzmann constant = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- $T_s$ = surface temperature (K)
- $\rho_{abl}$ = effective density of the ablating layer (kg/m³)
- $\Delta H_{eff}$ = effective heat of ablation (J/kg) — a lumped parameter absorbing pyrolysis enthalpy, sensible heat, and transpiration contributions

---

## 2.2 Polyimides — Chemistry, Imidization & Engineering Logic

### 2.2.1 The Two-Step Imidization Mechanism
**Step 1 — Poly(amic acid) (PAA) formation:**

$$n\,\text{PMDA} + n\,\text{ODA} \;\longrightarrow\; [\text{PAA}]_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

where PMDA = pyromellitic dianhydride; ODA = 4,4'-oxydianiline. The reaction is performed in **DMF, DMAc, or NMP** at room temperature to avoid premature imidization. PAA solutions are stable for months in cold storage.

**Step 2 — Thermal imidization (cyclodehydration):** Heating the PAA film at 80 °C → 200 °C → 300 °C (ramping) drives off water and closes the five-membered imide ring:

$$[\text{PAA}]_n \;\xrightarrow{\Delta\,(300\text{–}350\,^\circ\text{C})}\; [\text{Polyimide}]_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### 2.2.2 Why Polyimides Excel in Spacecraft
- **Aromatic backbone** → exceptional thermal-oxidative stability; ring structures absorb and dissipate radiation energy without chain scission.
- **Conjugated imide + aromatic system** → high bond dissociation energy (> 400 kJ/mol for C–N imide bond).
- **Low outgassing (TML, CVCM)** → critical to prevent contamination of optical surfaces, solar cells, and star trackers.
- **Low CTE, high modulus, low dielectric constant (≈ 3.4)** → ideal as a flexible substrate for **solar cell arrays** (CuInGaSe, GaAs, perovskite) and **flexible printed circuits (FPCs)**.
- **Resistant to atomic oxygen (AO) and UV** — although long-duration LEO exposure can cause surface erosion, Kapton maintains mechanical integrity.

### 2.2.3 Polyimide–Composite Variants Used in Space
| Variant | Trade Name | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Kapton HN (uncoated)** | DuPont | MLI thermal blankets, wire insulation |
| **Kapton FN (fluorinated)** | DuPont | Release films, cryogenic insulation |
| **Upilex-S** | UBE | Flexible solar array substrate |
| **PMR-15 (in-situ cured PI)** | NASA LaRC | High-temp composite matrix (350 °C service) |
| **Pyralin PI** | DuPont | FPC flex circuits |
| **LaRC-SI / LaRC-IA / AF-32** | NASA / Nexolve | Colorless, low-cte optical films |

> [!NOTE]
> **PMR-15 (Polymerization of Monomeric Reactants)** is a NASA-developed *addition-curable* polyimide used as a **resin matrix for carbon-fiber composites** in jet-engine and rocket structures. The monomers are mixed in alcohol, impregnated into fibers, and *in situ* imidized + cross-linked at 315 °C.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Ablative Recession Rate

We start with the **1-D semi-infinite ablating slab** energy balance. Let $x = 0$ be the receding surface, $x$ increasing into the virgin material, and $T_s$ the surface temperature.

### Step 1: Heat flux reaching the surface
The incident aerodynamic heat flux is $\dot{q}_{inc}$ (W/m²). Part of this energy is **re-radiated** from the hot char surface per Stefan–Boltzmann law:

$$\dot{q}_{rad} = \epsilon \, \sigma \, T_s^{4}$$

The **net heat flux conducted into the material** is therefore:

$$\dot{q}_{net} = \dot{q}_{inc} - \epsilon \sigma T_s^{4}$$

### Step 2: Energy absorbed by ablation
The ablation process (pyrolysis + transpiration + sensible heating of gases) requires an effective energy per unit mass, $\Delta H_{eff}$ (J/kg). The mass ablation rate per unit area is:

$$\dot{m}_{abl} = \rho_{abl} \, \dot{s}$$

where $\dot{s}$ (m/s) is the linear recession rate and $\rho_{abl}$ (kg/m³) is the bulk density of the ablative.

### Step 3: Equate absorbed energy to ablation enthalpy
For a steady-state ablation front, the **net conducted heat flux must equal the energy consumed by ablation**:

$$\dot{q}_{inc} - \epsilon \sigma T_s^{4} = \dot{m}_{abl} \, \Delta H_{eff} = \rho_{abl} \, \dot{s} \, \Delta H_{eff}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\dot{s}$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{s} = \dfrac{\dot{q}_{inc} - \epsilon \sigma T_s^{4}}{\rho_{abl} \, \Delta H_{eff}}\;}$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation tells a TPS designer that to minimize recession, they should (a) **increase $\rho_{abl}$** (use dense carbon/phenolic composites), (b) **increase $\Delta H_{eff}$** (use high-enthalpy char-yielding resins), and (c) **maximize $T_s$** (so that re-radiation bleeds off most of the incident heat — but with the limit that the char must survive).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Phenolic Ablative Shield Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A re-entry probe experiences an incident heat flux of $\dot{q}_{inc} = 2.5 \times 10^6$ W/m² for 60 s. The phenolic ablative has $\rho_{abl} = 1450$ kg/m³, $\Delta H_{eff} = 12 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $\epsilon = 0.90$, and reaches a steady surface temperature of $T_s = 2200$ K. Estimate the **ablative thickness required** (neglecting char insulation layer, conservative).

**Step 1 — Compute re-radiation heat flux:**

$$\dot{q}_{rad} = 0.90 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (2200)^{4}$$

$$\dot{q}_{rad} = 0.90 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.3426 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\dot{q}_{rad} = 1.195 \times 10^{6} \;\text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Net heat flux into the material:**

$$\dot{q}_{net} = 2.5 \times 10^{6} - 1.195 \times 10^{6} = 1.305 \times 10^{6} \;\text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Recession rate:**

$$\dot{s} = \frac{1.305 \times 10^{6}}{1450 \times 12 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.305 \times 10^{6}}{1.74 \times 10^{10}} = 7.50 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Total recession thickness over 60 s:**

$$\Delta x = \dot{s} \times t = 7.50 \times 10^{-5} \times 60 = 4.50 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m} = 4.5 \;\text{mm}$$

**Result:** A phenolic TPS of approximately **~ 6 – 8 mm thickness** (including safety factor of 1.5) is required for this 60 s heat-soak. This matches historical Galileo and Pioneer probe designs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Polyimide Imidization Kinetics

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A poly(amic acid) film is heated from 25 °C to 300 °C at 5 °C/min. Estimate the **time spent in the 150–250 °C imidization window** (where most cyclodehydration occurs).

**Step 1 — Time to reach 150 °C from 25 °C:**

$$\Delta t_1 = \frac{150 - 25}{5} = 25 \;\text{min}$$

**Step 2 — Time to traverse 150 → 250 °C (the imidization window):**

$$\Delta t_2 = \frac{250 - 150}{5} = 20 \;\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Total heating time to 250 °C:**

$$t_{250} = 25 + 20 = 45 \;\text{min}$$

A 1-h hold at 300 °C is then typically added to ensure full imidization and annealing. This is the **standard Kapton® film-curing protocol** used by DuPont.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Phenolic vs. Polyimide

| Property | **Phenolic** | **Polyimide (Kapton)** |
|---|---|---|
| Polymer type | Thermosetting (cross-linked) | Thermoplastic-like linear (uncrosslinked) |
| Synthesis | Condensation (PhOH + HCHO) | Two-step: polyaddition + cyclodehydration |
| Prepolymer form | Liquid (resole) / solid (novolac) | Soluble PAA solution |
| Cure mechanism | Heat + HMTA (novolac) or self-cure (resole) | Thermal imidization at 300 °C |
| Tg | Not defined (decomposes) | > 400 °C |
| Char yield (1000 °C, N₂) | 55 – 65 % | 50 – 60 % |
| LOI | > 40 % | 37 – 45 % |
| Max service T | 250 °C (2500 °C transient, ablative) | 400 °C continuous |
| Min service T | – 150 °C | – 269 °C |
| Tensile strength | 30 – 60 MPa (neat) | 70 – 230 MPa (film) |
| CTE | 25 – 60 ppm/K | 20 – 30 ppm/K |
| Dielectric constant | 4 – 8 | 3.0 – 3.5 |
| Outgassing TML/CVCM | Variable, can be high without post-cure | Passes NASA ASTM E-595 |
| Primary space use | **Ablative heat shields, rocket nozzles** | **MLI blankets, FPCs, solar array substrates, wire insulation** |
| Failure mode in space | Sacrificial char formation (desirable) | Thermo-oxidative embrittlement (undesirable, mitigated by coatings) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU conceptual takeaway:** Phenolics are **sacrificial ablative** materials — they *fail* gracefully into protective char. Polyimides are **structural/functional durable** materials — they are *designed to survive* the mission intact. Choosing between them is dictated by the **mission thermal profile**: short intense re-entry → phenolic; long-duration LEO/GEO exposure → polyimide.
