
### 4.3 Load Transfer Mechanism in a Single Fibre-Matrix Element

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeX1["APPLIED TENSILE LOAD ON COMPOSITE"]
    nodeX2["MATRIX ELONGATES MORE THAN FIBRE"]
    nodeX3["SHEAR STRESS AT FIBRE MATRIX INTERFACE"]
    nodeX4["FIBRE CARRIES TENSILE STRESS SIGMA F"]
    nodeX5["COMPOSITE EQUILIBRIUM ACHIEVED"]
    nodeX6["STRESS DISTRIBUTION ALONG FIBRE LENGTH"]
    nodeX7["MAXIMUM AT CENTRE ZERO AT ENDS"]
    nodeX8["CRITICAL LENGTH LC DETERMINES EFFICIENCY"]

    nodeX1 --> nodeX2
    nodeX2 --> nodeX3
    nodeX3 --> nodeX4
    nodeX4 --> nodeX5
    nodeX5 --> nodeX6
    nodeX6 --> nodeX7
    nodeX7 --> nodeX8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Fibre Reinforced Polymer (FRP). List the functions of the matrix phase in an FRP composite.*

**Model Answer:**
An FRP is a composite material consisting of high-strength fibres embedded in a polymer matrix, where the fibres carry the primary load and the matrix binds them, transfers stress, and protects them from environmental damage.

**Functions of the matrix:**
1. **Load transfer** — Distributes applied stress to the fibres via interfacial shear.
2. **Fibre protection** — Shields fibres from moisture, UV radiation, and micro-abrasion (critical in space).
3. **Shape retention** — Maintains fibre orientation and spacing during handling and service.
4. **Crack arrest** — Prevents propagation of micro-cracks across the composite.
5. **Toughness contribution** — Provides ductility that pure fibre bundles lack.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for functions with examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State and explain the Rule of Mixtures for longitudinal modulus of a unidirectional FRP composite.*

**Model Answer:**
The Rule of Mixtures expresses the longitudinal Young's modulus of a UD FRP composite as a weighted sum of fibre and matrix moduli, weighted by their volume fractions:

$$E_c^L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

It is derived under the **iso-strain assumption** — both fibre and matrix experience identical strain when loaded along the fibre direction. This gives the **upper bound** of composite stiffness (Voigt limit). For typical aerospace CFRP with $V_f = 0.6$, the fibres contribute over 95% of the longitudinal stiffness because $E_f \gg E_m$.

*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for assumption, 1 for interpretation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $V_f$ with $W_f$.** They are different by the density ratio. State which is given and convert if needed.
> 2. **Do NOT apply Rule of Mixtures in transverse direction.** That requires the *inverse* form.
> 3. **Always state assumptions** (iso-strain / iso-stress) explicitly — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks just for assumption statements.
> 4. **Units must be consistent.** Mixing GPa with MPa or g/cm³ with kg/m³ costs marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With neat derivations, obtain the expressions for longitudinal modulus ($E_c^L$) and transverse modulus ($E_c^T$) of a unidirectional fibre reinforced composite using the Rule of Mixtures.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A unidirectional glass fibre reinforced epoxy composite has the following properties: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $V_f = 0.55$. Calculate: (i) longitudinal modulus, (ii) transverse modulus, (iii) composite density, (iv) specific modulus.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivations:** *(present Section 3.1 and 3.2 derivations in full — already shown above)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Longitudinal Modulus:**
$$E_c^L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)$$
$$E_c^L = 39.6 + 1.575 = 41.175 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Transverse Modulus:**
$$\frac{1}{E_c^T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5}$$
$$\frac{1}{E_c^T} = 0.00764 + 0.12857 = 0.13621 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$
$$E_c^T = 7.34 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Composite Density:**
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45)$$
$$\rho_c = 1.4025 + 0.540 = 1.9425 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**(iv) Specific Modulus (longitudinal):**
$$\text{Specific Modulus} = \frac{E_c^L}{\rho_c} = \frac{41.175}{1.9425} = 21.20 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

> **Note the anisotropy:** $E_c^L \approx 5.6 \times E_c^T$ — this is the hallmark of UD FRP and explains why space structures use *multidirectional* layups to balance properties.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of **critical fibre length** ($L_c$) in FRP composites. Derive the expression for $L_c$ in terms of fibre tensile strength ($\sigma_f$), fibre diameter ($d$), and interfacial shear strength ($\tau_i$).

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A carbon fibre of diameter 7 µm and tensile strength 4.0 GPa is embedded in an epoxy matrix with interfacial shear strength 35 MPa. Calculate: (i) critical fibre length, (ii) aspect ratio at critical length, (iii) minimum fibre length for the fibre to develop 95% of its strength. Comment on the result in the context of space-grade CFRP.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept and Derivation:** *(present Section 3.3 derivation in full)*

The critical fibre length is the minimum length over which the interfacial shear stress can build up sufficient load to fracture the fibre. Shorter fibres pull out before reaching $\sigma_f$; longer fibres fracture (which is the desired efficient failure mode).

**Derivation result:**

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $d = 7 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\sigma_f = 4.0 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\tau_i = 35 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**(i) Critical Fibre Length:**
$$L_c = \frac{(4.0 \times 10^9)(7 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (35 \times 10^6)}$$
$$L_c = \frac{28{,}000}{70} = 400 \text{ µm} = 0.4 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Aspect Ratio at $L_c$:**
$$\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{400 \text{ µm}}{7 \text{ µm}} \approx 57.1$$

**(iii) Minimum Length for 95% Strength Development:**

For a fibre of length $L$, the average fibre stress is:
$$\sigma_{f,\text{avg}} = \sigma_f \left(1 - \frac{L_c}{2L}\right)$$

Setting $\sigma_{f,\text{avg}} = 0.95 \sigma_f$:

$$0.95 = 1 - \frac{L_c}{2L}$$

$$\frac{L_c}{2L} = 0.05$$

$$L = \frac{L_c}{2 \times 0.05} = \frac{400 \text{ µm}}{0.10} = 4000 \text{ µm} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Comment:** Since $L_c$ is extremely small (0.4 mm), any practical continuous fibre (length in metres) used in CFRP vastly exceeds $L_c$. Therefore, space-grade CFRP fibres operate at near-theoretical strength, justifying the use of continuous carbon fibre tows in satellite structures, rocket motor casings, and re-entry vehicle nose cones.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for FRP (Module 2):**
> - **FRP = Fibre (load bearer) + Polymer Matrix (load transfer & protection) + Interphase (coupling).**
> - **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal):** $E_c^L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — iso-strain, upper bound, derived from force equilibrium.
> - **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Transverse):** $1/E_c^T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ — iso-stress, lower bound.
> - **Composite Density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ — straight Rule of Mixtures (no inverse form).
> - **Critical Fibre Length:** $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ — fibre must be ≥ 15 $L_c$ to behave as continuous.
> - **Volume fractions must sum to 1:** $V_f + V_m = 1$ — always verify before substitution.
> - **Common fibres for space:** Carbon (CFRP, structural), Glass (GFRP, secondary), Aramid (Kevlar, impact).
> - **Common matrices for space:** Epoxy (most common), Bismaleimide (high-T), Cyanate Ester (low outgassing), PEEK (thermoplastic, tough).
> - **Key engineering metric:** Specific strength & specific modulus — these drive material selection for launch vehicles.
> - **Assumptions to ALWAYS state in KTU answers:** (i) Perfect fibre-matrix bonding, (ii) Uniform fibre distribution, (iii) No voids, (iv) Iso-strain (longitudinal) or iso-stress (transverse).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fibre (Polymer/Composite Engineering Definition):**
> A fibre is a slender, thread-like, highly anisotropic polymeric or inorganic material whose **axial dimension (length)** is at least **100 times** greater than its **transverse dimension (diameter)**, possessing a length-to-diameter (aspect) ratio typically greater than **1000:1**, and engineered to bear **axial tensile loads** through aligned molecular chains, crystalline domains, or microstructural filaments.

In the context of **Polymers for Space Applications (PEPOT746)**, fibres serve as the **primary load-bearing reinforcement** in **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs)**, where they impart directional strength, stiffness, thermal stability, and dimensional integrity to structures exposed to launch vibrations, cryogenic tankage conditions, orbital thermal cycling, and micrometeoroid impacts.

### 1.2 Broad Classification (KTU 2024 Scheme Framework)

Fibres used in space-grade polymer composites are classified into **two master families**:

1. **Natural Fibres** — derived from plant, animal, or mineral sources (used only in limited non-structural space applications such as ablative insulation interiors, fairing acoustic dampers).
2. **Synthetic / Man-Made Fibres** — engineered through chemical polymerization or melt/spun processes. These dominate **90 %+** of all space-grade reinforcement applications.

Synthetic fibres are further sub-divided into:

- **Organic Synthetic Fibres** — Carbon-based polymer chains (e.g., Aramid, PBI, PBO).
- **Inorganic Synthetic Fibres** — Non-carbon ceramic/glass/metallic crystalline filaments (e.g., Carbon fibre, Glass fibre, Boron fibre, Basalt fibre, Ceramic fibres).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Reinforced Concrete" Analogy:**
> Imagine a space rocket fairing as a building. The **polymer matrix (epoxy/cyanate ester)** is like the **concrete** — strong in compression but weak in tension and prone to cracking. The **fibres** are like the **steel rebar** embedded inside — they carry tensile and shear loads, prevent crack propagation, and give the composite its directional "muscle." Just as steel rebar comes in different grades (mild steel, HYSD, TMT bars) for different structural needs, **fibres come in different types** (E-glass, S-glass, Kevlar, T800 carbon) — each picked for a specific space-environment stress (thermal, mechanical, ablative, dielectric).

### 1.4 Why Fibres Matter in Space Engineering

| Property | Role in Spacecraft | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **High Specific Strength** (σ/ρ) | Reduces launch mass | Higher payload fraction |
| **High Specific Modulus** (E/ρ) | Resists buckling under compression | Stiffer panels & tanks |
| **Low Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** | Maintains dimensions in ±150 °C orbits | Optical & antenna stability |
| **Flame/Ablative Resistance** | Re-entry heating | Thermal Protection Systems (TPS) |
| **Dielectric Transparency** | Radome & antenna housings | Undisturbed RF signal |
| **Radiation Resistance** | GEO/MEO exposure | Long service life |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Fibres

#### 2.1.1 Natural Fibres

| Sub-Class | Examples | Composition | Space-Grade Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulosic (Plant)** | Flax, Jute, Hemp, Sisal | Cellulose + Lignin | Research-stage bio-composites for satellite interiors (low-cost CubeSats) |
| **Protein (Animal)** | Silk, Wool | Fibroin / Keratin | Limited; silk studied for impact-absorbing layers |
| **Mineral (Asbestos-class)** | Asbestos | Hydrated Mg-Silicate | **Banned** — health hazard; historical use only |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 emphasises synthetic fibres for space; natural fibres appear only as **comparative/introductory context**.

#### 2.1.2 Synthetic Organic Fibres (Polymer-Based)

| Fibre | Chemical Backbone | Key Trade Names | Tg / Tm (°C) | Space Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aramid (PPTA)** | Poly-*p*-phenylene terephthalamide | Kevlar 49, Kevlar 129, Twaron | Tg ≈ 360 | Pressure vessels, TPS, MMOD shielding |
| **PBI (Polybenzimidazole)** | Poly-2,2′-(*m*-phenylene)-5,5′-bibenzimidazole | Celazole PBI | Tg ≈ 427 | Re-entry ablatives, astronaut suits |
| **PBO (Polybenzoxazole)** | Poly-*p*-phenylene-2,6-benzobisoxazole | Zylon (Toyobo) | Tg ≈ 320, decomposes >650 | Tensile members, tethers |
| **UHMWPE** | Ultra-High-Molecular-Weight Polyethylene | Spectra, Dyneema | Tm ≈ 150 | Ballistic/MMOD layers, tether lines |
| **PI (Polyimide)** | Aromatic imide repeat units | P84, Kapton fibre form | Tg > 300 | High-temp insulation felts |

#### 2.1.3 Synthetic Inorganic Fibres

| Fibre | Composition | Density (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength (GPa) | Modulus (GPa) | Space Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E-Glass** | Al₂O₃-B₂O₃-CaO-SiO₂ | 2.55 | 3.4–4.7 | 72–85 | Radomes, antenna housings |
| **S-Glass / S-2 Glass** | MgO-Al₂O₃-SiO₂ (high strength) | 2.46 | 4.5–5.1 | 86–97 | Rocket motor casings, pressure tanks |
| **Quartz** | > 99.5 % SiO₂ | 2.20 | 3.5–4.0 | 69–75 | Radomes (radar-transparent) |
| **Carbon (PAN-based)** | 92–100 % C, graphitic | 1.75–2.15 | 2.4–7.0 | 230–900 | Primary structures (CFRP) |
| **Boron** | W-core / B deposit | 2.50–2.65 | 3.5–5.0 | 380–400 | Stiffeners, fighter-jet heritage; rare in spacecraft |
| **Ceramic (Alumina, SiC)** | Al₂O₃ / SiC | 3.0–3.4 | 1.5–3.0 | 200–420 | Hypersonic leading edges (research) |
| **Basalt** | Natural igneous rock fibre | 2.65 | 2.8–4.8 | 80–110 | Cost-effective TPS, propellant tanks |

### 2.2 Microstructural Origin of Fibre Properties

The extraordinary properties of high-performance fibres arise from **molecular-level alignment and bonding**:

- **Aramids**: Rigid-rod *para*-linked aromatic backbone → chains pack in hydrogen-bonded sheets → **highly crystalline, anisotropic**.
- **Carbon fibres**: Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) precursor is oxidised, carbonised (1000–1500 °C), and optionally graphitised (>2000 °C) → turbostratic/graphitic planes align along fibre axis → covalent C–C bond gives exceptional modulus.
- **Glass fibres**: Amorphous SiO₂ network; strength comes from drawing-induced surface flaw reduction and silica tetrahedral network continuity.
- **UHMWPE**: Extended-chain polyethylene with molecular weights > 3 × 10⁶ g/mol; ultra-draw (>30×) aligns chains to near-theoretical modulus (~300 GPa).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are essential for KTU numerical problems and derivations.**

| Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_f}$ | Pa (MPa/GPa) | Maximum stress fibre can sustain |
| Young's Modulus | $E_f = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon_f}$ | Pa (GPa) | Resistance to elastic deformation |
| Specific Strength | $\dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho_f}$ | $\mathrm{Pa \cdot m^3/kg}$ or $\mathrm{N \cdot m/kg}$ | Strength per unit mass (launch-critical) |
| Specific Modulus | $\dfrac{E_f}{\rho_f}$ | $\mathrm{Pa \cdot m^3/kg}$ | Stiffness per unit mass |
| Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$ | % | Ductility/toughness indicator |
| Linear Density (Tex) | $T = \dfrac{m\,[\text{g}]}{L\,[\text{km}]}$ | g/km | Mass per unit length of yarn |
| Denier | $D = \dfrac{m\,[\text{g}]}{L\,[\text{9000 m}]}$ | denier | 9× length of Tex |
| Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f}{A_c}$ (composite rule) | dimensionless | Reinforcement loading |
| Rule of Mixtures (Modulus) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1-V_f)$ | Pa | Composite axial stiffness |
| Rule of Mixtures (Strength, longitudinal) | $\sigma_c \approx \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m' (1-V_f)$ | Pa | Composite axial strength |
| CTE (Composite, longitudinal) | $\alpha_c = \dfrac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m (1-V_f)}{E_f V_f + E_m (1-V_f)}$ | 1/K | Dimensional stability under thermal cycling |
| Critical Fibre Length | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d_f}{2 \tau_i}$ | m | Min length for full fibre stress transfer |
| Aspect Ratio | $a = \dfrac{l_f}{d_f}$ | dimensionless | Must exceed $l_c/d_f$ for effective reinforcement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Fibre Length ($l_c$)** is the minimum length required for the fibre-matrix interfacial shear stress ($\tau_i$) to build up enough axial stress in the fibre to reach its ultimate tensile strength. If $l_f < l_c$, the fibre **pulls out** and never reaches full strength — a high-yield KTU concept.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Space

- **Specific strength & specific modulus** directly drive **payload mass fraction** (the Tsiolkovsky equation: $\Delta v = v_e \ln(m_0/m_f)$ — every kilogram saved by carbon-fibre reinforcement multiplies into launch-cost savings).
- **Low CTE** makes carbon-fibre composites the material of choice for **space telescope mirrors (JWST)**, **antenna reflectors**, and **optical benches**, where sub-micron dimensional stability across 200 K thermal swings is required.
- **Ablative behaviour of aramid/carbon fibre-reinforced phenolic** is exploited in **PICA / Avcoat** thermal protection systems used on SpaceX Dragon and NASA Orion crew capsule.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Composite Modulus (Longitudinal)

**Assumptions**: Fibres continuous, aligned parallel to load, perfect fibre-matrix bonding, both phases undergo equal strain (iso-strain, Voigt model).

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium** on a composite cross-section of area $A_c$ under axial load $P$:

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express each force in terms of stress and area**:

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 3 — Divide through by $A_c$ and define volume fractions** $V_f = A_f / A_c$ and $V_m = A_m / A_c = 1 - V_f$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply iso-strain condition** (fibre and matrix deform together):

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_c, \quad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_c
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the stress equation**:

$$
\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon_c V_f + E_m \varepsilon_c (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by the common strain** $\varepsilon_c$:

$$
\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

**Interpretation**: Composite modulus is a **weighted average** of fibre and matrix moduli, weighted by volume fraction. Since $E_f \gg E_m$ for carbon/glass/aramid in epoxy ($E_m \approx 3$ GPa), even a moderate $V_f \approx 0.6$ drives $E_c$ well above $E_m$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Strength Comparison (Worked Numerical)

**Problem**: Compare the specific strength of **Kevlar 49** and **T800 carbon fibre** for a satellite structural strut.

Given:
- Kevlar 49: $\sigma_f = 3.6$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1.44$ g/cm³
- T800 carbon: $\sigma_f = 5.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Convert units** to consistent SI:

$$
\rho_{\text{Kevlar}} = 1.44 \, \mathrm{g/cm^3} = 1440 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}
$$
$$
\rho_{\text{T800}} = 1.80 \, \mathrm{g/cm^3} = 1800 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute specific strength** (strength ÷ density):

$$
\left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)_{\text{Kevlar}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^9}{1440} = 2.50 \times 10^6 \, \mathrm{N \cdot m/kg}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)_{\text{T800}} = \frac{5.5 \times 10^9}{1800} = 3.06 \times 10^6 \, \mathrm{N \cdot m/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage advantage of T800 over Kevlar**:

$$
\Delta\% = \frac{3.06 - 2.50}{2.50} \times 100\% = 22.4\%
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation**:

T800 carbon fibre is **~22 % more mass-efficient** in tensile loading. However, Kevlar 49 still wins for **impact / MMOD (Micrometeoroid & Orbital Debris) shielding** due to its superior toughness and energy absorption — engineers therefore choose **by mission requirement**, not single number.

### 3.3 Derivation: Critical Fibre Length

**Setup**: A short fibre of length $l_f$ and diameter $d_f$ embedded in a matrix, loaded via interfacial shear $\tau_i$.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium** between fibre axial tension and surface shear:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d_f) \cdot \frac{l_f}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Cancel common terms** ($\pi d_f$ on both sides):

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d_f}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \frac{l_f}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the critical length** $l_c$ at which $\sigma_f$ reaches its ultimate value $\sigma_{fu}$:

$$
\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d_f}{2 \tau_i}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define efficiency condition**:

The fibre is "efficient" only if $l_f \geq l_c$. The **aspect ratio** must therefore satisfy:

$$
\frac{l_f}{d_f} \geq \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}
$$

**Worked Check**: For carbon fibre ($d_f = 7\,\mu$m, $\sigma_{fu} = 5$ GPa) in epoxy ($\tau_i = 40$ MPa):

$$
l_c = \frac{(5 \times 10^9)(7 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (40 \times 10^6)} = 0.44 \, \mathrm{mm}
$$

For typical $l_f = 6$ mm chopped fibre: $l_f/l_c \approx 14$ → efficient reinforcement. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fibre Selection for Space

| Selection Criterion | Best Fibre | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum stiffness in tension | **Carbon (HM)** | $E$ up to 900 GPa (Pitch-based) |
| Maximum tensile strength | **Carbon (IM/HS)** | $\sigma_f$ up to 7 GPa (T1000) |
| Impact / MMOD resistance | **Aramid (Kevlar)** | High tenacity, low brittleness |
| Radar transparency (radome) | **Quartz / E-Glass** | Low dielectric loss, $\tan \delta < 0.01$ |
| Ablation / re-entry heat shield | **Carbon-Phenolic / PBI** | High char yield, low thermal conductivity |
| Cryogenic tank (LH₂, LOX) | **S-Glass / T700 Carbon** | Low permeability, low CTE mismatch with Al-Li |
| Cost-sensitive (e.g., 1U CubeSat) | **E-Glass / Basalt** | $1/10$ cost of carbon; adequate for low-load panels |
| Radiation tolerance (GEO) | **Ceramic (SiC, Al₂O₃)** | Inorganic, immune to UV/EPR degradation |
