## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between natural and synthetic fibres. Why are synthetic fibres preferred for space applications?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Natural Fibres | Synthetic Fibres |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Plants, animals, minerals | Chemically polymerised/engineered |
| Composition | Cellulose, protein, silicate | Polymeric (organic) or ceramic/metallic (inorganic) |
| Consistency | Variable, batch-dependent | Highly uniform, reproducible |
| Moisture sensitivity | High (hygroscopic) | Low (engineered) |
| Space suitability | Limited | Preferred |

Synthetic fibres are preferred in space applications due to **high specific strength & modulus**, **tailorable thermal expansion**, **low outgassing** (when qualified per **NASA ASTM E595**), **predictable batch-to-batch mechanical behaviour**, and **compatibility with high-performance matrices** (epoxy, cyanate ester, polyimide). Natural fibres fail to meet NASA low-outgassing (< 1 % TML, < 0.1 % CVCM) criteria and exhibit poor reproducibility for flight hardware.

*(1 mark — distinction table; 1 mark — space-property linkage; 1 mark — example with ASTM E595 reference.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Why is carbon fibre the most widely used reinforcement in modern spacecraft primary structures? Justify with two specific quantitative properties."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Carbon fibre dominates spacecraft primary structures because:

1. **Exceptionally high specific modulus**: T800 carbon: $E/\rho \approx 230\,\mathrm{GPa}/1.80\,\mathrm{g/cm^3} \approx 128\,\mathrm{GPa \cdot cm^3/g}$, which is **5–7× higher** than E-glass and **~10× higher** than aluminium alloys. This allows minimum-mass stiff panels for satellite bus, antenna reflectors, and optical benches.

2. **Near-zero / negative CTE**: Pitch-based and high-modulus PAN carbon fibres exhibit $\alpha_f \approx -0.5 \times 10^{-6}/\mathrm{K}$ along the fibre axis, enabling **dimensionally stable** composite structures (JWST backplane, ALMA antenna panels) that survive ±150 K thermal cycling without warping.

3. Bonus point (1 mark): Low density (1.75–2.15 g/cm³) and excellent fatigue resistance under cyclic orbital loading.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of fibres used in space applications with suitable examples for each class. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the manufacturing process of PAN-based carbon fibre. Discuss how each processing stage influences the final fibre properties. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Fibres — 7 marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Fibres used in space applications are slender reinforcements with aspect ratio > 100:1, designed to bear axial tensile loads in polymer matrix composites (PMCs). They are classified into **two broad families**: natural and synthetic.

**Classification Table (4 marks):**

| Class | Sub-Class | Examples | Space Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural** | Cellulosic | Flax, Jute, Hemp | Bio-composite research; non-structural CubeSat panels |
| | Protein | Silk, Wool | Impact absorption layers (research) |
| | Mineral | Asbestos | **Banned** (health hazard) |
| **Synthetic Organic** | Aramid (PPTA) | Kevlar 49, Twaron | MMOD shielding, pressure vessels, TPS |
| | PBI | Celazole PBI | Re-entry ablatives, astronaut suit inner liners |
| | PBO | Zylon | Tethers, tensile members |
| | UHMWPE | Spectra, Dyneema | Ballistic layers, lightweight tethers |
| **Synthetic Inorganic** | Glass | E-glass, S-glass, Quartz | Radomes, tank reinforcements |
| | Carbon | PAN-based (T300, T800), Pitch-based | Primary structures (CFRP) |
| | Boron | B/W core | Stiffeners (limited spacecraft use) |
| | Ceramic | Al₂O₃, SiC | Hypersonic leading edges, radiation shielding |
| | Basalt | Basalt fibre | Cost-effective TPS, propellant tanks |

**Application-driven choice (2 marks):**
Selection depends on mission profile:
- **Stiffness-critical** (antennas, optical benches) → High-modulus carbon (M55J, M65J).
- **Impact-critical** (MMOD shielding) → Aramid (Kevlar 29/49) or UHMWPE (Dyneema).
- **Dielectric-critical** (radomes) → Quartz or E-glass.
- **Ablation-critical** (re-entry TPS) → Carbon-phenolic or PBI composites.

**(b) Manufacturing of PAN-based Carbon Fibre — 7 marks**

**Process Flow (Diagram: 2 marks):**

```
PAN Polymer → Spinning → Stabilisation (200–300 °C, air)
→ Carbonisation (1000–1500 °C, N₂)
→ Optional Graphitisation (2000–3000 °C, Ar)
→ Surface Treatment (electrolytic oxidation)
→ Sizing (epoxy-compatible) → Carbon Fibre Tow
```

**Stage-wise Explanation (5 marks):**

| Stage | Conditions | Purpose | Property Influence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spinning** | Wet or dry-jet wet | Forms continuous PAN filaments with diameter 5–7 µm | Sets precursor diameter & uniformity |
| **Stabilisation** | 200–300 °C in air, under tension | Converts linear PAN to ladder polymer via cyclisation & oxidation | Creates thermally stable infusible structure for carbonisation |
| **Carbonisation** | 1000–1500 °C in inert N₂ | Drives off non-carbon atoms (H, N, O); forms turbostratic carbon | Determines tensile strength; higher T → higher modulus, lower elongation |
| **Graphitisation** (optional) | 2000–3000 °C in Ar | Aligns graphitic planes along fibre axis | Dramatically increases modulus (HM-grade carbon: 400–900 GPa) |
| **Surface Treatment** | Electrolytic oxidation | Introduces polar -COOH/-OH groups | Improves fibre-matrix adhesion |
| **Sizing** | Epoxy/PU coating | Protects surface and enhances handling | Ensures good wettability by matrix resin |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall:**
> Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Confusing PAN precursor with pitch precursor** — PAN-based carbon is the *default* in spacecraft; pitch-based gives highest modulus but lower tensile strength.
> 2. **Omitting temperatures** — always quote stage temperature ranges (e.g., 200–300 °C stabilisation).
> 3. **Skipping surface treatment** — examiners specifically test whether you understand *why* surface treatment is needed (interfacial shear strength).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare the properties of glass, carbon, and aramid fibres with respect to their mechanical, thermal, and chemical behaviour. Identify the most suitable fibre for (i) a satellite antenna reflector and (ii) a micrometeoroid shielding layer, with justification. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **specific strength** and **specific modulus**. For a composite strut with $V_f = 0.55$, fibre modulus $E_f = 230$ GPa, fibre density $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³, matrix modulus $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, matrix density $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, compute: (i) composite longitudinal modulus, (ii) composite density, (iii) specific modulus of the composite. Comment on its suitability for a launch-vehicle payload adapter. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table & Selection — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | E-Glass | Carbon (T800) | Aramid (Kevlar 49) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.55 | 1.80 | 1.44 |
| Tensile Strength (GPa) | 3.4–4.7 | 5.5 | 3.6 |
| Tensile Modulus (GPa) | 72–85 | 230 | 130 |
| Elongation at Break (%) | 4.5 | 1.7 | 2.8 |
| CTE (10⁻⁶/K) axial | +5.0 | −0.5 to +1.0 | −2 to −6 |
| Max Service Temp (°C) | 550 | 400 (in air) | 250 |
| Moisture Absorption | Negligible | Negligible | ~3.5 % |
| Chemical Resistance | Good (HF attack) | Excellent | Good (UV-sensitive) |
| Cost | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |

**Selection (3 marks):**

(i) **Satellite antenna reflector** → **High-Modulus Carbon Fibre (e.g., M55J)**
*Justification*: Near-zero/negative CTE ensures dimensional stability across ±150 K LEO thermal cycles; high specific modulus minimises reflector mass; CFRP provides the surface accuracy (< 50 µm RMS) needed for Ka/Ku-band communication.

(ii) **Micrometeoroid & Orbital Debris (MMOD) shielding** → **Aramid (Kevlar 49) or UHMWPE (Dyneema)**
*Justification*: Highest impact energy absorption per unit mass; high tenacity and toughness prevent brittle perforation; layered "Whipple shield" architecture uses Kevlar as a "stuffed" or "curtain" layer behind the outer bumper, vaporising hypervelocity debris into expanding vapour clouds.

---

**(b) Specific Strength, Specific Modulus & Numerical Problem — 7 marks**

**Definitions (1 mark):**
- **Specific Strength** $\dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho_f}$ — tensile strength per unit mass; critical for weight-sensitive structures.
- **Specific Modulus** $\dfrac{E_f}{\rho_f}$ — elastic modulus per unit mass; critical for stiffness-sensitive structures.

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$
- $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³ = 1800 kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³ = 1200 kg/m³

**Part (i) — Composite longitudinal modulus** (Rule of Mixtures, Voigt iso-strain):

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (230)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)
$$

$$
E_c = 126.5 + 1.575 = 128.075 \, \mathrm{GPa}
$$

**Part (ii) — Composite density** (density rule of mixtures):

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (1.80)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 0.990 + 0.540 = 1.530 \, \mathrm{g/cm^3}
$$

**Part (iii) — Specific modulus of composite**:

$$
\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_c = \frac{128.075 \, \mathrm{GPa}}{1.530 \, \mathrm{g/cm^3}} = 83.7 \, \mathrm{GPa \cdot cm^3/g}
$$

Converting to SI: $\dfrac{128.075 \times 10^9 \, \mathrm{Pa}}{1530 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}} = 8.37 \times 10^7 \, \mathrm{N \cdot m/kg}$.

**Comment on suitability for launch-vehicle payload adapter (2 marks):**

A **payload adapter** (e.g., PSLV/XL, Ariane 5, Falcon 9) sits between the launcher's upper stage and the satellite, transmitting **compression, bending, and thrust loads** while adding **minimum mass**. The composite here has $E_c = 128$ GPa at $\rho_c = 1.53$ g/cm³ — roughly **5× stiffer than aluminium 2024-T3 ($E = 73$ GPa, $\rho = 2.78$ g/cm³)** at **45 % lower density**. The specific modulus of 83.7 GPa·cm³/g is **exceptionally high**, making this composite **highly suitable** for a payload adapter, provided:
- Outgassing per **NASA ASTM E595** is verified.
- Interlaminar shear strength is ensured via appropriate fibre-matrix coupling (e.g., sized T800/epoxy with $V_f \approx 0.55–0.60$).
- CTE mismatch with metallic interfaces is managed through hybrid ply schedules.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Numerical):**
> 1. **Always show unit conversions explicitly** (e.g., g/cm³ → kg/m³) — losing 0.5 mark if skipped.
> 2. **Apply the rule of mixtures in correct order** — $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$, NOT $E_c = (E_f + E_m)/2$.
> 3. **State assumptions**: iso-strain (Voigt), continuous aligned fibres, perfect bonding — without this, full marks may be withheld.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Fibres (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Definition**: Fibre = aspect ratio > 100:1, axial load-bearing reinforcement.
- ☐ **Two master families**: Natural (limited space use) vs Synthetic (dominant).
- ☐ **Synthetic split**: Organic (Aramid, PBI, PBO, UHMWPE, PI) vs Inorganic (Glass, Carbon, Boron, Ceramic, Basalt).
- ☐ **PAN route for carbon fibre** is the **default** spacecraft route — remember the 5 stages: Spinning → Stabilisation (200–300 °C, air) → Carbonisation (1000–1500 °C, N₂) → Graphitisation (optional, 2000–3000 °C) → Surface treatment + Sizing.
- ☐ **Specific strength & specific modulus** are the **two most important metrics** in space — they directly drive payload mass fraction.
- ☐ **Rule of Mixtures** (Voigt, iso-strain): $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ — used for *longitudinal* modulus with aligned continuous fibres.
- ☐ **Density rule of mixtures**: $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$.
- ☐ **CTE rule of mixtures (longitudinal)**: $\alpha_c = (\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m)/(E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$ — negative $\alpha_f$ in carbon/aramid allows near-zero composite CTE.
- ☐ **Critical fibre length**: $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d_f / (2\tau_i)$ — fibre must satisfy $l_f \geq l_c$ for efficient reinforcement.
- ☐ **Carbon fibre** = highest specific modulus & strength → primary structures, antennas, optical benches.
- ☐ **Aramid (Kevlar)** = highest impact toughness → MMOD shielding, pressure vessels.
- ☐ **Quartz / E-glass** = best dielectric → radomes & antenna housings.
- ☐ **PBI / Carbon-Phenolic** = ablation-resistant → re-entry TPS (PICA, Avcoat heritage).
- ☐ **Basalt** = cost-effective alternative to glass → non-critical propellant tanks & TPS.
- ☐ **Always cite NASA ASTM E595** outgassing limits (TML < 1 %, CVCM < 0.1 %) when justifying "space-grade" fibre selection.
- ☐ **Pitfall**: Don't confuse *pitch-based* carbon (highest modulus, lowest strength) with *PAN-based* carbon (highest strength, moderate modulus) — both exist; choice depends on stiffness vs strength priority.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Properties of Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties of Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **mechanical properties of fibres** refer to the suite of quantifiable responses exhibited by a fibre when subjected to external mechanical forces, including tension, compression, torsion, and flexure. In polymer science, these properties are intrinsic descriptors of the fibre's ability to transmit, distribute, and absorb load without undergoing permanent deformation or failure. For aerospace-grade polymeric fibres, the primary mechanical descriptors governed by KTU syllabus standards are:

- **Tensile Strength (σ):** Maximum axial stress a fibre can sustain before rupture. Units: **MPa** or **GPa**.
- **Young's Modulus (E):** Ratio of axial stress to axial strain in the elastic region. Units: **GPa**.
- **Elongation at Break (ε_b):** Percentage strain at the point of fibre failure. Units: **%**.
- **Toughness:** Total energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture. Units: **MJ/m³** or **J/g**.
- **Specific Strength and Specific Modulus:** Strength and modulus normalized by density (ρ). Units: **MPa/(g/cm³)** and **GPa/(g/cm³)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For space applications, *specific* mechanical properties (property-to-weight ratio) are more significant than absolute values because payload mass is the single most expensive commodity in launch economics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **steel bridge cable** holding up a heavy suspension bridge. Now imagine a **spider silk strand** of the same diameter trying to do the same job. The steel cable is strong, but it is *heavy*. The spider silk, in a relative sense, can absorb enormous energy per unit mass before snapping. This is exactly the philosophy of fibre selection in space applications.

Think of a fibre as a **bundle of microscopic chains** (polymer chains). When you pull on the fibre:

1. The polymer chains **straighten** out (elastic region).
2. They then begin to **slide past each other** (yielding/plastic region).
3. Finally, they **break** (failure point).

The "mechanical property" is just a measurement of how gracefully (or ungracefully) this chain of events happens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Mechanical properties of fibres are simply "how much can you pull, stretch, and load this thread before it gives up?" — measured by standardized pulling tests and reported as a family of stress-strain numbers.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Space-Application Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Density | Tex / Denier | g/km | Mass per unit length of fibre |
| Tensile Strength | σ_t | **GPa** | Maximum load capacity |
| Young's Modulus | E | **GPa** | Stiffness under axial load |
| Density | ρ | **g/cm³** | Critical for specific properties |
| Specific Strength | σ/ρ | **MPa/(g/cm³)** | Strength-to-weight efficiency |
| Specific Modulus | E/ρ | **GPa/(g/cm³)** | Stiffness-to-weight efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> A common benchmark constant in space-grade fibre evaluation: **Kevlar 49** possesses a specific strength of approximately **2,025 MPa/(g/cm³)** — about **5× that of structural steel** on a per-weight basis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stress–Strain Curve for Fibres

The fundamental descriptor of fibre mechanical behaviour is the **uniaxial tensile stress–strain curve**, obtained by gripping a single fibre (or fibre bundle) and stretching it under controlled displacement until failure. The curve is divided into four distinct regions:

### Region I: Hookean (Elastic) Region
- Stress is **linearly proportional** to strain.
- The slope of this linear region is the **Young's Modulus (E)**.
- Deformation is fully recoverable upon unloading.
- Polymer chains undergo **bond stretching** and minor **chain rotation**.

### Region II: Yield Region
- The point where the curve deviates from linearity is the **proportional limit**.
- Continued loading produces **plastic (permanent) deformation**.
- Crystalline regions begin to **reorient** along the loading axis.

### Region III: Strain Hardening (Orientation Hardening)
- Polymer chains align with the tensile axis.
- The fibre **stiffens** again as chains resist further sliding.
- Common in **ductile** fibres like nylon and UHMWPE.

### Region IV: Failure / Fracture
- The terminal point of the curve.
- At **ultimate tensile strength (σ_uts)**, the fibre ruptures.
- The corresponding strain is the **elongation at break (ε_b)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Note:** The area *under* the entire stress–strain curve equals the **toughness** of the fibre — i.e., the energy absorbed per unit volume before fracture. A high-toughness fibre is desired for impact-resistant space structures (e.g., micrometeoroid shielding).

## 2.2 Classification by Stress–Strain Behaviour

Fibres are broadly classified into **three** families based on their tensile response:

1. **High-Modulus, Low-Strain (Brittle) Fibres:** Carbon, Glass, Ceramics. Used where stiffness dominates.
2. **High-Strength, High-Modulus, Moderate-Strain Fibres:** Kevlar (Aramid), UHMWPE (Spectra/Dyneema), PBO (Zylon). Used in structural composites.
3. **High-Strain (Ductile) Fibres:** Nylon, Polyester, Polypropylene. Used in textiles and flexible space-deployable structures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variable Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | F = axial load, A₀ = original cross-sectional area | MPa |
| Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | ΔL = elongation, L₀ = gauge length | dimensionless |
| Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Slope of elastic region | GPa |
| Specific Strength | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\rho}$ | Tensile strength per density | MPa/(g/cm³) |
| Specific Modulus | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Modulus per density | GPa/(g/cm³) |
| Toughness | $U_t = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Area under stress-strain curve | MJ/m³ |
| Tenacity | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\text{Tex}} \times 10^4$ | Strength per linear density | cN/tex |
| Breaking Length | $L_b = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Length of fibre that breaks under own weight | km |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the space industry, mechanical property characterization is **mission-critical** for:

- **Re-entry Vehicle TPS (Thermal Protection Systems):** Fibrous felts (e.g., ceramic fibre blankets) must withstand aerodynamic loading without shedding.
- **Inflatable Habitat Modules:** Flexible fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) bladders require high tear strength and burst resistance.
- **Space Elevator Tether Concepts:** Theoretical tethers (e.g., carbon nanotube-reinforced polymer) demand specific strength > 50,000 MPa/(g/cm³).
- **Micrometeoroid Shielding (Whipple Shields):** Multi-layer Kevlar or Nextel blankets absorb hypervelocity particle impacts via fibre rupture.
- **Parachute Risers & Cords:** UHMWPE spectra lines replace steel for low mass and high tenacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The single most important mechanical parameter for *load-bearing* space structures is the **specific modulus**, while for *impact-absorbing* structures it is the **toughness**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Specific Strength from First Principles

The **specific strength** is a derived quantity that normalizes the absolute tensile strength against the density of the material. Derivation proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Definition of Tensile Strength**

Tensile strength is defined as the maximum axial force a fibre sustains divided by its original cross-sectional area.

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}$$

where $F_{max}$ is the breaking load (in N) and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area (in m²).

**Step 2 — Definition of Density**

Density is mass per unit volume. For a fibre of length $L$ and cross-section $A_0$:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{m}{A_0 \cdot L}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for Area in Terms of Mass**

Rearranging the density equation for $A_0$:

$$A_0 = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot L}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting Into the Stress Equation**

Plugging this expression for $A_0$ into the tensile strength formula:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F_{max}}{\left(\dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot L}\right)} = \frac{F_{max} \cdot \rho \cdot L}{m}$$

**Step 5 — Recognizing That F/m = Acceleration Equivalent**

Define $a_{equiv} = F_{max}/m$ (force per unit mass):

$$\sigma_t = a_{equiv} \cdot \rho \cdot L$$

**Step 6 — Definition of Specific Strength**

The specific strength is the tensile strength divided by density. This gives a "force-per-unit-mass per unit length" quantity with a clear physical meaning:

$$\sigma_{sp} = \frac{\sigma_t}{\rho}$$

Substituting $\sigma_t = F_{max} \cdot L / m$ into this division:

$$\sigma_{sp} = \frac{F_{max} \cdot L}{m \cdot \rho \cdot \rho} = \frac{F_{max} \cdot L}{m \cdot \rho^2}$$

This form, however, is not commonly used. In practice, we retain the working definition:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{sp} = \frac{\sigma_t}{\rho}}$$

with units $\dfrac{\text{MPa}}{\text{g/cm}^3}$ or equivalently $\dfrac{\text{N} \cdot \text{cm}^3}{\text{g} \cdot \text{m}^2}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Breaking Length

The **breaking length** is the length of a fibre suspended vertically that will cause the fibre to break under its own weight. It is an elegant way of comparing fibres independently of fibre diameter.

**Step 1 — Force Due to Self-Weight**

A fibre of length $L$, cross-section $A_0$, and density $\rho$ has weight:

$$W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot A_0 \cdot L \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Force-Equating to Maximum Tensile Capacity**

The fibre breaks when the weight equals the maximum tensile capacity:

$$F_{max} = \sigma_t \cdot A_0$$

**Step 3 — Equating the Two**

At the breaking condition:

$$\sigma_t \cdot A_0 = \rho \cdot A_0 \cdot L_b \cdot g$$

The cross-sectional area $A_0$ cancels (showing that the result is independent of fibre diameter):

$$\sigma_t = \rho \cdot L_b \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the Breaking Length**

$$\boxed{L_b = \frac{\sigma_t}{\rho \cdot g}}$$

**Example Numerical Evaluation:**

For **Kevlar 49**: $\sigma_t \approx 3.0$ GPa, $\rho \approx 1.44$ g/cm³ $= 1440$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$L_b = \frac{3.0 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}}{1440 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2}$$

$$\boxed{L_b \approx 212 \, \text{km}}$$

This means a Kevlar 49 fibre could theoretically suspend a 212 km length of itself before breaking under its own weight.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Space-Grade Fibres

| Fibre Type | Density ρ (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength σ_t (GPa) | Young's Modulus E (GPa) | Specific Strength (MPa/(g/cm³)) | Elongation at Break (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E-Glass | 2.55 | 3.45 | 72 | 1,353 | 4.5 |
| S-Glass | 2.49 | 4.65 | 88 | 1,867 | 5.3 |
| Carbon (PAN, HM) | 1.85 | 4.50 | 390 | 2,432 | 1.5 |
| Kevlar 49 (Aramid) | 1.44 | 3.00 | 112 | 2,083 | 2.5 |
| Spectra 1000 (UHMWPE) | 0.97 | 3.00 | 100 | 3,093 | 3.5 |
| Zylon (PBO) | 1.56 | 5.80 | 180 | 3,718 | 3.5 |
| Steel (Reference) | 7.85 | 1.50 | 200 | 191 | 12.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Observation:** UHMWPE (Spectra) and Zylon (PBO) have the **highest specific strength** values, making them the most weight-efficient structural fibres. Carbon fibre dominates in **specific stiffness** applications such as satellite booms and antenna reflectors.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Evaluating a Space Tether

**Problem:** A space tether of length 50 km is to be made from a single continuous fibre. The allowable payload mass is 1,200 kg. Determine whether **Kevlar 49** (σ_t = 3.0 GPa, ρ = 1,440 kg/m³) or **Spectra 1000** (σ_t = 3.0 GPa, ρ = 970 kg/m³) is the optimal choice.

**Step 1 — Required Cross-Section to Hold the Payload**

The static load on the tether is:

$$F = m \cdot g_{eff}$$

In orbit, the tether must support the centripetal acceleration differential. Assume effective acceleration = 0.1 m/s² (typical for rotating tethers):

$$F = 1200 \times 0.1 = 120 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Required Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A_{req} = \frac{F}{\sigma_t} = \frac{120}{3.0 \times 10^9} = 4.0 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^2 = 0.04 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Self-Weight of the Tether (Kevlar 49)**

$$m_{kevlar} = \rho \cdot A_{req} \cdot L = 1440 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-8} \times 50{,}000 = 2.88 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Self-Weight of the Tether (Spectra 1000)**

$$m_{spectra} = 970 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-8} \times 50{,}000 = 1.94 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison**

Mass saving using Spectra:

$$\Delta m = 2.88 - 1.94 = 0.94 \, \text{kg}$$

$$\text{Saving} = \frac{0.94}{2.88} \times 100\% \approx 32.6\%$$

**Conclusion:** **Spectra 1000** is the optimal choice for minimum tether mass, even though both fibres have identical absolute tensile strength. The difference emerges entirely from the **specific strength** property.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tensile Test Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load Frame Crosshead] --> B[Grip Assembly]
    B --> C[Single Fibre Specimen]
    C --> D[Lower Grip Assembly]
    D --> E[Load Cell - Force Measurement]
    E --> F[Crosshead Displacement Encoder]
    F --> G[Data Acquisition System]
    G --> H[Real-Time Stress-Strain Plotter]
    H --> I[Failure Point Detector]
    I --> J[Output: Sigma_t and Epsilon_b]
    A --> K[Motorized Drive System]
    K --> A
    G --> L[Storage: Raw Data]
```

## 4.2 Stress–Strain Curve Topology for Space-Grade Fibres

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Tensile Load] --> B{Deformation Type?}
    B -- "Elastic Region" --> C[Hookean Region: Linear Stress-Strain]
    C --> D[Young's Modulus E Calculated]
    B -- "Yield Region" --> E[Plastic Deformation Begins]
    E --> F[Chain Reorientation]
    B -- "Strain Hardening" --> G[Orientation Hardening]
    G --> H[Ultimate Tensile Strength sigma_uts]
    B -- "Failure" --> I[Fibre Rupture]
    I --> J[Elongation at Break epsilon_b]
    H --> I
    D --> E
    F --> G
    J --> K[Toughness = Area Under Curve]
    K --> L[Report Mechanical Properties]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block: Fibre Property Selection Logic for Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Space Application Requirement] --> B{Load Type?}
    B -- "Static Tensile" --> C[Prioritize: High sigma_sp]
    B -- "Dynamic Impact" --> D[Prioritize: High Toughness]
    B -- "Stiffness Critical" --> E[Prioritize: High E_sp]
    C --> F[Candidate Fibres: Kevlar, PBO, UHMWPE]
    D --> G[Candidate Fibres: Spectra, Nylon]
    E --> H[Candidate Fibres: HM Carbon, S-Glass]
    F --> I[Validate against Mission Thermal Range]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Final Selection}
    J -- "Selected" --> K[Document Property Matrix]
    J -- "Rejected" --> L[Return to Candidate Pool]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: From Raw Fibre to Tested Specimen

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polymer Resin] --> B[Melt Spinning or Solution Spinning]
    B --> C[Drawing and Orientation]
    C --> D[Heat Setting]
    D --> E[Finished Fibre]
    E --> F[Specimen Mounting on Tab]
    F --> G[Conditioning at 23C, 50% RH]
    G --> H[Mounting in Tensile Test Frame]
    H --> I[Application of Axial Load]
    I --> J[Generation of Stress-Strain Curve]
    J --> K[Extraction of E, sigma_t, epsilon_b]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define specific strength and specific modulus. Why are these properties more important than absolute strength and modulus in space applications?**

**Model Answer:**
Specific strength is the tensile strength of a material divided by its density: $\sigma_{sp} = \sigma_t / \rho$. Specific modulus is the Young's modulus divided by density: $E_{sp} = E / \rho$. In space applications, payload mass is the dominant economic constraint, and the launch cost per kilogram is enormous. Therefore, two fibres with identical absolute strength but different densities will behave very differently when used in satellite or spacecraft structures. A fibre with higher specific strength provides the same load-bearing capacity for a smaller mass penalty. Hence, the specific properties are the true performance metrics for space-grade fibre selection.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between tensile strength and toughness of a fibre. Which property is more critical for micrometeoroid shielding and why?**

**Model Answer:**
Tensile strength is the maximum stress a fibre can sustain before rupture — it is a single point value on the stress–strain curve. Toughness, on the other hand, is the *total energy absorbed per unit volume* up to the point of fracture, mathematically equal to the area under the stress–strain curve: $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$. For micrometeoroid shielding, the fibre is not loaded slowly to its breaking point; it is subjected to a hypervelocity impact. The energy of the incoming particle must be absorbed and dissipated by the fibre layer. A high-toughness fibre (with a large area under the curve) is therefore more critical for this application than a fibre with merely a high peak strength.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** With the aid of a well-labeled stress–strain curve, explain the four characteristic regions of tensile deformation in a polymeric fibre. Indicate the property calculated from each region.

**Model Solution:**

The uniaxial tensile stress–strain curve of a polymeric fibre exhibits four characteristic regions:

**Region 1 — Hookean Elastic Region:**
- The initial linear portion of the curve.
- Stress is proportional to strain: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$.
- Deformation is fully recoverable.
- **Property calculated:** Young's Modulus $E$ = slope of this linear region.
- Molecular mechanism: Bond stretching and minor rotation about backbone bonds.

**Region 2 — Yield Region:**
- The curve deviates from linearity at the proportional limit.
- Plastic (irreversible) deformation begins.
- **Property identified:** Yield stress $\sigma_y$.
- Molecular mechanism: Polymer chains begin to slide past one another and reorient.

**Region 3 — Strain Hardening (Orientation Hardening):**
- The curve steepens again as chains align with the tensile axis.
- The fibre becomes stiffer and resists further deformation.
- **Property identified:** Strain hardening modulus.
- Molecular mechanism: Progressive alignment of crystalline and amorphous regions.

**Region 4 — Failure Region:**
- The terminal point of the curve.
- The fibre fractures at the ultimate tensile strength.
- **Properties calculated:** Ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{uts}$ and elongation at break $\varepsilon_b$.
- Toughness is the area enclosed under the *entire* curve.

A well-labeled diagram would show the four regions marked I, II, III, IV, with $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_{uts}$, and $\varepsilon_b$ clearly annotated, and the area representing toughness shaded.

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** A space-grade aramid fibre (Kevlar 49) has a tensile strength of 3.0 GPa, a Young's modulus of 112 GPa, and a density of 1,440 kg/m³. Calculate its (i) specific strength, (ii) specific modulus, and (iii) breaking length. Compare these values to a structural steel fibre (σ_t = 1.5 GPa, E = 200 GPa, ρ = 7,850 kg/m³) and comment on the suitability of Kevlar 49 for space applications.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Specific Strength of Kevlar 49:**

$$\sigma_{sp,K} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^3 \, \text{MPa}}{1.44 \, \text{g/cm}^3} = 2{,}083 \, \text{MPa/(g/cm}^3)$$

For steel:

$$\sigma_{sp,S} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^3 \, \text{MPa}}{7.85 \, \text{g/cm}^3} = 191 \, \text{MPa/(g/cm}^3)$$

Ratio: Kevlar is **10.9× stronger per unit mass** than steel.

**(ii) Specific Modulus of Kevlar 49:**

$$E_{sp,K} = \frac{112 \, \text{GPa}}{1.44 \, \text{g/cm}^3} = 77.8 \, \text{GPa/(g/cm}^3)$$

For steel:

$$E_{sp,S} = \frac{200 \, \text{GPa}}{7.85 \, \text{g/cm}^3} = 25.5 \, \text{GPa/(g/cm}^3)$$

Ratio: Kevlar is **3.05× stiffer per unit mass** than steel.

**(iii) Breaking Length of Kevlar 49:**

$$L_{b,K} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}}{1440 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2} \approx 212{,}400 \, \text{m} \approx 212 \, \text{km}$$

For steel:

$$L_{b,S} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9}{7850 \times 9.81} \approx 19{,}470 \, \text{m} \approx 19.5 \, \text{km}$$

Ratio: Kevlar's breaking length is **10.9× greater** than steel's.

**Comment on Suitability for Space Applications:**
Kevlar 49 has a specific strength that is more than an order of magnitude greater than structural steel. For a satellite component, replacing steel with Kevlar 49 (at equivalent load capacity) reduces structural mass by approximately 90%. Furthermore, Kevlar 49 has a higher specific modulus than steel, making it stiffer per unit mass — an essential property for space structures where dimensional stability is critical. **Kevlar 49 is therefore highly suitable for space applications** such as pressure vessel overwrapping, payload tie-downs, and inflatable habitat reinforcement.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the factors that affect the mechanical properties of polymeric fibres. Discuss how each factor can be controlled during processing to enhance fibre performance for space applications.

**Model Solution:**

The mechanical properties of polymeric fibres are influenced by several interrelated factors:

**1. Molecular Weight:**
- Higher molecular weight leads to greater chain entanglement and improved load transfer between chains.
- **Control:** Optimize the polymerization conditions to achieve a number-average molecular weight $M_n$ in the range of 50,000 to 200,000 g/mol for high-performance fibres.

**2. Degree of Orientation:**
- The alignment of polymer chains along the fibre axis dramatically affects modulus and strength.
- A highly oriented fibre (orientation factor $f \rightarrow 1$) has much higher tensile strength along the axis.
- **Control:** Use *draw ratio* — a high draw ratio (e.g., 20:1 to 50:1) in the spinning line increases orientation and dramatically improves mechanical properties. For example, UHMWPE fibres are gel-spun and super-drawn to achieve ultra-high orientation.

**3. Degree of Crystallinity:**
- Higher crystallinity increases modulus and reduces creep.
- **Control:** Annealing (heat treatment under tension) increases crystalline content. For example, gel-spun UHMWPE fibres are annealed at 130–150°C to increase crystallinity from ~60% to >85%.

**4. Inter-chain Bonding (Hydrogen Bonding, Dipolar Interactions):**
- Aramid fibres (Kevlar) achieve high strength through extensive inter-chain hydrogen bonding.
- **Control:** Chemical structure selection — use of para-aramid rather than meta-aramid enables linear chain packing and H-bond network formation.

**5. Defects and Flaws:**
- Voids, inclusions, and surface defects act as stress concentrators and dramatically reduce strength.
- **Control:** Clean-room processing, filtration of spinning solutions, and minimal handling of fibres.

**6. Test Conditions:**
- Strain rate, temperature, and moisture content all affect the measured properties.
- **Control:** Standardize testing per ASTM D3822 (single-fibre tensile testing) at 23°C and 50% relative humidity.

For space applications, the key processing strategy is to **maximize orientation and crystallinity while minimizing defects**. This is why gel-spinning (for UHMWPE) and liquid-crystal spinning (for aramids) were developed — they produce fibres with molecular orientation approaching the theoretical limit.

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** A satellite antenna boom is to be manufactured from a pultruded unidirectional carbon-fibre/epoxy composite. The carbon fibre (PAN-based, high-modulus grade) has the following properties: σ_t = 4.5 GPa, E = 390 GPa, ρ = 1.85 g/cm³. The matrix epoxy has σ_t = 60 MPa, E = 3.5 GPa, ρ = 1.20 g/cm³. The fibre volume fraction is V_f = 0.60. Using the **rule of mixtures**, calculate the composite's longitudinal tensile strength, longitudinal Young's modulus, and density. Comment on the result.

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Fibre: σ_f = 4.5 GPa, E_f = 390 GPa, ρ_f = 1.85 g/cm³, V_f = 0.60
- Matrix: σ_m = 60 MPa = 0.060 GPa, E_m = 3.5 GPa, ρ_m = 1.20 g/cm³, V_m = 0.40

**(i) Longitudinal Tensile Strength (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m$$

$$\sigma_c = (4.5 \times 0.60) + (0.060 \times 0.40)$$

$$\sigma_c = 2.70 + 0.024 = 2.724 \, \text{GPa} = 2{,}724 \, \text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$E_c = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m$$

$$E_c = (390 \times 0.60) + (3.5 \times 0.40)$$

$$E_c = 234 + 1.4 = 235.4 \, \text{GPa}$$

**(iii) Composite Density (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m$$

$$\rho_c = (1.85 \times 0.60) + (1.20 \times 0.40)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.11 + 0.48 = 1.59 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Specific Strength and Specific Modulus of Composite:**

$$\sigma_{sp} = \frac{2{,}724}{1.59} \approx 1{,}713 \, \text{MPa/(g/cm}^3)$$

$$E_{sp} = \frac{235.4}{1.59} \approx 148.1 \, \text{GPa/(g/cm}^3)$$

**Comment:**
The composite achieves a specific modulus of 148 GPa/(g/cm³) — about **5.8× that of structural steel** (25.5 GPa/(g/cm³)). This makes it an excellent choice for a satellite antenna boom, where **high stiffness per unit mass** is required to maintain the antenna's pointing accuracy in orbit. The longitudinal tensile strength of 2.7 GPa is also more than sufficient to withstand deployment loads and the small vibrational loads encountered in low Earth orbit. The rule of mixtures result represents an **upper bound** (iso-strain condition); in practice, real values are slightly lower due to imperfect fibre-matrix bonding, fibre waviness, and void content.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Consistency Errors:** When using the rule of mixtures, ensure **all stresses are in the same unit (MPa or GPa)** before adding. A common mistake is mixing GPa for fibre and MPa for matrix without conversion.
> 2. **Volume Fraction Complement:** Students frequently forget that $V_m = 1 - V_f$. Always state this explicitly.
> 3. **Misapplication of Rule of Mixtures:** The rule of mixtures applies to **longitudinal** (unidirectional, iso-strain) properties. Do NOT apply it to transverse properties, where the **inverse rule of mixtures** ($1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$) is appropriate.
> 4. **Comparison Logic:** When asked to comment on suitability, students often forget to compute the *specific* properties and end up comparing absolute values — losing 2–3 marks.
> 5. **In Section 5.1 (Part A)**: Avoid one-line answers. KTU examiners expect 3–4 sentences with quantitative justification for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tensile Strength (σ_t):** Maximum stress before rupture; measured in **GPa** for high-performance fibres.
- **Young's Modulus (E):** Slope of the linear elastic region of the stress–strain curve; stiffness indicator in **GPa**.
- **Elongation at Break (ε_b):** Total strain at fracture; indicates ductility in **%**.
- **Toughness:** Area under the stress–strain curve; critical for impact and shielding applications.
- **Specific Strength (σ/ρ)** and **Specific Modulus (E/ρ):** The true performance metrics for space applications where mass is paramount.
- **Breaking Length:** A diameter-independent metric for comparing fibres; equal to $\sigma_t / (\rho g)$.
- **Stress–Strain Curve Regions:** Hookean Elastic → Yield → Strain Hardening → Failure.
- **Rule of Mixtures:** Applies to longitudinal composite properties: $P_c = P_f V_f + P_m V_m$ for modulus, strength, and density.
- **Space-Grade Fibre Hierarchy (by specific strength):** Zylon (PBO) > Spectra (UHMWPE) > Kevlar 49 (Aramid) > Carbon > Glass > Steel.
- **Space-Grade Fibre Hierarchy (by specific modulus):** HM Carbon > S-Glass > Kevlar 49 > Zylon > Spectra.
- **Toughness vs. Strength:** Toughness is energy absorption (area under curve); strength is peak stress — both are essential but serve different design purposes.
- **Processing–Property Linkage:** Higher draw ratio, higher crystallinity, lower defect density, and higher molecular weight all improve fibre mechanical performance.
- **Standard Test Methods:** ASTM D3822 (single-fibre tensile), ASTM D2256 (yarn tensile), ISO 11566 (carbon fibre).
- **Material Selection for Space:** Match the fibre's *dominant* property (specific strength, specific modulus, or toughness) to the *function* (load-bearing, stiffening, or impact absorption).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metal fibres** are defined as continuous or discontinuous filamentary metallic materials characterised by a high length-to-diameter ratio (aspect ratio typically greater than **100:1**), with diameters ranging from **1 µm to 500 µm** and possessing bulk metallic properties tailored through specific processing routes. In the context of space-grade polymer composites, metal fibres function as high-performance reinforcement phases that impart exceptional thermal, electrical, magnetic, and mechanical attributes to polymeric matrices, enabling the design of multifunctional structural components for spacecraft, satellites, re-entry vehicles, and space habitats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Metal fibres for space applications are NOT classified as ordinary reinforcement materials. They serve a dual role — *structural* (load-bearing) and *functional* (thermal/electrical management, EMI shielding, radiation protection). Students must internalise this multi-functional paradigm when answering Part B questions.

### 1.2 Classification of Metal Fibres

Metal fibres are broadly classified based on their composition, geometry, and functional behaviour:

| Class | Examples | Primary Function in Space |
|---|---|---|
| Ferrous fibres | Steel (AISI 302, 304, 316), Fe-Cr-Al alloys | Structural reinforcement, magnetic shielding |
| Refractory metal fibres | Tungsten (W), Molybdenum (Mo), Tantalum (Ta) | High-temperature resistance, radiation shielding |
| Light metal fibres | Aluminium (Al), Titanium (Ti), Beryllium (Be), Magnesium (Mg) | Weight-critical structures |
| Conductive non-ferrous fibres | Copper (Cu), Silver (Ag), Gold (Au) | Electrical/thermal conduction |
| Shape memory alloy fibres | Nitinol (Ni-Ti), Cu-Zn-Al | Adaptive deployable structures, damping |
| Metallic glass fibres | Amorphous Fe-Si-B, Co-based | High strength with elasticity |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine weaving a fabric of polymers (which are essentially long, tangled spaghetti chains) and inserting thin metallic threads throughout — like embedding copper wires into a cotton shirt. The cotton (polymer) provides flexibility and lightness, while the copper wires (metal fibres) provide strength, electrical conductivity, and thermal pathways.

Now scale this idea up to a spacecraft: the *polymer matrix* protects against micrometeoroids and provides lightweight sealing, while *embedded metal fibres* carry away heat from onboard electronics, shield sensitive circuits from cosmic radiation, and absorb structural loads during launch vibration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Think of metal fibres as the *skeletal + nervous system* of a polymer composite. The skeleton bears mechanical loads, while the nervous system conducts electricity and heat. In space, where every gram counts and temperatures swing from **−150 °C to +150 °C**, this dual functionality is invaluable.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and material metrics are essential for KTU calculations:

- **Density range of common metal fibres:** **ρ = 1.85 g/cm³ (Be)** to **19.3 g/cm³ (W)**
- **Ultimate tensile strength range:** **σₘₐₓ = 350 MPa (mild steel fibre)** to **4500 MPa (metallic glass fibre)**
- **Young's modulus range:** **E = 70 GPa (Al)** to **411 GPa (W)**
- **Standard aspect ratio (l/d) for reinforcement:** **≥ 100** (continuous); **10–100** (short fibres)
- **Electrical conductivity range:** **σₑ = 3.5 × 10⁷ S/m (Al)** to **6.3 × 10⁷ S/m (Ag)**
- **Thermal conductivity range:** **k = 21 W/m·K (Al)** to **401 W/m·K (Ag)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The engineering utility of metal fibres in space-grade polymers is governed by three interlocking theoretical frameworks:

#### 2.1.1 Micromechanical Load Transfer (Kelly–Tyson Theory)

When a load is applied to a metal-fibre-reinforced polymer composite, stress transfers from the weak polymer matrix to the strong metal fibre through interfacial shear stress (**τᵢ**). This mechanism is mathematically formalised by the **Kelly–Tyson critical fibre length** equation:

The fibre can only be loaded up to its ultimate tensile strength (**σf**) if its length exceeds a critical threshold. Below this length, the fibre is pulled out before reaching its breaking stress — wasting the fibre's intrinsic strength.

#### 2.1.2 Rule of Mixtures (Voigt Model)

For a unidirectional continuous metal-fibre composite aligned with the loading axis:

- The composite modulus is the volume-weighted average of the fibre and matrix moduli.
- The composite tensile strength depends on both fibre strength and the matrix stress at the fibre failure strain.

#### 2.1.3 Functional Conduction Models

For thermal and electrical conductivity, the **Springer–Tsai model** and **Lewis–Nielsen model** are commonly invoked. For fibres aligned in the conduction direction:

$$ k_c = k_f \cdot V_f + k_m \cdot V_m $$

where $k_c$, $k_f$, $k_m$ are thermal conductivities of composite, fibre, and matrix respectively.

### 2.2 Manufacturing Methods of Metal Fibres

| Method | Fibre Diameter Range | Typical Materials |
|---|---|---|
| Wire Drawing | 10 µm – 500 µm | Steel, Cu, Al, Ti, W, Mo |
| Taylor Wire Process | 1 µm – 25 µm | Stainless steel, W, Mo |
| In-Rotating-Water Spinning | 50 µm – 200 µm | Amorphous metallic alloys |
| Bundle Drawing | 5 µm – 100 µm | Steel, Cu, brass |
| Melt Extraction | 25 µm – 250 µm | Fe-, Co-, Ni-based alloys |
| Pendant Drop Melt Extraction | 10 µm – 200 µm | Pure metals & alloys |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Tables:** All mathematical relationships required for solving KTU 2024 Scheme problems on metal fibres are summarised below. Memorise these before attempting Part B numerical problems.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Validity Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = l/d$ | $l$ = fibre length (mm), $d$ = diameter (µm) | $AR \geq 10$ for reinforcement |
| 2 | Critical Fibre Length | $l_c = (\sigma_f \cdot d)/(2 \cdot \tau_i)$ | $\sigma_f$ = fibre strength (MPa), $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear stress (MPa) | $l \geq l_c$ for full efficiency |
| 3 | Fibre Efficiency | $\eta_l = 1 - (l_c/(2l))$ for $l < l_c$; $\eta_l = 1$ for $l \geq l_c$ | $\eta_l$ = length efficiency factor | $0 \leq \eta_l \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Volume Fraction | $V_f = (w_f/\rho_f) / (w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m)$ | $w$ = weight, $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) | $V_f + V_m = 1$ |
| 5 | Longitudinal Modulus (Rule of Mixtures) | $E_c = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (GPa) | Continuous, aligned fibres |
| 6 | Longitudinal Strength | $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + (\sigma_m)' \cdot V_m$ | $\sigma_{cu}$ = ultimate composite strength (MPa), $(\sigma_m)'$ = matrix stress at fibre failure strain | Fibre fails before matrix |
| 7 | Transverse Modulus (Inverse Rule) | $1/E_{c\perp} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | $E_{c\perp}$ = transverse modulus (GPa) | Loading perpendicular to fibres |
| 8 | Thermal Conductivity (Parallel) | $k_{c\parallel} = k_f \cdot V_f + k_m \cdot V_m$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K) | Aligned fibres parallel to heat flow |
| 9 | Thermal Conductivity (Series) | $1/k_{c\perp} = V_f/k_f + V_m/k_m$ | — | Perpendicular configuration |
| 10 | Specific Strength | $\sigma/\rho$ | $\sigma$ = strength (MPa), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) | Used for weight-critical space structures |
| 11 | Specific Modulus | $E/\rho$ | $E$ = modulus (GPa), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) | Used for stiffness-critical space structures |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Theory in Space Engineering

Theoretical models of metal-fibre composites are not academic exercises. They directly drive engineering decisions at ISRO, NASA, ESA, and private space entities (SpaceX, Blue Origin):

- **Satellite antenna reflectors:** Use aluminium or beryllium fibre composites for high stiffness-to-mass ratios.
- **Re-entry vehicle leading edges:** Reinforced with tungsten or molybdenum fibres for ablative thermal protection.
- **Deployable space structures (booms, trusses):** Use shape-memory alloy (Nitinol) fibres for self-actuating deployment.
- **Multi-layer insulation (MLI):** Embedded with extremely thin metallic mesh fibres for thermal radiation reflection.
- **Radiation shielding:** High-Z metal fibres (W, Ta) embedded in polymer matrices provide graded-Z shielding against galactic cosmic rays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Fibre Length (Kelly–Tyson Equation)

**Statement:** When a polymer matrix loaded in tension transfers stress to a metal fibre through interfacial shear, determine the minimum fibre length required for the fibre to attain its ultimate tensile stress.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of a Single Fibre:**

Consider a short cylindrical fibre of length $l$ and diameter $d$ embedded in a polymer matrix. Let $\tau_i$ be the constant interfacial shear stress acting on the fibre's lateral surface.

The shear force developed on each half of the fibre surface (length $l/2$) is:

$$ F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot (l/2) $$

**Step 2 — Equating Shear Force to Fibre Tensile Load:**

For force equilibrium at the fibre's mid-cross-section, the shear force must equal the tensile force in the fibre:

$$ \sigma_f \cdot (\pi d^2 / 4) = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot (l/2) $$

**Step 3 — Solving for the Critical Length $l_c$:**

Cancel $\pi$ from both sides and rearrange:

$$ \sigma_f \cdot d / 4 = \tau_i \cdot l_c / 2 $$

$$ \sigma_f \cdot d = 2 \cdot \tau_i \cdot l_c $$

$$ \boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i}} $$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**

- A *thicker* fibre (larger $d$) requires a *longer* critical length for full efficiency.
- A *stronger* fibre-matrix interface* (larger $\tau_i$) reduces $l_c$.
- A fibre with $l \geq l_c$ is termed a **continuous fibre**; $l < l_c$ is termed a **discontinuous fibre**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fibre Length Efficiency Factor

**Step 1 — Stress Distribution in a Sub-Critical Fibre:**

For a fibre of length $l < l_c$, the tensile stress builds up linearly from zero at the fibre ends to a maximum value $\sigma_{f,\max}$ at the centre:

$$ \sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{2 \cdot \tau_i \cdot l}{d} $$

**Step 2 — Average Fibre Stress:**

The average fibre stress is half the maximum (triangular distribution):

$$ \bar{\sigma_f} = \frac{\sigma_{f,\max}}{2} = \frac{\tau_i \cdot l}{d} $$

**Step 3 — Length Efficiency Factor Definition:**

The length efficiency factor is the ratio of the average fibre stress to the ultimate fibre stress:

$$ \eta_l = \frac{\bar{\sigma_f}}{\sigma_f} = \frac{\tau_i \cdot l}{d \cdot \sigma_f} = \frac{l}{l_c} $$

However, since maximum efficiency (1.0) is reached only when $l \geq l_c$, the corrected expression is:

$$ \eta_l = \begin{cases} l/l_c & \text{if } l < l_c \\ 1 & \text{if } l \geq l_c \end{cases} $$

**Step 4 — Application in Composite Strength:**

The effective longitudinal tensile strength becomes:

$$ \sigma_{cu} = \eta_l \cdot \sigma_f \cdot V_f + (\sigma_m)' \cdot V_m $$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rule of Mixtures — Longitudinal Composite Modulus

**Step 1 — Iso-Strain Assumption:**

For a unidirectional metal-fibre composite loaded parallel to the fibre axis, both fibre and matrix experience the same strain (iso-strain condition):

$$ \varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon $$

**Step 2 — Load Sharing:**

The total load on the composite cross-section is the sum of loads borne by fibre and matrix:

$$ P_c = P_f + P_m $$

$$ \sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m $$

**Step 3 — Dividing by Total Area:**

$$ \sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot (A_f/A_c) + \sigma_m \cdot (A_m/A_c) $$

Recognising $A_f/A_c = V_f$ and $A_m/A_c = V_m$:

$$ \sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m $$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law:**

Since $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ for linear elastic behaviour:

$$ E_c \cdot \varepsilon = E_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_m $$

Cancelling $\varepsilon$ from all terms:

$$ \boxed{E_c = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m} $$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Strength Calculation for Space Structure

**Problem Statement:** A spacecraft bracket is to be made from an aluminium-fibre-reinforced polyimide composite. Calculate the specific strength if the fibre volume fraction is 0.35.

**Given Data:**
- Aluminium fibre strength: $\sigma_f = 480$ MPa
- Aluminium fibre density: $\rho_f = 2.70$ g/cm³
- Polyimide matrix strength at fibre failure strain: $\sigma_m' = 90$ MPa
- Polyimide matrix density: $\rho_m = 1.42$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Composite Strength (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$ \sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m' \cdot V_m $$

$$ \sigma_c = (480 \cdot 0.35) + (90 \cdot 0.65) $$

$$ \sigma_c = 168 + 58.5 $$

$$ \sigma_c = 226.5 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 2 — Composite Density (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$ \rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m $$

$$ \rho_c = (2.70 \cdot 0.35) + (1.42 \cdot 0.65) $$

$$ \rho_c = 0.945 + 0.923 $$

$$ \rho_c = 1.868 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Specific Strength:**

$$ (\sigma/\rho)_c = 226.5 \text{ MPa} / 1.868 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

$$ \boxed{(\sigma/\rho)_c \approx 121.3 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}} $$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Monolithic Polyimide:**

$$ (\sigma/\rho)_{polyimide} = 90 / 1.42 \approx 63.4 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g} $$

The composite provides **91% improvement** in specific strength, validating its use in space structures.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Critical Fibre Length

**Problem:** A tungsten fibre of diameter 100 µm is embedded in an epoxy matrix. The fibre's ultimate tensile strength is 3000 MPa and the interfacial shear strength is 25 MPa. Calculate the critical fibre length and classify the fibre as continuous or discontinuous if its actual length is 4 mm.

**Solution:**

$$ l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i} $$

$$ l_c = \frac{3000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \cdot 25} $$

$$ l_c = \frac{0.300}{50} $$

$$ l_c = 0.006 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ mm} $$

Since actual fibre length $l = 4 \text{ mm} < l_c = 6 \text{ mm}$, this fibre is classified as **discontinuous** (sub-critical).

$$ \eta_l = l / l_c = 4 / 6 = 0.667 $$

The fibre only achieves **66.7%** of its potential reinforcement efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Metal Fibres


### 4.2 Load Transfer Mechanism in Metal Fibre Composites

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["Applied Tensile Load on Composite"] --> stepB["Stress Transfer via Matrix"]
    stepB --> stepC["Interfacial Shear at Fibre Matrix Boundary"]
    stepC --> stepD["Fibre Stress Builds from Ends to Centre"]
    stepD --> stepE{"Is l greater than or equal to l_c?"}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF["Fibre Attains Full sigma_f at Centre"]
    stepE -- No --> stepG["Fibre Pulls Out Before Failure"]
    stepF --> stepH["Composite Achieves Maximum Strength"]
    stepG --> stepI["Sub Optimal Reinforcement Eta_l less than 1"]

    style stepB fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff

    style stepF fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style stepG fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style stepH fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style stepI fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```
