## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define metal fibres and list any four types commonly used in space applications.

**Model Answer:**
Metal fibres are filamentary metallic materials with a high length-to-diameter aspect ratio (typically $> 100:1$) and diameters ranging from $1$ µm to $500$ µm. They serve as reinforcement or functional fillers in polymer matrices for space applications.

Four types commonly used in space applications:
1. **Aluminium fibres** — for lightweight structural components and antenna reflectors.
2. **Tungsten fibres** — for radiation shielding and high-temperature thermal protection.
3. **Nitinol (Ni-Ti) shape memory alloy fibres** — for self-deployable booms and trusses.
4. **Copper fibres** — for thermal management and EMI shielding enclosures.

*(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for four types = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between continuous and discontinuous metal fibres. What is the role of the critical fibre length?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Continuous Fibre | Discontinuous Fibre |
|---|---|---|
| Length | $l \geq l_c$ | $l < l_c$ |
| Stress in fibre | Reaches $\sigma_f$ at mid-length | Below $\sigma_f$ |
| Failure mode | Fibre fracture | Fibre pull-out |
| Efficiency $\eta_l$ | 1.0 | $< 1.0$ |

The **critical fibre length** $l_c = (\sigma_f \cdot d)/(2 \cdot \tau_i)$ is the minimum fibre length required for the fibre to achieve its ultimate tensile stress. Below this length, the fibre-matrix interface fails before the fibre breaks, leading to inefficient load transfer.

*(Marks: 1.5 for table + 1.5 for critical length explanation = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** Derive the Kelly–Tyson equation for the critical fibre length in a metal-fibre-reinforced polymer composite. Explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional continuous steel-fibre-reinforced epoxy composite has the following properties:

- Steel fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.40$
- Epoxy volume fraction $V_m = 0.60$
- Young's modulus of steel $E_f = 210$ GPa
- Young's modulus of epoxy $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- Ultimate tensile strength of steel $\sigma_f = 2500$ MPa
- Matrix stress at fibre failure strain $\sigma_m' = 35$ MPa

Calculate:
(i) Longitudinal Young's modulus of the composite.
(ii) Ultimate tensile strength of the composite in the longitudinal direction.
(iii) Specific modulus and specific strength of the composite, given densities: $\rho_f = 7.85$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Free body diagram setup:** Consider a cylindrical fibre of length $l$ and diameter $d$ in a matrix. Interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ acts on the fibre's curved surface.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium equation:**

$$ F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot (l/2) \quad \text{(per half of fibre)} $$

Setting equal to tensile load in fibre cross-section:

$$ \sigma_f \cdot (\pi d^2 / 4) = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot (l_c / 2) $$

**Step 3 — Solving for $l_c$:**

$$ \boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i}} $$

**Physical Significance:** This equation reveals that the minimum effective fibre length scales linearly with fibre strength and diameter, and inversely with interfacial shear strength. It dictates whether a fibre is treated as continuous or discontinuous in composite design. For space applications, designers typically select fibre lengths at least **10–20 times** $l_c$ to ensure a safety margin against statistical strength variability. *(7 marks — 3 for derivation, 2 for physical significance, 2 for space relevance)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$ E_{c,\parallel} = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m $$

$$ E_{c,\parallel} = (210 \cdot 0.40) + (3.5 \cdot 0.60) $$

$$ E_{c,\parallel} = 84 + 2.1 $$

$$ \boxed{E_{c,\parallel} = 86.1 \text{ GPa}} $$

**(ii) Ultimate Tensile Strength:**

$$ \sigma_{cu} = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m' \cdot V_m $$

$$ \sigma_{cu} = (2500 \cdot 0.40) + (35 \cdot 0.60) $$

$$ \sigma_{cu} = 1000 + 21 $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{cu} = 1021 \text{ MPa}} $$

**(iii) Specific Properties:**

Composite density:

$$ \rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m = (7.85 \cdot 0.40) + (1.20 \cdot 0.60) $$

$$ \rho_c = 3.14 + 0.72 = 3.86 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

Specific modulus:

$$ E/\rho = 86.1 / 3.86 = 22.3 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g} $$

Specific strength:

$$ \sigma/\rho = 1021 / 3.86 = 264.5 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g} $$

These results justify the use of steel-fibre composites for high-load space structural members. *(7 marks — 2 for modulus, 2 for strength, 3 for specific properties with density calculation)*

---

**QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Discuss the various types of metal fibres used in space applications. Compare ferrous, refractory, and shape memory alloy fibres in terms of composition, key properties, and typical space use cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spacecraft thermal management panel uses an aluminium-fibre-reinforced polyimide composite. The fibres are aligned in the direction of heat flow. Given:

- $V_f = 0.30$, $V_m = 0.70$
- Thermal conductivity of Al: $k_f = 205$ W/m·K
- Thermal conductivity of polyimide: $k_m = 0.20$ W/m·K
- Thermal conductivity of pure polyimide panel: $0.20$ W/m·K

Calculate the thermal conductivity enhancement factor compared to pure polyimide. Comment on the suitability of this composite for thermal management in LEO spacecraft. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Ferrous fibres (e.g., AISI 316L stainless steel):**
- Composition: Fe-18Cr-10Ni-2Mo
- Properties: $\sigma_f = 2000$ MPa, $\rho = 7.95$ g/cm³, magnetic
- Space use: Structural brackets, magnetic shielding for sensitive payloads

**Refractory metal fibres (W, Mo, Ta):**
- Composition: Pure tungsten, molybdenum, or tantalum
- Properties: $\sigma_f = 3000$–4500 MPa, very high melting points ($T_m > 2600$ °C), high density ($\rho_W = 19.3$ g/cm³)
- Space use: Radiation shielding, re-entry thermal protection systems, X-ray shielding

**Shape memory alloy fibres (Nitinol):**
- Composition: Ni-55% Ti (approximately equiatomic)
- Properties: Shape memory effect, superelasticity, recoverable strain up to 8%
- Space use: Self-actuating deployable booms, hinges for solar arrays, vibration dampers

The comparison highlights that no single metal fibre is optimal for all space applications; selection is driven by the dominant functional requirement (mechanical, thermal, or actuation). *(7 marks — 2 for ferrous, 2 for refractory, 2 for SMA, 1 for concluding comparison)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Composite Thermal Conductivity (Parallel Model):**

$$ k_{c,\parallel} = k_f \cdot V_f + k_m \cdot V_m $$

$$ k_{c,\parallel} = (205 \cdot 0.30) + (0.20 \cdot 0.70) $$

$$ k_{c,\parallel} = 61.5 + 0.14 $$

$$ \boxed{k_{c,\parallel} = 61.64 \text{ W/m·K}} $$

**Step 2 — Enhancement Factor:**

$$ \text{Enhancement Factor} = k_{c,\parallel} / k_{polyimide} = 61.64 / 0.20 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Enhancement Factor} = 308.2} $$

The composite's thermal conductivity is approximately **308 times** that of pure polyimide.

**Step 3 — Suitability for LEO Spacecraft:**

In Low Earth Orbit (LEO), spacecraft experience temperature cycling between approximately $-150$ °C and $+150$ °C. The high thermal conductivity ensures rapid dissipation of heat generated by onboard electronics. Additionally, the polyimide matrix retains its mechanical integrity at extreme temperatures, while aluminium fibres (low density, $2.70$ g/cm³) keep the panel lightweight. Therefore, the composite is **highly suitable** for LEO thermal management panels. *(7 marks — 3 for thermal conductivity, 2 for enhancement factor, 2 for LEO commentary)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Metal Fibre Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Rule of Mixtures for Modulus vs Strength:** Students often interchange the formulas. Remember: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ uses **moduli**; $\sigma_{cu}$ uses **strengths** plus a matrix stress term.
> 2. **Ignoring the Direction of Loading:** Longitudinal ($E_{c,\parallel}$) and transverse ($E_{c,\perp}$) moduli use *different* formulas — using the wrong one costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** Always convert µm to m and MPa to GPa consistently. Examiner specifically deducts **0.5–1 mark** per numerical answer for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Treating Short Fibres as Continuous:** If $l < l_c$, the fibre is discontinuous and the length efficiency factor $\eta_l$ must be included. This is a frequent Part B error worth **3 marks**.
> 5. **Missing the Functional Aspect:** When asked about space applications, students describe only mechanical reinforcement. Examiners expect mention of **thermal management, EMI shielding, radiation shielding, and deployable structures** for full credit.
> 6. **Formula without Derivation:** In 14-mark questions, simply stating the Kelly–Tyson equation without the free-body-diagram derivation forfeits **3–4 marks** out of 7.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Metal Fibres**
>
> - Metal fibres have aspect ratio **$\geq 100$** and diameter **1–500 µm**.
> - Critical fibre length: $l_c = (\sigma_f \cdot d)/(2 \cdot \tau_i)$; length efficiency $\eta_l = l/l_c$ for $l < l_c$, else $1$.
> - Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal modulus): $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
> - Longitudinal strength: $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m' V_m$.
> - Specific strength and specific modulus are the **key metrics** for space weight-critical design.
> - Tungsten fibres → radiation shielding; Aluminium fibres → lightweight structures; Copper fibres → thermal/electrical conduction; Nitinol fibres → deployable structures.
> - Manufacturing methods include wire drawing, Taylor wire process, melt extraction, and bundle drawing.
> - Always specify fibre **orientation** (aligned, random, or fabric) before applying any rule of mixtures.
> - Thermal conductivity in aligned-fibre direction follows the parallel model: $k_c = k_f V_f + k_m V_m$.
> - For Part B problems, *derive first, then substitute numerical values* — derivations carry separate marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alumina Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alumina Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Alumina Fibres (Al₂O₃ Fibres):** Inorganic, polycrystalline ceramic fibres composed primarily of aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃), often combined with stabilising oxides such as SiO₂, B₂O₃, ZrO₂, or Fe₂O₃. They are classified as high-performance refractory oxide fibres and are characterised by a melting point exceeding **2000 °C**, excellent thermal shock resistance, high specific stiffness, and chemical inertness in oxidising atmospheres. They form a critical reinforcement family in fibre-reinforced ceramic matrix composites (CMCs) and polymer matrix composites (PMCs) intended for extreme aerospace environments.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEPOT746, alumina fibres are grouped with other oxide fibres (silica, zirconia) as **refractory oxide reinforcements**—distinguished from non-oxide ceramic fibres (SiC, BN) by their inherent **oxidation resistance** and **dielectric transparency** to electromagnetic radiation.

### 1.2 Polymorphic Variants of Alumina

Alumina exhibits several crystallographic forms, but only two are commercially exploited as fibres:

| Polymorph | Crystal System | Density (g/cm³) | Stability Range | Relevance to Fibres |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **γ-Alumina (Gamma)** | Cubic (spinel defect) | **3.20** | Transition phase (< 1200 °C) | High surface area, intermediate stage in sol–gel route |
| **α-Alumina (Alpha, Corundum)** | Rhombohedral (HCP) | **3.98** | Thermodynamically stable up to melting point | Final, high-strength, high-temperature form |
| **θ-Alumina** | Monoclinic | **3.56** | Transition phase | Intermediate conversion stage |
| **δ-Alumina** | Orthorhombic | — | Transition phase | Rarely isolated in fibres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **α-alumina (corundum)** phase is the thermodynamically equilibrium phase and is the target for high-temperature structural fibres, whereas γ-alumina appears as a metastable, porous transitional phase during processing. Conversion γ → α occurs around **1000–1200 °C** and is accompanied by ~15 % volume shrinkage, which must be managed in sol–gel processing.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Steel Wool of Space":** Imagine the steel-wool scrubber used in your kitchen. It is a tangle of fine metallic strands that is light, fibrous, yet rigid. Now replace that metal with **synthetic ruby** (essentially what α-alumina is — the gemstone form of aluminium oxide used in watch bearings and scratch-resistant camera lenses). You now have a fibre that will not melt in a rocket nozzle, will not oxidise in re-entry plasma, and will not conduct electricity. **Alumina fibres are essentially synthetic "ruby threads" that engineers weave into plastics and metals to give them superhuman thermal tolerance.** The polymer binds them; the fibre does the structural heavy-lifting.

### 1.4 Classification of Alumina Fibres

Alumina fibres are classified by **morphology** and **composition**:

**A. By Morphology**
- **Continuous filaments** (e.g., Nextel 610, Nextel 720, Altex) — diameter 7–20 µm, supplied as yarns or rovings.
- **Discontinuous (chopped) fibres** — length 1–25 mm, used in short-fibre composite preforms.
- **Whiskers** — sub-micron diameter, near-single-crystal, exceptional strength.
- **Fibrous mats / blankets** — needled non-wovens for thermal insulation.

**B. By Al₂O₃ Content**
- **High-purity α-Al₂O₃ (> 95 wt.%)** — e.g., Nextel 610 (99 %), Almax, single-crystal sapphire fibre.
- **Alumina–silica (Al₂O₃ + SiO₂, typically 60–85 % Al₂O₃)** — e.g., Nextel 312 (62/24/14), Nextel 440, Nextel 720.
- **Alumina–borosilicate** — e.g., experimental fibres with B₂O₃ sintering aid.
- **Alumina–zirconia** — e.g., PRD-166 (Al₂O₃ + ZrO₂) for transformation toughening.

### 1.5 Significance in Space Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Space applications demand materials that survive temperatures from −157 °C (cryogenic fuel) to > 1600 °C (re-entry), resist atomic oxygen erosion in LEO, withstand UV/EUV radiation, and remain dimensionally stable. Alumina fibres uniquely combine **all four** in a single reinforcement, making them indispensable for **thermal protection systems (TPS)**, **rocket nozzle throat inserts**, **radomes**, and **ablative/insulation layers** in launch vehicles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Alumina Fibres? The Property Rationale

The motivation for using alumina fibres in space composites stems from the simultaneous satisfaction of six engineering criteria:

1. **High specific strength** — tensile strength normalised by density.
2. **Refractoriness** — retains mechanical integrity above 1000 °C.
3. **Oxidation resistance** — unlike carbon or SiC, alumina does NOT form volatile oxides in air up to ~1800 °C.
4. **Dielectric transparency** — alumina is an electrical insulator (band gap ~8.7 eV) — perfect for radomes that must transmit radar/RF signals.
5. **Thermal expansion tuning** — CTE can be matched to metallic matrices (e.g., titanium, aluminium).
6. **Radiation tolerance** — minimal degradation under proton/electron fluences encountered in orbit.

### 2.2 Manufacturing Routes — Process Flow Analysis

Alumina fibres cannot be drawn from the melt like glass fibres (alumina melts only at 2072 °C and is highly viscous). Therefore, alternative liquid-phase or vapour-phase routes are employed:

**A. Melt Spinning (Tyco / Safimax / Altex route)**
- Pure α-alumina is melted in an induction furnace at **~2100 °C**.
- The melt is extruded through a multi-hole spinneret into a stream of cold air.
- Solidification yields a green fibre which is then **annealed** to relieve stresses.
- Limitation: limited to fibres with diameter > 50 µm due to melt viscosity.

**B. Slag/Mineral Fibre Process**
- Bauxite or alumina-rich slag is melted and fibreised by steam jets or rotating discs.
- Produces discontinuous, low-cost fibres (e.g., refractory ceramic fibres, RCF).
- High impurity content; used for insulation, not structural composites.

**C. Sol–Gel Process (3M Nextel route)** — *The dominant high-purity route*
1. **Precursor solution**: aluminium salts (e.g., aluminium chlorohydrate, Al₂(OH)₅Cl·2H₂O) are dissolved in water.
2. **Spinning**: viscosity is adjusted (with silica sol, boron oxide precursor) and the solution is extruded through spinnerets.
3. **Gelation**: the spun filament is exposed to ammonia vapour, precipitating Al(OH)₃ gel.
4. **Drying**: controlled humidity to prevent cracking.
5. **Pyrolysis & sintering**: heating in air up to **1000–1500 °C** converts the gel to crystalline α-alumina; the γ → α transition is completed.
- This is the only route yielding **fine-diameter (7–12 µm), continuous, high-purity** filaments.

**D. Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) — Sapphire Fibre Route**
- Volatile aluminium precursor (e.g., AlCl₃) reacts with H₂O/O₂ on a heated substrate.
- Yields single-crystal α-Al₂O₃ (sapphire) filaments of exceptional strength (> 3 GPa).
- Very expensive; used in niche research and high-temperature sensors.

**E. Hydrothermal / Seeding-Assisted Growth**
- Nano-seeds (α-Al₂O₃ nanoparticles) are added to the precursor to lower the γ → α transformation temperature and refine grain size.

### 2.3 Mechanical Property Hierarchy

The **rule-of-mixtures** model predicts composite properties. For a unidirectional alumina-fibre/polymer-matrix composite loaded along the fibre axis:

$$E_c = \eta_l \cdot V_f \cdot E_f + (1 - V_f) \cdot E_m$$

$$\sigma_c = \eta_l \cdot V_f \cdot \sigma_f + (1 - V_f) \cdot \sigma_m'$$

where **ηₗ** is the fibre length-efficiency factor (≈ 1 for continuous filaments), **V_f** is the fibre volume fraction, **E_f**, **E_m** are fibre and matrix Young's moduli, **σ_f**, **σ_m'** are fibre tensile strength and matrix stress at composite failure strain.

**Critical fibre length** (for short-fibre load transfer via interfacial shear):

$$\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_f^{u}}{2 \tau_i}$$

where **τᵢ** is the interfacial shear strength.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently tests the **rule of mixtures** and the **critical aspect ratio** of fibres. Memorise the formulae in **bold** below.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units / Typical Values |
|---|----------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **$E_c = \eta_l \, V_f \, E_f + (1 - V_f) \, E_m$** | Longitudinal composite modulus (rule of mixtures) | **E_f(α-Al₂O₃) ≈ 380 GPa**, **E_m(epoxy) ≈ 3 GPa** |
| 2 | **$\sigma_c = \eta_l \, V_f \, \sigma_f + (1 - V_f)\,\sigma_m'$** | Longitudinal composite tensile strength | σ_f(Nextel 610) ≈ 3.1 GPa |
| 3 | **$\dfrac{L_c}{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_f^{u}}{2\,\tau_i}$** | Critical fibre aspect ratio (load transfer) | d = 7–20 µm; τ_i ≈ 10–30 MPa |
| 4 | **$\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{\tanh(\beta L/2)}{\beta L/2}$, $\beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,G_m}{E_f\,r^2\,\ln(R/r)}}$** | Shear-lag length efficiency factor | ηₗ → 1 for continuous fibre |
| 5 | $V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_m^{u}}{\sigma_f - \sigma_m' + \sigma_m^{u}}$ | Minimum fibre volume for strengthening | Typical ≈ 0.05–0.10 |
| 6 | $\rho_c = V_f\,\rho_f + (1 - V_f)\,\rho_m$ | Composite density | ρ_f(Al₂O₃) = **3.98 g/cm³** |
| 7 | $\alpha_c = \dfrac{V_f \, E_f \, \alpha_f + (1 - V_f)\,E_m\,\alpha_m}{V_f\,E_f + (1 - V_f)\,E_m$ | CTE of unidirectional composite | α_f(α-Al₂O₃) ≈ 8 × 10⁻⁶ /K |
| 8 | $k_c = V_f \, k_f + (1 - V_f)\,k_m$ | Thermal conductivity (parallel rule, upper bound) | k_f(Al₂O₃) ≈ 30 W/m·K |
| 9 | $\sigma^{th} = E_f \,\Delta\alpha \,\Delta T$ | Thermal mismatch stress | Critical for thermal cycling |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_f^u = \dfrac{\sigma_f^u}{E_f}$ | Ultimate fibre strain | ≈ 0.8 % for Nextel 610 |

### 2.5 Property Matrix of Commercial Alumina Fibres

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 2 stresses the **comparison of alumina fibre families**. The values below are industry-standard, frequently quoted in viva and short-answer questions.

| Fibre | Composition (wt.%) | Diameter (µm) | Tensile Strength (GPa) | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Max Use Temp (°C) |
|-------|--------------------|---------------|------------------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **Nextel 610** | 99 % Al₂O₃ (α) | 10–12 | **3.1** | **380** | 1000 |
| **Nextel 720** | 85 % Al₂O₃ / 15 % SiO₂ | 10–12 | 2.1 | 260 | 1200 |
| **Nextel 440** | 70 % Al₂O₃ / 28 % SiO₂ / 2 % B₂O₃ | 10–12 | 2.0 | 190 | 1300 |
| **Nextel 312** | 62 % Al₂O₃ / 24 % SiO₂ / 14 % B₂O₃ | 8–12 | 1.7 | 150 | 1200 |
| **Altex** | 85 % Al₂O₃ / 15 % SiO₂ | 15 | 1.8 | 210 | 1000 |
| **Safimax** | 95 % Al₂O₃ | 50–500 | 2.0 | 300 | 1400 |
| **PRD-166** | 80 % Al₂O₃ / 20 % ZrO₂ | — | 2.1 | 280 | 1400 |
| **Almax** | 99.5 % Al₂O₃ (α) | 10 | 1.8 | 340 | 1000 |
| **Sapphire (single crystal)** | 100 % Al₂O₃ | 50–500 | **3.5** | **460** | 2000 |

### 2.6 Why Alumina (Not SiC or Carbon) for Space?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering rationale** students must articulate in viva:
> 1. **Oxidation:** Carbon fibres burn away in air above 400 °C; SiC forms a passivating SiO₂ layer but is consumed over time. **α-Al₂O₃ is already an oxide — it cannot oxidise further.**
> 2. **Dielectric loss tangent:** Carbon and SiC are conductive/semi-conductive → reflect radar. Alumina has tan δ < 0.001 → RF-transparent radomes.
> 3. **Compatibility with polymer matrices:** Alumina is chemically compatible with phenolic, epoxy, and polyimide matrices used in launch vehicles.
> 4. **Cost vs. performance:** Cheaper than SiC fibre; more thermally capable than glass fibre.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Fibre Length for Load Transfer

Consider a short fibre of diameter **d**, length **L**, embedded in a polymer matrix and loaded in tension along the fibre axis. Shear stress **τᵢ** acts on the cylindrical fibre surface (lateral area = π · d · L/2 on each side). Equilibrium requires the shear force transferred to equal the fibre tensile load at the mid-section.

**Step 1 — Tensile load carried at fibre mid-plane:**

$$
P_{tensile} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

This represents the maximum tensile force in the fibre (at its centre, x = L/2).

**Step 2 — Shear force transferred from matrix to fibre over half-length:**

$$
P_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L}{2}
$$

This is the total shear force acting over the half-length of the fibre surface, where the matrix pushes the fibre by interfacial shear stress τᵢ.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium (force balance):**

$$
\tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L_c}{2} = \sigma_f^u \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

Setting the shear-transfer force equal to the tensile load at fibre fracture gives the condition for the fibre to be just long enough to reach its ultimate strength σ_f^u before pull-out.

**Step 4 — Solve for L_c:**

$$
L_c = \frac{\sigma_f^u \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_f^{u}}{2\,\tau_i}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Given: Nextel 610 fibre with **σ_f^u = 3.1 GPa**, diameter **d = 12 µm**, interfacial shear strength with epoxy **τᵢ = 20 MPa**.

$$
\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{3.1 \times 10^9}{2 \times 20 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.1 \times 10^9}{4.0 \times 10^7} = 77.5
$$

$$
L_c = 77.5 \times 12 \, \mu m = 930 \, \mu m \approx 1 \, mm
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** Fibres shorter than ~1 mm will pull out rather than fracture — they contribute poorly to load transfer. Hence, for effective reinforcement in short-fibre composites, fibre lengths must exceed L_c.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Fibre Volume Fraction for Strengthening

For the composite to be stronger than the neat polymer matrix, the rule-of-mixtures strength must exceed the matrix strength:

$$
\sigma_c > \sigma_m^u
$$

Substituting:

$$
\eta_l \, V_f \, \sigma_f + (1 - V_f) \, \sigma_m' > \sigma_m^u
$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange:**

$$
\eta_l \, V_f \, \sigma_f + \sigma_m' - V_f \, \sigma_m' > \sigma_m^u
$$

**Step 2 — Collect V_f terms:**

$$
V_f \, (\eta_l \, \sigma_f - \sigma_m') > \sigma_m^u - \sigma_m'
$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$
V_f > \frac{\sigma_m^u - \sigma_m'}{\eta_l \, \sigma_f - \sigma_m'}
$$

Approximating σ_m' ≈ σ_m^u (matrix fails at its ultimate strength) gives:

$$
\boxed{\;V_{f,crit} = \frac{\sigma_m^u}{\sigma_f - \sigma_m' + \sigma_m^u}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**

Epoxy matrix σ_m^u = 70 MPa, σ_m' ≈ 50 MPa (stress in matrix at fibre failure strain), Nextel 610 σ_f = 3100 MPa:

$$
V_{f,crit} = \frac{70}{3100 - 50 + 70} = \frac{70}{3120} = 0.0224 \approx 2.24\,\%
$$

Even a modest fibre volume fraction delivers significant strengthening — explaining why alumina-fibre composites are attractive for weight-critical space structures.

### 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Mismatch Stress in Alumina/Polymer Composite

When cooling from cure temperature T_cure to operating temperature T_op, differential contraction generates internal stresses:

**Step 1 — Free thermal strain mismatch:**

$$
\Delta\varepsilon = (\alpha_f - \alpha_m)(T_{cure} - T_{op})
$$

**Step 2 — If matrix and fibre are perfectly bonded, the composite strains uniformly:**

The internal stress generated in the fibre (constrained by the matrix):

$$
\sigma_f^{th} = E_f \, \Delta\varepsilon = E_f (\alpha_f - \alpha_m)(T_{cure} - T_{op})
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical estimation for alumina/epoxy:**

- α_f(α-Al₂O₃) = **8 × 10⁻⁶ /K**
- α_m(epoxy) = **60 × 10⁻⁶ /K**
- E_f = 380 GPa
- ΔT = 125 K (cooling from 150 °C cure to 25 °C)

$$
\Delta\varepsilon = (8 - 60) \times 10^{-6} \times 125 = -6.5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\sigma_f^{th} = 380 \times 10^9 \times (-6.5 \times 10^{-3}) = -2.47 \, GPa
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check:** This exceeds the fibre strength! In reality, the matrix yields, microcracks form, and the actual stress is much lower. This calculation nevertheless highlights why **CTE matching** (e.g., Al₂O₃ + low-CTE polyimide) is critical in space composites.

### 3.4 Derivation: Weight Savings vs. Metallic TPS

For a thermal protection panel of area A, replacing aluminium (ρ = 2.7 g/cm³, k = 200 W/m·K) with an alumina-fibre/phenolic composite (ρ_c ≈ 1.8 g/cm³, k_c ≈ 1.5 W/m·K):

**Mass ratio for equal flexural rigidity D:**

$$
\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{comp}} = \frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_c} \cdot \left(\frac{E_{comp}}{E_{Al}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

With E_Al ≈ 70 GPa, E_comp (Nextel 610/phenolic, V_f = 0.4) ≈ **30 GPa**:

$$
\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{comp}} = \frac{2.7}{1.8} \cdot \left(\frac{30}{70}\right)^{1/3} = 1.5 \cdot (0.428)^{1/3} = 1.5 \cdot 0.754 = 1.13
$$

Composite is ~13 % lighter at equal rigidity, with **130× lower thermal conductivity** → far superior insulator. This is the core reason alumina-fibre composites dominate re-entry TPS.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Sol–Gel Route for Alumina Fibres (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    B --> C["Addition of Silica Sol and Boric Acid for Sintering Aid"]
    C --> D["Spinning: Viscous Solution Extruded Through Multi-hole Spinneret"]
    D --> E["Gelation: Passage Through Ammonia Vapour Chamber"]
    E --> F["Drying Tunnel: Controlled RH 60 Percent to Prevent Cracking"]
    F --> G["Pyrolysis Furnace: 800 C in Air Removes Residual Organics"]
    G --> H["Crystallisation Sintering: 1000 to 1500 C converts gamma to alpha Alumina"]
    H --> I["Continuous Filament Winding onto Bobbin"]
    I --> J["Quality Tests: Diameter, Tensile, Density"]
    J --> K["Nextel 610 or 720 Alumina Fibre Yarn"]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between γ-alumina and α-alumina fibre phases. Why is α-phase preferred for high-temperature structural applications?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | γ-Alumina | α-Alumina (Corundum) |
|---------|-----------|----------------------|
| Crystal structure | Cubic spinel-type defect | Rhombohedral HCP, dense |
| Density | 3.20 g/cm³ | **3.98 g/cm³** |
| Stability | Metastable, transition phase | Thermodynamically stable |
| Surface area | High (porous) | Low (dense) |
| Temperature limit | ~ 800 °C | > 1600 °C |
| Mechanical strength | Low (porous) | High (dense grains) |
| Occurrence | Intermediate during sol–gel processing | Final product |

α-phase is preferred because of its **higher density**, **thermodynamic stability above 1200 °C**, and **superior mechanical strength** resulting from a close-packed oxygen sublattice that resists creep and grain growth.

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** State three reasons why alumina fibres are preferred over carbon and silicon carbide fibres for thermal protection systems in space vehicles.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Oxidation resistance** — Carbon oxidises above 400 °C; SiC slowly degrades via volatile SiO/Si(OH)ₓ formation. α-Al₂O₃ is already an oxide; it cannot oxidise further and is stable to ~1800 °C in air.
2. **Dielectric transparency** — Alumina has band gap 8.7 eV and loss tangent < 0.001, making it RF-transparent for radomes. Carbon and SiC reflect/absorb radar signals.
3. **Compatibility with polymer matrices** — Alumina is chemically inert with phenolic, epoxy, and polyimide binders used in launch vehicle ablatives, ensuring long shelf life and no galvanic corrosion with aluminium substructures.

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module Choice Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. The examiner must offer a choice between Question A and Question B for every module.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **sol–gel route** for manufacturing continuous alumina fibres. Compare its advantages and limitations with the melt-spinning route. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional composite is made of **Nextel 610 alumina fibre** (σ_f = 3.1 GPa, E_f = 380 GPa, d = 12 µm) embedded in an **epoxy matrix** (σ_m = 70 MPa, E_m = 3.5 GPa, interfacial shear strength τ_i = 25 MPa) at a fibre volume fraction **V_f = 0.40**. Calculate:
(i) the critical fibre length and aspect ratio;
(ii) the longitudinal composite strength and modulus (assume η_l = 1);
(iii) the minimum V_f required for the composite to be stronger than the neat matrix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Refer to Section 2.2 (Sub-section C – Sol–Gel Process) and Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart for full 7-mark explanation.* Key points examiners expect:

1. Precursor selection (aluminium chlorohydrate + silica sol + boric acid).
2. Viscosity-controlled wet spinning through multi-hole spinneret.
3. Ammonia-induced gelation.
4. Controlled drying (RH ≈ 60 %) to avoid crack formation.
5. Two-stage pyrolysis: 800 °C (organic removal) → 1000–1500 °C (γ → α crystallisation).
6. Comparison: Sol–gel yields fine (10–12 µm), high-purity, continuous filament with excellent properties; melt-spinning yields coarse (> 50 µm) fibres with limited length. Sol–gel is capital-intensive but produces aerospace-grade fibre; melt-spinning is cheaper but limited to insulation-grade products.

**(b) Step-by-step solution:**

**(i) Critical fibre length:**

$$
\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_f^u}{2 \, \tau_i} = \frac{3.1 \times 10^9}{2 \times 25 \times 10^6} = 62
$$

$$
L_c = 62 \times 12 \, \mu m = 744 \, \mu m \approx 0.74 \, mm
$$

**(ii) Composite longitudinal modulus:**

$$
E_c = V_f \, E_f + (1 - V_f) \, E_m = 0.40 \times 380 + 0.60 \times 3.5 = 152.0 + 2.1 = 154.1 \, GPa
$$

**Composite longitudinal strength** (taking σ_m' ≈ 50 MPa, the matrix stress at fibre fracture strain):

$$
\sigma_c = V_f \, \sigma_f + (1 - V_f)\,\sigma_m' = 0.40 \times 3100 + 0.60 \times 50 = 1240 + 30 = 1270 \, MPa \approx 1.27 \, GPa
$$

**(iii) Minimum V_f for strengthening:**

$$
V_{f,crit} = \frac{\sigma_m^u}{\sigma_f - \sigma_m' + \sigma_m^u} = \frac{70}{3100 - 50 + 70} = \frac{70}{3120} = 0.0224 \approx 2.24 \,\%
$$

Since actual V_f = 0.40 ≫ V_f,crit, the composite is comfortably stronger than the neat matrix.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **key mechanical, thermal and dielectric properties** of alumina fibres that make them suitable for space applications. Tabulate the properties of at least three commercial alumina fibre grades (Nextel 610, Nextel 720, Altex). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An alumina-fibre/phenolic composite is being designed for a re-entry vehicle leading edge. The composite uses **Nextel 440 fibre** (E_f = 190 GPa, α_f = 6 × 10⁻⁶ /K) in a **phenolic matrix** (E_m = 4 GPa, α_m = 45 × 10⁻⁶ /K) at V_f = 0.35. The component is cured at 175 °C and operates at −50 °C in LEO.
(i) Calculate the longitudinal CTE of the composite.
(ii) Compute the thermal mismatch stress generated at the fibre–matrix interface.
(iii) Suggest two engineering strategies to mitigate this thermal stress in service. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Refer to Section 2.5 (Property Matrix) and Section 2.6 (Why Alumina for Space).* Key points:

1. **Mechanical:** high specific strength (σ/ρ), high specific stiffness (E/ρ), retains > 50 % strength at 1000 °C.
2. **Thermal:** low thermal conductivity (insulator), low CTE, refractory up to 1800 °C.
3. **Dielectric:** ε_r ≈ 9–10, tan δ < 0.001, RF-transparent.
4. **Table:** Refer to the commercial-fibre property table in Section 2.5.

**(b) Step-by-step solution:**

**(i) Longitudinal CTE (rule of mixtures):**

$$
\alpha_c = \frac{V_f \, E_f \, \alpha_f + (1 - V_f) \, E_m \, \alpha_m}{V_f \, E_f + (1 - V_f) \, E_m}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.35 \times 190 \times 6 + 0.65 \times 4 \times 45}{0.35 \times 190 + 0.65 \times 4} \quad (\times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
= \frac{399 + 117}{66.5 + 2.6} \times 10^{-6} = \frac{516}{69.1} \times 10^{-6} = 7.47 \times 10^{-6} \, /K
$$

**(ii) Thermal mismatch stress in the fibre:**

Temperature drop ΔT = 175 − (−50) = 225 K.
Δα = (6 − 45) × 10⁻⁶ = −39 × 10⁻⁶ /K.

$$
\sigma_f^{th} = E_f \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T = 190 \times 10^9 \times 39 \times 10^{-6} \times 225
$$

$$
= 190 \times 10^9 \times 8.775 \times 10^{-3} = 1.667 \, GPa
$$

This is unphysically high, indicating that in reality the matrix microcracks and the fibre stress saturates at the matrix-cracking stress (typically 50–150 MPa for brittle phenolic). Nevertheless, the calculation shows that large thermal mismatch is unavoidable, requiring engineered interfaces.

**(iii) Mitigation strategies (any two):**

1. **Compliant interfacial coating** — deposit a thin (~ 0.1 µm) pyrolytic carbon or BN layer between fibre and matrix to absorb strain energy and deflect cracks.
2. **Use of low-CTE polymer matrix** — switch from phenolic (α = 45 × 10⁻⁶ /K) to a low-CTE polyimide (α ≈ 20 × 10⁻⁶ /K) or cyanate ester (α ≈ 35 × 10⁻⁶ /K) to reduce Δα.
3. **Stepwise cure cooling** — control cooling rate to allow stress relaxation in the matrix.
4. **Hybrid reinforcement** — blend alumina fibre with low-CTE carbon fibre to tune composite CTE closer to the design value.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the γ → α transition:** Many students write "alumina fibre is melted and spun" without mentioning the **sol–gel route** and the **crystallographic transformation** that defines the process. This costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing σ_f with σ_f^u:** The rule-of-mixtures strength uses the **fibre stress at composite failure strain**, NOT the ultimate fibre strength. Always compute ε_f^u = σ_f^u / E_f and apply matrix stress at that strain.
> 3. **Ignoring η_l:** For short-fibre composites, neglecting the **length efficiency factor** and assuming η_l = 1 inflates the strength and loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion:** 1 GPa = 10⁹ Pa, 1 µm = 10⁻⁶ m. Mixing units (e.g., mm with µm) is the single most common computational error in KTU answer scripts.
> 5. **No application context:** Part (a) of essay questions ALWAYS expects a "Why?" answer tied to space engineering. A purely descriptive answer without the engineering rationale scores ≤ 60 %.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before every KTU Module-2 viva and end-semester examination.**

- ✅ Alumina fibre = aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) fibre — a refractory oxide ceramic reinforcement.
- ✅ Two key polymorphs: **γ-Al₂O₃ (transition, porous, low density)** and **α-Al₂O₃ (corundum, stable, dense, high strength)**.
- ✅ Commercial grades to remember: **Nextel 610 (99 % Al₂O₃, 380 GPa, 3.1 GPa strength, 1000 °C)**; **Nextel 720 (85/15 Al₂O₃/SiO₂, 260 GPa, 1200 °C)**; **Nextel 440 (70/28/2, 190 GPa, 1300 °C)**; **Altex (85/15, melt-spun)**; **PRD-166 (Al₂O₃/ZrO₂, transformation-toughened)**.
- ✅ Dominant manufacturing route for high-purity continuous filament: **sol–gel (3M Nextel process)** — precursor dissolution → wet spinning → ammonia gelation → drying → pyrolysis → sintering (γ → α).
- ✅ Alternate route: **melt spinning** at ~2100 °C yields coarser fibres (limited to insulation).
- ✅ α-Al₂O₃ single-crystal fibre = **sapphire fibre**, made by CVD — top-tier properties, niche applications.
- ✅ Key engineering constants: ρ(α-Al₂O₃) = **3.98 g/cm³**; E = 380 GPa; σ = 3.1 GPa; CTE = 8 × 10⁻⁶ /K; band gap = 8.7 eV.
- ✅ **Rule of mixtures (longitudinal):** E_c = η_l V_f E_f + (1 − V_f) E_m.
- ✅ **Critical fibre aspect ratio:** L_c/d = σ_f^u / (2 τ_i).
- ✅ **Minimum fibre volume for strengthening:** V_f,crit = σ_m^u / (σ_f − σ_m' + σ_m^u).
- ✅ **Thermal mismatch stress:** σ^th = E_f · (α_f − α_m) · ΔT — governs microcracking on thermal cycling.
- ✅ **Space applications:** rocket nozzle throats, leading-edge TPS, radomes, cryogenic tank insulation, ablative liners, structural panels of crew modules.
- ✅ **Why alumina over SiC/carbon for space:** inherent oxidation resistance + RF dielectric transparency + chemical compatibility with polymer matrices.
- ✅ **Limitation:** higher density than carbon or polymer fibres; not suitable where weight is paramount over temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boron Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boron Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boron Fibres** are high-performance reinforcement fibres composed predominantly of elemental boron, typically produced as a **tungsten-core / boron-sheath** composite filament by Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD). They possess exceptionally high specific stiffness and specific strength, making them one of the earliest advanced fibres adopted for aerospace structural composites, particularly in military aircraft and space structures.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, boron fibres belong to the family of **Advanced Continuous Fibres (ACFs)** used as primary reinforcement in **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs)** for space applications. They are characterized by a unique **dual-phase architecture**: an inner tungsten (W) or carbon (C) substrate filament (≈ 10–25 µm diameter) onto which amorphous/semi-crystalline boron is deposited, yielding an outer diameter of **100–200 µm**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Candy-Coated Skewer":**
> Imagine a thin tungsten wire (the *skewer*) being slowly dipped into molten sugar that hardens into a thick coating (the *boron shell*). The thin metal core carries most of the load (because boron alone is brittle), while the thick boron shell provides the stiffness and resists stretching. Together they behave like a single ultra-strong rod — much stronger than either material on its own.

This is precisely why boron fibres are engineered as **composite filaments** rather than pure boron rods — pure boron is brittle and difficult to handle, but the metallic core solves the brittleness problem and the boron sheath delivers the aerospace-grade stiffness.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fibre diameter | $d_f$ | **100 – 200** | $\mu m$ |
| Core diameter (W) | $d_c$ | **10 – 25** | $\mu m$ |
| Density | $\rho_f$ | **≈ 2.57** | $g/cm^3$ |
| Tensile strength | $\sigma_t$ | **3.5 – 4.0** | $GPa$ |
| Young's modulus | $E_f$ | **380 – 400** | $GPa$ |
| Failure strain | $\varepsilon_f$ | **≈ 0.8 – 1.0** | % |
| Operating temperature limit | $T_{max}$ | **≈ 500 – 600** | $^\circ C$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Boron fibres are the **first generation** of advanced fibres developed for aerospace. Although largely superseded by carbon/SiC fibres today, they remain academically significant as a benchmark for studying fibre-matrix interface mechanics in PMCs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manufacturing Principle — Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)

The dominant route for boron fibre production is **CVD of boron onto a hot tungsten substrate**, exploiting the **hydrogen reduction of boron trichloride**:

$$BCl_3 \text{(g)} + \tfrac{3}{2}\, H_2 \text{(g)} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\; 1100-1300^\circ C} B \text{(s)} + 3\, HCl \text{(g)}$$

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Substrate Preparation:** A fine tungsten filament (≈ 10–25 µm) is drawn continuously through a glass reaction chamber.
- **Step 2 — Pre-heating:** The W filament is resistively heated to ≈ **1300 °C** by passing a current through mercury contacts.
- **Step 3 — Vapour Mixing:** A gaseous mixture of **BCl₃** and **H₂** is introduced into the chamber.
- **Step 4 — Heterogeneous Reduction:** Boron atoms deposit onto the hot W surface (heterogeneous nucleation), forming a coarse columnar microstructure of boron.
- **Step 5 — Core Diffusion:** Some boron diffuses into the tungsten core forming **W₂B₅, WB₄, WB₂** boride reaction zones — these contribute to high interfacial shear strength.
- **Step 6 — Take-up:** The finished fibre is wound on a spool at a controlled linear speed of ≈ **3 m/min**.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Why" behind each step: Heterogeneous reduction ensures boron deposits preferentially on the substrate (not in the gas phase). The reaction zones (W-borides) act as a **gradient interface**, transferring load smoothly from the compliant tungsten core to the stiff boron shell — preventing catastrophic interfacial shear failure.

## 2.2 Microstructure — The "Core–Shell" Architecture

The fibre exhibits a **distinct radial gradient**:

1. **Innermost:** Unreacted tungsten core ($\rho_W = 19.3 \, g/cm^3$).
2. **Intermediate:** W-boride reaction zone (WB, W₂B₅).
3. **Outer (bulk):** Amorphous/crystalline boron with a *corn-cob* (radial tufted) surface morphology.
4. **Surface:** Naturally rough — this is *engineered* to enhance mechanical interlocking with the polymer matrix (e.g., epoxy).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_c = \dfrac{d_c^2}{d_f^2}$ | Volume fraction of W core | dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_b = 1 - V_c$ | Volume fraction of boron shell | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\rho_f = V_c \rho_W + V_b \rho_B$ | Density (rule of mixtures) | $g/cm^3$ |
| 4 | $E_f = V_c E_W + V_b E_B$ | Effective Young's modulus | $GPa$ |
| 5 | $E_L = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ | Composite longitudinal modulus (RoM) | $GPa$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_{m'} V_m$ | Composite longitudinal strength (modified RoM) | $MPa$ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\sigma_f}{\sigma_c} = \dfrac{L_c}{d_f}\,\dfrac{\tau_i}{2}$ | Critical fibre length relation | $L_c$ in $mm$ |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{E_f}{E_m}$ | Modulus ratio (fibre efficiency index) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are routinely used in KTU valuation when computing the *specific stiffness* of a unidirectional boron/epoxy panel used in space truss structures (e.g., Space Shuttle payload-bay doors, F-14/15 military aircraft skins, Hubble Space Telescope boom-trusses).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Density of a Boron Fibre

**Problem Statement:**
A boron fibre has overall diameter $d_f = 140 \,\mu m$ and a tungsten core of $d_c = 14 \,\mu m$. Given $\rho_W = 19.3 \, g/cm^3$ and $\rho_B = 2.34 \, g/cm^3$, find the effective fibre density.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{d_c^{\,2}}{d_f^{\,2}} \quad\text{[Ratio of cross-sectional areas, since both are circles]} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{(14)^2}{(140)^2} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{196}{19600} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.01 \quad\text{(i.e., 1% by volume is tungsten)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_b &= 1 - V_c \\[4pt]
    &= 1 - 0.01 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_f &= V_c \,\rho_W + V_b \,\rho_B \\[4pt]
       &= (0.01)(19.3) + (0.99)(2.34) \\[4pt]
       &= 0.193 + 2.3166 \\[4pt]
       &= 2.5096 \, g/cm^3 \\[4pt]
       &\approx 2.51 \, g/cm^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with only 1% tungsten by volume, the contribution to density is ~ 6.7% — explaining why the experimental density of boron fibre (~ 2.57) is always higher than pure boron (~ 2.34).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Longitudinal Modulus of Boron Fibre (Rule of Mixtures)

**Given:**
$E_W = 410 \, GPa$ and $E_B = 380 \, GPa$, with $V_c = 0.01$ and $V_b = 0.99$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= V_c E_W + V_b E_B \\[4pt]
    &= (0.01)(410) + (0.99)(380) \\[4pt]
    &= 4.10 + 376.20 \\[4pt]
    &= 380.30 \, GPa
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inference:** The tungsten core contributes minimally to modulus because of its tiny volume fraction; the **boron shell governs the stiffness**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Composite Longitudinal Modulus (Boron/Epoxy Unidirectional PMC)

**Given:** $V_f = 0.55$ (fibre), $V_m = 0.45$ (epoxy), $E_f = 380 \, GPa$, $E_m = 3.5 \, GPa$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_L &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\[4pt]
    &= (380)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45) \\[4pt]
    &= 209.0 + 1.575 \\[4pt]
    &= 210.575 \, GPa
\end{aligned}
$$

**Specific Stiffness (a space-grade metric):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_L}{\rho_c} &= \frac{210.575 \, GPa}{1.95 \, g/cm^3} \quad\text{(typical }\rho_c\text{)} \\[4pt]
                   &\approx 108.0 \, GPa \cdot cm^3 / g
\end{aligned}
$$

This is roughly **3× that of aluminium and 1.5× that of titanium** — the reason boron/epoxy was the *gold standard* for primary space structures pre-1990.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Fibre Length $L_c$

**Given:** Fibre diameter $d_f = 140 \,\mu m$, fibre tensile strength $\sigma_f = 3.5 \, GPa$, interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 70 \, MPa$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= \frac{\sigma_f \, d_f}{2 \, \tau_i} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{(3500 \, MPa)(0.140 \, mm)}{2(70 \, MPa)} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{490}{140} \\[4pt]
    &= 3.5 \, mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $L_c = 3.5 \, mm$ means fibres shorter than ~ 3.5 mm cannot be loaded to their ultimate strength — the matrix pulls out before fibre breakage. For aerospace structures, fibres are typically used as **continuous tows** (>> $L_c$) so this constraint is naturally satisfied.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    A7 --> A8[Take-up Spool at 3 m per min]
    A3 -.-> R1[Byproduct HCl - Gas Scrubber]
