# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define boron fibres. Mention any two of their characteristic properties that make them suitable for space applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Boron fibres are advanced reinforcement filaments produced by **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)** of boron onto a heated tungsten or carbon substrate, yielding a core–shell composite filament typically 100–200 µm in diameter.

Two key properties for space applications:

1. **High specific stiffness** — $E/\rho \approx 150 \, GPa \cdot cm^3/g$, far exceeding aluminium (~ 26) and titanium (~ 26), enabling ultra-lightweight structural trusses.
2. **Low coefficient of thermal expansion** — $\approx 4 \times 10^{-6} \, /K$, providing **dimensional stability** for optical instruments and antenna booms in orbit (which experience temperature swings from −150 °C to +120 °C).

*(3 marks — definition 1 m + two properties 1 m each)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Write the chemical reaction involved in the production of boron fibres by CVD.**

**Model Answer:**

$$BCl_3 \text{(g)} + \tfrac{3}{2}\, H_2 \text{(g)} \xrightarrow{1100-1300^\circ C} B \text{(s)} + 3\, HCl \text{(g)}$$

Here, **BCl₃** (boron trichloride) vapour is reduced by **hydrogen** over a resistively heated **tungsten** substrate. Solid boron deposits on the substrate while **HCl** is the gaseous byproduct.

*(3 marks — equation 2 m + explanation 1 m)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### `Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **manufacturing process of boron fibres by Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)**, with a neat sketch of the reactor and the chemistry involved.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional boron/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.50$. If $E_f = 380 \, GPa$, $E_m = 3.5 \, GPa$, $\rho_f = 2.57 \, g/cm^3$, and $\rho_m = 1.20 \, g/cm^3$, calculate (i) the longitudinal modulus $E_L$, (ii) the composite density $\rho_c$, and (iii) the specific stiffness $E_L / \rho_c$. Compare the specific stiffness with that of **aluminium** ($E_{Al} = 70 \, GPa$, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70 \, g/cm^3$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

The CVD process for boron fibre production involves the following sequential steps:

1. **Substrate Selection:** A continuous tungsten filament (diameter 10–25 µm) is used as the substrate because of its high melting point (3422 °C) and ability to form borides for strong bonding.
2. **Pre-heating:** The tungsten wire is drawn through mercury electrodes that resistively heat it to **1100–1300 °C**.
3. **Gas Introduction:** A mixture of **BCl₃ vapour and H₂ gas** is introduced into a sealed glass reactor.
4. **Chemical Reduction (Heterogeneous):** At the hot W surface, boron trichloride is reduced:
   $$BCl_3 + \tfrac{3}{2} H_2 \rightarrow B + 3\, HCl$$
   Boron atoms deposit on the substrate; some diffuse inward to form tungsten borides (WB, W₂B₅, WB₄).
5. **Layer Buildup:** Continuous passage produces a 50–100 µm boron shell, giving a final fibre diameter of **100–200 µm**.
6. **Take-up:** The finished fibre is wound on a spool at ~ 3 m/min.

**Key features for space applications:** The corn-cob outer morphology enhances mechanical interlocking with epoxy; the W-boride gradient interface prevents catastrophic debonding under thermal cycling.

*(7 marks — chemistry 2 m + steps 3 m + reactor description 2 m)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Longitudinal Modulus** (Rule of Mixtures):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_L &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\[4pt]
    &= (380)(0.50) + (3.5)(0.50) \\[4pt]
    &= 190 + 1.75 \\[4pt]
    &= 191.75 \, GPa
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Composite Density** (Rule of Mixtures):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m \\[4pt]
       &= (2.57)(0.50) + (1.20)(0.50) \\[4pt]
       &= 1.285 + 0.600 \\[4pt]
       &= 1.885 \, g/cm^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Specific Stiffness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_L}{\rho_c} &= \frac{191.75}{1.885} \\[4pt]
                  &\approx 101.7 \, GPa \cdot cm^3 / g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with aluminium:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_{Al}}{\rho_{Al}} &= \frac{70}{2.70} \\[4pt]
                         &\approx 25.9 \, GPa \cdot cm^3 / g
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{101.7}{25.9} \approx 3.93
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Boron/epoxy at $V_f = 0.5$ offers nearly **4× the specific stiffness** of aluminium — a decisive advantage for satellite and launch-vehicle structures where every kilogram costs ≈ ₹ 5–10 lakhs to place in orbit.

*(7 marks — RoM 3 m + specific stiffness 2 m + comparison 2 m)*

---

### `Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice`

**(a)** Discuss the **microstructure of a boron fibre** with a labelled cross-sectional diagram. Explain the role of the tungsten core, the W-boride reaction zone, and the boron shell in the load-transfer mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A boron fibre has $d_f = 100 \,\mu m$ and $\sigma_f = 3.5 \, GPa$. If the matrix has interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 70 \, MPa$, calculate the **critical fibre length** $L_c$. If the actual fibre length is 5 mm, will the fibre achieve its ultimate tensile strength? Comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

The boron fibre microstructure comprises three concentric zones (refer to the schematic in **Section 4.2**):

1. **Tungsten Core (innermost):** Diameter ≈ 14 µm, provides *toughness* and prevents catastrophic brittle failure of pure boron. It is compliant ($E_W \approx 410 \, GPa$) relative to the shell.
2. **Tungsten Boride Reaction Zone (intermediate):** Compounds such as WB, W₂B₅, WB₄ form by solid-state diffusion. This zone acts as a *gradient interface* that gradually transfers axial load from the stiff boron shell to the compliant tungsten core without producing a sudden stress singularity.
3. **Boron Shell (outermost):** Thick, amorphous-to-crystalline boron layer (≈ 70 µm) with a characteristic *corn-cob* (radial tufted) surface. The shell provides the **stiffness** ($E_B \approx 380 \, GPa$) and bears the bulk of the tensile load. The rough morphology *mechanically interlocks* with the epoxy matrix, enhancing interfacial shear strength.

**Load-transfer mechanism:** When the composite is loaded in tension, the matrix transfers stress to the fibre through **interfacial shear**. Because the W-boride zone has intermediate mechanical properties, load is shared progressively — no single interface is overloaded. This makes boron fibres exceptionally resistant to *debonding* under cyclic thermal loading in orbit.

*(7 marks — microstructure 3 m + roles 2 m + load transfer 2 m)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

The critical fibre length is derived by force balance on a fibre loaded through interfacial shear:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= \frac{\sigma_f \, d_f}{2 \, \tau_i} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{(3500 \, MPa)(0.100 \, mm)}{2 \times 70 \, MPa} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{350}{140} \\[4pt]
    &= 2.5 \, mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with actual fibre length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 5 \, mm \\[4pt]
\frac{L}{L_c} &= \frac{5}{2.5} = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L = 2 L_c$, the fibre is **2× the critical length**. By the rule, continuous fibres with $L \gg L_c$ achieve *near-full* efficiency ($\eta_l \approx 1$ for $L \ge 10 L_c$ and $\eta_l \approx 0.5$ for $L = L_c$). At $L = 2 L_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_l &= 1 - \frac{L_c}{2L} \\[4pt]
       &= 1 - \frac{2.5}{10} \\[4pt]
       &= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The fibre will **not** achieve its ultimate tensile strength — only ~ **75 %** of $\sigma_f$ is realized before matrix shear failure initiates pull-out. In practice, boron fibres are always used as **continuous tows** (lengths of meters), so this limitation is circumvented in real space structures.

*(7 marks — formula 2 m + calculation 2 m + efficiency 2 m + conclusion 1 m)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Rule of Mixtures for modulus vs. density.** Both use the *same form* but with different constituent properties — many students swap the values and get $\rho_c = 1.99 \, g/cm^3$ instead of $1.885 \, g/cm^3$. **Always verify** units and the property symbol.
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma_f$ in $L_c$ with composite strength.** $L_c$ uses the *fibre* tensile strength, not the composite strength. A common error is to plug in $\sigma_c$.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2.** The derivation of $L_c = \sigma_f d_f / (2 \tau_i)$ has a factor of 2 from the *two shear surfaces* per fibre. Many students omit it.
> 4. **Stating "BCl₃ + H₂ → B + HCl"** without balancing — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for an unbalanced equation.
> 5. **Writing "tungsten substrate" without specifying the boride interlayer** — this misses the *gradient interface concept* and costs marks on the load-transfer question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Boron fibres are **CVD-deposited** elemental boron on a tungsten substrate; diameter 100–200 µm.
- **Manufacturing:** CVD reaction $BCl_3 + \tfrac{3}{2} H_2 \rightarrow B + 3\, HCl$ at 1100–1300 °C.
- **Core–Shell Architecture:** Tungsten core (toughness) + W-boride interface (gradient load transfer) + boron shell (stiffness) + corn-cob surface (mechanical interlock).
- **Key Metrics:** $\rho_f \approx 2.57 \, g/cm^3$, $E_f \approx 380 \, GPa$, $\sigma_f \approx 3.5 \, GPa$, $\varepsilon_f \approx 1\%$, CTE $\approx 4 \times 10^{-6}/K$.
- **Composite Modulus:** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — always a Rule-of-Mixtures form.
- **Critical Length:** $L_c = \sigma_f d_f / (2\tau_i)$ — fibres must be $\gg L_c$ for full efficiency.
- **Space Applications:** Space Shuttle payload-bay doors, F-14/F-15 skins, Hubble truss booms — anywhere **specific stiffness** is the design driver.
- **Advantages over carbon fibre (historical):** Higher compressive strength, positive CTE matching with metals, easier to handle (less brittle).
- **Disadvantages:** High cost (CVD is slow), large diameter (poor drapability), heavy compared to carbon — superseded in most modern spacecraft by **carbon/IM7/977** and **T1100** systems.
- **KTU Hot Keyword Triggers:** *"specific stiffness", "rule of mixtures", "chemical vapour deposition", "critical fibre length", "gradient interface", "load transfer", "corn-cob morphology"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicon Carbide Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Silicon Carbide Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Silicon Carbide (SiC) Fibres** are continuous, polycrystalline or near-stoichiometric ceramic reinforcing fibres composed primarily of silicon and carbon in a covalent tetrahedral bonding arrangement, with the empirical formula approaching **SiC** in third-generation grades. They are manufactured through precursor pyrolysis (Yajima route), chemical vapour deposition (CVD), or sintering routes, and serve as high-performance reinforcement in Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs), Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs), and Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs) used in **aerospace, rocket propulsion, turbine engines, and space-reentry thermal protection systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEPOT746 / Module 2**
> SiC fibres are positioned as a critical bridge between polymeric precursors and ceramic end-products. Students must master the *three-generation classification*, the *Yajima polycarbosilane route*, and the *property evolution across generations*, as these are repeatedly tested in ESE.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Steel Rebar of the 1000 °C World"

Imagine constructing a concrete pillar for a building: you embed steel rods (rebar) inside the concrete to make it strong in tension. The concrete handles compression, the steel handles tension — together they outperform either material alone.

**Silicon Carbide fibres play exactly this role** — but at temperatures where ordinary steel would melt, oxidize, or disintegrate (above **1200 °C**).

- The "concrete" here is a brittle ceramic matrix (e.g., SiC, alumina, or a high-temperature polymer).
- The "rebar" is the SiC fibre, providing tensile strength, fracture toughness, and crack-bridging capability.
- Without the fibre, the ceramic shatters like glass on impact; with the SiC fibre, it behaves like a tough, damage-tolerant material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Space Applications:** Rocket nozzles, leading edges of re-entry vehicles, and jet engine turbine blades experience *thermal shock* (rapid temperature swings of > 1000 °C in seconds) and *mechanical loads* simultaneously. SiC-fibre-reinforced CMCs are among the only materials that survive this dual extreme.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics

| Property (English Symbol) | Typical Range (3rd Gen) | Engineering Significance |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Tensile Strength ($\sigma_t$) | **2.5 – 3.5 GPa** | Load-bearing capacity of single filament |
| Young's Modulus ($E$) | **350 – 420 GPa** | Stiffness under axial load |
| Density ($\rho$) | **2.5 – 3.1 g/cm³** | Specific strength (σ/ρ) calculation |
| Diameter ($d$) | **7 – 15 µm** | Filament thickness |
| Thermal Stability | **Up to 1800 °C (inert)** | Maximum service temperature |
| CTE ($\alpha$) | **~ 4.0 × 10⁻⁶ /K** | Dimensional change with temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Strength** of SiC fibre (3 GPa / 2.8 g/cm³) ≈ **1070 kNm/kg**, which is **~3× that of titanium alloys** and **~10× that of stainless steel**, making it indispensable where weight-saving is mission-critical (rockets, satellites).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Generations of SiC Fibres — Conceptual Logic

The evolution of SiC fibres is driven by a single engineering objective: **remove excess oxygen and free carbon** that degrade high-temperature performance.

### 2.1.1 First-Generation SiC Fibres (1970s – 1980s)

- **Composition:** Si–C–O ternary, with significant free carbon and SiO$_x$C$_y$ phases.
- **Precursor:** Polycarbosilane (PCS) — synthesised via the **Yajima process** (described below).
- **Key Issue:** High oxygen content (10 – 15 wt%) leads to **decomposition above 1200 °C** due to carbothermal reduction:
  $$\text{SiO}_2 + 3\,\text{C} \longrightarrow \text{SiC} + 2\,\text{CO} \uparrow$$
- **Commercial Examples:** Nicalon NL-200 (Nippon Carbon), Tyranno LoxM (Ube Industries).
- **Maximum Use Temperature:** **~ 1100 °C**.

### 2.1.2 Second-Generation SiC Fibres (Late 1980s – 1990s)

- **Innovation:** Cross-linking of PCS by **electron-beam (EB) irradiation** in inert atmosphere — eliminates oxygen introduction.
- **Composition:** Si–C (low oxygen, < 1 wt%).
- **Key Issue:** Excess free carbon (~ 10 – 15 wt%) still present — limits creep resistance.
- **Commercial Examples:** Hi-Nicalon (Nippon Carbon), Tyranno ZM.
- **Maximum Use Temperature:** **~ 1300 °C**.

### 2.1.3 Third-Generation (Near-Stoichiometric) SiC Fibres (2000s – Present)

- **Innovation:** Use of **polycrystalline, near-stoichiometric Si–C** with near-zero oxygen and minimal free carbon, achieved through hydrogen sintering or advanced pyrolysis.
- **Composition:** SiC with C/Si ratio ≈ 1.05, oxygen < 0.5 wt%.
- **Commercial Examples:** Hi-Nicalon Type S, Tyranno SA, Sylramic.
- **Maximum Use Temperature:** **> 1800 °C (inert)**, **~ 1400 °C (oxidising)**.
- **Status:** **Standard reinforcement for SiC/SiC CMCs in aerospace** (used in GE's LEAP engine, F414, and SpaceX Draco thruster nozzles).

## 2.2 The Yajima Process — Polycarbosilane (PCS) Precursor Route

The most academically important synthesis route for SiC fibres is the **Yajima process** (invented by Prof. S. Yajima, 1975, Tohoku University).

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Starting Material:** Polydimethylsilane (PDMS), $[\text{–Si(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{–}]_n$.
2. **Thermal Rearrangement (Kumada rearrangement):** PDMS is heated to **~ 400 – 470 °C** in an inert atmosphere (N₂ or Ar), converting it to **polycarbosilane (PCS)** with the repeat unit:
   $$\text{–[–SiH(CH}_3\text{)–CH}_2\text{–]}_n\text{–}$$
3. **Melt Spinning:** PCS is melt-spun at **~ 300 °C** through a spinneret to form green fibres of diameter **~ 15 – 20 µm**.
4. **Curing (Cross-linking):** Green fibres are cured in air at **~ 200 °C** (introducing controlled oxygen for cross-linking — *this is the source of the problematic oxygen in 1st-gen fibres*) or by EB irradiation (for 2nd gen).
5. **Pyrolysis:** Cured fibres are pyrolysed at **~ 1200 – 1400 °C** in nitrogen, converting organic PCS to inorganic **SiC ceramic fibre** with mass loss of ~ 30 – 40 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Yajima route is the **only economically viable method** for mass production of continuous SiC fibre tows (500 – 1600 filaments/tow). CVD methods (e.g., SCS-6 from Textron) produce monofilaments with larger diameter (~ 140 µm) used in metal-matrix composites, but they are too expensive for large aerospace structures.

## 2.3 Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) Route — A Brief Note

A tungsten or carbon substrate filament (~ 10 – 30 µm) is heated to **~ 1300 °C** while silane (SiH₄) or methyltrichlorosilane (CH₃SiCl₃) vapours are passed over it. SiC is deposited layer-by-layer:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{SiCl}_3 \, (g) \xrightarrow{\Delta, H_2} \text{SiC} \, (s) + 3\,\text{HCl} \, (g)$$

This produces a **monofilament** (e.g., SCS-6) with a graded C-rich outer coating that protects against fibre–matrix interfacial reactions.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM TIP:** KTU frequently tests (i) the % mass yield of the Yajima route, (ii) specific strength (σ/ρ), and (iii) the empirical oxide-decomposition equation. Memorise the table below.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\rho}$ | Specific strength (Nm/kg) | Weight-critical design (rockets) |
| 2 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Specific modulus (Nm/kg) | Stiffness-critical design (satellite booms) |
| 3 | $\Delta V_{carb} = \dfrac{m_0 - m_f}{m_0} \times 100\%$ | Pyrolysis mass loss (%) | Yield calculation |
| 4 | $\text{SiO}_2 + 3\text{C} \to \text{SiC} + 2\text{CO}\uparrow$ | Carbothermal decomposition | Max-use-temperature limit (1st gen) |
| 5 | $\alpha_{fiber} - \alpha_{matrix} = \Delta \alpha$ | CTE mismatch | Residual thermal stress in CMC |
| 6 | $\sigma_{crit} = \dfrac{2\,\gamma_s}{r}\sqrt{\dfrac{E_f}{E_m}}$ | Kelly-Tyson critical fibre strength | Minimum fibre strength needed |
| 7 | $K_{IC}^{CMC} = f(V_f, d_f, \tau_i, \sigma_f)$ | CMC fracture toughness | Function of fibre vol. fraction, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where $\gamma_s$** is the fibre–matrix interfacial energy, **$r$** is the fibre radius, and **$\tau_i$** is the interfacial shear strength. The Kelly–Tyson equation is the basis of the famous *fibre–matrix debonding* model that allows CMCs to fail gracefully (toughness) rather than catastrophically (brittleness).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Specific Use Case | Why SiC Fibre? |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Jet Engines (GE LEAP) | High-pressure turbine shrouds, combustor liners | 20 % weight saving vs. Ni-superalloys, 1500 °C capability |
| Rocket Nozzles | SpaceX Draco / SuperDraco throat inserts | Thermal shock resistance, ablation resistance |
| Re-entry Vehicles | Space Shuttle leading-edge replacements (X-37B) | Oxidation resistance at 1600 °C |
| Nuclear Reactors | SiC/SiC cladding for next-gen reactors | Low activation, radiation tolerance |
| Satellite Structures | High-stiffness booms and panels | Near-zero CTE, dimensional stability |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Specific Strength Comparison of 3rd-Gen SiC Fibre vs. Titanium Alloy

> **Problem Statement (KTU ESE Style):** A 3rd-generation SiC fibre has tensile strength $\sigma_t = 3.2\,\text{GPa}$ and density $\rho_f = 2.8\,\text{g/cm}^3$. A Ti-6Al-4V titanium alloy has $\sigma_t = 1.1\,\text{GPa}$ and $\rho_{Ti} = 4.43\,\text{g/cm}^3$. Compare their specific strengths and comment on the implications for aerospace structural design.

### Step 1 — Convert All Units to SI (kg/m³ for density, GPa = 10⁹ Pa for stress)

$$\rho_f = 2.8\,\text{g/cm}^3 = 2.8 \times 10^3\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_{Ti} = 4.43\,\text{g/cm}^3 = 4.43 \times 10^3\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Specific Strength Formula

$$\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\rho}$$

### Step 3 — Compute for SiC Fibre

$$\sigma_{sp,SiC} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}}{2.8 \times 10^3\,\text{kg/m}^3}$$

$$\sigma_{sp,SiC} = 1.143 \times 10^6\,\text{Nm/kg} = 1143\,\text{kNm/kg}$$

### Step 4 — Compute for Ti-6Al-4V

$$\sigma_{sp,Ti} = \dfrac{1.1 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}}{4.43 \times 10^3\,\text{kg/m}^3}$$

$$\sigma_{sp,Ti} = 0.2483 \times 10^6\,\text{Nm/kg} = 248\,\text{kNm/kg}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Ratio

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{sp,SiC}}{\sigma_{sp,Ti}} = \dfrac{1143}{248} \approx 4.61$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

The SiC fibre delivers **4.6 times the specific strength** of Ti-6Al-4V. For a rocket payload fairing, this means that for the **same load-bearing capacity**, the SiC-fibre composite structure weighs only **~ 22 %** of the titanium equivalent — translating directly into **payload mass gain** (a critical economic factor in launch cost, where every kilogram delivered to orbit costs ~ \$2 000 – \$10 000).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Cue:** Full marks require *all 6 steps*, unit consistency, and a final engineering interpretation sentence. Skipping the interpretation is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in ESE.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Pyrolysis Mass Yield in the Yajima Route

> **Problem Statement:** 1.000 kg of polycarbosilane (PCS, formula mass per repeat unit = 58.13 g/mol with empirical composition SiC$_2$H$_6$ in precursor) is pyrolysed. Theoretically, what mass of pure SiC is obtained, assuming complete conversion of the Si and C content, and that all H is lost as H$_2$?

### Step 1 — Establish Atomic Masses (Bold Constants in KTU Notation)

- Si = **28.09 g/mol**
- C = **12.01 g/mol**
- H = **1.008 g/mol**

### Step 2 — Determine Moles of PCS Repeat Unit

$$n_{PCS} = \dfrac{1000\,\text{g}}{58.13\,\text{g/mol}} = 17.20\,\text{mol}$$

### Step 3 — Determine Moles of Si Available

Each repeat unit contains 1 Si atom. Therefore:

$$n_{Si} = 17.20\,\text{mol}$$

### Step 4 — Theoretical SiC Mass Produced

The molar mass of SiC is:

$$M_{SiC} = 28.09 + 12.01 = 40.10\,\text{g/mol}$$

$$m_{SiC} = n_{Si} \times M_{SiC} = 17.20 \times 40.10 = 689.7\,\text{g}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Pyrolysis Mass Yield

$$Y = \dfrac{m_{SiC}}{m_{PCS}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{689.7}{1000} \times 100\% = 68.97\%$$

### Step 6 — Compare with Practical Yield (Critical Exam Note)

In practice, the pyrolysis mass yield is **~ 60 – 70 %** (vs. our theoretical 68.97 %), with the rest escaping as:

- **Hydrogen gas (H₂)** — from Si–H and C–H bond scission.
- **Methane (CH₄)** — from methyl group decomposition.
- **Higher hydrocarbons** — minor volatile species.

The empirical **mass loss** is therefore **~ 30 – 40 %**, consistent with the literature value. This explains the volume shrinkage and density change during fibre manufacture.

$$\Delta m \, (\%) = 100\% - Y = 100\% - 68.97\% = 31.03\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Engineering Implication:** The ~ 30 % mass loss during pyrolysis causes **~ 40 – 50 % linear shrinkage** and **internal porosity** in the green fibre. To prevent defect formation, manufacturers use **carefully controlled heating ramps** (1 – 5 °C/min) and apply **tension** during pyrolysis. This is a frequent 3-mark sub-question in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SiC Fibre Generations at a Glance

| Property | 1st Gen (Nicalon) | 2nd Gen (Hi-Nicalon) | 3rd Gen (Hi-Nicalon Type S) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Composition | Si–C–O (high O) | Si–C (low O) | Si–C (near-stoichiometric) |
| Oxygen content (wt%) | **10 – 15** | **< 1.0** | **< 0.5** |
| Free carbon (wt%) | ~ 10 | ~ 10 | **< 2** |
| Tensile strength (GPa) | 2.5 – 3.0 | 2.5 – 3.0 | **2.5 – 3.5** |
| Modulus (GPa) | 180 – 200 | 250 – 270 | **350 – 420** |
| Diameter (µm) | 10 – 15 | 10 – 15 | **7 – 12** |
| Max use T (°C, inert) | 1100 | 1300 | **> 1800** |
| Max use T (°C, air) | 800 | 1100 | **~ 1400** |
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.55 | 2.74 | **3.05 – 3.10** |
| Cost (USD/m, indicative) | ~ 500 | ~ 1500 | **> 3000** |
| Primary application | Polymer-Matrix CMC | Early CMC prototypes | **Modern SiC/SiC aerospace CMC** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SiC Fibre Production Flow

The diagram below maps the industrial process flow for manufacturing 3rd-generation SiC fibre via the Yajima route (the academically most-tested pathway).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Silicon Source: SiCl4 or Si-metal] --> B[Synthesis of Polydimethylsilane PDMS]
    B --> C[Yajima Kumada Rearrangement at 450 C in N2]
    C --> D[Polycarbosilane PCS Polymer]
    D --> E[Melt Spinning at 300 C]
    E --> F[Green Fibre 15-20 um diameter]
    F --> G{Curing Method}
    G -->|Air 200 C| H1[Oxygen Cross-linked Green Fibre - 1st gen route]
    G -->|EB Irradiation| H2[Oxygen Free Cross-linked Fibre - 2nd gen route]
    H1 --> I1[Pyrolysis 1200 C in N2]
    H2 --> I2[Pyrolysis 1300 C in N2]
    I2 --> J[Hydrogen Sintering at 1500 C]
    I1 --> K1[1st Gen SiC Fibre: Si-C-O]
    I2 --> K2[2nd Gen SiC Fibre: Si-C]
    J --> K3[3rd Gen Stoichiometric SiC Fibre]
    K1 --> L[Quality Tests: Strength, Modulus, Composition]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[CMC or PMC Composite Layup]
    M --> N[Aerospace Component: Turbine, Nozzle, Reentry Edge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular block is a process step; the diamond (`{}`) at step G is a decision node representing the *two alternative curing routes* that distinguish 1st-generation from 2nd/3rd-generation fibres. The final output `K3` (3rd-gen stoichiometric SiC) is the gold standard for current aerospace CMCs.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Cross-Sectional Architecture of a CVD Monofilament (SCS-6)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Core[Core: W or C Substrate 33 um] --> Inner[Inner SiC Deposit Layer]
    Inner --> Mid[Mid Layer: SiC with controlled C:Si ratio]
    Mid --> Outer[Outer Carbon-Rich Coating 3 um]
    Outer --> Coat[Final Protective Carbon Overcoat 1-2 um]
    Outer --> Interface[Interface with Matrix: Forms Reaction Barrier]
    Coat --> Interface
    Interface --> EndUse[Use in Ti-MMC or Ni-MMC for Engine Components]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale for Layered Architecture:** The graded C-rich outer layer acts as a *diffusion barrier* — preventing chemical reaction between the SiC fibre and the metal matrix (e.g., Ti or Ni) at high operating temperatures. Without this barrier, brittle reaction products (e.g., TiSi₂, TiC) would form at the interface and destroy the composite.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Map — Why SiC Fibres Need a Compliant Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crack Approaches Fibre] --> B[Strong Interface = Crack Penetrates Fibre = Brittle Failure]
    A --> C[Weak Interface = Crack Deflects]
    C --> D[Debonding and Fibre Pull-out]
    D --> E[Energy Absorbed = High Toughness]
    B --> F[Catastrophic Fracture: KIC ~ 3 MPa sqrt m]
    E --> G[Toughness: KIC ~ 25-30 MPa sqrt m]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Gold:** KTU frequently tests the concept that the *fibre–matrix interface* in SiC/SiC CMCs is intentionally weak (using a **pyrolytic carbon or BN coating** of ~ 0.1 – 0.5 µm thickness). This is the entire basis of damage-tolerant ceramic composites. The numerical contrast ($\sim 3$ vs. $\sim 30$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$) is worth memorising.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEPOT746] *Define "Silicon Carbide Fibre" and list any three characteristic properties that make it suitable for aerospace applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Silicon Carbide Fibre is a continuous ceramic fibre composed primarily of silicon carbide (SiC), typically manufactured by the pyrolysis of polycarbosilane (PCS) precursor (Yajima process) or by chemical vapour deposition. It is used as reinforcement in ceramic, polymer, and metal matrix composites.

**Three characteristic properties for aerospace suitability:**

1. **High specific strength** (σ/ρ ≈ 1143 kNm/kg for 3rd-gen fibres) — critical for weight-critical launch vehicles.
2. **Excellent thermal stability** up to 1800 °C in inert atmosphere — survives rocket nozzle and re-entry conditions.
3. **Low thermal expansion coefficient** (~ 4 × 10⁻⁶/K) — provides dimensional stability for satellite structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking note:** Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for first property, 1 mark for any two additional correct properties with engineering relevance.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEPOT746] *Distinguish between 1st-generation and 3rd-generation SiC fibres in terms of oxygen content and maximum use temperature.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | 1st Generation (e.g., Nicalon) | 3rd Generation (e.g., Hi-Nicalon Type S) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Oxygen content | **10 – 15 wt%** (introduced by air-curing) | **< 0.5 wt%** (oxygen-free processing) |
| Curing method | Air-curing at 200 °C | EB irradiation or hydrogen sintering |
| Max use temperature | **~ 1100 °C** (limited by SiO₂ + 3C → SiC + 2CO decomposition) | **> 1800 °C (inert)**, ~ 1400 °C (air) |
| Composition | Si–C–O with SiO$_x$C$_y$ phases | Near-stoichiometric Si–C |

> [!NOTE]
> The oxygen in 1st-gen fibres causes catastrophic carbothermal decomposition above 1200 °C, severely limiting high-temperature service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEPOT746, Module 2]** *"With the help of a flow diagram, describe the Yajima process for the manufacture of SiC fibres from polycarbosilane. Discuss the role of oxygen in 1st-generation SiC fibres and how 2nd/3rd-generation fibres address this limitation. Include the relevant chemical equations."*

#### (a) Yajima Process Description and Flow Diagram (7 marks)

The Yajima process (developed by Prof. S. Yajima, 1975) is a four-stage precursor-route for manufacturing SiC fibres:

**Stage 1 — Polycarbosilane (PCS) synthesis:**

Polydimethylsilane (PDMS) is heated in an inert atmosphere (N₂) at **400 – 470 °C**, undergoing the **Kumada rearrangement** to form polycarbosilane (PCS):

$$[\text{–Si(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{–}]_n \xrightarrow{450\,°\text{C, N}_2} [\text{–SiH(CH}_3\text{)–CH}_2\text{–}]_n$$

**Stage 2 — Melt spinning:**

PCS is melt-extruded at **~ 300 °C** through a spinneret (multi-hole die, typically 100 – 1000 holes) to form continuous green fibres of **15 – 20 µm** diameter.

**Stage 3 — Curing (cross-linking):**

Green fibres must be cured (made infusible) to prevent them from melting during the next high-temperature step. Methods:

- **Air-curing (1st gen):** Heating in air at ~ 200 °C. Oxygen bridges form Si–O–Si cross-links.
- **EB irradiation (2nd/3rd gen):** Exposure to electron beam in inert atmosphere — cross-links via Si–C–Si bonds without oxygen.

**Stage 4 — Pyrolysis:**

Cured fibres are heated in N₂ at **1200 – 1400 °C**, converting organic PCS into inorganic SiC:

$$[\text{–SiH(CH}_3\text{)–CH}_2\text{–}]_n \xrightarrow{1300\,°\text{C, N}_2} \text{SiC} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow + \text{CH}_4 \uparrow + \text{hydrocarbons} \uparrow$$

Mass loss during this step is **~ 30 – 40 %**; careful heating rate control is critical to avoid pore formation.

---

#### (b) Role of Oxygen and Generational Improvement (7 marks)

**Problem in 1st-Generation Fibres:**

The air-curing step introduces **10 – 15 wt% oxygen** into the fibre, which remains as an amorphous **SiO$_x$C$_y$** phase distributed in the SiC matrix. Above ~ 1200 °C, the following **carbothermal reduction** occurs:

$$\text{SiO}_2 + 3\,\text{C} \longrightarrow \text{SiC} + 2\,\text{CO} \uparrow$$

This reaction is **destructive**: it generates CO gas bubbles, creates porosity, causes fibre shrinkage, and severely degrades mechanical strength. Therefore, 1st-gen fibres are limited to **~ 1100 °C** service.

**How 2nd/3rd-Generation Fibres Address This:**

- **2nd Gen (Hi-Nicalon):** Replaces air-curing with **electron-beam irradiation** in inert atmosphere — cross-links are formed without oxygen. Oxygen drops to **< 1 wt%**, raising the maximum use temperature to **~ 1300 °C**.
- **3rd Gen (Hi-Nicalon Type S, Tyranno SA):** Goes further — applies **hydrogen sintering** to remove excess free carbon and densify the microstructure. Oxygen is **< 0.5 wt%**, free carbon is **< 2 wt%**, and the composition is near-stoichiometric SiC. Maximum use temperature exceeds **1800 °C (inert)** and **~ 1400 °C (air)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently write "EB curing removes oxygen" — this is misleading. EB curing *avoids introducing* oxygen; it does not actively *remove* it. The key term is "inert-atmosphere cross-linking." Marks are deducted for imprecise wording.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEPOT746, Module 2]** *"Discuss the major applications of SiC fibres in aerospace with specific examples. Explain the role of fibre–matrix interface design in achieving damage tolerance in SiC/SiC ceramic matrix composites (CMCs)."*

#### (a) Aerospace Applications of SiC Fibres (7 marks)

**Application 1 — Jet Engine Components (Hot Section):**

The GE LEAP engine (used in Airbus A320neo, Boeing 737 MAX) uses **SiC/SiC CMCs** in the high-pressure turbine stage. The 3rd-gen Hi-Nicalon Type S fibres reinforce a SiC matrix, replacing traditional nickel-based superalloys. Result: **20 % weight reduction**, **1500 °C capability**, and **lower cooling air requirements** (improved thermodynamic efficiency by 1 – 2 %).

**Application 2 — Rocket Nozzle Throats:**

SpaceX's **Draco and SuperDraco** thruster nozzles use SiC-fibre-reinforced CMCs at the throat — the region of highest thermal and mechanical stress. The combination of ablation resistance, thermal shock resistance, and high specific strength is unmatched by metallic alternatives.

**Application 3 — Re-entry Vehicle Thermal Protection:**

The X-37B spaceplane and proposed next-generation re-entry vehicles use SiC/SiC CMC panels for leading edges and nose caps, surviving sustained **~ 1600 °C** during atmospheric re-entry with minimal ablation.

**Application 4 — Satellite Structural Booms and Panels:**

The near-zero CTE (~ 4 × 10⁻⁶/K) and high specific modulus of 3rd-gen SiC fibres make them ideal for dimensionally stable satellite structures that must maintain alignment across extreme orbital temperature cycles (-150 °C to +150 °C).

---

#### (b) Role of Fibre–Matrix Interface in Damage Tolerance (7 marks)

In monolithic ceramics, a propagating crack leads to **catastrophic fracture** because there is no mechanism to absorb energy. In SiC/SiC CMCs, the interface is engineered to be **intentionally weak** through the application of a thin interphase coating:

**Common Interphase Materials:**

- **Pyrolytic Carbon (PyC):** 0.1 – 0.5 µm layer deposited by CVD.
- **Boron Nitride (BN):** Alternative to PyC, more oxidation resistant.

**Mechanism of Damage Tolerance:**

1. A matrix crack initiates and propagates under load.
2. When the crack reaches the fibre, it encounters the **weak interphase**.
3. The crack **deflects** along the interface (debonding) rather than penetrating the fibre.
4. The fibre **bridges** the crack, holding the structure together.
5. Subsequent **fibre pull-out** dissipates energy through frictional sliding.
6. The composite fails *gradually and gracefully*, not catastrophically.

**Quantitative Impact:**

- Monolithic SiC: fracture toughness $K_{IC}$ ≈ **3 MPa·m$^{1/2}$**.
- SiC/SiC CMC with engineered interface: $K_{IC}$ ≈ **25 – 30 MPa·m$^{1/2}$** (a 10-fold improvement).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse the "fibre–matrix interface" with "adhesion." In a CMC, the interface is *deliberately weak* to promote debonding. State this explicitly in the answer — writing "strong interface" is the most common 2-mark deduction in this question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these 10 bullets before entering the KTU ESE Hall:**

- ☐ SiC fibre is a **ceramic reinforcement**, not a polymer — but it is *made from* a polymeric precursor (polycarbosilane).
- ☐ The **Yajima process** has **4 stages**: PCS synthesis → melt spinning → curing → pyrolysis.
- ☐ **1st-gen SiC fibre** contains **10 – 15 wt% oxygen** (from air-curing) and is limited to **~ 1100 °C**.
- ☐ **2nd-gen SiC fibre** uses **EB irradiation curing** in inert atmosphere — oxygen drops to **< 1 wt%**.
- ☐ **3rd-gen SiC fibre** is **near-stoichiometric**, oxygen **< 0.5 wt%**, used above **1400 °C in air**.
- ☐ The carbothermal decomposition equation **SiO₂ + 3C → SiC + 2CO↑** is the *root cause* of 1st-gen's temperature limit.
- ☐ **Pyrolysis mass loss** in Yajima route ≈ **30 – 40 %** — memorize the calculation method.
- ☐ **Specific strength** of 3rd-gen SiC fibre ≈ **1143 kNm/kg**, which is **~ 4.6× that of Ti-6Al-4V**.
- ☐ SiC/SiC CMCs achieve **toughness ~ 25 – 30 MPa·m$^{1/2}$** (vs. monolithic SiC ~ 3) by using a **weak PyC or BN interphase** to promote debonding and fibre pull-out.
- ☐ Major aerospace users: **GE LEAP engine** (turbine shrouds), **SpaceX thrusters** (nozzle throats), **X-37B** (re-entry leading edges).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silica Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Silica Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Silica fibres** are high-purity inorganic fibres composed predominantly of silicon dioxide (SiO₂), where the silica content is typically greater than **95%**, with the highest grades reaching **99.9999% (six nines)** purity. They are produced as continuous filaments, staple fibres, or woven textiles and belong to the class of *refractory oxide fibres*. In the context of polymer-based space materials, silica fibres are most commonly employed as **reinforcing fillers in organic polymer matrices** (phenolic, silicone, cyanate ester) to manufacture ablative thermal protection systems (TPS), flexible thermal blankets, and radome composites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Within this module, silica fibres are studied as *high-performance reinforcement materials* that, when combined with aerospace-grade thermoset polymers, yield composites capable of surviving re-entry thermal loads exceeding **1000 °C**.

| Classification | SiO₂ Content | Designation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type I | ≥ 99.95 % | High-purity fused silica |
| Type II | 95–99 % | Silicate-based |
| Type III | 60–95 % | Composite / doped silica |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a microscopic **glass thread thinner than a human hair (≈ 9 µm diameter)** that refuses to melt even when a blowtorch is aimed at it. That is essentially a silica fibre. Now imagine weaving thousands of those threads into a soft cloth (resembling a high-tech blanket) and stitching it onto the underside of a spacecraft. When the spacecraft screams back through Earth's atmosphere at **Mach 25**, the blanket chars, slowly erodes, and carries the brutal heat away — *ablation* — keeping the delicate electronics at human-friendly temperatures. Silica fibres, in the polymer-composite sense, are the **invisible body armour** of every re-entry vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "polymers for space" still features an inorganic fibre?** Because the *matrix* (phenolic, silicone rubber) is polymeric, while the *reinforcement* is inorganic silica. The composite is the engineering material — a marriage of polymer chemistry and ceramic physics.

## 1.3 Salient Engineering Metrics

- **Tensile strength:** **3.5 – 5.0 GPa** (virgin filament)
- **Density:** **2.20 g/cm³**
- **Specific strength:** ≈ **1.6 – 2.3 GPa·cm³/g**
- **Continuous service temperature:** **1050 °C**
- **Softening point:** **1665 °C**
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE):** **0.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**
- **Dielectric constant (1 MHz):** **3.78**
- **Refractive index (n_D):** **1.458**
- **LOI:** *Not applicable* (inorganic, non-combustible)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Why silica for space?
The harsh space environment subjects materials to **cyclic thermal shock** (–150 °C to +1200 °C), **vacuum UV radiation**, **atomic oxygen erosion**, and **plasma heating**. Polymers alone cannot survive. Silica (Si–O–Si backbone) is the strongest covalent bond in mineralogy with bond energy **≈ 452 kJ/mol**, providing unmatched thermal and chemical stability.

### Step 2 — Why combine with polymers?
Pure silica is **brittle** (elongation < 1%) and difficult to shape. By embedding silica fibres in a **polymer matrix** (phenolic, silicone, cyanate ester), engineers obtain a **tough, moldable, char-yielding composite** — the polymer volatilizes controllably while the silica skeleton remains, providing **recession-based thermal protection**.

### Step 3 — Manufacturing Route
Two principal routes exist:
1. **Melt-spinning of high-purity quartz** (energy-intensive, high-temp).
2. **Sol–gel process** — chemical solution route using tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), enabling fine filaments at lower temperature.

### Step 4 — Composite Fabrication
The pre-impregnated (*prepreg*) route, resin-transfer moulding (RTM), and hand lay-up of silica-cloth / phenolic prepregs are common. The cured laminate is **pyrolysed in service**, leaving a *silica-phenolic char* that insulates the substrate.

### Step 5 — Performance Verification
Specific strength, ablative recession rate, and back-face temperature are measured via **oxy-acetylene torch test (ASTM E285)** and **arc-jet testing**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Tensile strength of fibre | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{F}{A_f}$ | GPa | Load-bearing capacity |
| 2 | Specific strength | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho_f}$ | GPa·cm³/g | Payload optimization |
| 3 | Specific modulus | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_f}{\rho_f}$ | GPa·cm³/g | Stiffness-to-weight |
| 4 | Rule of mixtures (strength) | $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ | MPa | Composite strength |
| 5 | Rule of mixtures (modulus, longitudinal) | $E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | GPa | Composite stiffness |
| 6 | Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse) | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | — | Cross-ply stiffness |
| 7 | Critical fibre length | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$ | m | Reinforcement efficiency |
| 8 | Ablative recession rate | $\dot{r} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ | mm/s | TPS life prediction |
| 9 | Heat of ablation (effective) | $H_{eff} = \dfrac{\dot{q}_{inc}}{\rho \, \dot{r}}$ | J/kg | Material ranking |
| 10 | Emissivity-augmented back-face temp | $T_b = T_s - \dfrac{q_{inc} \, t}{k \, \rho \, c_p}$ | K | 1-D thermal model |
| 11 | Beer–Lambert optical loss | $I = I_0 \, 10^{-\alpha L}$ | W/m² | Optical attenuation |
| 12 | Snell's law (radome) | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | — | RF transparency |
| 13 | Dielectric loss tangent | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ | — | RF heating |
| 14 | Thermal expansion (composite) | $\alpha_c = \dfrac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$ | /K | Dimensional stability |

> [!NOTE]
> In the rule-of-mixtures formulas, $V_f + V_m = 1$. For ablative silica/phenolic TPS, $V_f$ typically ranges **0.45 – 0.60**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Critical Fibre Length for Effective Load Transfer

A short fibre embedded in a polymer matrix cannot be loaded to its full strength because shear failure at the fibre–matrix interface occurs first. The **critical length $L_c$** is the minimum length for which the fibre can rupture (rather than pull out).

**Step 1 — Consider force equilibrium on a cylindrical fibre of diameter $d$ and length $L$ under tensile load $F$.**

$$
F = \sigma_f \cdot A_f = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — The load is transferred from matrix to fibre through interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ acting on the cylindrical surface area $A_i$.**

$$
F = \tau_i \cdot A_i = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d L)
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for the maximum load a fibre can sustain.**

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d L
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the length $L$ required to build up full fibre stress $\sigma_f$. This is the critical length $L_c$.**

$$
L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{4 \, \tau_i}
$$

> The factor is sometimes written as $\sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ when shear is assumed to act on **both** sides of the fibre (double-ended load transfer). The KTU 2024 module adopts the **single-ended convention** above.

**Step 5 — Practical implication for silica fibres in a phenolic matrix:**

Take $\sigma_f = 3.8 \text{ GPa}$, $d = 9 \text{ µm}$, $\tau_i = 18 \text{ MPa}$.

$$
L_c = \frac{3.8 \times 10^{9} \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 18 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
L_c = \frac{34.2}{72} \times 10^{3} \, \mu\text{m} = 475 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

Hence, silica filaments must be **≥ 475 µm** to fully exploit their tensile strength in the composite.

## 3.2 Derivation — Specific Strength Comparison for Space-Grade Fibres

The specific strength of a fibre is the ratio of its tensile strength to its density. It is the figure-of-merit that determines payload savings in launch vehicles.

**Step 1 — Tabulate engineering data for three high-performance fibres.**

| Fibre | $\sigma_f$ (GPa) | $\rho_f$ (g/cm³) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Silica | 3.8 | 2.20 |
| E-glass | 3.5 | 2.54 |
| Carbon (T700) | 4.9 | 1.80 |
| Kevlar 49 | 3.0 | 1.44 |

**Step 2 — Compute specific strength $\sigma_{sp} = \sigma_f / \rho_f$ for each.**

$$
\sigma_{sp}^{\text{Silica}} = \frac{3.8}{2.20} = 1.727 \text{ GPa·cm}^{3}\text{/g}
$$

$$
\sigma_{sp}^{\text{E-glass}} = \frac{3.5}{2.54} = 1.378 \text{ GPa·cm}^{3}\text{/g}
$$

$$
\sigma_{sp}^{\text{Carbon}} = \frac{4.9}{1.80} = 2.722 \text{ GPa·cm}^{3}\text{/g}
$$

$$
\sigma_{sp}^{\text{Kevlar}} = \frac{3.0}{1.44} = 2.083 \text{ GPa·cm}^{3}\text{/g}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering conclusion.**

Although silica's specific strength is **lower than Kevlar and carbon**, its decisive advantage in space is the combination of **thermal endurance + low CTE + RF transparency** — a trio no organic fibre can match. This is why silica is chosen not for stiffness, but for *survivability*.

## 3.3 Derivation — Ablative Recession (Stagnation-Point Energy Balance)

For a silica-phenolic TPS in stagnation-point re-entry heating, the **steady-state energy balance** per unit area is:

$$
\dot{q}_{inc} = \dot{m} \cdot H_{eff}
$$

where $\dot{q}_{inc}$ is the incident heat flux (W/m²), $\dot{m}$ the mass-loss rate per unit area (kg/m²·s), and $H_{eff}$ the effective heat of ablation (J/kg). Since $\dot{m} = \rho \, \dot{r}$ (recession rate × density),

$$
\dot{r} = \frac{\dot{q}_{inc}}{\rho \, H_{eff}}
$$

**Numerical example.** A typical Apollo-style silica/phenolic ablator has $\rho = 1450 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ and $H_{eff} = 22 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$. For a peak $\dot{q}_{inc} = 4 \text{ MW/m}^{2}$:

$$
\dot{r} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{1450 \times 22 \times 10^{6}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} = 0.125 \text{ mm/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Silica-phenolic TPS recession rates of **0.1 – 0.3 mm/s** are standard for crewed re-entry vehicles, confirming the material's ability to "spend itself" slowly while protecting the underlying structure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between Type I and Type III silica fibres in terms of composition and end-use.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Type I (1 m):** Contains ≥ 99.95 % SiO₂, derived from high-purity fused quartz, used in **optical waveguides, radomes, high-frequency antenna windows** where RF transparency and ultra-low loss are required.
- **Type III (1 m):** Contains 60–95 % SiO₂ with other oxides (Al₂O₃, B₂O₃, CaO), derived from multi-component silicate glasses, used in **cost-sensitive filtration, high-temperature gaskets and insulation** where chemical durability is needed but optical purity is not critical.
- **Distinction summary (1 m):** Type I is *electronic/optical grade* with near-zero impurity scattering; Type III is *industrial/insulation grade* with controlled devitrification behaviour.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**Why are silica fibres preferred over organic aramid (Kevlar) fibres for re-entry thermal protection, despite Kevlar's higher specific strength?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 m) Kevlar decomposes above **~500 °C** through chain scission of amide linkages, releasing combustible volatiles; silica is stable to **>1000 °C**.
- (1 m) Kevlar's coefficient of thermal expansion (~ –2 × 10⁻⁶ /K negative) leads to micro-cracking under thermal shock; silica has a near-zero **(+0.5 × 10⁻⁶ /K)** CTE, ensuring dimensional stability.
- (1 m) Silica contributes a **refractory char** that continues to insulate even after the polymer matrix has pyrolysed, giving ablative protection; Kevlar leaves almost no char residue.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **sol–gel route** for the synthesis of high-purity silica fibres. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A silica fibre of diameter **10 µm** and tensile strength **3.6 GPa** is embedded in a phenolic matrix with interfacial shear strength **20 MPa**. Calculate the **critical fibre length** and comment on whether a 5 mm chopped fibre is sufficient for full reinforcement. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sol–Gel Synthesis of Silica Fibre (7 m)**

1. **Precursor preparation (2 m):** Tetraethyl orthosilicate (Si(OC₂H₅)₄, TEOS) is dissolved in ethanol. Acidified water (HCl, pH ≈ 2) is added drop-wise. Hydrolysis yields silanol groups (Si–OH).

$$
\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_4 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Si(OH)}_4 + 4\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}
$$

2. **Polycondensation (2 m):** Silanol groups condense to form a 3-D siloxane network (Si–O–Si) and release water.

$$
\equiv \text{Si–OH} + \text{HO–Si} \equiv \;\rightarrow\; \equiv \text{Si–O–Si} \equiv + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The resulting viscous sol is aged into a gel.

3. **Drying and spinning (2 m):** The gel is dried at 80 °C to remove solvent, redissolved in a carrier polymer to tune viscosity, and **dry-spun** through spinnerets at 200 °C.

4. **Pyrolysis and densification (1 m):** The green filaments are heat-treated at **800–1000 °C** to burn off organics and yield amorphous SiO₂ fibres of > 99.99 % purity.

Schematic: see Mermaid block in Section 4.2 above.

**(b) Critical Fibre Length Calculation (7 m)**

Given: $d = 10 \text{ µm} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $\sigma_f = 3.6 \text{ GPa} = 3.6 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$, $\tau_i = 20 \text{ MPa} = 20 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$.

**Step 1 — Apply critical length formula (3 m):**

$$
L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{4 \, \tau_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (2 m):**

$$
L_c = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{9} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 20 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
L_c = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{4}}{8 \times 10^{7}} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.45 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with 5 mm chopped fibre (2 m):**

$$
\frac{L_{\text{chopped}}}{L_c} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{0.45 \text{ mm}} \approx 11.1
$$

Since the chopped length is **> 10 × $L_c$**, the fibre is well within the *continuous-fibre regime* (often quoted as $L > 10 L_c$ for near-full strength translation). Therefore, a 5 mm chopped silica fibre **is sufficient for effective reinforcement** of the phenolic matrix.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Discuss the **role of silica fibres in ablative thermal protection systems (TPS)** for re-entry vehicles, with reference to the silica/phenolic composite used in crewed capsules. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A spacecraft flexible thermal blanket uses a silica-fibre batting of areal density **0.4 kg/m²** and effective thermal conductivity **0.04 W/m·K** at 800 °C. If the incident heat flux is **60 kW/m²** and the outer skin reaches **950 °C**, estimate the steady-state back-face temperature for a blanket thickness of **6 mm**. State any assumptions. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of Silica Fibres in Ablative TPS (7 m)**

1. **Refractory reinforcement (2 m):** Silica fibres retain strength to 1050 °C and form a continuous ceramic skeleton as the phenolic matrix pyrolyses. The skeleton resists shear and mechanical erosion caused by high-enthalpy gas flow.

2. **Thermal barrier formation (2 m):** As phenolic resin chars (carbon yield ≈ 60 %), the silica network traps pyrolysis gases in a low-conductivity foam. The effective thermal conductivity falls to **0.1 – 0.3 W/m·K**, reducing back-face heat flux by 80 – 90 %.

3. **Recession control (2 m):** Surface silica oxidises only above 1100 °C, providing a *low regression rate* (≈ 0.1 mm/s). Combined with endothermic decomposition of phenolic, this yields a high *effective heat of ablation* $H_{eff} \approx 22$ MJ/kg.

4. **Engineering precedent (1 m):** The Apollo command module, Orion crew capsule, and several Chinese crewed vehicles have used silica/phenolic ablators in their heatshields; the technology remains the workhorse for human-rated re-entry.

**(b) Steady-State Back-Face Temperature (7 m)**

**Step 1 — Identify Fourier's law for 1-D steady conduction through the blanket (2 m):**

$$
q = \frac{k \, A \, (T_{\text{outer}} - T_{\text{back}})}{t}
$$

Solve for $T_{\text{back}}$:

$$
T_{\text{back}} = T_{\text{outer}} - \frac{q \, t}{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (3 m):**

Given: $q = 60 \text{ kW/m}^{2} = 6 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2}$, $t = 6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $k = 0.04 \text{ W/m·K}$, $T_{\text{outer}} = 950 \text{ °C}$.

$$
T_{\text{back}} = 950 - \frac{6 \times 10^{4} \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.04}
$$

$$
T_{\text{back}} = 950 - \frac{360}{0.04} = 950 - 9000 = -8050 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3 — Re-check: this unphysical result means the 1-D Fourier model breaks down. (2 m)**

The reason: most of the heat is **re-radiated and convected away by ablation** at the outer face, not conducted through. In a real blanket, *surface emissivity* $\varepsilon \approx 0.85$ governs the equilibrium. The proper energy balance at steady state is:

$$
q_{\text{inc}} = \varepsilon \, \sigma_{SB} \, T_{\text{outer}}^{4} + q_{\text{cond}}
$$

With $\sigma_{SB} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4}$, $T_{\text{outer}} = 1223 \text{ K}$:

$$
\varepsilon \sigma_{SB} T^{4} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1223)^{4} \approx 1.06 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/m}^{2}
$$

which is already **greater** than the incident flux, so the outer surface temperature would be lower in reality. The conduction term is negligible compared to the radiative term.

$$
T_{\text{back}} \approx T_{\text{outer}} - \frac{(q_{\text{inc}} - \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}) t}{k} \approx 950 - \text{small correction} \approx 920 - 940 \text{ °C}
$$

**Assumptions (1 m):** (i) 1-D heat flow, (ii) constant $k$ (in reality $k$ falls with temperature), (iii) steady state, (iv) negligible contact resistance between plies, (v) re-radiation dominates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Read Before You Submit:**
> - **Mark loss #1 (Frequent):** Students use $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ (double-ended) when the syllabus specifies single-ended shear transfer, leading to a 2× error. Re-read the question stem!
> - **Mark loss #2 (Common):** In ablative heat-flux problems, students apply Fourier's law without checking whether the outer surface is in radiative equilibrium. Always write the **energy balance**, not just the conduction equation.
> - **Mark loss #3 (Subtle):** Neglecting to mention that silica is **amorphous** (not crystalline quartz). Crystalline SiO₂ undergoes displacive α↔β transition at 573 °C, causing catastrophic volume change — *fused* amorphous silica is therefore chosen for thermal-shock resistance.
> - **Mark loss #4 (Definition):** Confusing "silica" (SiO₂) with "silicone" (polysiloxane, a polymer). Examiners award **zero** credit for mixing the two terminologies.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Composition:** Silica fibres are > 95 % SiO₂; "fused" amorphous silica is preferred over crystalline quartz for space use.
- **Purity grades:** Type I (≥ 99.95 %) — optical/RF; Type II — insulation; Type III (60–95 %) — industrial.
- **Manufacturing routes:** Melt-spinning of high-purity quartz (high-T) and sol–gel from TEOS (low-T, fine filament).
- **Key properties to memorise:** $\sigma_f \approx 3.5$–$5.0$ GPa, $\rho = 2.20$ g/cm³, CTE = $+0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /K, $T_{\text{softening}} = 1665$ °C, $\varepsilon_r = 3.78$, $n_D = 1.458$.
- **Critical fibre length formula (single-ended):** $L_c = \sigma_f d / (4 \tau_i)$.
- **Rule of mixtures (longitudinal modulus):** $E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$.
- **Ablation rate:** $\dot{r} = \dot{q}_{inc} / (\rho H_{eff})$; silica/phenolic: $H_{eff} \approx 22$ MJ/kg.
- **Space applications — must list at least three in any 14-m answer:** (i) ablative heatshield (Apollo, Orion), (ii) radome for hypersonic missiles, (iii) flexible thermal blankets / RSI, (iv) rocket nozzle insulation, (v) cryogenic tank supports.
- **Always distinguish:** *Silica* (inorganic SiO₂ ceramic) vs *Silicone* (organic polysiloxane polymer) vs *Silicon* (semiconductor element).
- **Why amorphous?** Fused silica avoids the 573 °C $\alpha \leftrightarrow \beta$ quartz phase transition that would shatter a crystalline part.
- **Why with polymers?** The phenolic/silicone matrix provides toughness, moldability and ablative char; the silica skeleton provides refractoriness and dimensional stability.
- **Dielectric constant is low (3.78)** → excellent RF transparency for radomes and antenna windows operating at GHz frequencies.
- **Optical transmission:** > 90 % across UV–visible–near-IR when high purity, due to absence of impurity absorption.
- **Common board question pattern:** "Differentiate" (3 m), "Discuss with neat diagram" (7 m), "Calculate and comment" (7 m) — practice all three.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Reinforcement: Property Dependence on Resin and Reinforcement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theory of Reinforcement: Property Dependence on Resin and Reinforcement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fiber-reinforced polymer composites (FRPCs) intended for **space applications**, the macroscopic engineering properties of the composite are not intrinsic to a single material; rather, they are **emergent properties** that arise from the synergistic combination of two distinct phases:

1. **The Matrix Phase (Resin):** A continuous polymer phase (e.g., epoxy, polyimide, cyanate ester, bismaleimide) that binds the reinforcement, transfers load to it, protects it from environmental degradation, and maintains fiber orientation.
2. **The Reinforcement Phase (Fiber/Particulate):** A dispersed, discontinuous or continuous high-strength phase (e.g., carbon fiber, glass fiber, aramid/Kevlar, boron fiber, ceramic whiskers) that carries the majority of the applied load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "The *Theory of Reinforcement* is the set of mathematical and physical principles (Rule of Mixtures, Inverse Rule of Mixtures, Halpin-Tsai, Kelly-Tyson theory) that quantitatively predict how a composite's bulk properties — stiffness, strength, density, thermal expansion, electrical conductivity — depend on the individual constituent properties and their *geometric/morphological arrangement* (volume fraction, fiber length, fiber orientation, fiber-matrix interfacial shear strength)."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bamboo in Concrete"**
> Imagine a concrete pillar (resin) into which thousands of bamboo sticks (fibers) are embedded along its length. If you press down on this pillar, the concrete alone would crumble, but the bamboo resists the load. The pillar becomes far stronger than either material alone. However, this strengthening is NOT automatic — it depends on three conditions:
>
> 1. **How much bamboo** is inside (volume fraction).
> 2. **How well the concrete grips the bamboo** (interfacial shear strength between matrix and reinforcement).
> 3. **How long each bamboo stick is** (critical fiber length — too short and the bamboo will simply slip out, just like pulling a smooth nail out of soft wood).
>
> The composite is a *team sport*: the resin "hands off" the load to the fibers via shear at the interface. The properties of the team depend on the *individual player stats* (fiber/matrix properties) and the *team coordination* (interface + geometry).

### 1.3 Why This Topic is Crucial for Space Applications

In space, polymers face extreme conditions: **cryogenic temperatures** ($-196\,^\circ\text{C}$ for LOX tanks), **vacuum outgassing**, **atomic oxygen erosion** (LEO), **thermal cycling** ($\pm 150\,^\circ\text{C}$), and **micrometeoroid impact**. Engineers MUST be able to *predict* a composite's behavior under these conditions *before* fabrication, because space-qualified parts cannot be "tested and iterated" cheaply. The Theory of Reinforcement provides that predictive toolkit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric in Industry:** Fiber volume fraction $V_f$ in aerospace-grade composites is typically maintained between **0.55 and 0.65** (55%–65%) because the rule-of-mixtures curves peak in stiffness around $V_f \approx 0.6$ for carbon/epoxy systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Foundational Assumptions

The entire theory rests on two key assumptions used in KTU board examinations:

**Assumption 1 — Iso-strain (Voigt Model, Longitudinal Loading):**
When load is applied *parallel* to continuous, aligned fibers, both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$). This is valid because fibers and matrix are bonded at the interface, so they must deform together.

**Assumption 2 — Iso-stress (Reuss Model, Transverse Loading):**
When load is applied *perpendicular* to continuous, aligned fibers, both phases experience the **same stress** ($\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$) but different strains. This is because the cross-section acts like two resistors in series.

### 2.2 Volume Fraction & Weight Fraction Interconversion

A composite contains fiber and matrix, occupying fractions of total volume ($V_f + V_m = 1$) and total weight ($W_f + W_m = 1$).

The interconversion formulas are:

$$
V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}
$$

$$
W_f = \frac{V_f \cdot \rho_f}{V_f \cdot \rho_f + V_m \cdot \rho_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in space:** Aerospace engineers specify composites by **weight fraction** (to minimize launch mass) but predict properties by **volume fraction** (because the rule of mixtures is volumetric). The conversion above is the bridge.

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property | Governing Equation | Model / Loading Direction | Units |
|---|----------|-------------------|---------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Composite Density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Rule of Mixtures (ROM) | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | Longitudinal Young's Modulus | $E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Iso-strain / Voigt | $\text{GPa}$ |
| 3 | Transverse Young's Modulus | $\dfrac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Iso-stress / Reuss | $\text{GPa}$ |
| 4 | Major Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{c,LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Iso-strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | Longitudinal Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ (continuous aligned) | ROM, fibers unbroken | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 6 | Longitudinal Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f' V_f + \sigma_m (1-V_f)$ (short fibers) | Kelly-Tyson | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 7 | Critical Fiber Length | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Load transfer via shear | $\text{mm}$ |
| 8 | Critical Aspect Ratio | $(l_c/d) = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i}$ | Dimensionless form | -- |
| 9 | Fiber Efficiency Factor | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{\tanh(\beta l/2)}{\beta l/2}$, $\beta^2 = \dfrac{-8 \tau_i}{E_f d^2 \ln(V_f)}$ | Short-fiber length correction | dimensionless |
| 10 | Halpin-Tsai Modulus | $\dfrac{E_c}{E_m} = \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$, $\eta = \dfrac{(E_f/E_m) - 1}{(E_f/E_m) + \xi}$ | General, $\xi$ = geometry factor | $\text{GPa}$ |
| 11 | Coeff. of Thermal Expansion (L) | $\alpha_{c,L} = \dfrac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Turner model | $\text{K}^{-1}$ |
| 12 | Void Content | $V_{void} = 1 - \rho_{measured}/\rho_{theoretical}$ | Quality control | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exams:** *"Voigt stretches together, Reuss bears together."* Longitudinal = Voigt (iso-strain). Transverse = Reuss (iso-stress). This single sentence answers 30% of KTU questions on this module.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application | Which Formula is Used |
|--------|------------|----------------------|
| Spacecraft structures | Carbon/epoxy payload fairings | Longitudinal ROM for stiffness |
| Cryogenic fuel tanks | Glass/epoxy LOX tanks | Thermal expansion (Turner) to prevent leaks |
| Antenna reflectors | Carbon-fiber-reinforced cyanate ester | Density ROM (mass minimization) |
| Re-entry TPS | Phenolic/carbon ablative | Critical fiber length (ablation resistance) |
| Satellite booms | Aramid/epoxy tubes | Longitudinal strength, weight-fraction-limited |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus ($E_{c,L}$)

We start with the **iso-strain assumption**: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$.

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium across the cross-section:**
A unidirectional composite of cross-sectional area $A_c$ carries total load $P_c$. Since the fiber and matrix are bonded in parallel:

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express each load in terms of stress and area:**

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's law** ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$) to each phase, using iso-strain ($\varepsilon$ is common):

$$
\sigma_c A_c = E_f \varepsilon A_f + E_m \varepsilon A_m
$$

**Step 4 — Divide the entire equation by $A_c$:**

$$
\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon \left(\frac{A_f}{A_c}\right) + E_m \varepsilon \left(\frac{A_m}{A_c}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that the area ratios equal the volume fractions** (for fibers aligned along the loading axis with uniform cross-section):

$$
\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f, \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m
$$

**Step 6 — Since $\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon$**, divide by $\varepsilon$:

$$
\boxed{E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often award 2 marks for stating the iso-strain assumption, 2 marks for force balance, and 2 marks for the volume-fraction substitution. Do not skip any of these steps.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Inverse Rule of Mixtures for Transverse Modulus ($E_{c,T}$)

We start with the **iso-stress assumption**: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$.

**Step 1 — Strain compatibility (total elongation = sum of elongations of layers in series):**

$$
\Delta_c = \Delta_f + \Delta_m
$$

**Step 2 — Each layer has the same thickness fraction** equal to its volume fraction (since area is common):

$$
\varepsilon_c \cdot t_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot t_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot t_m
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$:**

$$
\frac{\sigma}{E_c} \cdot t_c = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} \cdot t_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} \cdot t_m
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $t_c$ and recognize $t_f/t_c = V_f$:**

$$
\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}
$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$)

A short fiber transfers load to the matrix via **interfacial shear stress** $\tau_i$ acting over the fiber's surface. The maximum tensile stress the fiber can develop is set by the force balance at the moment of fiber pull-out.

**Step 1 — Shear force transferred to a fiber of length $l$ and diameter $d$:**

$$
F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \left(\frac{l}{2}\right)
$$

> The factor $l/2$ arises because shear load builds up from both ends of the fiber, reaching its maximum at the fiber's mid-point.

**Step 2 — Tensile force the fiber can sustain at its breaking stress $\sigma_f$:**

$$
F_{tensile} = \sigma_f \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^2}{4}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two forces at the critical condition** (the fiber is just long enough to be loaded to its fracture stress before pull-out):

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by cancelling $\pi d$:**

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $l_c$:**

$$
l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide both sides by $d$ to obtain the dimensionless form:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation for KTU Answers:** If a fiber's actual length $l < l_c$, it will *pull out* of the matrix without ever reaching its fracture stress. The composite fails by interfacial shear, not fiber fracture. This is why $l/d$ (aspect ratio) is a design parameter in space-grade composites — typically engineered to be $\geq 10$ for carbon/epoxy systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Property Predictions with Real Space-Grade Systems

Below is a worked numerical example tying the formulas together. Consider a **carbon-fiber/epoxy composite** with the following data (representative of T700/2510 system used in satellite structures):

| Property | Carbon Fiber (T700) | Epoxy (2510) |
|----------|---------------------|--------------|
| Young's Modulus, $E$ | $230\,\text{GPa}$ | $3.5\,\text{GPa}$ |
| Density, $\rho$ | $1800\,\text{kg/m}^3$ | $1250\,\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Tensile Strength, $\sigma_f$ | $4900\,\text{MPa}$ | $90\,\text{MPa}$ |
| Interfacial Shear Strength, $\tau_i$ | $70\,\text{MPa}$ | -- |
| Fiber Diameter, $d$ | $7\,\mu\text{m}$ | -- |
| Fiber Volume Fraction, $V_f$ | $0.60$ | $V_m = 0.40$ |

**Sub-problem (a):** Compute the longitudinal modulus.

$$
E_{c,L} = (230)(0.60) + (3.5)(0.40)
$$

$$
E_{c,L} = 138.0 + 1.4 = 139.4\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Sub-problem (b):** Compute the transverse modulus.

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{0.60}{230} + \frac{0.40}{3.5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = 0.002609 + 0.114286 = 0.116895
$$

$$
E_{c,T} = 8.55\,\text{GPa}
$$

> **Critical observation:** $E_{c,T} \ll E_{c,L}$ (8.55 GPa vs 139.4 GPa — a 16× anisotropy). This is *why* space structures are designed with fibers aligned along the load direction. The transverse direction is the *Achilles heel* of unidirectional composites.

**Sub-problem (c):** Compute the critical fiber length and aspect ratio.

$$
l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(4900)(7 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 70}
$$

$$
l_c = \frac{0.0343}{140} = 2.45 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.245\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{0.245 \times 10^{-3}}{7 \times 10^{-6}} = 35
$$

> **Interpretation:** Any carbon fiber shorter than 0.245 mm will pull out before breaking. In practice, T700 fibers are supplied as continuous tows ($l \gg l_c$), ensuring full strength utilization.

**Sub-problem (d):** Compute composite density (for launch-mass budget).

$$
\rho_c = (1800)(0.60) + (1250)(0.40) = 1080 + 500 = 1580\,\text{kg/m}^3
$$

Compare to aluminum alloy Al-7075 ($\rho = 2810\,\text{kg/m}^3$). The composite is **44% lighter** at near-equal stiffness — the entire rationale for CFRP in space.

### 3.5 The Kelly-Tyson Strength Equation for Short Fibers

For *short, aligned* fibers of length $l < l_c$ (sub-critical), only a fraction of $\sigma_f$ is achieved. The Kelly-Tyson modified rule of mixtures is:

$$
\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f \left[1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\right] V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f), \quad \text{for } l > l_c
$$

$$
\sigma_{c,L} = \frac{\tau_i \cdot l}{d} V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f), \quad \text{for } l < l_c
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Watch:** The Kelly-Tyson equation is a **14-mark question favorite** because it integrates (i) critical length concept, (ii) rule of mixtures, AND (iii) the sub- vs super-critical distinction in one formula. Be ready to draw the bilinear $\sigma_c$ vs $l$ curve in your answer script.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Flow: How Resin & Reinforcement Properties Combine to Yield Composite Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Constituent Properties] --> B[Resin Matrix Properties]
    A --> C[Reinforcement Properties]

    B --> B1[E_m: Modulus of Matrix]
    B --> B2[sigma_m: Strength of Matrix]
    B --> B3[rho_m: Density of Matrix]
    B --> B4[nu_m: Poisson Ratio of Matrix]

    C --> C1[E_f: Modulus of Fiber]
    C --> C2[sigma_f: Strength of Fiber]
    C --> C3[rho_f: Density of Fiber]
    C --> C4[d: Fiber Diameter]

    A2[Geometric Design Variables] --> D1[V_f: Fiber Volume Fraction]
    A2 --> D2[l: Fiber Length]
    A2 --> D3[theta: Fiber Orientation]
    A2 --> D4[tau_i: Interfacial Shear Strength]

    B1 --> E[Composite Property Engine]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F1[Rule of Mixtures ROM Longitudinal]
    E --> F2[Inverse ROM Transverse]
    E --> F3[Critical Fiber Length]
    E --> F4[Kelly Tyson Strength]
    E --> F5[Halpin Tsai General Model]

    F1 --> G[Predicted Composite Properties]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G

    G --> G1[E_cL Longitudinal Modulus]
    G --> G2[E_cT Transverse Modulus]
    G --> G3[sigma_cL Longitudinal Strength]
    G --> G4[rho_c Composite Density]
    G --> G5[alpha_cL Thermal Expansion]

    G1 --> H[Space Application Validation]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H

    H --> H1[Satellite Structures]
    H --> H2[Cryogenic Tanks]
    H --> H3[Antenna Reflectors]
    H --> H4[Re entry Heat Shields]
    H --> H5[Launch Vehicle Fairings]
```

### 4.2 Load Transfer Mechanism at the Fiber-Matrix Interface (Conceptual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Tensile Load on Composite] --> B[Matrix Carries Initial Load]
    B --> C[Shear Transfer at Interface]
    C --> D[Fiber Tip A Stress Zero]
    C --> E[Fiber Midpoint Maximum Stress]
    D --> F[Fiber Stress Builds Linearly with x]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Is l greater than l_c}
    G -->|Yes| H[Fiber Reaches sigma_f and Fractures]
    G -->|No| I[Fiber Pulls Out of Matrix]
    H --> J[Composite Fails by Fiber Fracture]
    I --> K[Composite Fails by Interfacial Debonding]
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Property Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Composite Property Prediction Required] --> Q1{Continuous Fibers}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Loading Direction}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Aspect Ratio l/d}
    Q2 -->|Parallel to Fiber| R1[Use Voigt ROM: E_cL = E_fV_f + E_mV_m]
    Q2 -->|Perpendicular to Fiber| R2[Use Reuss IROM: 1/E_cT = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m]
    Q3 -->|Greater than 35| R3[Use Halpin Tsai with xi = 2l/d]
    Q3 -->|Less than 35| R4[Use Kelly Tyson: sigma_c depends on l relative to l_c]
    R1 --> Out[Predicted E_cL for Design]
    R2 --> Out
    R3 --> Out
    R4 --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Visualization Tip:** When asked to "draw the variation of composite strength with fiber length" in an exam, sketch a **bilinear curve**: a linear rise from $l=0$ to $l=l_c$ (governed by $\tau_i \cdot l / d$), then a *plateau* for $l > l_c$ (governed by $\sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$). The "knee" at $l_c$ is the single most-marked feature.
