<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a *lamina* and a *laminate*. How does the choice of lay-up influence the in-plane stiffness of a polymer composite used in spacecraft panels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **lamina** is a single layer of unidirectional or woven fibre-reinforced polymer in which the reinforcing fibres are aligned within a continuous polymer matrix. A **laminate** is a cured stack of two or more such laminae bonded together.

> The **lay-up** — i.e., the ply orientations and stacking sequence — controls the orientation of high-stiffness fibre directions relative to applied loads. By rotating plies (e.g., $[0/\pm 45/90]_s$), the laminate's effective in-plane stiffness in the global $x$–$y$ plane becomes quasi-isotropic, preventing directional weakness. In spacecraft panels this ensures **uniform thermal expansion, balanced buckling resistance, and dimensionally stable reflectors** under on-orbit thermal cycling.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by a *quasi-isotropic* laminate? Why is it the preferred lay-up for satellite structural panels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **quasi-isotropic laminate** is a *symmetric, balanced* lay-up — typically $[0/+45/-45/90]_s$ — whose **in-plane extensional stiffness** $[A]$ behaves as if the laminate were isotropic, i.e., $A_{11}\approx A_{22}$ and $A_{16}\approx A_{26}\approx 0$.

> It is preferred for satellite panels because it offers **near-equal stiffness in all in-plane directions**, eliminating directional bias under multi-axial launch loads and orbital thermal gradients. This results in **uniform in-plane thermal expansion**, **balanced in-plane strength**, and **predictable, isotropic-like buckling behaviour** — critical for reflector flatness and antenna pointing accuracy.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In ABD-matrix problems, students frequently (i) confuse $\nu_{21}$ with $\nu_{12}$, (ii) forget to use $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ for off-axis plies, (iii) drop the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in $B_{ij}$ or $\frac{1}{3}$ in $D_{ij}$, and (iv) mis-identify $z_k$ values for the top vs bottom of each ply. **Always tabulate $z_k$, $\theta_k$, and $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}$ for every ply before integrating.** Marks are lost primarily in the *substitution step*, not the formula recall.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of neat sketches, derive the expressions for the **extensional stiffness matrix $[A]$**, the **coupling matrix $[B]$**, and the **bending stiffness matrix $[D]$** of a general laminate using Classical Lamination Theory. State the units of each matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A symmetric cross-ply laminate is laid up as $[0/90]_s$ with **8 plies**, each of thickness **0.125 mm**, made of **T300/914 carbon–epoxy** ($E_1 = 140\,\text{GPa}$, $E_2 = 10\,\text{GPa}$, $G_{12} = 5\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu_{12} = 0.3$). Calculate the **extensional stiffness matrix $[A]$**, the **coupling matrix $[B]$**, and comment on the in-plane isotropy of the laminate. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation of ABD matrices** *(7 marks)*

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps to be written in the answer script:

**Step 1:** Write Hooke's law for an off-axis ply rotated by $\theta_k$ in the laminate $x$–$y$ axes using transformed reduced stiffnesses $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_x^{(k)} &= \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)}\varepsilon_x + \bar{Q}_{12}^{(k)}\varepsilon_y + \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)}\gamma_{xy} \\
\sigma_y^{(k)} &= \bar{Q}_{12}^{(k)}\varepsilon_x + \bar{Q}_{22}^{(k)}\varepsilon_y + \bar{Q}_{26}^{(k)}\gamma_{xy} \\
\tau_{xy}^{(k)} &= \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)}\varepsilon_x + \bar{Q}_{26}^{(k)}\varepsilon_y + \bar{Q}_{66}^{(k)}\gamma_{xy}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Assume Kirchhoff–Love kinematic hypothesis — strain varies linearly through thickness:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon(z) = \varepsilon^0 + z\,\kappa
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Define force and moment resultants per unit width:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_i &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\sigma_i\,dz, \qquad M_i = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\sigma_i\,z\,dz
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and split the integrals — recognising the three laminate stiffness matrices:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{ij} &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\bar{Q}_{ij}(z)\,dz = \sum_{k=1}^{N}\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}(z_k - z_{k-1}) \;\;[\text{N/m}] \\
B_{ij} &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\bar{Q}_{ij}(z)\,z\,dz = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}(z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2) \;\;[\text{N}] \\
D_{ij} &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\bar{Q}_{ij}(z)\,z^2\,dz = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}(z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3) \;\;[\text{N·m}]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Write the CLT governing equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} &= 
\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sketch:** Show cross-section of laminate with ply numbering top-to-bottom, $z_k$ values, and indicate how $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}$ is constant within each ply.

---

**(b) Numerical computation** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — On-axis reduced stiffnesses $Q_{ij}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_{21} &= 0.3\times\frac{10}{140} = 0.02143 \\
1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21} &= 0.99357 \\
Q_{11} &= \frac{140}{0.99357} = 140.92\,\text{GPa} \\
Q_{22} &= \frac{10}{0.99357} = 10.07\,\text{GPa} \\
Q_{12} &= \frac{0.3\times 10}{0.99357} = 3.020\,\text{GPa} \\
Q_{66} &= 5.0\,\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ for each ply orientation** (cross-ply → $\bar{Q}_{16}=\bar{Q}_{26}=0$):

| Ply Group | $\bar{Q}_{11}$ | $\bar{Q}_{22}$ | $\bar{Q}_{12}$ | $\bar{Q}_{66}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0^\circ$ plies (4) | $140.92$ | $10.07$ | $3.020$ | $5.0$ |
| $90^\circ$ plies (4) | $10.07$ | $140.92$ | $3.020$ | $5.0$ |

All values in GPa.

**Step 3 — $z_k$ coordinates** (top to bottom, total thickness $h = 1.0\,\text{mm}$):

| Ply $k$ | Orientation | $z_{k-1}$ (mm) | $z_k$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0^\circ$ | $+0.500$ | $+0.375$ |
| 2 | $90^\circ$ | $+0.375$ | $+0.250$ |
| 3 | $90^\circ$ | $+0.250$ | $+0.125$ |
| 4 | $0^\circ$ | $+0.125$ | $0.000$ |
| 5 | $0^\circ$ | $0.000$ | $-0.125$ |
| 6 | $90^\circ$ | $-0.125$ | $-0.250$ |
| 7 | $90^\circ$ | $-0.250$ | $-0.375$ |
| 8 | $0^\circ$ | $-0.375$ | $-0.500$ |

**Step 4 — Compute $[A]$:**

Each ply contributes $\bar{Q}_{ij}\times 0.125\,\text{mm}$. With 4 plies each at $0^\circ$ and $90^\circ$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{11} &= 4\times(140.92\times 0.125) + 4\times(10.07\times 0.125) = 70.46 + 5.035 \\
       &= 75.50\,\text{GPa·mm} = 75.5\times 10^{6}\,\text{N/m} \\
A_{22} &= 4\times(10.07\times 0.125) + 4\times(140.92\times 0.125) = 5.035 + 70.46 \\
       &= 75.50\,\text{GPa·mm} = 75.5\times 10^{6}\,\text{N/m} \\
A_{12} &= 8\times(3.020\times 0.125) = 3.020\,\text{GPa·mm} = 3.02\times 10^{6}\,\text{N/m} \\
A_{66} &= 8\times(5.0\times 0.125) = 5.0\,\text{GPa·mm} = 5.0\times 10^{6}\,\text{N/m} \\
A_{16} &= A_{26} = 0 \quad \text{(cross-ply: } \bar{Q}_{16}=\bar{Q}_{26}=0\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

So:

$$[A] = \begin{bmatrix} 75.5 & 3.02 & 0 \\ 3.02 & 75.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5.0 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^{6}\,\text{N/m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $[B]$:**

Because the lay-up is symmetric about $z=0$, every $+\bar{Q}$ at $+z_k$ is matched by the same $\bar{Q}$ at $-z_k$. Hence each term in $B_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum\bar{Q}_{ij}(z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$ cancels in pairs:

$$[B] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on in-plane isotropy:**

Since $A_{11} = A_{22} = 75.5\times 10^6\,\text{N/m}$, $A_{16}=A_{26}=0$, this **[0/90]s cross-ply laminate is orthotropic but not isotropic**. For true quasi-isotropy we would need additional $\pm 45^\circ$ plies, e.g., $[0/\pm45/90]_s$, which gives $A_{11}\approx A_{22}$ *and* a near-vanishing $A_{12}$ coupling. The present laminate, however, has *balanced* in-plane stiffness in $0^\circ$ and $90^\circ$ directions and zero bend-stretch coupling.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain, with neat sketches, the different types of laminate lay-up configurations used in polymer composites. Discuss the **stacking-sequence notation** with at least three examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various **coupling phenomena** in a laminate — namely *shear–extension*, *bending–extension*, and *bending–twist* coupling. Why is a **symmetric lay-up** strongly preferred for space applications? **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Lay-up configurations and stacking-sequence notation** *(7 marks)*

**Types of laminate lay-ups:**

1. **Unidirectional (UD) lay-up $[0]_N$:** All plies aligned along the reference axis $x$. Provides **maximum longitudinal stiffness** $E_x$ but very low transverse stiffness $E_y$. Used where load is purely along fibre direction, e.g., pressure vessels with helical winding.

2. **Cross-ply lay-up $[0/90]_s$:** Plies at $0^\circ$ and $90^\circ$ in alternating or symmetric order. Eliminates $\bar{Q}_{16}, \bar{Q}_{26}$, so $A_{16}=A_{26}=0$. Used in flat satellite panels requiring stiffness in two orthogonal directions.

3. **Angle-ply lay-up $[\pm\theta]$:** Equal numbers of $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies. **Shear-extension coupling** is present in $[A]$ but bending-extension is decoupled if symmetric. Typical for rocket motor casings (often $\pm 55^\circ$ for filament winding).

4. **Quasi-isotropic lay-up $[0/\pm45/90]_s$:** Combination giving near-isotropic in-plane response. **Preferred** for satellite structures, antenna reflectors, and aircraft skins where load direction is multi-axial.

5. **Symmetric lay-up $[\ldots]_s$:** Mirror image about the mid-plane. **[B] = 0** ⇒ no bending-extension coupling. Used to avoid warping under thermal loads.

6. **Antisymmetric lay-up $[0/90/\ldots]$:** No mirror symmetry. $[B]\neq 0$ ⇒ extension causes bending. Rarely used in primary space structures.

**Stacking-sequence notation — examples:**

| Code | Expansion | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $[0/45/90/-45]_s$ | $[0/45/90/-45/-45/90/45/0]$ | Symmetric quasi-iso, 8 plies |
| $[0_2/90_3]_s$ | $[0/0/90/90/90/90/90/0/0]$ | Symmetric, 9 plies, subscript = repetition |
| $[90/\pm45/0_2]$ | $[90/45/-45/0/0]$ | Non-symmetric, 5 plies |
| $[\pm 55]_{10}$ | 10 plies of $\pm 55^\circ$ alternating | Filament-wound pressure vessel |

**Sketch:** Show a cross-section of each lay-up, mark ply angles, indicate mid-plane for symmetric ones, and label top/bottom plies.

---

**(b) Coupling phenomena in laminates** *(7 marks)*

A general laminate exhibits three principal coupling mechanisms, visualised through the ABD matrix:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} &= 
\begin{bmatrix} \underbrace{A}_{\text{extension}} & \underbrace{B}_{\text{coupling}} \\ \underbrace{B}_{\text{coupling}} & \underbrace{D}_{\text{bending}} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

| Coupling | Mathematical Indicator | Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear–Extension** | $A_{16}, A_{26}\neq 0$ | In-plane shear load $\rightarrow$ extension; in-plane extension $\rightarrow$ shear strain. Plies stretched in $x$ also shear. |
| **Bending–Extension** | $B_{ij}\neq 0$ | In-plane force produces both mid-plane strain *and* curvature. The laminate **warps** (twists/curls) under axial load. |
| **Bending–Twist** | $D_{16}, D_{26}\neq 0$ | Bending moment $M_x$ produces twist $\kappa_{xy}$, and vice-versa. Distorts cross-section under out-of-plane loads. |

**When does each occur?**

- *Shear–extension coupling*: present whenever any ply is at an angle $\theta \notin \{0^\circ, 90^\circ\}$ and the laminate is **not balanced** (or in angle-plies).
- *Bending–extension coupling*: present whenever the laminate is **not symmetric** about its mid-plane.
- *Bending–twist coupling*: present whenever the laminate is **not symmetric and not balanced**.

**Why a symmetric lay-up is preferred for space applications:**

1. **Eliminates $B_{ij}$** ⇒ bending–extension coupling vanishes. The laminate's curvature is determined *only* by applied moments, not by in-plane forces. This simplifies analysis considerably.

2. **No thermal warping.** In space, laminates experience extreme thermal cycling (e.g., $-180^\circ\text{C}$ shadow to $+120^\circ\text{C}$ sunlight for LEO satellites). If $[B]\neq 0$, differential thermal expansion across plies induces **out-of-plane twist** — disastrous for antenna reflectors and solar arrays where flatness is critical.

3. **Predictable mechanical behaviour.** Symmetric laminates decouple the membrane (in-plane) and flexural (out-of-plane) responses, allowing independent sizing of $[A]$ for strength and $[D]$ for stiffness/buckling.

4. **Manufacturability.** Symmetric stacks are easier to lay up and inspect, reducing human error during fabrication of flight hardware.

5. **Standard practice** in space agencies (ISRO, NASA, ESA): nearly all primary composite space structures — *INSAT* bus panels, *PSLV* fairings, *Chandrayaan* components — use symmetric, balanced, quasi-isotropic CFRP lay-ups.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Lamina & Laminate Lay-up**

- ✅ **Lamina = single ply**, **Laminate = stack of plies**, **Lay-up = ply arrangement recipe**.
- ✅ On-axis reduced stiffnesses: $Q_{11} = E_1/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{22} = E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{12} = \nu_{12}E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{66} = G_{12}$.
- ✅ Use $\bar{Q}_{ij}(\theta)$ for any ply off the laminate $x$-axis; **never mix** on-axis $Q_{ij}$ with laminate coordinates.
- ✅ Units: $[A]\to$N/m, $[B]\to$N, $[D]\to$N·m.
- ✅ **Symmetric lay-up ⇒ $[B] = 0$** ⇒ no bending–extension coupling ⇒ preferred in space hardware.
- ✅ **Balanced lay-up ⇒ $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$** ⇒ no in-plane shear–extension coupling.
- ✅ **Cross-ply $[0/90]_s$** ⇒ orthotropic, $[B]=0$, $A_{16}=A_{26}=0$.
- ✅ **Quasi-iso $[0/\pm45/90]_s$** ⇒ in-plane near-isotropic; standard for satellite structures.
- ✅ **Angle-ply $[\pm\theta]$** ⇒ $A_{16},A_{26}\neq 0$ unless balanced and symmetric.
- ✅ CLT equation: $\begin{Bmatrix}N\\M\end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}A&B\\B&D\end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix}\varepsilon^0\\\kappa\end{Bmatrix}$.
- ✅ Stacking-sequence code: subscripts denote repetition, subscript $s$ denotes symmetry about mid-plane.
- ✅ Always tabulate $z_k$, $\theta_k$, $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}$ for every ply before integration — **mandatory for full marks**.
- ✅ For KTU, mention **space applications** in every answer — satellite panels, rocket motor casings, payload fairings, antenna reflectors, cryogenic tanks.
- ✅ Reciprocal relation: $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}E_2/E_1$ — **commonly missed**, costing marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ply Orientation Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ply Orientation Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ply Orientation (θ):** The angle, expressed in degrees, measured between the **fiber direction** (longitudinal axis, denoted as the **1-axis** or **L-direction**) of a single unidirectional composite ply/layer and a **global reference axis** (typically the **x-axis** of the laminate coordinate system) in the plane of the ply. It is conventionally represented by the Greek letter **θ (theta)** and is considered **positive when measured counter-clockwise** from the x-axis to the fiber direction.

In the context of **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs)** used in space structures (rocket motor casings, satellite panels, heat shields), individual unidirectional plies are stacked together to form a **laminate**. The mechanical behavior of such a laminate is governed not only by the material properties of each ply but also critically by **how each ply is oriented** with respect to the loading direction. This orientation angle θ is the single most influential design parameter in composite laminate design.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$\theta = \angle(x_{\text{global}} \rightarrow 1_{\text{ply}})$$

where $x_{\text{global}}$ is the laminate reference axis and $1_{\text{ply}}$ is the local fiber direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** Ply orientation forms the foundation for the classical **lamination theory (CLT)** and the **transformed reduced stiffness matrix $[\bar{Q}_{ij}]$**, both of which are examinable concepts in this module.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wooden Sticks in a Box" Intuition

Imagine you have a **flat rectangular box** (the laminate) and a pile of **identical thin wooden sticks** (the unidirectional plies, each one having strong fibers only along its length).

- If you lay the first stick horizontally (left-to-right) → the **ply orientation is 0°**.
- If you lay the second stick tilted upward to the right → the **ply orientation is +45°**.
- If you tilt a stick upward to the left → the **ply orientation is −45°** (or equivalently 135°).
- If the stick points straight up → the **ply orientation is 90°**.

Now, the **stiffness of the box under a horizontal pull** will be drastically different depending on how the sticks are angled! If most sticks run along the pull direction, the box is very stiff; if they run across it, the box is floppy. This is precisely why **ply orientation is the designer's tuning knob** in polymer composites for space applications.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Space Example:** The **Falcon 9** payload fairings and the **SpaceX Dragon** trunk structures use carbon-fiber/epoxy laminates with carefully chosen ply angles (±45°, 0°, 90°) to balance stiffness, strength, and thermal stability. Each ±45° ply contributes primarily to **shear stiffness** and **torsional rigidity**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Conventions in KTU/Industry Practice

| Symbol | Meaning | Convention |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ | Ply orientation angle | Measured from **x-axis** to **1-axis** (fiber) |
| 1-axis | Local longitudinal (fiber) | Direction of maximum stiffness $E_1$ |
| 2-axis | Local transverse | Perpendicular to fibers in the ply plane |
| x-axis | Global longitudinal | Reference axis of the laminate |
| y-axis | Global transverse | Perpendicular to x in the laminate plane |
| Positive θ | **Counter-clockwise** (CCW) | Standard math convention |
| Negative θ | **Clockwise** (CW) | Equivalent to $(180° - \theta)$ in some codes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants & Conventions Used:** Angle is in **degrees** in design practice (e.g., $\theta = \pm 45°$); the same value must be converted to **radians** when fed into trigonometric functions in any transformation equation: $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \times \pi / 180$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Ply Orientation Matters — The Underlying Physics

A single unidirectional ply is **transversely isotropic**: it is very stiff along the fibers ($E_1 \approx 130$–$150$ GPa for typical carbon/epoxy) but much weaker across them ($E_2 \approx 8$–$10$ GPa). When a laminate is loaded, the **global response** depends on how the stiff direction of each ply is aligned with the load vector.

**The fundamental design rule:**

> [!NOTE]
> A load applied at an angle $\theta$ to the fibers is decomposed into a **fiber-parallel component** (carried efficiently by the stiff fibers) and a **fiber-transverse component** (carried weakly by the matrix). The ratio of these two is $\tan(\theta)$, which directly drives the stress state inside the ply.

---

## 2.2 The Stiffness Transformation — How θ Enters the Equations

When a ply is rotated by angle $\theta$, its **principal material stiffnesses** ($Q_{11}, Q_{22}, Q_{12}, Q_{66}$) must be mathematically **rotated** into the **global x–y coordinate system** to obtain the **transformed reduced stiffnesses** $\bar{Q}_{ij}(\theta)$.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Identify the principal stiffnesses** of the unidirectional ply in its own (1,2) frame.
2. **Recognize that rotation by θ** mixes normal and shear stiffnesses — a 0° ply has no $x$–$y$ coupling, but a 45° ply does.
3. **Apply the transformation matrix $[T]$** (depending on $m = \cos\theta$ and $n = \sin\theta$) to convert $[Q]$ into $[\bar{Q}]$.
4. **Use $[\bar{Q}]$ in the laminate constitutive equation** $\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every high-yield equation** involving ply orientation θ that appears in KTU Module 3 questions. Memorize the trigonometric coefficients and their squares.

### 2.3.1 Trigonometric Coefficients of θ

| Coefficient | Definition | Square | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| $m$ | $\cos\theta$ | $m^2 = \cos^2\theta$ | $m^2 + n^2 = 1$ |
| $n$ | $\sin\theta$ | $n^2 = \sin^2\theta$ | Used in all stiffness transforms |
| $m^2$ | $\cos^2\theta$ | — | Coefficient of $Q_{11}$ in $\bar{Q}_{11}$ |
| $n^2$ | $\sin^2\theta$ | — | Coefficient of $Q_{22}$ in $\bar{Q}_{11}$ |
| $mn$ | $\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | $m^2n^2 = \sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta$ | Half of $\sin 2\theta$ |
| $\cos 2\theta$ | $m^2 - n^2$ | — | Used in off-diagonal terms |
| $\sin 2\theta$ | $2mn$ | — | Sign defines CCW (+) vs CW (−) |

### 2.3.2 Transformed Reduced Stiffness Matrix $[\bar{Q}_{ij}]$

| Term | Formula (in terms of $Q_{ij}$ and $\theta$) |
|---|---|
| $\bar{Q}_{11}$ | $Q_{11} m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2 Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{22} n^4$ |
| $\bar{Q}_{22}$ | $Q_{11} n^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2 Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{22} m^4$ |
| $\bar{Q}_{12}$ | $(Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4 Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{12}(m^4 + n^4)$ |
| $\bar{Q}_{66}$ | $(Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2 Q_{12} - 2 Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{66}(m^4 + n^4)$ |
| $\bar{Q}_{16}$ | $(Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2 Q_{66}) m^3 n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2 Q_{66}) m n^3$ |
| $\bar{Q}_{26}$ | $(Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2 Q_{66}) m n^3 + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2 Q_{66}) m^3 n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the bar notation:** The bar above $Q$ (i.e., $\bar{Q}_{ij}$) signifies that the stiffness has been **transformed from the material (1,2) coordinate system to the laminate (x,y) coordinate system** by rotating through angle $\theta$. This is the standard notation in **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)**.

### 2.3.3 Special-Case Ply Orientations (Memorize These!)

| θ | Common Name | Dominant Contribution | Typical Use in Space Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0°** | Warp / Longitudinal | Axial stiffness $E_x$ | Rocket motor casings, pressure vessels |
| **90°** | Fill / Transverse | Transverse stiffness $E_y$ | Hoop-direction reinforcement |
| **+45°** | Right shear ply | Shear stiffness $\bar{Q}_{66}$, torsion | Satellite panels, helicopter blades |
| **−45°** | Left shear ply | Shear stiffness, balances twist | Always paired with +45° to form ±45° |
| **0°/90°** | Cross-ply | Orthotropic, no coupling | Flat satellite panels |
| **0°/±45°/90°** | Quasi-isotropic | Isotropic-like in-plane response | Aircraft skins, space antennas |
| **[0°/90°/±45°]ₛ** | Symmetric quasi | Balanced, coupling-free | Most common aerospace layup |

### 2.3.4 Engineering Utility in Space Applications

- **0° plies** carry the **majority of axial loads** in launch vehicle structures.
- **±45° plies** provide **shear stiffness and torsional rigidity** — critical for satellite booms and solar-array panels subjected to deployment torques.
- **90° plies** resist **transverse loads and prevent splitting/cracking** between 0° plies.
- **Balanced–symmetric layups** (e.g., $[0/45/-45/90]_s$) **eliminate extension–shear coupling ($B_{ij} = 0$)**, preventing the laminate from twisting or warping under in-plane loads — a strict requirement for precision space structures like **reflector dishes and optical benches**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Principal Stiffness $[Q]$ to Transformed Stiffness $[\bar{Q}(\theta)]$

The following is the **exhaustive, step-by-step derivation** of how the ply orientation angle θ enters the stiffness transformation — a guaranteed high-yield KTU derivation question.

---

### Step 1 — Start with the Principal Material Stiffness Matrix $[Q]$

For a unidirectional ply in its own (1,2) coordinate system, the **plane-stress reduced stiffness matrix** is:

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where the principal stiffnesses are given by:

$$
Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \qquad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \qquad Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \qquad Q_{66} = G_{12}
$$

These are properties of the **material** and are **independent of θ**.

---

### Step 2 — Define the Rotation Matrix $[T]$ (Transformation of Stress/Strain)

The angle θ enters through the **Reuter matrix** (or transformation matrix), which relates strains in the (1,2) frame to strains in the (x,y) frame:

$$
[T] = \begin{bmatrix} m^2 & n^2 & 2mn \\ n^2 & m^2 & -2mn \\ -mn & mn & m^2 - n^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $m = \cos\theta$ and $n = \sin\theta$.

**Verification of unit determinant:** $\det([T]) = m^2(m^2 - n^2)(m^2) + \ldots = 1$ ✓ (rotation preserves volume in plane strain).

---

### Step 3 — Apply the Stiffness Transformation Rule

The general transformation rule for a 2nd-order tensor (like stiffness) under rotation is:

$$
[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-T} \, [Q] \, [T]^{-1}
$$

Because $[T]$ is an orthogonal rotation matrix, $[T]^{-1} = [T]^T$. For the Reuter matrix, the inverse takes a slightly different form. After carrying out the full matrix multiplication, we obtain the **transformed reduced stiffnesses** in the (x,y) frame as functions of θ.

---

### Step 4 — Expand $\bar{Q}_{11}$ Explicitly (Sample Detailed Derivation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{11} &= Q_{11}\,(m^2)^2 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})\,(m^2)(n^2) + Q_{22}\,(n^2)^2 \\[4pt]
&= Q_{11}\,m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{22}\,n^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 1 — Logic:** $Q_{11}$ is multiplied by $m^4$ because it is associated with $\sigma_1$ contributing to $\varepsilon_x$ in the rotated frame, and the double rotation gives $m^2 \times m^2 = m^4$.

**Row 2 — Logic:** $Q_{22}$ is multiplied by $n^4$ for the symmetric reason.

**Row 3 — Logic:** The cross term $2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})$ arises from both the normal-shear coupling $Q_{12}$ and the doubling of the shear contribution $Q_{66}$ due to the Reuter matrix's 2mn entries.

---

### Step 5 — Expand $\bar{Q}_{12}$ and $\bar{Q}_{66}$ (Coupling Terms)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{12} &= (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{12}\,(m^4 + n^4) \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{66} &= (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{66}\,(m^4 + n^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Both expressions share the term $(m^4 + n^4)$ because $m^4 + n^4 = 1 - 2m^2n^2$, a useful identity for simplifying these forms.

---

### Step 6 — Expand $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ (The Extension–Shear Coupling Terms)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{16} &= (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})\,m^3 n \;+\; (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})\,m n^3 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{26} &= (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})\,m n^3 \;+\; (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})\,m^3 n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** These terms are **odd functions of θ** (they change sign when θ → −θ), which is why they **vanish for θ = 0° or 90°** and are **maximized in magnitude for θ = ±45°**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check — Boundary Cases:**
> - If $\theta = 0°$ → $m = 1$, $n = 0$ → $\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11}$, $\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{22}$, $\bar{Q}_{12} = Q_{12}$, $\bar{Q}_{66} = Q_{66}$, $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$. ✓
> - If $\theta = 90°$ → $m = 0$, $n = 1$ → $\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{22}$, $\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11}$, $\bar{Q}_{12} = Q_{12}$, $\bar{Q}_{66} = Q_{66}$, $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$. ✓
> - If $\theta = 45°$ → $m^2 = n^2 = 0.5$, $mn = 0.5$ → all coupling terms $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ become **maximal**. ✓

---

### Step 3.2 — Numerical Worked Example (Highly Examinable)

**Given:** A T700-carbon/epoxy ply has the following principal reduced stiffnesses (in GPa):

$$
Q_{11} = 138.0, \quad Q_{22} = 9.50, \quad Q_{12} = 3.20, \quad Q_{66} = 4.80
$$

**Find:** $\bar{Q}_{11}$, $\bar{Q}_{12}$, $\bar{Q}_{66}$, and $\bar{Q}_{16}$ for a ply oriented at $\theta = 30°$.

**Step A — Compute trigonometric values:**

$$
m = \cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660, \quad n = \sin 30° = 0.5000
$$

$$
m^2 = 0.7500, \quad n^2 = 0.2500, \quad m^2 n^2 = 0.1875, \quad m^4 = 0.5625, \quad n^4 = 0.0625
$$

**Step B — Compute $\bar{Q}_{11}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{11} &= Q_{11}\,m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{22}\,n^4 \\[4pt]
&= 138.0 \times 0.5625 + 2(3.20 + 2 \times 4.80) \times 0.1875 + 9.50 \times 0.0625 \\[4pt]
&= 77.625 + 2(12.80)(0.1875) + 0.59375 \\[4pt]
&= 77.625 + 4.800 + 0.59375 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{11} &\approx 83.02 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute $\bar{Q}_{12}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{12} &= (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{12}\,(m^4 + n^4) \\[4pt]
&= (138.0 + 9.50 - 4 \times 4.80)(0.1875) + 3.20\,(0.5625 + 0.0625) \\[4pt]
&= (128.30)(0.1875) + 3.20(0.6250) \\[4pt]
&= 24.056 + 2.000 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{12} &\approx 26.06 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Compute $\bar{Q}_{66}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{66} &= (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{66}\,(m^4 + n^4) \\[4pt]
&= (138.0 + 9.50 - 2(3.20) - 2(4.80))(0.1875) + 4.80(0.6250) \\[4pt]
&= (131.50)(0.1875) + 3.000 \\[4pt]
&= 24.656 + 3.000 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{66} &\approx 27.66 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Compute $\bar{Q}_{16}$ (extension–shear coupling):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{16} &= (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})\,m^3 n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})\,m n^3 \\[4pt]
&= (138.0 - 3.20 - 9.60)(0.6495)(0.5000) + (3.20 - 9.50 + 9.60)(0.8660)(0.1250) \\[4pt]
&= (125.20)(0.3248) + (3.30)(0.1083) \\[4pt]
&= 40.665 + 0.357 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{16} &\approx 41.02 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of the Result:** The original ply had $\bar{Q}_{16} = 0$ (no coupling at θ = 0°). Rotating the ply by 30° **induces a strong extension–shear coupling** of ~41 GPa. This means a **normal load along x will now generate shear strain**, and the ply will **try to twist or skew** — a phenomenon called **"shear coupling"** that is critical to manage in space structures.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Logical Flow of Ply Orientation Analysis

The following block diagram maps the **functional flow** of how ply orientation θ is defined, transformed, and used in laminate analysis — from material properties to final structural response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Unidirectional PMC Ply] --> stepB[Identify Principal Stiffnesses Q11 Q22 Q12 Q66]
    stepB --> stepC[Choose Ply Orientation Angle Theta]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute Trig Coefficients m cos Theta and n sin Theta]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply Stiffness Transformation Rule]
    stepE --> stepF[Compute Transformed Stiffness Matrix Qbar Theta]
    stepF --> stepG{Is Layup Symmetric and Balanced?}
    stepG -->|Yes| stepH[Coupling Matrix B equals Zero]
    stepG -->|No| stepI[Coupling Exists, Shear and Twist Induced]
    stepH --> stepJ[Assemble Extensional Matrix A]
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK[Compute Global Laminate Response]
    stepK --> stepL[Predict Space Structure Performance]
    stepL --> stepM[End: Design Verification for Space Hardware]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Follow the arrows top-to-bottom. The two diamond-shaped decision nodes (`stepG`) are the **branching points** where the designer must choose a layup philosophy. **Symmetric–balanced** layups eliminate unwanted coupling; **unbalanced** layups exploit coupling for tailored performance (e.g., aeroelastic tailoring of helicopter rotor blades and satellite solar arrays).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Common Layup Architectures for Space Applications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Quasi Isotropic 0 plus or minus 45 and 90] --> nodeB[In Plane Isotropic Response]
    nodeC[Cross Ply 0 and 90] --> nodeD[Orthotropic No Coupling]
    nodeE[Angle Ply plus or minus Theta] --> nodeF[Strong Shear Coupling]
    nodeG[Unidirectional 0 only] --> nodeH[Maximum Axial Stiffness]
    nodeI[Symmetric Layup with subscript s] --> nodeJ[No Bending Stretching Coupling]
    nodeK[Antisymmetric Layup] --> nodeL[Bending Stretching Coupling Utilized]
    nodeB --> nodeM[Used in Satellite Skins and Aircraft Fuselages]
    nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeF --> nodeN[Used in Torsion Dominated Space Booms]
    nodeH --> nodeO[Used in Rocket Motor Casings and Pressure Vessels]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway from the diagram:** The **quasi-isotropic [0/±45/90]ₛ** stack is the **default "go-to" layup** in most aerospace and space applications because it gives equal in-plane stiffness in all directions — a property known as **in-plane isotropy**. However, when a specific loading direction dominates (e.g., axial thrust in a rocket), designers will deliberately bias the layup toward more 0° plies.

---

## 4.3 Schematic Representation of the Angle Convention

The following describes the standard **θ = +45°** configuration in text form (since physical drawings are rendered through Mermaid blocks above):

> **Ply Orientation Schematic (θ = +45°):**
>
> - **Global x-axis:** horizontal, pointing right.
> - **Global y-axis:** vertical, pointing up.
> - **Local 1-axis (fiber direction):** tilted **+45° counter-clockwise** from x.
> - **Local 2-axis (transverse):** perpendicular to 1, tilted **−45°** from x.
> - **Convention:** The angle is positive because the rotation from x to 1 is counter-clockwise.
>
> **Equivalent angles** that produce the **same physical orientation** (modulo 180° due to fiber direction being unidirectional along its axis): $\theta = +45° \equiv 45° + 180° = 225°$, etc.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions (KTU-style):** *Answer any **TWO** questions. Each carries **3 marks**. Maximum word limit: 80–100 words.*

---

### Q1. Define ply orientation in a polymer composite laminate. State the standard sign convention used. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer:**

Ply orientation is the angle $\theta$ between the **fiber direction (1-axis)** of a single unidirectional composite ply and the **global reference axis (x-axis)** of the laminate, measured in the plane of the ply.

**Sign convention:** $\theta$ is **positive when measured counter-clockwise (CCW)** from the x-axis to the fiber direction (1-axis), and **negative when measured clockwise (CW)**.

**Standard notation examples:** A ply with fibers along x is denoted as a **0° ply**; fibers at 45° tilted upward to the right is a **+45° ply**; fibers at 45° tilted upward to the left is a **−45° ply**. Equivalent angles: $\theta$ and $\theta + 180°$ represent the same physical ply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (3-mark question):** Always state **both** the geometric definition **and** the sign convention. A definition without the convention is incomplete and loses 1 mark.

---

### Q2. What is the physical significance of the ±45° plies in a laminate used for space applications? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer:**

The **±45° plies** in a laminate provide the **majority of the in-plane shear stiffness** ($\bar{Q}_{66}$) and the **torsional rigidity** of the structure. They also exhibit **extension–shear coupling** ($\bar{Q}_{16}, \bar{Q}_{26} \neq 0$) when the angles are not perfectly balanced, which can be exploited for twist control.

In space hardware (satellite solar panels, deployable booms, payload fairings), ±45° plies are **essential for resisting shear loads** generated during launch vibrations, in-orbit maneuvering, and thermal gradients. A balanced ±45° pair provides **pure shear response without twist coupling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (3-mark question):** Use the phrase **"in-plane shear stiffness"** explicitly. Avoid vague terms like "they make the structure stronger" — examiners deduct marks for imprecise language.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU-style):** *Answer any **ONE** full question. Each carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts.*

---

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> Explain the **physical and mathematical significance of ply orientation** in polymer composites used for space applications. With the help of neat diagrams, describe the standard angle convention and the meaning of the **transformed reduced stiffness matrix** $[\bar{Q}_{ij}]$.

**Model Solution:**

**Physical Significance:**
Ply orientation θ controls **how the load is distributed among the fibers and the matrix** in a composite laminate. Since a unidirectional ply is highly anisotropic (stiff along fibers, weak across them), the orientation of each ply relative to the load direction directly determines the **stress state, stiffness, strength, and failure mode** of that ply. In space applications, where structures must withstand **multi-axial loads (axial thrust + bending + torsion + thermal)**, the designer chooses the orientation of each ply to tailor the laminate response.

**Angle Convention:**
The angle θ is measured from the **global x-axis** (laminate reference) to the **local 1-axis** (fiber direction), positive in the **counter-clockwise** sense. The trig shorthand is $m = \cos\theta$, $n = \sin\theta$.

**Mathematical Significance — $[\bar{Q}_{ij}]$:**
The principal stiffnesses $Q_{ij}$ of the ply are defined in the (1,2) frame. When the ply is rotated by θ, the stiffnesses must be mathematically rotated into the (x,y) frame to obtain $\bar{Q}_{ij}$. The transformation equations (derived in Section 3 of these notes) are:
- $\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11}m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{22}n^4$
- $\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11}n^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{22}m^4$
- $\bar{Q}_{12} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{12}(m^4 + n^4)$
- $\bar{Q}_{66} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{66}(m^4 + n^4)$
- $\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^3n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})mn^3$
- $\bar{Q}_{26} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})mn^3 + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})m^3n$

**Key Insight:** The $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ terms are **odd functions of θ** and represent **extension–shear coupling** — they vanish for 0° and 90° plies and are maximal at ±45°.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> A unidirectional carbon/epoxy ply has the following properties: $E_1 = 138$ GPa, $E_2 = 9.5$ GPa, $G_{12} = 4.8$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$. Compute the **transformed reduced stiffnesses** $\bar{Q}_{11}$, $\bar{Q}_{12}$, $\bar{Q}_{66}$, and $\bar{Q}_{16}$ for a ply oriented at $\theta = 60°$. Comment on the physical meaning of $\bar{Q}_{16}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute principal stiffnesses $Q_{ij}$:**

$$
Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{138}{1 - (0.30)(0.0938)} = \frac{138}{0.9719} \approx 142.0 \;\text{GPa}
$$

where $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}(E_2/E_1) = 0.30 \times 9.5/138 = 0.02065$.

(Note: Using the standard simplification $\nu_{12}\nu_{21} \approx 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 9.5/138 \approx 0.0062$, we get $Q_{11} \approx 138.9$ GPa. Either form is acceptable in KTU evaluation; the **commonly used approximation** gives the values below.)

For KTU exam purposes, the **directly given stiffnesses** are often:

$$
Q_{11} \approx 138.0 \;\text{GPa}, \quad Q_{22} \approx 9.5 \;\text{GPa}, \quad Q_{12} \approx 3.0 \;\text{GPa}, \quad Q_{66} = 4.8 \;\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Trigonometric values for θ = 60°:**

$$
m = \cos 60° = 0.5000, \quad n = \sin 60° = 0.8660
$$

$$
m^2 = 0.2500, \quad n^2 = 0.7500, \quad m^2n^2 = 0.1875, \quad m^4 = 0.0625, \quad n^4 = 0.5625
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\bar{Q}_{11}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{11} &= Q_{11}\,m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{22}\,n^4 \\[4pt]
&= 138.0(0.0625) + 2(3.0 + 9.6)(0.1875) + 9.5(0.5625) \\[4pt]
&= 8.625 + 2(12.6)(0.1875) + 5.344 \\[4pt]
&= 8.625 + 4.725 + 5.344 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{11} &\approx 18.69 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\bar{Q}_{12}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{12} &= (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{12}\,(m^4 + n^4) \\[4pt]
&= (138.0 + 9.5 - 4 \times 4.8)(0.1875) + 3.0(0.0625 + 0.5625) \\[4pt]
&= (128.3)(0.1875) + 3.0(0.625) \\[4pt]
&= 24.056 + 1.875 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{12} &\approx 25.93 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\bar{Q}_{66}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{66} &= (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})\,m^2 n^2 + Q_{66}\,(m^4 + n^4) \\[4pt]
&= (138.0 + 9.5 - 6.0 - 9.6)(0.1875) + 4.8(0.625) \\[4pt]
&= (131.9)(0.1875) + 3.0 \\[4pt]
&= 24.731 + 3.0 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{66} &\approx 27.73 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\bar{Q}_{16}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{16} &= (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})\,m^3 n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})\,m n^3 \\[4pt]
&= (138.0 - 3.0 - 9.6)(0.1250)(0.8660) + (3.0 - 9.5 + 9.6)(0.5000)(0.6495) \\[4pt]
&= (125.4)(0.1083) + (3.1)(0.3248) \\[4pt]
&= 13.58 + 1.007 \\[4pt]
\bar{Q}_{16} &\approx 14.59 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Physical meaning of $\bar{Q}_{16}$:**

The term $\bar{Q}_{16} \neq 0$ at θ = 60° indicates **extension–shear coupling** — applying a normal stress $\sigma_x$ will produce both normal strain $\varepsilon_x$ **and shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$**. Physically, the laminate will tend to **skew or twist** under axial load. This coupling is **zero for 0° and 90° plies** and **maximum near ±45°**. In space applications, such coupling is usually undesirable and is eliminated by using **balanced–symmetric layups**.

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> With a clear sketch, explain the **difference between a cross-ply, an angle-ply, and a quasi-isotropic laminate**. For each, give **one space-application example** and state whether the laminate exhibits extension–shear coupling.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Cross-Ply Laminate** — Contains **only 0° and 90° plies** in any stacking order.
- **Example:** $[0/90/0/90]$
- **Application:** Flat satellite panels, circuit-board substrates.
- **Coupling:** $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$ for each ply → **no extension–shear coupling at the ply level**. The laminate is orthotropic.

**2. Angle-Ply Laminate** — Contains **plies at ±θ** (other than 0°/90°).
- **Example:** $[+45/-45/+45/-45]$
- **Application:** Torsion-dominated space booms, helicopter rotor blades.
- **Coupling:** Each ±θ ply has $\bar{Q}_{16} \neq 0$, but in a **balanced** angle-ply (equal +θ and −θ), the **laminate-level coupling cancels** → $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$, but $B_{ij}$ may be nonzero unless symmetric.

**3. Quasi-Isotropic Laminate** — Contains plies at **0°, +45°, −45°, 90°** in equal proportion.
- **Example:** $[0/+45/-45/90]_s$ (symmetric quasi-isotropic)
- **Application:** Aircraft wings, satellite skins, payload fairings.
- **Coupling:** In a **symmetric, balanced** quasi-isotropic layup, **all extension–shear and extension–bending coupling terms vanish** ($A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ and $B_{ij} = 0$). The laminate behaves as if it were **isotropic in the plane**, meaning its in-plane stiffness is the same in all directions.

**Sketch (textual):**

```
Cross-ply:    |  /  |   /  |  /  |  (all 0 or 90 deg)
Angle-ply:    \  /  \  /  \  /  \  / (alternating plus/minus theta)
Quasi-iso:    ---  \  /  ---  |  (0, +45, -45, 90 in equal fractions)
```

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> A 4-ply laminate has the stacking sequence $[0°/45°/-45°/90°]$ with each ply of thickness $0.125$ mm and equal material. Using the principal stiffnesses $Q_{11} = 140$ GPa, $Q_{22} = 10$ GPa, $Q_{12} = 3.5$ GPa, $Q_{66} = 5.0$ GPa, compute the **extensional stiffness matrix entry $A_{11}$**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of $A_{ij}$:**

$$
A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \, t_k
$$

For a laminate where **all plies have equal thickness** $t$:

$$
A_{ij} = t \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar{Q}_{11}$ for each of the four plies:**

**Ply 1 (θ = 0°):** $m = 1$, $n = 0$, so $m^4 = 1$, $n^4 = 0$, $m^2n^2 = 0$.

$$
\bar{Q}_{11}^{(1)} = 140(1) + 2(3.5 + 10)(0) + 10(0) = 140.0 \;\text{GPa}
$$

**Ply 2 (θ = 45°):** $m^2 = n^2 = 0.5$, $m^2n^2 = 0.25$, $m^4 = n^4 = 0.25$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{11}^{(2)} &= 140(0.25) + 2(3.5 + 10)(0.25) + 10(0.25) \\
&= 35.0 + 2(13.5)(0.25) + 2.5 \\
&= 35.0 + 6.75 + 2.5 \\
\bar{Q}_{11}^{(2)} &= 44.25 \;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ply 3 (θ = −45°):** Since $\bar{Q}_{11}$ depends only on $m^2, n^2, m^2n^2$ (all **even functions of θ**), it has the **same value** as for +45°:

$$
\bar{Q}_{11}^{(3)} = 44.25 \;\text{GPa}
$$

**Ply 4 (θ = 90°):** $m = 0$, $n = 1$, so $m^4 = 0$, $n^4 = 1$, $m^2n^2 = 0$.

$$
\bar{Q}_{11}^{(4)} = 140(0) + 2(3.5 + 10)(0) + 10(1) = 10.0 \;\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the four $\bar{Q}_{11}$ values:**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{4} \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)} = 140.0 + 44.25 + 44.25 + 10.0 = 238.5 \;\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $A_{11}$:**

$$
A_{11} = t \times \sum \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)} = 0.125 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m} \times 238.5 \times 10^9 \;\text{Pa}
$$

$$
A_{11} = 0.125 \times 10^{-3} \times 238.5 \times 10^{9} = 29.8125 \times 10^{6} \;\text{N/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{11} \approx 29.81 \;\text{MN/m}}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** $A_{11}$ is the **in-plane extensional stiffness per unit width** in the x-direction. The 0° ply contributes the most (140 GPa × t), while the 90° ply contributes the least (10 GPa × t). The ±45° plies contribute moderately (44.25 GPa × t). This laminate is **quasi-isotropic in nature** and would give a similar $A_{22}$ value, confirming balanced in-plane behavior.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $A_{ij}$ has units of **N/m** (force per unit width), NOT GPa. Students who write "$A_{11} = 238.5$ GPa·mm" without unit conversion lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up ±45° values:** The ±45° plies have **the same $\bar{Q}_{11}$** but **opposite $\bar{Q}_{16}$ signs**. Forgetting the sign flip in $\bar{Q}_{16}$ leads to an incorrect $A_{16}$ calculation.
> 3. **Skipping the $\nu_{12}\nu_{21}$ step:** Some students compute $Q_{11}$ from raw $E_1$ directly without accounting for Poisson's ratio. Always show the formula $Q_{11} = E_1/(1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ if the data is given in engineering constants.
> 4. **Not stating the symmetry condition:** If the question says "symmetric laminate", remember that $B_{ij} = 0$ automatically — don't waste time computing $B_{ij}$.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary for last-minute revision:**

- **Ply orientation (θ):** Angle from the **global x-axis** to the **local fiber direction (1-axis)**, positive **counter-clockwise**.
- **Trig shorthand:** $m = \cos\theta$, $n = \sin\theta$; memorize $m^2 + n^2 = 1$ and $m^4 + n^4 = 1 - 2m^2n^2$.
- **Transformed stiffness $\bar{Q}_{ij}(\theta)$:** All stiffnesses of the rotated ply in the (x,y) frame — **derived via the Reuter matrix** $[T]$.
- **Even terms in θ:** $\bar{Q}_{11}, \bar{Q}_{22}, \bar{Q}_{12}, \bar{Q}_{66}$ — depend only on $m^2, n^2, m^2n^2$ and are **unchanged when θ → −θ**.
- **Odd terms in θ:** $\bar{Q}_{16}, \bar{Q}_{26}$ — change sign when θ → −θ; **zero at 0° and 90°**, **maximum at ±45°**.
- **Key special cases:** $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \bar{Q}_{ij} = Q_{ij}$ (with $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$); $\theta = 90° \Rightarrow$ swap 1↔2 indices.
- **Three canonical layups:** **Cross-ply** (0/90 — orthotropic), **Angle-ply** (±θ — shear-rich), **Quasi-isotropic** (0/±45/90 — isotropic in-plane).
- **Symmetric layup:** Mirror-image about the mid-plane; eliminates $B_{ij}$ (no bending–stretching coupling).
- **Balanced layup:** Equal numbers of +θ and −θ plies; eliminates $A_{16}, A_{26}$ (no in-plane shear–normal coupling).
- **Quasi-isotropic rule of thumb:** $A_{11} = A_{22}$ and $A_{66} = (A_{11} - A_{12})/2$ when [0/±45/90]ₛ is used with equal ply fractions.
- **Space applications:** 0° plies → axial loads (rocket casings); ±45° plies → torsion and shear (satellite booms); 90° plies → transverse loads and crack arrest.
- **Most important equation to memorize:** $\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11}m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{22}n^4$.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never confuse **ply angle** with **stacking sequence** — a 45° ply at the bottom of the stack is still 45°, regardless of position.
- **Sign discipline:** Always carry the sign of $\sin 2\theta = 2mn$ explicitly through the $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strength of Laminates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strength of Laminates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) laminates** used in space structures, **Strength of Laminates** is defined as the maximum load-carrying capacity of a multi-ply composite laminate before the onset of any irreversible mechanical degradation, quantified through a hierarchy of failure criteria evaluated at the **constituent ply (micromechanics)** and **laminate (macromechanics)** scales.

For KTU 2024 Scheme assessment purposes, laminate strength is **not a single number** but a **vector of failure envelopes** governed by:

- **Intralaminar strength** — failure *within* a ply (fiber breakage, matrix cracking).
- **Interlaminar strength** — failure *between* plies (delamination).
- **First-Ply Failure (FPF)** — the load level at which the *most critical* ply first violates an allowable.
- **Last-Ply Failure (LPF) / Ultimate Failure** — collapse of the *final* load-bearing ply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The strength of a laminate is **NOT** the average of the strengths of individual plies. It depends on stacking sequence, ply orientation, and the *order* in which plies fail. This directional and sequential nature is the most-tested concept in Module 3.

### 1.2 The Governing Equation Form

The fundamental relationship assessed in KTU papers is:

$$\sigma_x^{global} = \frac{P}{A}$$

where the global laminate stress is resolved into **on-axis ply stresses** $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12}$ via the **transformed-reduced stiffness matrix** $[\bar{Q}_{ij}]$, which are then compared against **strength allowables** $X_t, X_c, Y_t, Y_c, S$ through a **failure criterion** $f(\sigma_{ij}, X_k) \le 1$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Plywood Sheet vs. the Oak Log:**
> Imagine a single oak log. It is incredibly strong along its grain (fiber direction) but splits easily across it. Now glue many thin oak veneers together in **crossed orientations** (0°/90°/45°...). The resulting **plywood** is strong in *every* direction in the plane.
>
> However, if you glue them in the *wrong order* (e.g., all 0° plies stacked on one side), the board will **warp and twist** the moment humidity changes — a phenomenon called **spring-back** or **warping** caused by **mismatch in hygrothermal expansion**. This is why *stacking sequence* matters as much as the *material* itself.
>
> **Laminate strength = (Material Strength) × (Stacking Geometry) × (Failure Criterion)**

The space-grade analogue: a **CFRP honeycomb sandwich panel** on a satellite antenna must maintain dimensional stability across $-180^{\circ}\text{C}$ (lunar shadow) to $+120^{\circ}\text{C}$ (solar flux). A single misoriented ply can cause catastrophic microcracking in orbit.

### 1.4 Key Terms & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Space-Grade Value (CFRP/IM7-8552) |
|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal tensile strength | $X_t$ | **2,800 MPa** |
| Longitudinal compressive strength | $X_c$ | **1,500 MPa** |
| Transverse tensile strength | $Y_t$ | **60 MPa** |
| Transverse compressive strength | $Y_c$ | **250 MPa** |
| In-plane shear strength | $S$ | **90 MPa** |
| Interlaminar shear strength (ILSS) | $\tau_{ILS}$ | **90–110 MPa** |
| Standard safety factor (NASA-STD-5001) | $SF$ | **1.4 (ultimate), 2.0 (yield)** |
| Margin of safety | $MoS$ | $\ge 0.0$ for flight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **transverse strength** $Y_t$ is typically **40–50× smaller** than $X_t$ for unidirectional CFRP. This is why off-axis plies (and their failure) dominate laminate strength analysis — a frequent KTU question stem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer Hierarchy of Laminate Failure

KTU 2024 questions test you across three analytical levels. Master this hierarchy before touching a calculator:

**Level 1 — Ply Stress Recovery (Global → Local)**
- The laminate is loaded globally ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ or $N_x, N_y, N_{xy}$).
- Through the **inverse of the [A] matrix** we obtain mid-plane strains $\{\varepsilon^0\}$ and curvatures $\{\kappa\}$.
- Ply stresses $\{\sigma\}_k$ at any ply $k$ are computed by applying $[\bar{Q}]_k$ (rotated reduced stiffness).

**Level 2 — Failure Criterion Evaluation (Per-Ply)**
- Plug $\{\sigma\}_k$ into one of the four classical criteria: **Max Stress, Max Strain, Tsai-Hill, or Tsai-Wu**.
- The criterion returns a scalar **Failure Index (FI)** or **Reserve Factor (RF)**.
- $\text{FI} \le 1 \Rightarrow$ Ply is safe; $\text{FI} > 1 \Rightarrow$ Ply has failed.

**Level 3 — Laminate-Level Judgment (FPF & LPF)**
- **First-Ply failure (FPF):** smallest load multiplier that causes $FI = 1$ in *any* ply.
- **Last-Ply failure (LPF):** largest load multiplier; requires progressive ply degradation modeling.

### 2.2 The Four Classical Failure Criteria — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four criteria verbatim.** KTU 2024 ESE questions (Part B, 14 marks) frequently ask: *"Derive the Tsai-Wu criterion and discuss its advantages over the Tsai-Hill criterion"* or *"Apply the Maximum Strain criterion to a [0/±45/90]s laminate."*

**Criterion 1 — Maximum Stress (Independent, Linear)**
$$\text{Fail if any of:} \quad -X_c < \sigma_1 < X_t, \quad -Y_c < \sigma_2 < Y_t, \quad \vert\tau_{12}\vert < S$$

**Criterion 2 — Maximum Strain (Independent, Linear)**
$$\text{Fail if any of:} \quad -\varepsilon_{1c} < \varepsilon_1 < \varepsilon_{1t}, \quad -\varepsilon_{2c} < \varepsilon_2 < \varepsilon_{2t}, \quad \vert\gamma_{12}\vert < \gamma_{12}^{ult}$$

where strains are recovered from $[\bar{Q}]^{-1}\{\sigma\}$.

**Criterion 3 — Tsai-Hill (Interactive, Quadratic — *No Shear–Normal Coupling*)**
$$\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X}\right)^2 - \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X^2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 \le 1$$

with strength ratios chosen appropriately: $X = X_t$ if $\sigma_1 > 0$, else $X_c$; $Y = Y_t$ if $\sigma_2 > 0$, else $Y_c$.

**Criterion 4 — Tsai-Wu (Interactive, Quadratic — *WITH Shear–Normal Coupling*, most general)**
$$F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 \le 1$$

with the strength coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{1}{X_t} - \frac{1}{X_c} \\
F_2 &= \frac{1}{Y_t} - \frac{1}{Y_c} \\
F_{11} &= \frac{1}{X_t X_c} \\
F_{22} &= \frac{1}{Y_t Y_c} \\
F_{66} &= \frac{1}{S^2} \\
F_{12} &= -\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11}F_{22}} \quad \text{(commonly used, but experimentally calibrated in practice)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Tsai-Wu** criterion collapses to **Tsai-Hill** when $F_1 = F_2 = F_{12} = 0$ (i.e., equal strength in tension and compression, and no interaction term). This is a frequently-asked *differentiator* in KTU viva-voce and 14-mark derivation questions.

### 2.3 KTU Master Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\{N\} = [A]\{\varepsilon^0\} + [B]\{\kappa\}$ | Force–strain (CLT) | N/m, N |
| 2 | $\{M\} = [B]\{\varepsilon^0\} + [D]\{\kappa\}$ | Moment–curvature | N·m/m, N·m |
| 3 | $\{\sigma\}_k = [\bar{Q}]_k (\{\varepsilon^0\} + z_k\{\kappa\})$ | Ply stress recovery | MPa |
| 4 | $RF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\text{FI}}}$ (Tsai-Hill form) | Reserve factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $N_x^{FPF} = \dfrac{N_x^{applied}}{RF_{min}}$ | First-ply failure load | N/m |
| 6 | $\sigma_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3P}{4bh}$ (Short-Beam Shear) | Interlaminar shear (ASTM D2344) | MPa |
| 7 | $MoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_{applied} \cdot SF} - 1$ | NASA Margin of Safety | dimensionless |

> **Caution on Table Notation:** I have deliberately replaced all absolute-value bars with the LaTeX macro `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser stable — an examiner's-eye view of clean presentation.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Space Engineering

- **Payload mass budgets:** Every kg of *unnecessary* structural margin costs $\approx \$ 20{,}000$ per kg to launch. Designers squeeze $MoS$ toward 0.0 — this demands **accurate FPF prediction**, not crude averaging.
- **Thermal cycling:** Parked-in-shadow temperature $-180\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to sunlit $+120\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ produces **thermal residual stresses** $[\sigma^{th}]_k = [\bar{Q}]_k \{\alpha\}_k \Delta T$ which *pre-load* plies before launch load is even applied.
- **Micrometeoroid / orbital debris (MMOD):** Hypervelocity impact is modeled as a **compressive interlaminar shock** — ILSS, not intralaminar strength, governs survivability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Tsai-Wu Failure Criterion

The Tsai-Wu criterion is a **second-order tensor polynomial** in stress space. Its derivation begins with the **scalar failure function** $f(\sigma_{ij}) = k$, expanded as a Taylor series about the stress-free state (assumed safe), truncated at second order. The following shows the complete KTU-board-style derivation.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Scalar Failure Function**

Define a scalar function $f$ such that $f < 1$ (safe), $f = 1$ (failure surface), $f > 1$ (failed). For a plane-stress ply, the independent stress components are $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12}$.

**Step 2 — Taylor Series Expansion about the Safe Origin**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12}) &= a_0 + a_1 \sigma_1 + a_2 \sigma_2 + a_6 \tau_{12} \\
&\quad + a_{11}\sigma_1^2 + a_{22}\sigma_2^2 + a_{66}\tau_{12}^2 \\
&\quad + a_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 + a_{16}\sigma_1\tau_{12} + a_{26}\sigma_2\tau_{12} + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Physical Symmetry**

Because the lamina is **orthotropic** (two planes of material symmetry), pure shear $\pm\tau_{12}$ must produce identical $f$ (failure is independent of shear sign). This forces:

$$a_6 = 0, \quad a_{16} = 0, \quad a_{26} = 0$$

Furthermore, the **origin is safe** (no load → no failure):

$$f(0,0,0) = 0 \implies a_0 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Reduced Polynomial**

The function simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12}) =&\ a_1 \sigma_1 + a_2 \sigma_2 \\
&+ a_{11}\sigma_1^2 + a_{22}\sigma_2^2 + a_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2a_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor of 2 on the interaction term is conventional; alternative conventions absorb it into $a_{12}$.

**Step 5 — Identify Coefficients from Uniaxial Strength Tests**

**Test A — Uniaxial tension along fiber ($\sigma_2 = \tau_{12} = 0$, $\sigma_1 = X_t$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= a_1 X_t + a_{11} X_t^2 \\
\therefore\ 1 &= X_t(a_1 + a_{11} X_t) \tag{3.5a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Test B — Uniaxial compression along fiber ($\sigma_1 = -X_c$, others zero):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= -a_1 X_c + a_{11} X_c^2 \\
\therefore\ 1 &= X_c(-a_1 + a_{11} X_c) \tag{3.5b}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving (3.5a) and (3.5b) simultaneously:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{11} &= \frac{1}{X_t X_c} \\
a_1 &= \frac{1}{X_t} - \frac{1}{X_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Test C — Uniaxial transverse tension ($\sigma_2 = Y_t$):**

$$a_{22} = \frac{1}{Y_t Y_c}, \quad a_2 = \frac{1}{Y_t} - \frac{1}{Y_c}$$

**Test D — Pure in-plane shear ($\tau_{12} = S$):**

$$a_{66} = \frac{1}{S^2}$$

**Step 6 — The Interaction Coefficient $a_{12}$**

The $a_{12}$ term **cannot** be obtained from a single uniaxial test. It must be determined from a **biaxial stress experiment** (e.g., $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$, with $\tau_{12}=0$):

$$1 = (a_1 + a_2)\sigma + (a_{11} + a_{22} + 2a_{12})\sigma^2$$

A widely used engineering approximation (and KTU-acceptable default) is the **Tsai-Pagano approximation**:

$$a_{12} = -\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{a_{11} a_{22}} = -\frac{1}{2\sqrt{X_t X_c Y_t Y_c}}$$

**Step 7 — Final Tsai-Wu Equation in Standard Form**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\left(\frac{1}{X_t} - \frac{1}{X_c}\right)\sigma_1 + \left(\frac{1}{Y_t} - \frac{1}{Y_c}\right)\sigma_2 \\
&+ \frac{\sigma_1^2}{X_t X_c} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{Y_t Y_c} + \frac{\tau_{12}^2}{S^2} + 2 a_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \; \le \; 1
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability constraint (for the failure surface to be a closed ellipsoid):**
> $$a_{11}a_{22} - a_{12}^2 > 0$$
> A KTU examiner *will* award a half-mark for noting this; it is the mathematical reason the Tsai-Pagano approximation uses a minus sign and a square root.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Module 3, KTU Pattern):** A $[0/45/-45/90]_s$ graphite-epoxy laminate (IM7/8552) is subjected to a uniaxial in-plane load $N_x = 1000$ N/mm, with $N_y = N_{xy} = 0$. The laminate is symmetric and balanced, so $\{B\} = \{0\}$ and $[\kappa] = \{0\}$. Determine the **First-Ply Failure** load using the **Tsai-Wu** criterion. Material data:
> $X_t = 2800$ MPa, $X_c = 1500$ MPa, $Y_t = 60$ MPa, $Y_c = 250$ MPa, $S = 90$ MPa. The reduced stiffnesses $Q_{11}=145\,\text{GPa}$, $Q_{22}=10\,\text{GPa}$, $Q_{12}=3.5\,\text{GPa}$, $Q_{66}=5\,\text{GPa}$.

**Step 1 — Ply-Level Stiffnesses in Global Coordinates**

For each ply angle $\theta$, compute $[\bar{Q}]_k = [T]^{-1} [Q] [T]^{-T}$. The transformed $[\bar{Q}]$ entries are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{11} &= Q_{11}\cos^4\theta + 2(Q_{12}+2Q_{66})\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + Q_{22}\sin^4\theta \\
\bar{Q}_{22} &= Q_{11}\sin^4\theta + 2(Q_{12}+2Q_{66})\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + Q_{22}\cos^4\theta \\
\bar{Q}_{12} &= (Q_{11}+Q_{22}-4Q_{66})\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + Q_{12}(\cos^4\theta+\sin^4\theta) \\
\bar{Q}_{66} &= (Q_{11}+Q_{22}-2Q_{12}-2Q_{66})\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + Q_{66}(\cos^4\theta+\sin^4\theta) \\
\bar{Q}_{16} &= (Q_{11}-Q_{12}-2Q_{66})\sin\theta\cos^3\theta + (Q_{12}-Q_{22}+2Q_{66})\sin^3\theta\cos\theta \\
\bar{Q}_{26} &= (Q_{11}-Q_{12}-2Q_{66})\sin^3\theta\cos\theta + (Q_{12}-Q_{22}+2Q_{66})\sin\theta\cos^3\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A_{ij}$**

For an 8-ply symmetric laminate of ply thickness $t = 0.125$ mm:

$$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{8} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \cdot t_k$$

Numerically (carried out for the $[0/45/-45/90]_s$ stack):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{11} &\approx 89\,400 \text{ N/mm} \\
A_{22} &\approx 89\,400 \text{ N/mm} \\
A_{12} &\approx 31\,200 \text{ N/mm} \\
A_{66} &\approx 29\,000 \text{ N/mm} \\
A_{16} &= A_{26} \approx 0 \text{ (cross-ply + balanced)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Mid-Plane Strains**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x^0 \\ \varepsilon_y^0 \\ \gamma_{xy}^0 \end{Bmatrix} &= [A]^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} 1000 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{Bmatrix} 1.27 \times 10^{-5} \\ -4.4 \times 10^{-6} \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Ply-Stress Recovery**

For each ply $k$, on-axis stresses are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix}_k = [\bar{Q}]_k \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x^0 \\ \varepsilon_y^0 \\ \gamma_{xy}^0 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the 90° ply (matrix-dominated, $\theta = 90°$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1^{(90)} &= \bar{Q}_{12}^{(90)} \varepsilon_x^0 + \bar{Q}_{22}^{(90)} \varepsilon_y^0 \\
&\approx (3.5\,\text{GPa})(1.27\times 10^{-5}) + (10\,\text{GPa})(-4.4\times 10^{-6}) \\
&\approx 44.5 - 44.0 = 0.5 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(very small — good)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the 45° ply (compute $[\bar{Q}]$ for $\theta = 45°$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1^{(45)} &\approx 96 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2^{(45)} &\approx 96 \text{ MPa} \\
\tau_{12}^{(45)} &\approx 18 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the 0° ply (fiber-dominated):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1^{(0)} &\approx 1840 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{well below } X_t = 2800) \\
\sigma_2^{(0)} &\approx 44 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{close to } Y_t = 60) \\
\tau_{12}^{(0)} &\approx 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Tsai-Wu to the Critical Ply (the 0° ply, with $\sigma_2$ closest to $Y_t$)**

Compute the coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{1}{2800} - \frac{1}{1500} = -3.10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ MPa}^{-1} \\
F_2 &= \frac{1}{60} - \frac{1}{250} = 1.27 \times 10^{-2} \text{ MPa}^{-1} \\
F_{11} &= \frac{1}{2800 \times 1500} = 2.38 \times 10^{-7} \text{ MPa}^{-2} \\
F_{22} &= \frac{1}{60 \times 250} = 6.67 \times 10^{-5} \text{ MPa}^{-2} \\
F_{66} &= \frac{1}{90^2} = 1.23 \times 10^{-4} \text{ MPa}^{-2} \\
F_{12} &= -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11}F_{22}} = -1.99 \times 10^{-6} \text{ MPa}^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Plug in $\sigma_1 = 1840$, $\sigma_2 = 44$, $\tau_{12} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FI} &= F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + 2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \\
&= (-3.10 \times 10^{-4})(1840) + (1.27 \times 10^{-2})(44) \\
&\quad + (2.38 \times 10^{-7})(1840)^2 + (6.67 \times 10^{-5})(44)^2 \\
&\quad + 2(-1.99 \times 10^{-6})(1840)(44) \\
&\approx -0.570 + 0.559 + 0.806 + 0.129 - 0.322 \\
&\approx 0.602
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\text{FI} = 0.602 < 1$, the 0° ply is safe at $N_x = 1000$ N/mm.

**Step 6 — Find FPF by Scaling**

Because the Tsai-Wu polynomial is homogeneous of degree 2 (in stresses) only when the linear terms are neglected, scaling must be done **iteratively** (or by solving the quadratic for $\lambda$):

$$1 = \lambda F_1 \sigma_1^a + \lambda F_2 \sigma_2^a + \lambda^2(F_{11}\sigma_1^{a2} + F_{22}\sigma_2^{a2} + 2F_{12}\sigma_1^a\sigma_2^a)$$

Solving numerically gives $\lambda_{FPF} \approx 1.18$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{N_x^{FPF} \approx 1.18 \times 1000 = 1180 \text{ N/mm}}$$

> **Reserve Factor** = $1.18$, **Margin of Safety** = $1.18 - 1.0 = 0.18$ (acceptable for space structures once overall safety factor is applied).

### 3.3 Ply-Failure Sequence Diagram (Analytical Matrix)

| Ply Orientation | Failure Mode | Typical Failure Stress (MPa) | Criticality Order |
|---|---|---|---|
| 90° | **Matrix tensile cracking** along fibers | $\sigma_2 \to Y_t \approx 60$ | **First to fail** |
| $\pm 45°$ | **Matrix shear yielding** | $\tau_{12} \to S \approx 90$ | Second to fail |
| 0° | **Fiber breakage** | $\sigma_1 \to X_t \approx 2800$ | **Last to fail** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Failure Criterion Selection Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block maps the *decision logic* an engineer uses to select a failure criterion during laminate strength analysis — a topic frequently asked in 14-mark KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Ply stresses sigma1 sigma2 tau12 known] --> B{Is shear-normal coupling significant?}
    B -- No --> C{Is interaction between sigma1 and sigma2 important?}
    B -- Yes --> D[Use Tsai-Wu Criterion]
    C -- No --> E[Use Maximum Stress Criterion]
    C -- Yes --> F{Does material have different tension and compression strengths?}
    F -- No --> G[Use Tsai-Hill Criterion]
    F -- Yes --> D
    D --> H[Compute Failure Index FI]
    E --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{FI less than 1?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Ply SAFE]
    I -- No --> K[Ply FAILED at this load]
    K --> L[Reduce load and recompute FPF]
    L --> A
    J --> M[Check next ply in stack]
    M --> N{All plies safe?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Laminate SAFE at this load]
    N -- No --> K
```

### 4.2 Ply Stress Recovery & Failure Assessment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Global Loads Nx Ny Nxy] --> AINV[Inverse of A matrix]
    AINV --> EPS0[Mid plane strains epsilon0]
    EPS0 --> SIGMA[Per ply stress via bar Q]
    SIGMA --> CRIT{Apply Failure Criterion}
    CRIT --> T1[Maximum Stress]
    CRIT --> T2[Maximum Strain]
    CRIT --> T3[Tsai Hill]
    CRIT --> T4[Tsai Wu]
    T1 --> FI[Failure Index]
    T2 --> FI
    T3 --> FI
    T4 --> FI
    FI --> DEC{All FI less than 1?}
    DEC -- Yes --> SAFE[Safe: Report Reserve Factor]
    DEC -- No --> FPF[Identify First Ply Failure]
    FPF --> ITER[Iterate load scaling lambda]
    ITER --> SAFE
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Multi-Mode Failure Map of a $[0/\pm 45/90]_s$ Laminate

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LOAD[Increasing Applied Load Nx]
        L1[Stage 1: Matrix microcracks in 90 plies] --> L2[Stage 2: Shear yielding in 45 plies]
        L2 --> L3[Stage 3: Fiber-matrix debond in 0 plies]
        L3 --> L4[Stage 4: Fiber breakage in 0 plies = LPF]
    end
    L1 -.FI crosses 1.-> FPF1((FPF))
    L4 -.FI crosses 1.-> FPF2((LPF))
```

### 4.4 Hygrothermal + Mechanical Loading — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MECH[Mechanical Load Nx] --> SUM
    THERM[Thermal Load deltaT times alpha] --> TSIG[Thermal residual stress sigmath]
    MOIST[Moisture deltaC times beta] --> TSIG
    TSIG --> SUM((Superpose))
    MECH --> SUM
    SUM --> PLY[Ply level combined stress]
    PLY --> CRIT[Tsai Wu with shifted allowables]
    CRIT --> OUT[Effective laminate strength]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above architecture is a **functional block diagram** rather than a true physical drawing — a deliberate adaptation to the Mermaid compiler. It conveys the *information flow* that determines real laminate strength in space environments, where thermal and moisture loads often *exceed* the mechanical load.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Differentiate between **First-Ply Failure (FPF)** and **Last-Ply Failure (LPF)** in a composite laminate. Why is FPF considered conservative for design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **FPF (1 mark):** The load level at which the *most critically stressed* ply in a laminate first reaches its allowable strength as predicted by a chosen failure criterion. It marks the onset of *non-linear* behavior but not collapse.
- **LPF (1 mark):** The load level at which the *last* load-bearing ply (typically the 0° fiber-dominated plies) fails, resulting in complete laminate collapse.
- **Why FPF is conservative (1 mark):** Beyond FPF, the laminate does not suddenly fail — load is redistributed to surviving plies. Using FPF as the design limit introduces a built-in safety margin, which is standard in aerospace design (e.g., NASA-STD-5001).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** State the **Tsai-Hill failure criterion** for an orthotropic lamina. Mention one key limitation of this criterion.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):**
$$ \left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X}\right)^2 - \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X^2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 \le 1 $$
where $X$ and $Y$ are chosen as $X_t, X_c$ or $Y_t, Y_c$ depending on the sign of $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$.
- **Limitation (1 mark):** It cannot distinguish between tensile and compressive strengths (no linear terms $F_1, F_2$), so for materials like CFRP where $X_t \ne X_c$, the Tsai-Hill prediction deviates from experiment. Tsai-Wu overcomes this by including the linear terms.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU ESE Module 3 – Choice A, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Derive the **Tsai-Wu failure criterion** for a unidirectional orthotropic lamina under plane stress, clearly identifying the strength coefficients $F_1, F_2, F_{11}, F_{22}, F_{66}, F_{12}$. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A $[0/90/90/0]_s$ glass-epoxy laminate (ply thickness 0.2 mm) is subjected to $N_x = 500$ N/mm, $N_y = 100$ N/mm, $N_{xy} = 0$. Using the **Maximum Stress criterion**, determine which ply fails first. Material data: $E_1 = 38$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.3$, $G_{12} = 4.5$ GPa, $X_t = 1200$ MPa, $X_c = 700$ MPa, $Y_t = 40$ MPa, $Y_c = 130$ MPa, $S = 60$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Full derivation as in **Section 3.1, Steps 1–7** above. Award step-wise marks as:
- Setup of scalar failure function and Taylor series (1)
- Symmetry reduction and orthotropy arguments (1)
- Coefficient identification from four uniaxial tests ($a_1, a_2, a_{11}, a_{22}, a_{66}$) (3)
- Discussion of $a_{12}$ and Tsai-Pagano approximation + closed-ellipsoid stability condition (1)
- Final boxed equation with physical interpretation of each $F_{ij}$ (1)

**Physical significance of each term (to be elaborated by student):**
- $F_1, F_2$ — capture the **strength differential effect** (tension ≠ compression).
- $F_{11}, F_{22}, F_{66}$ — square (quadratic) terms representing the **shape of the failure envelope** along each principal stress axis.
- $F_{12}$ — **interaction** term; couples normal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the [A] matrix.** (2 marks)

Use $[\bar{Q}]$ for 0° and 90° plies; with the laminate being symmetric $[0/90/90/0]_s$ (8 plies total, equal thickness $t=0.2$ mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{11} &= 4t(Q_{11} + Q_{22}) \quad \text{(half of plies at } 0°, \text{ half at } 90°) \\
A_{22} &= 4t(Q_{11} + Q_{22}) = A_{11} \\
A_{12} &= 8t Q_{12} \\
A_{66} &= 8t Q_{66}
\end{aligned}
$$

With $Q_{11} = 38/(1-0.3 \times 0.3 \times 8.5/38) \approx 40.7$ GPa, $Q_{22} \approx 9.1$ GPa, $Q_{12} \approx 3.1$ GPa, $Q_{66} = 4.5$ GPa:

$$A_{11} = A_{22} = 0.8(40.7 + 9.1) \times 10^3 = 39{,}840 \text{ N/mm}$$
$$A_{12} = 1.6 \times 3100 = 4960 \text{ N/mm}, \quad A_{66} = 1.6 \times 4500 = 7200 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Mid-plane strains.** (1 mark)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix}\varepsilon_x^0 \\ \varepsilon_y^0\end{Bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} \\ A_{12} & A_{22} \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix}500\\100\end{Bmatrix} \\
\det[A] &= A_{11}^2 - A_{12}^2 = 39{,}840^2 - 4960^2 \approx 1.562 \times 10^9 \\
\varepsilon_x^0 &\approx (500 \times 39{,}840 - 100 \times 4960) / (1.562 \times 10^9) \approx 1.144 \times 10^{-5} \\
\varepsilon_y^0 &\approx (100 \times 39{,}840 - 500 \times 4960) / (1.562 \times 10^9) \approx 9.69 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ply stress recovery.** (2 marks)

For 0° ply: $\sigma_1 = Q_{11}\varepsilon_x^0 + Q_{12}\varepsilon_y^0 = 40700(1.144\times 10^{-5}) + 3100(9.69\times 10^{-7}) \approx 468.6$ MPa; $\sigma_2 = Q_{12}\varepsilon_x^0 + Q_{22}\varepsilon_y^0 \approx 38.4$ MPa.

For 90° ply: $\sigma_1 = 38.4$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 468.6$ MPa (by symmetry, swapped indices).

**Step 4 — Apply Maximum Stress criterion.** (2 marks)

- 0° ply: $\sigma_1 = 468.6$ MPa $< X_t = 1200$ ✓; $\sigma_2 = 38.4$ MPa $< Y_t = 40$ ✓ (just barely safe).
- 90° ply: $\sigma_2 = 468.6$ MPa $\gg Y_t = 40$ **FAILURE**.

**Conclusion:** The **90° ply fails first** by transverse tensile cracking, because its $\sigma_2 = 468.6$ MPa vastly exceeds $Y_t = 40$ MPa.

---

> **Question B. [KTU ESE Module 3 – Choice B, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Discuss the role of **interlaminar shear strength (ILSS)** in the design of space-grade laminates. Describe the **Short-Beam Shear (SBS) test** (ASTM D2344) used to measure it. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A carbon-epoxy composite satellite panel (8-ply quasi-isotropic $[0/45/-45/90]_s$, $t = 0.125$ mm/ply) operates between $-180\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $+120\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. The cured (stress-free) temperature is $175\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Using the **classical lamination theory** thermal stress formulation, show how a single thermal cycle induces **matrix microcracking** in the 90° plies. Use $\alpha_1 = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{K}$, $\alpha_2 = 25 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{K}$, $E_1 = 140$ GPa, $E_2 = 10$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.3$, $G_{12} = 5$ GPa, $Y_t = 50$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) (7 marks):**
- **Role of ILSS (3 marks):** ILSS governs delamination resistance; in space it is the *weakest link* because through-thickness properties are matrix-dominated. Out-of-plane thermal gradients, MMOD impact, and bolt-bearing all create interlaminar shear. Typical IM7/8552 ILSS = 90–110 MPa.
- **SBS Test description (3 marks):** Three-point bend on a short beam (span-to-thickness ratio ~ 4); ILSS = $3P/(4bh)$ where $P$ is failure load, $b$ is width, $h$ is thickness. Test produces interlaminar shear failure rather than flexure or bearing.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** Non-uniform shear distribution; pure ILSS requires Iosipescu or V-notched rail shear.

**(b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute effective $\Delta T$.** For cooldown to $-180\,^{\circ}\text{C}$: $\Delta T = -180 - 175 = -355$ K. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute effective mid-plane strains (CLT).** With $N_x = N_y = N_{xy} = 0$ (mechanical load-free):

$$
\begin{Bmatrix}\varepsilon_x^0 \\ \varepsilon_y^0 \\ \gamma_{xy}^0\end{Bmatrix} = [A]^{-1} [N^{th}]
$$

where $N^{th}_i = \sum_k [\bar{Q}]_k \cdot \{\alpha\}_k \cdot \Delta T \cdot t_k$. (2 marks)

**Step 3 — Show that the 90° ply experiences $\sigma_2 \gg Y_t$.** Because $\alpha_2 \approx 50 \alpha_1$, the 90° plies try to contract 50× more than the 0° plies in the transverse direction; they are constrained, generating **thermal mismatch stresses** in the 90° ply:

$$\sigma_2^{(90)} \approx E_2 (\varepsilon_y^0 - \alpha_2 \Delta T) \approx 200{-}400 \text{ MPa}$$

(3 marks)

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** Since $\sigma_2^{(90)} \gg Y_t = 50$ MPa, the 90° plies crack on first cooldown. This is a well-documented phenomenon called **transverse ply cracking** and is *unavoidable* in CFRP space structures — designers account for it through **knock-down factors** and **ply crack-resistant tape layups**. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Strength of Laminates**
> 1. **Sign error in Tsai-Wu linear terms ($F_1, F_2$):** When $\sigma_1$ is *compressive*, $F_1 \sigma_1$ is *positive* (since $F_1 < 0$ for $X_t > X_c$). Confusing this sign costs **2 marks** on a 14-mark problem.
> 2. **Forgetting thermal residual stresses in space applications:** A pure mechanical-load analysis will *over-predict* laminate strength and *fail* the safety check. Always include $\Delta T$ effects for orbital structures.
> 3. **Mixing up [A] inverse with [D] inverse:** Mid-plane strains come from $[A]^{-1}$ applied to $\{N\}$; curvatures from the full $[A B; B D]$ inverse applied to $\{N; M\}$.
