   - By grafting –ONO₂ onto the polymer backbone, the energetic plasticiser is "locked in," eliminating migration, sweating, and long-term ageing issues.
   - This produces a *minimum-smoke, minimum-signature* propellant capable of being formulated entirely with chlorine-free ingredients.

4. **Polymerization Mechanism**
   - Cationic ring-opening polymerisation initiated by **BF₃·OEt₂** or **BF₃·THF** at low temperature (−20 °C to −40 °C).
   - Propagation proceeds via an oxocarbenium / tertiary carbenium intermediate.
   - Chain transfer is suppressed at low T, giving well-defined molar masses ($M_n$ ~ 2000–5000 g/mol for prepolymers).

5. **Compatibility logic with nitrate ester plasticisers**
   - PolyNIMMO is chemically *similar* (both contain –ONO₂) to plasticisers like **BDNPA**, **BDNPF**, **TMETN**, **DEGDN**.
   - "Like dissolves like" → excellent mutual miscibility, allowing very high solids loading (≥ 80 wt% oxidiser) without phase separation.

6. **Engineering trade-offs**
   - Higher density (1.26 g/cm³) compared to HTPB (0.92 g/cm³) → higher volumetric energy.
   - Slightly more brittle → plasticised with nitrate esters to retain low-temperature flexibility.
   - Slightly higher cost → justified for military and high-performance space applications.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit formula | C₅H₉NO₄ | — |
| Repeat unit molar mass | $M_o = 5(12)+9(1)+14+4(16)$ | **147.13 g/mol** |
| Number-average DP | $\overline{X_n} = M_n / M_o$ | dimensionless |
| Oxygen balance (per repeat unit) | $\Omega = \frac{1600}{M_o}(2y + z/2 - 2x)$ | **−65.9 %** |
| Mass fraction of nitrogen | $w_N = \frac{14}{M_o} \times 100$ | **9.52 %** |
| Density | $\rho_p$ | **1.26 g/cm³** |
| Glass transition temperature | $T_g$ | **≈ −28 °C** |
| Heat of formation (per mol repeat) | $\Delta H_f$ | **−385 kJ/mol** |
| Volumetric energy advantage over HTPB | $\Delta(\rho I_{sp})/\rho_{HTPB}$ | **+18 to +22 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU LaTeX rule (valuation script-safety):** When writing Oxygen Balance $\Omega$ in derivations, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute-value brackets — NEVER the vertical bar `|` — to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax during PDF compilation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Molar Mass of the PolyNIMMO Repeat Unit

The repeat unit formula is **C₅H₉NO₄** (5 carbon, 9 hydrogen, 1 nitrogen, 4 oxygen atoms).

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_o &= 5 \cdot M_C + 9 \cdot M_H + 1 \cdot M_N + 4 \cdot M_O \\
M_o &= 5(12.011) + 9(1.008) + 1(14.007) + 4(15.999) \\
M_o &= 60.055 + 9.072 + 14.007 + 63.996 \\
M_o &= 147.130 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- Carbon atomic mass 12.011 g/mol × 5 = 60.055
- Hydrogen atomic mass 1.008 g/mol × 9 = 9.072
- Nitrogen atomic mass 14.007 g/mol × 1 = 14.007
- Oxygen atomic mass 15.999 g/mol × 4 = 63.996
- Summation yields the molar mass of one repeat unit.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Oxygen Balance (OB) Calculation

For a compound **CₓHᵧNᶻOᵥ**, the oxygen balance (expressed as percent of oxygen needed for complete combustion to CO₂, H₂O, N₂) is:

$$
\Omega = \frac{1600}{M_o}\left(2x + \frac{y}{2} - z - v\right)
$$

For PolyNIMMO repeat unit C₅H₉NO₄: x = 5, y = 9, z = 1, v = 4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega &= \frac{1600}{147.13}\left(2(5) + \frac{9}{2} - 1 - 4\right) \\
&= \frac{1600}{147.13}\left(10 + 4.5 - 1 - 4\right) \\
&= \frac{1600}{147.13}(9.5) \\
&= 10.873 \times 9.5 \\
\Omega &= -103.3 \;\text{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait — the per-repeat-unit OB is ≈ −103 %. However, the *propellant-level* OB after blending with ammonium perchlorate (AP) and aluminium is what matters in practice.** Recomputing carefully using the conventional formula:

$$
\Omega_{per\;100g} = \frac{-1600}{M_o}\left(z + \frac{v}{2} - x - \frac{y}{4}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega_{100g} &= \frac{-1600}{147.13}\left(1 + \frac{4}{2} - 5 - \frac{9}{4}\right) \\
&= \frac{-1600}{147.13}(1 + 2 - 5 - 2.25) \\
&= \frac{-1600}{147.13}(-4.25) \\
&= -10.873 \times (-4.25) \\
\Omega_{100g} &= +46.2 \;\text{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The positive oxygen balance of the *repeat unit alone* (+46 %) is misleading in isolation. When PolyNIMMO is used as a binder (typically 12–18 % by mass) in a formulation dominated by AP (oxidiser), the formulation is balanced near zero OB to maximise $I_{sp}$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Impulse Advantage over HTPB (Density × Energy Reasoning)

Assume a baseline HTPB/AP/Al propellant with $I_{sp,HTPB}$ = 260 s at $\rho_{HTPB}$ = 1.72 g/cm³. Replacing HTPB with PolyNIMMO (assuming same mass fractions of AP, Al, plasticiser), the gain in density impulse $\rho I_{sp}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(\rho I_{sp}) &= \rho_{PN} I_{sp,PN} - \rho_{HTPB} I_{sp,HTPB} \\
\frac{\Delta(\rho I_{sp})}{\rho_{HTPB} I_{sp,HTPB}} &= \frac{\rho_{PN}}{\rho_{HTPB}}\cdot\frac{I_{sp,PN}}{I_{sp,HTPB}} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

With $\rho_{PN} / \rho_{HTPB} = 1.78/1.72 = 1.035$ and $I_{sp,PN}/I_{sp,HTPB} \approx 1.04$ (energetic binder contributes extra combustion energy):

$$
\frac{\Delta(\rho I_{sp})}{\rho_{HTPB} I_{sp,HTPB}} = 1.035 \times 1.04 - 1 = 1.076 - 1 = +7.6 \;\%
$$

Thus the **volumetric specific impulse gain ≈ +7 to +10 %** is purely from binder replacement — a key reason PolyNIMMO is preferred for volume-limited upper-stage and missile motors.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Monomer Synthesis Pathway (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[trimethylolpropane TME] --> B[intramolecular cyclodehydration H2SO4 catalyst 200C]
    B --> C[3-methyl-3-oxetanemethanol MMO alcohol]
    C --> D[nitration with HNO3 acetic anhydride 0 to 10C]
    D --> E[NIMMO monomer 3-methyl-3-nitratomethyl oxetane]
    E --> F[cationic ring opening polymerisation BF3 OEt2 initiator -30C]
    F --> G[PolyNIMMO polynitrato methyl methyloxetane energetic binder]
    G --> H[blending with BDNPA F plasticiser 14 to 18 wt percent]
    H --> I[propellant formulation with AP 70 percent and Al 18 percent]
    I --> J[solid rocket propellant grain]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Repeat-Unit Architecture and Functional Roles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PolyNIMMO repeat unit] --> Q[C5H9NO4 backbone]
    Q --> R[oxetane derived polyether chain O CH2 C CH2]
    Q --> S[pendant methyl group steric control Tg depression]
    Q --> T[pendant CH2ONO2 nitrate ester energetic group]
    T --> T1[oxidising oxygen 4 atoms per repeat]
    T --> T2[exothermic decomposition NO2 release]
    T --> T3[plasticiser compatibility same ONO2 chemistry]
    P --> U[end groups typically OH for polyurethane cure]
    U --> V[cure with isocyanate NCO to give elastomeric network]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Role of PolyNIMMO in Solid Propellant Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph propellant[solid rocket propellant grain]
        OXS[ammonium perchlorate oxidiser 70 wt percent]
        ALM[aluminium metal fuel 18 wt percent]
        BIN[PolyNIMMO binder 14 wt percent]
        PLA[BDNPA F energetic plasticiser 6 wt percent]
        ADD[burn rate modifier and bonding agent 2 wt percent]
    end
    BIN --> role1[holds oxidiser and fuel grains in matrix]
    BIN --> role2[contributes positive enthalpy of formation]
    BIN --> role3[raises bulk density 1.78 vs 1.72 g per cm3]
    BIN --> role4[enables chlorine free minimum smoke formulation]
    BIN --> role5[replaces inert HTPB for higher Isp]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Define Polynitrato Methyl Methyloxetane. State any two of its advantages over HTPB as a propellant binder.

**Model Answer:**
Polynitrato Methyl Methyloxetane (PolyNIMMO) is a preformed energetic oxetane-based polymer obtained by cationic ring-opening polymerisation of 3-methyl-3-nitratomethyl oxetane. Its repeat unit contains a chemically bonded nitrate ester (–ONO₂) group that makes the polymer self-energetic.

Two advantages over HTPB:
1. Higher density (1.26 g/cm³ vs 0.92 g/cm³) gives higher volumetric specific impulse.
2. Positive enthalpy of formation due to –ONO₂ groups, releasing additional energy during combustion and enabling chlorine-free minimum-signature formulations.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** Write the repeat-unit structure of PolyNIMMO and identify the functional group responsible for its energetic behaviour.

**Model Answer:**
Repeat unit of PolyNIMMO:

$$\left[-CH_2-C(CH_3)(CH_2ONO_2)-CH_2-O-\right]$$

The **nitrate ester functional group (–ONO₂)** attached to the pendant methylene is responsible for energetic behaviour, since its decomposition to NO₂ and aldehydes is highly exothermic.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, describe the synthesis of Polynitrato Methyl Methyloxetane starting from trimethylolpropane. Indicate the role of the catalyst and the polymerisation initiator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain why PolyNIMMO is classified as a *preformed energetic binder*. Compare its properties with HTPB in a tabular form covering density, $T_g$, heat of formation and oxygen balance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Synthesis:**

Step 1 — Cyclodehydration of trimethylolpropane (TME) using concentrated **H₂SO₄** at ~200 °C to form **3-methyl-3-oxetanemethanol** (the methyl methyloxetane alcohol). Role of H₂SO₄: protonates an –OH and facilitates intramolecular ring closure (Williamson-type etherification).

Step 2 — Nitration of the alcohol with **HNO₃ / acetic anhydride** at 0–10 °C to give **3-methyl-3-nitratomethyl oxetane (NIMMO monomer)**. The mixed acid converts –CH₂OH to –CH₂ONO₂ while preserving the oxetane ring.

Step 3 — Cationic ring-opening polymerisation of NIMMO with **BF₃·OEt₂** at –30 °C in CH₂Cl₂. The BF₃–ether complex generates a true cation that opens the strained 4-membered oxetane ring, propagating through tertiary carbenium species to yield PolyNIMMO with hydroxyl chain ends (suitable for further polyurethane cure).

**(b) Tabular comparison:**

| Property | PolyNIMMO | HTPB |
|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.26 | 0.92 |
| $T_g$ (°C) | −28 | −80 |
| Heat of formation (kJ/mol repeat) | −385 | +62 |
| Oxygen balance of polymer | positive | highly negative |
| Energetic? | yes (preformed) | no (inert) |

PolyNIMMO is *preformed energetic* because the energetic –ONO₂ group is covalently bonded to the polymer chain rather than physically mixed in as a plasticiser. This prevents migration and ageing losses.

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the role of PolyNIMMO in composite solid propellant formulations. How does it contribute to (i) specific impulse, (ii) density, (iii) mechanical properties, and (iv) smoke signature? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PolyNIMMO sample has $M_n$ = 4500 g/mol. Compute (i) the degree of polymerisation, (ii) the mass fraction of nitrogen, and (iii) the percentage oxygen balance per 100 g of polymer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** (i) It replaces inert HTPB, contributing additional combustion energy through its –ONO₂ groups → raises $I_{sp}$ by 2–4 % mass-specific, 6–10 % volumetric. (ii) Its bulk density 1.26 g/cm³ is ~37 % higher than HTPB, raising bulk density of propellant from 1.72 to ~1.78 g/cm³. (iii) Provides good low-temperature flexibility ($T_g$ −28 °C); however, mechanical strength is usually enhanced by curing –OH termini with isocyanate to form a polyurethane network. (iv) Allows chlorine-free (no AP) minimum-smoke propellants, since the polymer itself supplies oxidising oxygen.

**(b) Numerical calculations:**

(i) Degree of polymerisation:

$$
\overline{X_n} = \frac{M_n}{M_o} = \frac{4500}{147.13} = 30.6 \approx 31
$$

(ii) Mass fraction of nitrogen:

$$
w_N = \frac{14}{147.13} \times 100 = 9.51 \;\%
$$

(iii) Oxygen balance per 100 g:

$$
\Omega_{100g} = \frac{-1600}{M_o}\left(z + \frac{v}{2} - x - \frac{y}{4}\right) = \frac{-1600}{147.13}\left(1 + 2 - 5 - 2.25\right) = +46.2 \;\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse the repeat-unit oxygen balance with the *propellant-level* oxygen balance. Always state which one you are computing.
> 2. Do **not** confuse PolyNIMMO with PolyBAMO or PolyAMMO — they are azide-bearing oxetane polymers, not nitrate-ester bearing.
> 3. Do **not** write the polymerisation as free-radical; PolyNIMMO is **cationic** ring-opening. Writing "radical" will cost you full marks.
> 4. Always show the BF₃·OEt₂ initiator (or equivalent Lewis acid) — naming it is mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PolyNIMMO = poly(3-methyl-3-nitratomethyl oxetane)**, an oxetane-based energetic binder.
- Repeat unit formula **C₅H₉NO₄**, $M_o$ ≈ **147.13 g/mol**.
- Polymerised by **cationic ring-opening polymerisation (BF₃·OEt₂, low T)**.
- Synthesised from **trimethylolpropane → 3-methyl-3-oxetanemethanol → NIMMO → PolyNIMMO**.
- Energy source: **pendant nitrate ester (–ONO₂)** group.
- Density ≈ **1.26 g/cm³**, $T_g$ ≈ **−28 °C**, $\Delta H_f$ ≈ **−385 kJ/mol repeat unit**.
- N content ≈ **9.5 wt%**; per-100-g oxygen balance ≈ **+46 %**.
- Compatible with nitrate-ester plasticisers (BDNPA/F, TMETN, DEGDN).
- Gives **higher density impulse** and supports **chlorine-free minimum-smoke propellants**.
- Distinct from azide-oxetane polymers (BAMO, AMMO, GAP).
- Cure via terminal –OH with isocyanate → polyurethane elastomer network.
- Used in **upper-stage rocket motors, tactical missiles, and gas generators**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Testing of Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Testing of Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mechanical Testing of Composites** refers to the systematic, standardized experimental evaluation of the mechanical response of fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites and advanced composite laminates under controlled loading conditions, performed to determine properties such as tensile strength, compressive strength, flexural modulus, interlaminar shear strength (ILSS), impact resistance, fatigue life, and fracture toughness, in accordance with ASTM, ISO, and EN standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Mechanical testing of composites* is the experimental characterization of anisotropic, heterogeneous composite materials using standardized coupon-level tests (tensile, compressive, flexural, shear, impact, fatigue) that capture the unique orthotropic behaviour arising from the synergy of fibre, matrix, and the fibre–matrix interphase.

The mechanical behaviour of composites is fundamentally **anisotropic** — properties differ along the fibre direction (longitudinal) versus transverse to it. Therefore, unlike isotropic metals, a single test is insufficient; a suite of tests aligned with the principal material axes is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Spacecraft Skeleton Stress Test:**
> Imagine a satellite's structural panel as a *layered cake*, where each layer is a sheet of carbon fibres held together by epoxy resin. Before sending this "cake" to orbit, engineers must poke, push, pull, bend, and hammer it on Earth to predict how it will survive launch vibrations and the vacuum of space.
> - **Tensile test** = stretching the cake apart to see how strong the fibres are when pulled.
> - **Compression test** = squashing the cake to see if it buckles under rocket thrust.
> - **Flexural test** = bending the cake like a diving board to mimic pressure differentials.
> - **Short-beam shear test** = checking whether the icing (resin layers) slides between the cake layers — this is **interlaminar shear strength (ILSS)**.
> - **Impact test** = simulating a micrometeoroid strike with a swinging pendulum.

## 1.3 Why Mechanical Testing Matters for Space-Grade Composites

In spacecraft applications (rocket nozzle liners, payload fairings, satellite bus structures, thermal protection systems, ablative shields), composite laminates must be:

| Property Required | Reason in Space Environment |
|---|---|
| **High specific stiffness** | Minimum mass for maximum payload |
| **High specific strength** | Withstand launch thrust (typically **3–8 g**) |
| **Thermal stability** | Operate between **−150 °C and +200 °C** |
| **Low outgassing** | Prevent contamination of optics (TML < **1 %**, CVCM < **0.1 %**) |
| **Fatigue resistance** | Survive **10⁶–10⁸** load cycles |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** In KTU examinations, always emphasize that composites are *not* isotropic — test coupons must be cut at **0°, 90°, and ±45°** fibre orientations to fully characterize the laminate.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Standards

- **ASTM D3039** — Tensile properties of polymer matrix composite materials
- **ASTM D3410** — Compressive properties (Modified IITRI fixture)
- **ASTM D790** — Flexural properties (three-point bending)
- **ASTM D2344** — Short-beam strength (ILSS)
- **ASTM D6110** — Charpy impact resistance
- **ASTM D7137** — Compression after impact (CAI)
- **ASTM D7791** — Open-hole compression
- **ISO 527**, **EN 2562**, **EN 2597** — European equivalents
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Mechanical Tests

The mechanical testing of composites follows a **four-tier hierarchical framework**:

1. **Constituent-level tests** — single fibre, neat resin, fibre–matrix interface (microbond, pull-out).
2. **Laminate-level tests** — unidirectional (UD), cross-ply, angle-ply coupons.
3. **Structural-element tests** — stiffened panels, stringers, honeycomb sandwich.
4. **Full-scale tests** — entire spacecraft sub-assemblies.

For KTU Module 4, the focus is on **laminate-level coupon tests**.

## 2.2 Test-by-Test Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Tensile Testing (ASTM D3039)

**Principle:** A rectangular coupon of standard geometry is gripped in a universal testing machine (UTM) and loaded uniaxially until failure. Strain is measured via **extensometer** or **strain gauges**.

**Coupon geometry (ASTM D3039):**
- Length **L** = 150–250 mm
- Width **w** = 25 mm (for UD tape), 12.5 mm (for fabric)
- Thickness **t** = 1–3 mm
- End tabs: GFRP (±45° woven), length 50 mm, taper 5–10°

**Measured properties:**
- Ultimate tensile strength (UTS), **σ_ult**
- Young's modulus **E**
- Poisson's ratio **ν_12**
- Failure strain **ε_f**

**Failure modes observed (KTU-favoured question):**
- Fibre rupture (brittle explosive failure in 0° UD)
- Matrix cracking (transverse tension in 90° UD)
- Fibre pull-out
- Delamination at tab region (improper tabbing)

### 2.2.2 Compression Testing (ASTM D3410 / D695)

**Why compression is critical:** Composites fail in compression at *lower* stresses than in tension due to **microbuckling** of fibres.

**Test fixtures:**
- **IITRI fixture** (Illinois Institute of Technology Research Institute) — most common
- **Celanese fixture** — for thin laminates
- **Wyoming-modified D695 fixture** — for thick sections

### 2.2.3 Flexural Testing (ASTM D790 / D7264)

Two configurations:
- **Three-point bending (3PB):** span-to-depth ratio **L/h = 16:1** (ASTM D790) or **32:1** (ASTM D7264).
- **Four-point bending (4PB):** provides region of *pure* bending moment.

### 2.2.4 Short-Beam Shear Test (ASTM D2344)

Determines **Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS)** — the resin-dominated through-thickness property. Uses a small span-to-depth ratio (**L/h = 4:1** or **5:1**) so that shear failure precedes flexural failure.

### 2.2.5 Impact Testing (ASTM D6110, D7136)

Two principal methods:
- **Charpy** (pendulum impact) — energy absorbed to break notched specimen.
- **Drop-weight impact** — simulates low-velocity impact (tool-drop on aircraft skin).

**Compression After Impact (CAI, ASTM D7137)** is the **most critical damage-tolerance metric** for aerospace composites.

### 2.2.6 Fatigue Testing (ASTM D3479)

Composite fatigue is governed by **S–N curves** (Wöhler curves), with the distinct feature of *gradual stiffness degradation* before final fracture — unlike metals which exhibit a clear fatigue limit.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulae assume linear-elastic orthotropic behaviour in the principal material axes (1–2).**

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Standard |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--|:--:|
| 1 | Tensile strength | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{w \cdot t}$ | **MPa** | D3039 |
| 2 | Young's modulus | $E = \dfrac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$ | **GPa** | D3039 |
| 3 | Poisson's ratio | $\nu_{12} = \dfrac{-\varepsilon_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1}}$ | dimensionless | D3039 |
| 4 | Flexural strength (3PB) | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b h^{2}}$ | **MPa** | D790 |
| 5 | Flexural modulus (3PB) | $E_f = \dfrac{L^{3}\, m}{4 b h^{3}}$ | **GPa** | D790 |
| 6 | Flexural strength (4PB) | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P L}{4 b h^{2}}$ | **MPa** | D7264 |
| 7 | ILSS (Short-beam) | $\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3 P_{max}}{4 b h}$ | **MPa** | D2344 |
| 8 | Compressive strength | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | **MPa** | D3410 |
| 9 | Strain energy density | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon$ | **MJ/m³** | — |
| 10 | Composite density | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + (1-V_f) \rho_m$ | **kg/m³** | — |
| 11 | Specific strength | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{\rho_c}$ | **MPa·cm³/g** | — |
| 12 | Specific modulus | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{\rho_c}$ | **GPa·cm³/g** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key:**
> $P$ = applied load (N), $L$ = support span (mm), $b$ = specimen width (mm), $h$ = specimen thickness (mm), $m$ = slope of load–deflection curve (N/mm), $A_0$ = cross-sectional area (mm²), $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction, $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$ = longitudinal and transverse strains.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Field

| Sector | Critical Test | Property Required |
|---|---|---|
| Launch-vehicle fairings | Compression & CAI | Damage tolerance, buckling resistance |
| Satellite bus panels | Tensile (0° & 90°) | Stiffness, dimensional stability |
| Cryogenic fuel tanks | Flexural at −196 °C | Cryogenic embrittlement resistance |
| Re-entry ablatives | Charpy impact | Thermal-shock resistance |
| Space-telescope mirrors | Specific modulus | Surface-figure stability |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world benchmark:** Carbon-fibre/epoxy (T700/2510) typically exhibits: $E_1 \approx 135$ **GPa**, $\sigma_{1t} \approx 1500$ **MPa**, $\rho \approx 1.55$ **g/cm³**, giving specific modulus ≈ **87 GPa·cm³/g** — higher than titanium (≈ 26).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Flexural Strength (Three-Point Bending)

**Starting point — classical beam theory for an Euler–Bernoulli beam of span L under central point load P.**

### Step 1 — Support reactions

The load P is applied at the centre of a simply-supported beam.

Therefore the vertical reactions at the two supports are equal:

$$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum bending moment

The bending moment is maximum directly under the central load:

$$M_{max} = R_A \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{P L}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Flexural stress (Euler–Bernoulli)

The bending stress at a distance *y* from the neutral axis is:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

For a rectangular cross-section of width *b* and height *h*:

$$I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$$

### Step 4 — Substituting into the stress expression

The maximum stress occurs at the outer fibre ($y = h/2$):

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max} \cdot (h/2)}{I} = \dfrac{(P L / 4) \cdot (h/2)}{b h^{3}/12}$$

### Step 5 — Algebraic simplification

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P L h}{8} \cdot \dfrac{12}{b h^{3}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{12 \, P L}{8 \, b h^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3 \, P L}{2 \, b h^{2}}$$

### Step 6 — Final flexural-strength equation

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b h^{2}}}$$

At the moment of failure $P \rightarrow P_{max}$, so this expression gives the **flexural strength** of the composite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic explained:** The factor of 3/2 emerges because the central reaction $P/2$ multiplied by half the span $L/2$ yields $PL/4$; combined with the section modulus $bh^2/6$, the result is $3PL/(2bh^2)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Interlaminar Shear Strength (Short-Beam Test)

### Step 1 — Maximum shear force in a 3PB specimen

The shear force diagram for a simply-supported beam with a central point load:

$$V_{max} = \dfrac{P}{2}$$

This is constant between the support and the loading nose.

### Step 2 — Average interlaminar shear stress

For a rectangular section the average transverse shear stress across the neutral axis is:

$$\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

For a rectangle, $Q = bh^{2}/8$ at the neutral axis, and $I = bh^{3}/12$:

$$\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{(P/2) \cdot (b h^{2}/8)}{(b h^{3}/12) \cdot b}$$

### Step 3 — Simplification

$$\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{P \, b \, h^{2}}{16} \cdot \dfrac{12}{b^{2} h^{3}}$$

$$\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{12 \, P}{16 \, b \, h} = \dfrac{3 P}{4 b h}$$

### Step 4 — Final ILSS expression

$$\boxed{\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3 P_{max}}{4 b h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The factor 3/4 arises from the ratio of parabolic shear-stress distribution maximum to average (= 3/2) multiplied by the shear-force-to-load ratio (= 1/2), giving the net coefficient of 3/4.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A unidirectional carbon-fibre/epoxy composite coupon is tested in 3-point bending per ASTM D790.
> Given: span $L = 80$ **mm**, width $b = 12.7$ **mm**, thickness $h = 4$ **mm**, maximum load $P_{max} = 1100$ **N**.
> Calculate the flexural strength.

### Solution — Step 1: Identify formula

Use the derived flexural strength equation:

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b h^{2}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 \times 1100 \times 80}{2 \times 12.7 \times (4)^{2}}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate denominator

$$2 \times 12.7 \times 16 = 406.4 \; \text{mm}^{2} \cdot \text{(scaled)}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate numerator

$$3 \times 1100 \times 80 = 264000 \; \text{N}\cdot\text{mm}$$

### Step 5: Compute final ratio

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{264000}{406.4} \approx 649.6 \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$$

### Step 6: State answer

$$\boxed{\sigma_f \approx 650 \; \text{MPa}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification check:** This value is consistent with typical CFRP flexural strength (700–1100 MPa). A smaller answer would indicate matrix-dominated failure or poor fibre alignment.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Identification (KTU High-Yield)

| Test Type | Dominant Failure Mode | Property Characterized | Fibre Orientation for Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0° Tensile (D3039) | Fibre fracture (brittle, explosive) | $E_1$, $\sigma_{1t}$ | Unidirectional, fibres aligned with load |
| 90° Tensile (D3039) | Matrix cracking, fibre/matrix debond | $\sigma_{2t}$, $E_2$ | UD, fibres transverse to load |
| ±45° Tensile | Shear yielding of matrix | $G_{12}$ | Angle-ply |
| 3PB Flexural (D790) | Compressive buckling (top), tensile (bottom), or shear (short beam) | $\sigma_f$, $E_f$ | UD or cross-ply |
| Short-beam shear (D2344) | Interlaminar shear (delamination) | ILSS | UD, $L/h = 4$ |
| Compression (D3410) | Fibre microbuckling, kink-band formation | $\sigma_{1c}$, $E_{1c}$ | UD |
| Charpy impact (D6110) | Mixed: delamination + fibre breakage + matrix shear | Impact energy (J/m) | Cross-ply or quasi-ISO |
| Drop-weight (D7136) | BVID (Barely Visible Impact Damage) | $E_{abs}$, peak load | Quasi-isotropic lay-up |
| CAI (D7137) | Sublaminate buckling post-impact | Residual $\sigma_{c,CAI}$ | QI laminate |
| Fatigue (D3479) | Progressive stiffness loss, final fracture | S–N curve, fatigue limit | UD or QI |

---

## 3.5 Theory of Composite Specific Strength

**Why specific strength matters in space:**
The structural mass fraction of a launch vehicle is critical. For a given load $P$, the cross-sectional area scales as $A = P / \sigma$. The mass scales as $m = \rho A L = (\rho/\sigma) P L$.

Therefore the mass-to-performance metric is the **specific strength**:

$$\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho} = \dfrac{\text{strength}}{\text{density}}$$

For carbon-fibre/epoxy: $\sigma/\rho \approx 970 \; \text{MPa} / 1.55 \; \text{g/cm}^{3} \approx 626 \; \text{kN\cdot m/kg}$

Compared to aluminium alloy 7075: $\sigma/\rho \approx 572 / 2.81 \approx 204 \; \text{kN\cdot m/kg}$

**Conclusion:** CFRP delivers **~3× the specific strength** of aluminium — explaining its dominance in space structures.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

- All node IDs are purely **alphanumeric** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `testA`).
- No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) used as standalone node names.
- All labels use raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Test Classification

```mermaid

    nodeA["MECHANICAL TESTING OF COMPOSITES"]
    nodeB["CONSTITUENT LEVEL TESTS"]

    nodeF["SINGLE FIBRE TENSILE TEST"]

    nodeA --> nodeB

    nodeB --> nodeF

    nodeC --> nodeK

    nodeC --> nodeN

    nodeD --> nodeO
    nodeD --> nodeP

    nodeE --> nodeR
    nodeE --> nodeS

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Coupon Test Architecture and Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    specA["SPECIMEN COUPON"]
    specB["STRAIN GAUGE OR EXTENSOMETER"]
    specC["UNIVERSAL TESTING MACHINE UTM"]
    specD["LOAD CELL"]
    specE["DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM DAQ"]
    specF["FAILURE MODE CLASSIFIER"]
    specG["COMPUTED PROPERTY"]
    specH["REPORT GENERATOR"]

    specA -->|MOUNTED IN GRIPS| specC
    specC -->|LOAD SIGNAL| specD
    specA -->|STRAIN SIGNAL| specB
    specB -->|ANALOG SIGNAL| specE
    specD -->|ANALOG SIGNAL| specE
    specE -->|LOAD VS STRAIN CURVE| specF
    specF -->|VISUAL INSPECTION| specH
    specE -->|NUMERIC DATA| specG
    specG -->|PROPERTY VALUE| specH
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Failure-Mode Map for a 3-Point Bending Specimen

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bendA["LOAD APPLICATION INCREASES"]
    bendB["WHICH FAILURE INITIATES FIRST"]
    bendC["MICROBUCKLING TOP FIBRE COMPRESSION"]
    bendD["TENSILE FRACTURE BOTTOM FIBRE"]
    bendE["INTERLAMINAR SHEAR DELAMINATION"]

    bendF["DOMINATES WHEN L OVER H LARGE"]
    bendG["DOMINATES WHEN L OVER H MODERATE"]
    bendH["DOMINATES WHEN L OVER H LESS THAN 5"]

    bendA --> bendB
    bendB --> bendC
    bendB --> bendD
    bendB --> bendE

    bendC --> bendF
    bendD --> bendF
    bendD --> bendG
    bendE --> bendH
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Specimen Geometry for the Three Principal Tests

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    geoA["RECTANGULAR COUPON WITH TABS"]
    geoB["GEOMETRY VARIANT BY TEST"]
    geoC["TENSILE COUPON D3039 L 150 TO 250 MM W 25 MM T 1 TO 3 MM"]
    geoD["COMPRESSION COUPON D3410 L 80 TO 150 MM W 12 MM T 2 TO 4 MM WITH IITRI FIXTURE"]
    geoE["FLEXURAL COUPON D790 L 16H W 12 TO 25 MM H 1 TO 6 MM"]
    geoF["SHORT BEAM SHEAR D2344 L 4H W 6 MM H 2 TO 3 MM"]

    geoA --> geoB
    geoB --> geoC
    geoB --> geoD
    geoB --> geoE
    geoB --> geoF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** For KTU examination diagrams, the examiner expects you to *labour the axis labels* (fibre direction, load direction, support points) and *numerically specify the span-to-depth ratio* whenever describing a bending test.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

> Define **Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS)** of a fibre-reinforced composite and name the standard test used to evaluate it. Write the relevant formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS) is the maximum shear stress a laminated composite can sustain in the through-thickness direction (along the plane of the plies) before delamination occurs. It is a **matrix-dominated** property governed by the shear strength of the resin and the quality of the fibre–matrix interfacial bond.

**Standard:** ASTM D2344 — Short-Beam Shear (SBS) test, using a three-point bending fixture with span-to-depth ratio $L/h = 4:1$.

**Formula:**

$$\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3 P_{max}}{4 \, b \, h}$$

where $P_{max}$ is the first-peak load, $b$ is specimen width, and $h$ is specimen thickness. Typical values: **70–110 MPa** for CFRP/epoxy.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

> What is **Compression After Impact (CAI)** strength, and why is it considered the most critical damage-tolerance metric for aerospace composites?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CAI strength is the residual compressive strength of a composite laminate that has been subjected to a standardized low-velocity impact (typically 6.67 J per ASTM D7136) and subsequently tested in compression (ASTM D7137).

**Why critical:** Low-velocity impacts (tool drops, runway debris, hail) cause **Barely Visible Impact Damage (BVID)** — internal delaminations that drastically reduce compressive strength (often by **40–60 %**) without visible external damage. For spacecraft structures loaded in compression (e.g., fairings, payload adapters), BVID can trigger catastrophic **sublaminate buckling** under service loads. Hence CAI defines the *knockdown factor* used in design allowables.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Choice A]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **three-point bending test** for polymer composites as per ASTM D790. Discuss the flexural-strength formula and identify the failure modes observed in 3-point bending of unidirectional composites. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A unidirectional carbon/epoxy composite is tested in short-beam shear (ASTM D2344). The specimen has dimensions: $b = 6$ **mm**, $h = 3$ **mm**, span $L = 12$ **mm** ($L/h = 4$). The recorded peak load is $P_{max} = 1200$ **N**. Compute the ILSS and comment on the quality of the fibre–matrix interface. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Diagram description (2 marks):**
The 3-point bending fixture consists of two cylindrical bottom supports and a single cylindrical top loading nose, with the specimen centrally loaded. The load is applied perpendicular to the flat face of the coupon. The span $L$ is the distance between the centres of the bottom supports, and the recommended span-to-depth ratio is $L/h = 16:1$.

**2. Procedure (1 mark):**
- Mount specimen centrally.
- Apply load at constant cross-head rate (typically 1–5 mm/min).
- Record load–deflection curve continuously.
- Identify first-peak load (for ILSS) or failure load (for flexural strength).

**3. Formula (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b h^{2}}$$

(derived earlier in Section 3.1).

**4. Failure modes (2 marks):**
- **Tensile failure at bottom fibre** — fibre rupture and matrix cracking on the tension side.
- **Compressive failure at top fibre** — fibre microbuckling and kink-band formation on the compression side.
- **Interlaminar shear failure** — when $L/h < 5$, horizontal delamination precedes flexural failure (used in the short-beam shear test).
- **Shear failure of matrix** — diagonal cracking near mid-span.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply ILSS formula (3 marks):**

$$\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3 P_{max}}{4 b h}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3 \times 1200}{4 \times 6 \times 3}$$

$$\tau_{ILSS} = \dfrac{3600}{72}$$

$$\tau_{ILSS} = 50 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on interfacial quality (4 marks):**

The measured ILSS value of **50 MPa** is at the **lower end** of the typical range for CFRP/epoxy systems (60–110 MPa). This indicates:
- **Sub-optimal fibre–matrix adhesion** — possible insufficient surface treatment of fibres.
- **Matrix-dominated failure**, possibly due to under-cured resin or excess plasticizer.
- **Recommendations:** Use fibre sizing (e.g., epoxy-compatible silane coating), improve cure cycle (e.g., 175 °C for 2 h), or consider toughened matrix systems (e.g., T800/3900-2 with ILSS ≈ 95 MPa).
- For space-grade applications, an ILSS below **70 MPa** is generally **not acceptable** for primary structures.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\tau_{ILSS} = 50 \; \text{MPa — borderline quality; redesign of interface recommended.}}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Choice B]**

> **(a)** Explain the **tensile testing** of unidirectional fibre-reinforced composites as per ASTM D3039. Discuss specimen geometry, gripping, tabbing, and typical failure modes for 0° and 90° fibre orientations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cross-ply CFRP laminate [0°/90°]₄ₛ is tested in tension. The coupon has width $w = 25$ **mm**, thickness $t = 2.4$ **mm**, gauge length $L_0 = 100$ **mm**. The peak load is $P_{max} = 35$ **kN** and the elongation at failure is $\Delta L = 0.9$ **mm**. Compute the tensile strength, Young's modulus, and the specific strength, given fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.6$ and densities $\rho_f = 1.80$ **g/cm³**, $\rho_m = 1.20$ **g/cm³**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Specimen geometry (2 marks):**
As per ASTM D3039:
- Rectangular straight-sided coupon.
- Length: 150–250 mm; gauge length: 100–150 mm.
- Width: 25 mm for UD tape, 12.5 mm for fabric.
- Thickness: 1–3 mm (typically 8 plies for 0.13 mm-thick prepreg).

**2. Tabs and gripping (2 marks):**
- End tabs (GFRP, ±45° woven) of length 50 mm, taper 5–10° bonded with FM300 adhesive.
- Tabs distribute gripping pressure and prevent grip-induced crushing (which would falsely reduce measured strength by 20–40 %).
- Hydraulic wedge grips with controlled pressure (3–7 MPa) recommended.

**3. Failure modes (3 marks):**
- **0° UD:** Fibre rupture — explosive, broom-shaped failure; longitudinal splitting precedes final fracture.
- **90° UD:** Matrix cracking — clean, flat fracture plane perpendicular to load; fibre/matrix debonding.
- **±45°:** Shear yielding of matrix — large inelastic deformation, sigmoid-shaped coupon.
- **Improper tabbing:** Delamination near tabs, premature failure.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area (1 mark):**

$$A_0 = w \times t = 25 \times 2.4 = 60 \; \text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Tensile strength (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{ult} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \dfrac{35000 \; \text{N}}{60 \; \text{mm}^{2}} = 583.3 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Young's modulus (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{0.9}{100} = 0.009$$

$$E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{583.3}{0.009} = 64.8 \; \text{GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Composite density (1 mark):**

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + (1 - V_f) \rho_m$$

$$\rho_c = 0.6 \times 1.80 + 0.4 \times 1.20 = 1.08 + 0.48 = 1.56 \; \text{g/cm}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Specific strength (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{583.3 \; \text{MPa}}{1.56 \; \text{g/cm}^{3}} \approx 374 \; \text{MPa\cdot cm}^{3}/\text{g}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{ult} = 583 \; \text{MPa}, \quad E = 64.8 \; \text{GPa}, \quad \sigma/\rho = 374 \; \text{MPa\cdot cm}^{3}/\text{g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The specific strength of **374 MPa·cm³/g** is approximately **1.6×** that of aluminium alloy 7075 (~225 MPa·cm³/g), making this laminate a strong candidate for satellite structural panels.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Mechanical Testing of Composites**
> 1. **Forgetting span-to-depth ratio** — Examiners frequently dock 1–2 marks if you write "3-point bending test" without specifying $L/h$. Always state the ratio: 16:1 for flexural strength, 4:1 for ILSS.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — $L$, $b$, $h$ in mm, load in N → strength in MPa (N/mm²). Do **not** convert to SI units mid-calculation.
> 3. **Ignoring anisotropy** — A common error is reporting "the tensile strength of carbon/epoxy" without specifying fibre orientation. Always state 0°, 90°, or ±45°.
> 4. **Wrong ILSS formula** — Some students write $\tau = P/(bh)$. The correct coefficient is $3/4$, not $1$.
> 5. **Missing tab purpose** — In tensile test answers, students often forget to explain *why* end tabs are used (stress concentration relief).
> 6. **Not mentioning CAI** — For space-aerospace questions on damage tolerance, failure to mention Compression After Impact (CAI) is considered an incomplete answer.
> 7. **Treating composites as isotropic** — Never apply Hooke's law with a single $E$; use orthotropic relations $E_1$, $E_2$, $G_{12}$, $\nu_{12}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanical Testing of Composites**
>
> ☐ Composites are **anisotropic** — always specify fibre orientation in test answers.
>
> ☐ The five primary tests: **Tensile (D3039), Compression (D3410), Flexural (D790), Short-beam shear (D2344), Impact (D6110/D7136)**.
>
> ☐ Three-point bending formula: $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bh^2)$; flexural modulus: $E_f = L^{3}m/(4bh^{3})$.
>
> ☐ Short-beam shear formula: $\tau_{ILSS} = 3P_{max}/(4bh)$; uses $L/h = 4:1$.
>
> ☐ Tensile formula: $\sigma = P/A_0$, $E = \Delta\sigma/\Delta\varepsilon$; **end tabs mandatory** for UD composites.
>
> ☐ Compression: IITRI fixture, microbuckling is dominant failure mode.
>
> ☐ **CAI** (Compression After Impact) is the **gold-standard damage-tolerance metric** in aerospace.
>
> ☐ **Fatigue** of composites shows progressive stiffness loss, *no* sharp fatigue limit like steel.
>
> ☐ Specimen geometries (memorize):
>  – Tensile: 150–250 × 25 × 1–3 mm (with tabs).
>  – Flexural: $L/h = 16:1$ (D790) or $32:1$ (D7264).
>  – ILSS: $L/h = 4:1$.
>
> ☐ **Specific strength** ($\sigma/\rho$) and **specific modulus** ($E/\rho$) drive selection for space.
>
> ☐ Typical CFRP/epoxy values (memorize for numericals):
>  – $E_1 \approx 135$ GPa, $\sigma_{1t} \approx 1500$ MPa.
>  – ILSS $\approx 70$–110 MPa.
>  – $\rho \approx 1.55$–1.60 g/cm³.
>
> ☐ Failure modes (memorize): **fibre rupture (0° tensile), matrix cracking (90° tensile), microbuckling/kink-band (compression), interlaminar shear/delamination (ILSS and impact)**.
>
> ☐ Standards to cite: ASTM D3039, D3410, D790, D2344, D6110, D7136, D7137, D3479; ISO 527 equivalents.
>
> ☐ For space applications, always discuss **outgassing (TML < 1 %)** alongside mechanical testing — they are co-requirements for spacecraft qualification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intra-laminar Shear Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intra-laminar Shear Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intra-laminar Shear Testing** is a family of standardized mechanical characterization techniques employed to determine the **in-plane shear properties** (specifically, the in-plane shear strength $S_{12}$ or $S_{L T}$ and the in-plane shear modulus $G_{12}$ or $G_{L T}$) of a **single unidirectional ply (lamina)** within a fibre-reinforced polymer matrix composite (FRPC), by inducing a state of **pure shear** within the principal material coordinate system of that ply.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT746)**, intra-laminar testing is distinguished from **inter-laminar** (through-thickness) testing because the shear plane lies **parallel to the fibre direction and within the same ply**, rather than between adjacent plies. For space-grade composites (CFRP, GFRP, cyanate esters, PEEK, PMR-15, etc.), intra-laminar shear governs matrix-dominated failure modes such as matrix microcracking, fibre/matrix debonding, and in-plane shear buckling of stiffened space panels.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Sticky Deck of Cards"**
> Imagine a deck of playing cards glued together with a thin layer of rubber cement (the matrix). If you slide the **top card** forward while holding the **bottom card** fixed, the rubber cement layer between any *two* cards experiences **inter-laminar shear**. Now imagine an alternative: hold a **single thick card** in both hands and try to distort it so that it becomes a **parallelogram** (the rectangle's top edge slides to the right relative to the bottom edge). The internal fibres and matrix of that *one* card experience **intra-laminar shear**. The card is the *lamina*, the parallelogram distortion is the *shear strain γ₁₂*, and the force you apply divided by the card's cross-section is the *shear stress τ₁₂*.

## 1.3 Why It Matters for Space Structures

- Spacecraft structures (satellite panels, antenna reflectors, rocket motor casings, payload fairings) experience **combined in-plane shear + axial loads** during launch, orbital manoeuvring, and thermal cycling.
- Intra-laminar shear strength is often the **weakest in-plane property** in a unidirectional lamina and governs **first-ply failure** envelopes.
- Empirical data are mandatory for input into **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)** and **Puck / Tsai-Wu / Hashin** failure criteria used in KTU-level design projects.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Constant & Definitions)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (CFRP) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| In-plane shear strength | $S_{L T}$ or $S_{12}$ | **60 – 100** | **MPa** |
| In-plane shear modulus | $G_{L T}$ or $G_{12}$ | **4.0 – 7.0** | **GPa** |
| In-plane shear strain at failure | $\gamma_{12}^{ult}$ | **0.02 – 0.05** | **–** (dimensionless) |
| Major Poisson's ratio | $\nu_{L T}$ | **0.25 – 0.35** | – |
| Standard lab temperature | $T_{ref}$ | **23 ± 2** | **°C** |
| Standard lab humidity | RH | **50 ± 10** | **%** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Pure Intra-Laminar Shear Is Generated

Generating a region of **pure, uniform shear** within a single lamina is experimentally non-trivial because real specimens also experience bending, transverse normal stress, and edge effects. The three most common KTU-recognised strategies are:

### Step 1 — Define the Material Coordinate Frame
For a unidirectional lamina, the **1-axis (L)** is parallel to the fibre direction, the **2-axis (T)** is transverse to the fibre in the plane, and the **3-axis** is through the thickness.

### Step 2 — Apply a Loading That Maximises τ₁₂ While Minimising σ₁, σ₂, σ₃
- **Strategy A (Iosipescu / V-notched beam):** Two symmetric V-notches create a **pure shear zone** in the specimen mid-section under opposite couples. Bending moment cancels in the central region → uniform $\tau_{12}$.
- **Strategy B (±45° off-axis tension):** A specimen with fibres oriented at $\pm 45°$ to the load axis experiences $\sigma_x$ in the test frame. Rotating $-45°$ to the material frame converts this into pure $\sigma_{11}$ and $\sigma_{22}$ components, producing a state equivalent to **pure intra-laminar shear** on the $1{-}2$ plane.
- **Strategy C (Rail / Two-rail shear):** The specimen is clamped along both longitudinal edges and a relative axial displacement is enforced, producing a uniform shear strain field.

### Step 3 — Instrument with Biaxial Strain Gauges (Rosette)
A **rectangular rosette** ($0°/45°/90°$) is bonded to the specimen centre to extract the engineering shear strain $\gamma_{12}$:

$$
\gamma_{12} = \varepsilon_{0} - \varepsilon_{90}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Stress from Force / Geometry
For each method, $\tau_{12}$ is calculated from the applied load and the local cross-section at the pure-shear region.

### Step 5 — Plot the τ–γ Curve and Extract Engineering Properties
- **Shear modulus** $G_{12}$ = slope of the linear elastic region (typically between $\gamma_{12} = 0.0005$ and $0.0025$).
- **Shear strength** $S_{12}$ = maximum $\tau_{12}$ before failure.

## 2.2 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Strategy

| Strategy | 'Why' It Produces Pure Shear | 'How' It Is Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| **Iosipescu (ASTM D5379)** | Symmetric V-notches convert a bending-dominated load into a **scissoring** action; at the notch root the moment is zero, leaving pure shear. | Opposite-direction couples applied via fixture; force $P$ measured by load cell. |
| **±45° Tension (ASTM D3518)** | Coordinate rotation of uniaxial tension $\sigma_x$ by $\pm 45°$ yields equal-magnitude principal stresses in 1- and 2-axes of opposite sign → **maximum shear stress state**. | Rectangular coupon with fibres at $\pm 45°$ to load axis, pulled in a standard tensile machine. |
| **Two-Rail Shear (ASTM D4255)** | Kinematic constraint: the bottom rail moves opposite to the top rail, imposing a **uniform displacement field** $\Delta u = \gamma_{12} \cdot h$. | Specimen clamped between two rigid rails; bolts apply clamping pressure. |
| **Off-Axis Tension (ASTM D3039)** | A unidirectional specimen loaded at angle $\theta$ to the fibres produces a combination of $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12}$; back-calculation via **classical transformation** isolates $\tau_{12}$. | Single off-axis coupon ($\theta = 10°$ is common for high $G_{12}$ composites). |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorise These Equations for the ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering shear strain (rosette) | $\gamma_{12} = \varepsilon_0 - \varepsilon_{90}$ | – | Rectangular rosette, small strain |
| 2 | Shear strain (full rosette) | $\gamma_{12} = 2\varepsilon_{45} - (\varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon_{90})$ | – | Account for Poisson effect |
| 3 | Iosipescu shear stress | $\tau_{12} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot h}$ | MPa | $P$ = applied load, $w$ = width at notch, $h$ = distance between notch roots |
| 4 | ±45° shear stress | $\tau_{12} = \dfrac{P}{2 A} = \dfrac{P}{2 w t}$ | MPa | $A$ = full cross-section, $t$ = thickness |
| 5 | Shear modulus | $G_{12} = \dfrac{\tau_{12}}{\gamma_{12}}$ | GPa | Linear region of τ–γ curve |
| 6 | Shear strength | $S_{12} = \tau_{12}^{\max}$ | MPa | Peak value before failure |
| 7 | Stress transformation | $\tau_{12} = -\sigma_x \sin\theta \cos\theta$ | MPa | For $\theta = 45°$, $\tau_{12} = \pm 0.5\,\sigma_x$ |
| 8 | Strain transformation | $\gamma_{12} = 2(\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + (\gamma_{xy})(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$ | – | Off-axis case |
| 9 | Relationship to engineering constants | $\dfrac{1}{G_{12}} = \dfrac{4}{E_{45°}} - \dfrac{1}{E_1} - \dfrac{1}{E_2} + \dfrac{2\nu_{12}}{E_1}$ | – | Used in ±45° test reduction |
| 10 | Compliance contribution | $S_{66} = \dfrac{1}{G_{12}}$ | 1/GPa | Reduced compliance in $[S]$ matrix |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations feed directly into the **A, B, D** matrices of Classical Lamination Theory. The $[S]$ (compliance) matrix entry $S_{66} = 1/G_{12}$ is required for every CLT problem — a guaranteed question type in Module 4.

## 2.4 Practical Utility in Space Industry

- **Satellite qualification:** ECSS (European Cooperation for Space Standardisation) requires shear allowables for $G_{12}$ and $S_{12}$ to be reported in material property datasheets.
- **Damage tolerance:** Intra-laminar shear cracks appear as the first sub-damage under low-velocity impact and are precursors to delamination.
- **Manufacturing QA:** Statistical process control charts for $G_{12}$ monitor cure quality of prepreg layups.
- **Micromechanics validation:** Experimental $G_{12}$ is benchmarked against the rule of mixtures:
$$
G_{12}^{ROM} = \dfrac{G_m}{1 - V_f \left( 1 - \dfrac{G_m}{G_{f12}} \right)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Shear Stress in the ±45° Tensile Test

> **Goal:** Show that a uniaxial tension $P$ on a $\pm 45°$ specimen produces pure intra-laminar shear $\tau_{12} = P/(2wt)$.

### Step 1 — Start with the global stress state in the test (x–y) frame

A standard tensile coupon carries only a normal stress in the loading direction. The global stress tensor in the test frame is:

$$
\sigma^{xy} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The axial stress is computed from the applied load and the full cross-section:

$$
\sigma_x = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot t}
$$

Here $w$ is the specimen **width** and $t$ is the **thickness**.

### Step 2 — Apply the in-plane stress transformation equation

The general in-plane stress transformation for rotation by angle $\theta$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\sigma_2 &= \sigma_x \sin^2\theta + \sigma_y \cos^2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\tau_{12} &= -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

For our case, $\sigma_y = 0$ and $\tau_{xy} = 0$, so the expression simplifies to:

$$
\tau_{12} = -\sigma_x \sin\theta \cos\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute θ = 45° (fibre orientation relative to load axis)

Recall the trigonometric identity $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{12} &= -\sigma_x \cdot \sin(45°)\cos(45°) \\
&= -\sigma_x \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) \\
&= -\sigma_x \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign is a **convention indicator** showing the shear acts on the negative face in the positive direction; its magnitude is:

$$
\vert \tau_{12} \vert = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2} = \dfrac{P}{2 w t}
$$

This is the canonical KTU board result.

### Step 4 — Express the shear strain from rosette data

The shear strain in the material frame, using the rectangular rosette where the $\pm 45°$ gauges of the **specimen** align with the $1$ and $2$ material axes (because the fibres are at $\pm 45°$), is:

$$
\gamma_{12} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2
$$

If the rosette is bonded **on the specimen in the test frame** (fibres at 45°, gauges at 0°, 45°, 90°), use the **full rosette** formula:

$$
\gamma_{12} = 2\varepsilon_{45} - (\varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon_{90})
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the in-plane shear modulus

The slope of the linear τ₁₂–γ₁₂ curve gives $G_{12}$:

$$
G_{12} = \dfrac{\tau_{12}}{\gamma_{12}} = \dfrac{P / (2 w t)}{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation B — Stress Concentration Reduction in Iosipescu

> **Goal:** Justify why the Iosipescu specimen produces uniform shear between the notch roots.

### Step 1 — Free-body of the central section

Consider a thin slice of the specimen between the two V-notch roots. The applied couples create forces $V$ on the top and bottom of the slice.

### Step 2 — Shear force equilibrium

Vertical equilibrium of the slice gives:

$$
V_{top} = V_{bottom} = V
$$

The shear force is $V = P/2$ (each notch carries half the applied load).

### Step 3 — Bending moment in the central section

By symmetry, the bending moment at the mid-length of the specimen is **zero**:

$$
M_{mid} = 0
$$

This is the key insight — the moment diagram crosses zero at the centre, so the section between the notches experiences **pure shear** with no normal stress contribution.

### Step 4 — Average shear stress between notches

The cross-section between the notches has width $w$ (at the notch root) and height $h$ (notch-to-notch distance). Average shear stress:

$$
\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{V}{w \cdot h} = \dfrac{P}{2 w h}
$$

### Step 5 — FEA correction factor (Industry refinement)

In practice, finite-element analysis shows the shear stress is **not exactly uniform** — there is a peak at the notch roots. A correction factor $K$ is applied:

$$
\tau_{12}^{corrected} = K \cdot \dfrac{P}{2 w h}
$$

For the standard Iosipescu geometry with $90°$ V-notches, $K \approx 0.9$ to $1.0$ in the central region. KTU problems typically ignore $K$ for board examinations.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Compliance Relationship for the [S] Matrix

The reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ of a unidirectional lamina in material coordinates is:

$$
Q = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The corresponding compliance matrix $[S]$ is the inverse:

$$
S = Q^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} & 0 \\ S_{12} & S_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_{66} \end{bmatrix}
$$

By direct inversion of the $2 \times 2$ block, the off-diagonal block gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{66} &= \dfrac{1}{Q_{66}} = \dfrac{1}{G_{12}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why **measuring $G_{12}$ intra-laminar shear is non-optional** — it is the only path to the $[S]$ matrix element that decouples from the other components.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A ±45° CFRP coupon (T700/2510) is tested per ASTM D3518. Width $w = 25$ mm, thickness $t = 2$ mm. Applied load at failure $P_f = 8.5$ kN. At 50% of failure load, rosette readings are $\varepsilon_0 = 0.0042$, $\varepsilon_{45} = 0.0028$, $\varepsilon_{90} = -0.0014$. Compute (a) the in-plane shear strength $S_{12}$ and (b) the in-plane shear modulus $G_{12}$ at 50% load.

### Part (a) — Shear strength

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_x &= \dfrac{P_f}{w t} = \dfrac{8500 \text{ N}}{(0.025 \text{ m})(0.002 \text{ m})} = 170 \text{ MPa} \\
S_{12} &= \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2} = \dfrac{170}{2} = 85 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Shear modulus at 50% load

At 50% load: $P_{50} = 4.25$ kN.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{12,50} &= \dfrac{P_{50}}{2 w t} = \dfrac{4250}{2 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 0.002} = 42.5 \text{ MPa} \\
\gamma_{12,50} &= 2\varepsilon_{45} - (\varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon_{90}) \\
&= 2(0.0028) - (0.0042 + (-0.0014)) \\
&= 0.0056 - 0.0028 = 0.0028 \\
G_{12} &= \dfrac{\tau_{12,50}}{\gamma_{12,50}} = \dfrac{42.5 \text{ MPa}}{0.0028} = 15.18 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

This high value indicates a high-modulus CFRP system; the result must be sanity-checked against the rule-of-mixtures estimate for $G_{12}$ from fibre and matrix data.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Intra-Laminar Shear Testing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Composite Lamina Preparation] --> B[Specimen Geometry Selection]
    B --> C1[Iosipescu V-Notch Coupon]
    B --> C2[Plus Minus 45 deg Tensile Coupon]
    B --> C3[Two Rail Shear Coupon]
    B --> C4[Off Axis Tensile Coupon]
    C1 --> D1[Apply Opposite Couples via Fixture]
    C2 --> D2[Apply Uniaxial Tension on Universal Testing Machine]
    C3 --> D3[Apply Relative Axial Rail Displacement]
    C4 --> D4[Apply Uniaxial Tension at Angle Theta]
    D1 --> E1[Measure Load P and Notch Geometry w and h]
    D2 --> E2[Measure Load P and Cross Section w and t]
    D3 --> E3[Measure Rail Displacement Delta and Specimen Length L]
    D4 --> E4[Measure Load P and Cross Section w and t]
    E1 --> F1[Compute tau12 from P over w h]
    E2 --> F2[Compute tau12 from P over 2 w t]
    E3 --> F3[Compute gamma12 from Delta over L]
    E4 --> F4[Compute tau12 via Stress Transformation]
    F1 --> G[Strain Rosette Instrumentation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Compute gamma12 from Rectangular or Full Rosette]
    H --> I[Plot tau12 versus gamma12 Curve]
    I --> J[Extract G12 from Linear Slope]
    I --> K[Extract S12 from Peak Value]
    J --> L[Report to CLT and Failure Criteria]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Material Allowable Datasheet for Space Structure]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Tree for Test Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Selection] --> q1{Is the Lamina Unidirectional?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is Pure Shear State Mandatory?}
    q1 -- No --> q1b[Use V Notch or Rail Shear on Laminated Plate]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3a[Choose Iosipescu Test ASTM D5379]
    q2 -- No --> q3b{Is High Throughput Required?}
    q3b -- Yes --> q3c[Choose Plus Minus 45 deg Test ASTM D3518]
    q3b -- No --> q3d{Is Specimen Size Constrained?}
    q3d -- Yes --> q3e[Choose Off Axis Tension ASTM D3039]
    q3d -- No --> q3a
    q3a --> out1[Recommended for Space Grade CFRP]
    q3c --> out2[Recommended for Quality Control and Screening]
    q3e --> out3[Recommended for Coupon Level Screening]
    q1b --> out4[Valid for Thick Laminate Screening Only]
```

```mermaid
        a2 --> a3[Specimen Central Section]

    end
    a3 --> b1[Pure Shear Zone Between Notches]
    b1 --> c1[Rosette at Centre]

```

The block diagrams above accurately capture the **functional architecture** — load path, instrumentation chain, and data reduction flow — which is what the KTU 2024 examiner values.
