# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between intra-laminar shear and inter-laminar shear testing in polymer composites. Which ASTM standard is most commonly used for each?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Intra-laminar shear** testing determines the in-plane shear properties ($G_{12}$, $S_{12}$) of a single ply. The shear plane lies **within the lamina**, parallel to the fibres. Common standards: **ASTM D5379 (Iosipescu)** and **ASTM D3518 (±45° tension)**.
- **Inter-laminar shear** testing determines the through-thickness shear strength $\tau_{13}$ between adjacent plies. The shear plane is **parallel to the laminate plane but acts between plies**. Common standard: **ASTM D2344 (Short Beam Shear, 3-point bending)**. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for test identification, 1 mark for distinguishing the shear plane location).**

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**A ±45° tensile specimen (width 25 mm, thickness 2 mm) fails at a load of 9 kN. Calculate the in-plane shear strength.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_x &= \dfrac{P}{w t} = \dfrac{9000}{0.025 \times 0.002} = 180 \text{ MPa} \\
S_{12} &= \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2} = \dfrac{180}{2} = 90 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer with units).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the in-plane shear stress $\tau_{12}$ in a ±45° tensile test specimen, starting from the uniaxial stress state in the test frame and applying the stress transformation equations. State the relevant ASTM standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data are obtained from a ±45° tensile test on a T800/cyanate-ester composite coupon: width = 25 mm, thickness = 2.4 mm, load at 40% of $P_f$ = 3.6 kN, rosette readings at 40% $P_f$: $\varepsilon_0 = 0.0036$, $\varepsilon_{45} = 0.0014$, $\varepsilon_{90} = -0.0008$. Calculate (i) the in-plane shear modulus $G_{12}$, and (ii) the in-plane shear strength $S_{12}$ if the failure load is $P_f = 9.0$ kN. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Test frame stress state (1 mark):** A ±45° tensile coupon loaded axially with force $P$ carries only a normal stress $\sigma_x = P/(wt)$ in the test (x–y) frame:

$$
\sigma^{xy} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Stress transformation (2 marks):** Rotating by $\theta = 45°$ into the material (1–2) frame, using the standard in-plane stress transformation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \sigma_x \cos^2 45° + 2\tau_{xy}\sin 45°\cos 45° \\
\sigma_2 &= \sigma_x \sin^2 45° - 2\tau_{xy}\sin 45°\cos 45° \\
\tau_{12} &= -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 45°\cos 45° + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2 45° - \sin^2 45°)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\sigma_y = 0$ and $\tau_{xy} = 0$ (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_{12} = -\sigma_x \sin 45° \cos 45° = -\dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of shear stress (1 mark):**

$$
\vert \tau_{12} \vert = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2} = \dfrac{P}{2 w t}
$$

**Step 5 — ASTM standard (1 mark):** **ASTM D3518 / D3518M** is the standard test method for *In-Plane Shear Response of Polymer Matrix Composite Materials by Tensile Test of a ±45° Laminate*. Note: the standard tests a **laminate** of $\pm 45°$ plies, not a single off-axis ply, which is why this method is sometimes called the ±45° *laminate* test.

**Step 6 — Why ±45° produces pure shear (1 mark):** At $\theta = 45°$, $\sin\theta\cos\theta = 0.5$ is at its maximum, and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_x/2$. The principal stresses are equal, so the **maximum shear stress theory** state is achieved with $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$ replaced by the actual $\tau_{12}$ in the material frame.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Shear modulus at 40% of $P_f$ (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x,40} &= \dfrac{P_{40}}{w t} = \dfrac{3600}{0.025 \times 0.0024} = 60 \text{ MPa} \\
\tau_{12,40} &= \dfrac{\sigma_{x,40}}{2} = 30 \text{ MPa} \\
\gamma_{12,40} &= 2\varepsilon_{45} - (\varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon_{90}) \\
&= 2(0.0014) - (0.0036 + (-0.0008)) \\
&= 0.0028 - 0.0028 = 0.0000 \text{ … (check: re-evaluate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Correction during evaluation:** The rosette readings are inconsistent with the expected signs (a ±45° laminate usually shows $\gamma_{12} > 0$). Recomputing with proper sign convention:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \gamma_{12} &= \varepsilon_0 - \varepsilon_{90} = 0.0036 - (-0.0008) = 0.0044 \\
> G_{12} &= \dfrac{\tau_{12,40}}{\gamma_{12}} = \dfrac{30 \text{ MPa}}{0.0044} = 6.82 \text{ GPa}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

**(ii) Shear strength at $P_f$ = 9.0 kN (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x,f} &= \dfrac{9000}{0.025 \times 0.0024} = 150 \text{ MPa} \\
S_{12} &= \dfrac{\sigma_{x,f}}{2} = 75 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $G_{12} = 6.82$ GPa, $S_{12} = 75$ MPa.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Iosipescu shear test** for determining the in-plane shear properties of a unidirectional composite lamina. State the relevant ASTM standard and explain why the central section between the notches experiences pure shear. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An Iosipescu specimen has a width between notches $w = 12$ mm and notch-to-notch distance $h = 20$ mm. The applied load at failure is $P_f = 12$ kN. A strain rosette at the centre reads $\varepsilon_0 = 0.0010$, $\varepsilon_{45} = 0.0026$, $\varepsilon_{90} = 0.0042$ at a load of $P = 6$ kN. Calculate the in-plane shear modulus $G_{12}$ and the in-plane shear strength $S_{12}$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Iosipescu Test Description (7 marks)

**1. Specimen geometry (2 marks):** A rectangular straight-sided coupon of width ~20 mm, length ~75 mm, thickness equal to the as-fabricated lamina thickness. Two **V-notches** (typically 90° included angle, ~2.5 mm deep) are machined at the mid-length on opposite faces.

**2. Loading configuration (2 marks):** The specimen is mounted in the Iosipescu fixture such that the upper edge is loaded **downward** on one notch side and the lower edge is loaded **upward** on the other side, producing a **scissoring / couple** action. ASTM standard: **ASTM D5379 / D5379M**.

**3. Pure shear justification (2 marks):** Bending moment diagram of the notched section has a **zero-crossing** at the mid-length. Vertical force equilibrium gives $V = P/2$ at each notch. Therefore, the section between the notches carries shear force with **zero bending moment** and (in an idealised case) **zero transverse normal stress** — i.e., a state of pure shear.

**4. Shear stress formula (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_{12} = \dfrac{P}{2 w h}
$$

where $w$ = width at notch root, $h$ = notch-to-notch distance.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Shear modulus from rosette (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{12} &= 2\varepsilon_{45} - (\varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon_{90}) \\
&= 2(0.0026) - (0.0010 + 0.0042) \\
&= 0.0052 - 0.0052 = 0.0000 \text{ (anomalous; use rectangular formula)} \\
\gamma_{12} &= \varepsilon_0 - \varepsilon_{90} = 0.0010 - 0.0042 = -0.0032 \\
\vert \gamma_{12} \vert &= 0.0032
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{12} \text{ at } P=6 \text{ kN} &= \dfrac{6000}{2 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 0.020} = 12.5 \text{ MPa} \\
G_{12} &= \dfrac{12.5 \text{ MPa}}{0.0032} = 3.91 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Shear strength (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{12} &= \dfrac{P_f}{2 w h} = \dfrac{12000}{2 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 0.020} = 25 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $G_{12} = 3.91$ GPa, $S_{12} = 25$ MPa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 in the ±45° test.** The most common error is writing $S_{12} = P/(wt)$ instead of $S_{12} = P/(2wt)$. This is a **2-mark penalty** in typical KTU grading.
> 2. **Confusing rosette conventions.** For a **±45° specimen** the material 1-axis aligns with the **45° gauge**, not the 0° gauge. For an **Iosipescu** or **on-axis** specimen, the material 1-axis aligns with the **0° gauge**. Using the wrong rosette formula leads to incorrect $\gamma_{12}$.
> 3. **Ignoring the moment-zeroing principle in Iosipescu.** Examiners expect you to mention that the central section is moment-free (zero normal stress) — this is the **core concept** and worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up intra-laminar vs. inter-laminar.** Inter-laminar (short beam shear, ASTM D2344) is a **3-point bend** test on a short span; do not confuse it with Iosipescu. Wrong identification = full marks lost on the standard question.
> 5. **Not reporting units.** KTU grading is strict; MPa/GPa units on every numerical answer is mandatory.
> 6. **Using $E$ instead of $G_{12}$ in the compliance matrix.** In the $[S]$ matrix, the (3,3) entry is $S_{66} = 1/G_{12}$, **not** $1/E_1$ or $1/E_2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Intra-laminar** = shear within a single ply; **inter-laminar** = shear between plies.
- [x] Three primary methods: **Iosipescu (D5379)**, **±45° tension (D3518)**, **two-rail shear (D4255)**.
- [x] **±45° test** stress formula: $\tau_{12} = P / (2 w t)$ (memorise the factor of 1/2).
- [x] **Iosipescu** stress formula: $\tau_{12} = P / (2 w h)$, where $w$ = notch width, $h$ = notch-to-notch distance.
- [x] Rosette extraction: $\gamma_{12} = 2\varepsilon_{45} - (\varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon_{90})$ (full rosette).
- [x] $G_{12} = \tau_{12} / \gamma_{12}$ from the **linear** region of the τ–γ curve.
- [x] $S_{12} = \tau_{12}^{max}$ at failure.
- [x] $S_{66}$ compliance entry $= 1/G_{12}$ — feeds directly into CLT.
- [x] Pure shear region in Iosipescu arises because the **bending moment is zero** between the notches.
- [x] Pure shear in ±45° arises because the **stress transformation maximises $\sin\theta\cos\theta$** at $\theta = 45°$.
- [x] For space-grade CFRP, typical $G_{12} = 4$–$7$ GPa and $S_{12} = 60$–$100$ MPa.
- [x] Standard lab conditions: **23 ± 2 °C, 50 ± 10% RH**.
- [x] Failure mode in ±45° test is typically **matrix shear yielding → fibre rotation → diffuse damage**, not clean shear fracture.
- [x] In KTU board exams, **always state the ASTM standard number** when asked about a test method.
- [x] Numerical answers must include **units**; missing units = up to 1-mark deduction.
- [x] Iosipescu specimen is preferred for **pure shear modulus**; ±45° is preferred for **screening/QC** because of simple geometry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fracture Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fracture Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fracture Testing** is a suite of standardized experimental methodologies employed to quantify the resistance of a material — particularly polymers and polymer composites used in space structures — to the propagation of cracks and the ultimate failure of the material under mechanical, thermal, or environmental loading. It involves applying controlled stress fields to specimens containing known or induced flaws and recording the critical parameters (force, displacement, energy) at the onset of unstable crack growth.

In the context of the **KTU PEPOT746 syllabus**, fracture testing of polymers for space applications focuses on how polymeric materials behave under:

- **Monotonic quasi-static loading** (tensile/flexural fracture)
- **Dynamic/impact loading** (Charpy, Izod, Falling Weight)
- **Fracture mechanics characterization** (Stress Intensity Factor $K_{IC}$, Strain Energy Release Rate $G_{IC}$, J-integral $J_c$)
- **Environment-assisted cracking** (thermal cycling, vacuum outgassing, UV/ionizing radiation induced embrittlement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - *Strength* = resistance to plastic deformation.
> - *Toughness* = resistance to fracture (energy absorption).
> - *Fracture Toughness* = quantitative measure of a material's resistance to crack propagation when a sharp crack is present.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Cracked Drinking Glass**
> Imagine a glass tumbler with a tiny invisible scratch on its rim. You can pour cold water into it hundreds of times without issue. But one day, you pour boiling water — and it shatters instantly. Why? Because the **stress concentration at the crack tip** combined with thermal shock exceeded the glass's **fracture toughness** ($K_{IC}$).
> In space, a polymer — say, a **Kapton thermal blanket** or an **Epoxy-carbon composite panel** — may carry micro-defects from manufacturing, micrometeoroid impacts, or thermal-cycling fatigue. Fracture testing is the engineering discipline that asks: *"At what stress level, and at what energy input, will that hidden crack suddenly run?"* This is critical because in orbit, there is **no possibility of repair**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following physical quantities and standard symbols form the **core vocabulary** of any KTU answer on Fracture Testing. Mastery of these is essential for scoring in ESE and internal assessments.

- **$K_{IC}$** — Plane-Strain Fracture Toughness ($\text{MPa}\cdot\sqrt{\text{m}}$)
- **$G_{IC}$** — Critical Strain Energy Release Rate ($\text{J/m}^2$ or $\text{N/m}$)
- **$J_c$** — Critical value of the J-integral ($\text{kJ/m}^2$)
- **$a$** — Crack length ($\text{m}$ or $\text{mm}$)
- **$\sigma_c$** — Critical applied stress at fracture ($\text{MPa}$)
- **$E$** — Young's Modulus ($\text{GPa}$)
- **$\nu$** — Poisson's ratio (dimensionless)
- **$K_t$** — Stress concentration factor (dimensionless)
- **$a_c$** — Critical (limiting) crack length ($\text{m}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Space Polymers:** Polymers in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and Geostationary Orbit (GEO) are exposed to atomic oxygen (AO), UV radiation, electron/proton bombardment, and thermal cycling between **$-150^\circ\text{C}$ and $+150^\circ\text{C}$**. These degrade fracture toughness over time. **Fracture testing gives the quantitative baseline for predicting in-orbit service life.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Fracture Mechanics

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate the **fracture mechanics dichotomy**:

| Pillar | Focus | Key Quantity | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)** | Sharp cracks in brittle/elastic materials | Stress Intensity Factor $K$ | Glassy polymers, epoxies, ceramic-matrix composites |
| **Elastic-Plastic Fracture Mechanics (EPFM)** | Crack-tip plasticity dominates | J-integral $J$, CTOD $\delta$ | Ductile polymers, elastomers, toughened thermoplastics |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Crack-Tip Stress Field (Williams' Solution)

Around a sharp crack tip in an isotropic linear-elastic body, the **mode I** (opening) stress field in polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{xx} &= \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\!\left[1 - \sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{3\theta}{2}\right)\right] \\
\sigma_{yy} &= \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\!\left[1 + \sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{3\theta}{2}\right)\right] \\
\tau_{xy} &= \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{3\theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Why this matters:** It shows that stresses at the crack tip scale as $1/\sqrt{r}$ → they are **singular** as $r \to 0$. Classical strength-of-materials yield criteria fail here. This is the foundation of fracture mechanics.

### Step 2 — Griffith's Energy Criterion (1921)

A crack will propagate if the **elastic strain energy released** by its growth exceeds the **surface energy** required to create the new crack faces.

$$
G \geq 2\gamma_s
$$

Where:
- $G$ = Strain energy release rate ($\text{J/m}^2$)
- $\gamma_s$ = Specific surface energy of the material ($\text{J/m}^2$)

For an infinite plate with central crack of length $2a$ under remote tensile stress $\sigma$:

$$
G = \frac{\pi a \sigma^2}{E}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Griffith Insight:** A crack grows not because the average stress is high, but because the *energy balance* favors the creation of new surfaces. Doubling the crack length **quadruples** the energy release rate (for fixed $\sigma$).

### Step 3 — Irwin's Modification and the Stress Intensity Factor

Irwin extended Griffith's work to include **plastic dissipation** at the crack tip. He defined:

$$
G = \frac{K_I^2}{E'} \quad \text{where} \quad E' = \begin{cases} E & \text{(plane stress)} \\ \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2} & \text{(plane strain)} \end{cases}
$$

The **critical condition** for unstable fracture is:

$$
K_I = K_{IC}
$$

### Step 4 — Fracture Toughness of Polymers (Space-Grade Data)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical $K_{IC}$ values relevant to PEPOT746:**

- **PMMA (Plexiglas):** $\sim 1.0\text{–}1.6\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ (brittle)
- **Epoxy (cured DGEBA):** $\sim 0.5\text{–}1.0\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ (highly cross-linked, brittle)
- **Polycarbonate (Lexan):** $\sim 2.0\text{–}3.0\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ (tough, ductile)
- **PEEK (Victrex):** $\sim 3.0\text{–}5.0\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ (high-performance thermoplastic, used in space)
- **Kapton HN (PI film):** $\sim 1.5\text{–}2.5\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ (used in MLI blankets)

### Step 5 — Impact Testing (Dynamic Fracture)

- **Charpy Impact Test** (ASTM D6110, ISO 179): Pendulum strikes a notched bar supported as a simple beam. Energy absorbed = $E_{abs}$ (in J). Notch-to-failure.
- **Izod Impact Test** (ASTM D256, ISO 180): Pendulum strikes a notched cantilever specimen.
- **Falling Weight Impact Test (FWIT)** (ASTM D3029, ISO 6603): A weighted dart is dropped onto a clamped plate. Reports **brittle-ductile transition temperature (BDTT)**.

The impact strength is computed as:

$$
I_s = \frac{E_{abs}}{A_{cs}}
$$

Where $A_{cs}$ is the cross-sectional area at the notch (in $\text{m}^2$ or $\text{mm}^2$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — questions are typically derived directly from it.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | KTU Frequency |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity factor (Mode I) | $\text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $G = \dfrac{\pi a \sigma^2}{E}$ | Energy release rate (Griffith, plane stress) | $\text{J/m}^2$ | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $G_{IC} = \dfrac{K_{IC}^2}{E}$ (plane stress) | Critical energy release rate | $\text{J/m}^2$ | ★★★★ |
| 4 | $G_{IC} = \dfrac{K_{IC}^2(1-\nu^2)}{E}$ (plane strain) | Critical energy release rate | $\text{J/m}^2$ | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a_c}}$ | Critical failure stress | $\text{MPa}$ | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^{\!2}$ | Critical crack length | $\text{m}$ | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}}$ | Stress concentration at elliptical hole | dimensionless | ★★★ |
| 8 | $I_s = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{A_{cs}}$ | Impact strength | $\text{J/m}^2$ | ★★★★ |
| 9 | $G_{IC} = 2(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)$ | Total crack resistance (Irwin) | $\text{J/m}^2$ | ★★★ |
| 10 | $J = -\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial a}\!\bigg\vert_{\Delta}$ | J-integral (Rice, 1968) | $\text{kJ/m}^2$ | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric correction factor $Y$**: For an infinite plate with central crack of length $2a$, $Y = 1$. For a single-edge-notch tension (SENT) specimen, $Y \approx 1.12$ for $a/W \ll 1$. For a single-edge-notch bend (SENB, three-point bend) specimen per ASTM E399, $Y$ is a tabulated function of $a/W$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Space Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance for KTU:**

1. **Damage-Tolerant Design of Composite Pressure Vessels (CPVs):** Composite overwrapped pressure vessels (COPVs) on spacecraft use polymer liners (HDPE, PA11). $K_{IC}$ of the liner determines the **leak-before-burst (LBB)** criterion.
2. **Solar Array Substrates:** Kapton and polyimide films undergo **thermal-cycling fatigue** in orbit (-150 °C ↔ +150 °C). Fracture testing at cryogenic temperatures ensures survival.
3. **Micrometeoroid & Orbital Debris (MMOD) Shielding:** Whipple shields use **Kevlar/E-glass fibre layers** in a polymer matrix. Hypervelocity impact testing simulates MMOD and evaluates energy absorption.
4. **Adhesive Joints & Bondlines:** Spacecraft structures rely on epoxy bonded joints. Fracture mechanics (Mode I, Mode II) predicts **debond propagation** under vibration and thermal loads.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Griffith Energy Criterion for an Infinite Plate with a Central Crack

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Standard:** Show all steps clearly. The derivation links the energy balance to the critical stress for crack propagation. This is a *guaranteed 14-mark question* in some form.

### Setup
Consider an infinite elastic plate of unit thickness, containing a central through-thickness crack of length $2a$, subjected to a uniform remote tensile stress $\sigma$ (Mode I loading). The plate has Young's modulus $E$.

### Step 1 — Elastic Strain Energy of the Uncracked Plate
The strain energy per unit volume in the plate (far from the crack) is:

$$
u_0 = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}
$$

### Step 2 — Strain Energy of the Cracked Plate
Using Inglis' result (1913) for the stress concentration around an elliptical hole, and integrating the local strain energy density around the crack, the *reduction* in stored elastic energy due to the presence of the crack is:

$$
\Delta U_{\text{elastic}} = -\frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E}
$$

(The negative sign indicates energy *released* by the plate when the crack opens.)

### Step 3 — Surface Energy Required for New Crack Faces
The creation of two new crack surfaces (each of area $1 \times a$, top and bottom) requires:

$$
\Delta U_{\text{surface}} = 2 \gamma_s \cdot a = 2 a \gamma_s
$$

### Step 4 — Total Energy of the System
The total potential energy of the cracked plate under load is:

$$
U_{\text{total}} = -\frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E} + 2 a \gamma_s
$$

### Step 5 — Equilibrium Condition (Griffith's Criterion)
For a quasi-static crack growth at constant $\sigma$, the crack is in equilibrium when:

$$
\frac{\partial U_{\text{total}}}{\partial a} = 0
$$

Performing the differentiation:

$$
\frac{\partial U_{\text{total}}}{\partial a} = -\frac{2\pi a \sigma^2}{E} + 2 \gamma_s = 0
$$

Solving for the critical stress $\sigma_c$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}
$$

### Step 6 — Verification via Energy Release Rate
The strain energy release rate is defined as:

$$
G = -\frac{\partial U_{\text{total}}}{\partial a}\bigg\vert_{\Delta} = \frac{2\pi a \sigma^2}{E}
$$

The critical condition is $G = G_{IC} = 2\gamma_s$, giving:

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{E G_{IC}}{\pi a}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}
$$

Both forms are consistent. **This is the celebrated Griffith equation for brittle fracture.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Highlight:** Note the **inverse-square-root dependence on crack length**. Doubling the crack length reduces the critical failure stress by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. This is why *inspection intervals* for space structures (e.g., cryogenic tanks) are based on detectable crack sizes, not on stress levels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between $K_{IC}$ and $G_{IC}$ (Plane-Strain)

### Step 1 — Start from the Stress Field
For Mode I loading, the displacement field near the crack tip is:

$$
u_x = \frac{K_I}{2\mu}\sqrt{\frac{r}{2\pi}}\cos\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\!\left[\kappa - 1 + 2\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right]
$$

$$
u_y = \frac{K_I}{2\mu}\sqrt{\frac{r}{2\pi}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\!\left[\kappa + 1 - 2\cos^2\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\right]
$$

Where:
- $\mu = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ is the shear modulus
- $\kappa = 3 - 4\nu$ (plane strain)

### Step 2 — Compute the Strain Energy Density
The elastic strain energy density is:

$$
W = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij}
$$

Integrating $W$ over a small annular region near the crack tip and dividing by the area gives the **energy flux into the crack tip**, which equals $G$:

$$
G = \lim_{\delta \to 0}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\!\left[W\cos\theta - \sigma_{ij}u_{i,n}\sin\theta\right]\,r\,d\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Carry Out the Integration
After substantial algebra (omitted here for brevity but required at the M.Tech level), the result is:

$$
G = \frac{K_I^2}{E'}
$$

Where:

$$
E' = \begin{cases} E & \text{(plane stress)} \\ \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2} & \text{(plane strain)} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Critical Condition
At fracture, $K_I = K_{IC}$, so:

$$
\boxed{\,G_{IC} = \frac{K_{IC}^2}{E}\ (\text{plane stress})\quad\text{or}\quad G_{IC} = \frac{K_{IC}^2(1-\nu^2)}{E}\ (\text{plane strain})\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**
> A space-grade **PEEK** specimen has $K_{IC} = 4.0\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$, $E = 3.6\ \text{GPa}$, and $\nu = 0.38$. Compute $G_{IC}$ (plane strain).
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> E' &= \frac{3.6 \times 10^9}{1 - (0.38)^2} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.1444} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^9}{0.8556} \\
> E' &\approx 4.208 \times 10^9\ \text{Pa} \\
> G_{IC} &= \frac{(4.0 \times 10^6)^2}{4.208 \times 10^9} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{13}}{4.208 \times 10^9} \\
> G_{IC} &\approx 3802\ \text{J/m}^2
> \end{aligned}
> $$

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Mode I Crack-Tip Stress Field (Text Representation)

> [!NOTE]
> **A physical schematic of a Mode I crack is described here in text form for the student's mental model. A typical KTU question asks students to draw a labelled sketch showing $\sigma_{xx}$, $\sigma_{yy}$, and $\tau_{xy}$ on an infinitesimal element at distance $r$ from the crack tip, oriented at angle $\theta$.**

**Elements to include in the sketch (referenced from Section 2.1):**

- A horizontal central crack of length $2a$ in an infinite plate.
- Remote tensile stress $\sigma$ applied perpendicular to the crack plane.
- A small element at the crack tip, located at polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ where $r \ll a$.
- Three stress components on the element faces:
  - $\sigma_{xx}$ (normal stress parallel to crack)
  - $\sigma_{yy}$ (normal stress perpendicular to crack — the dominant opening stress)
  - $\tau_{xy}$ (in-plane shear)
- The crack-tip opening displacement $\delta_t$ shown as a gap at $r = 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **Drawing Tip for KTU Exams:** Always show the **Cartesian axes** at the crack tip, the **angle $\theta$** measured from the $x$-axis, and indicate with an arrow the direction of **crack propagation**. Examiners reward clearly labelled diagrams with **at least 4 annotations**.
