# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part A format: short, direct answer in 50–80 words. No choice. Each question tests one CO and one cognitive level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define atomic oxygen erosion yield ($E_y$) and state its typical value for Kapton® HN in LEO. Why is it a critical parameter for polymer composite selection in spacecraft exteriors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Atomic oxygen erosion yield $E_y$ is defined as the **volume of material removed per incident oxygen atom**, expressed in cm³/atom. Mathematically, $E_y = \Delta m / (\rho \cdot F_{AO})$.
- **Typical value (1 mark):** For Kapton® HN (uncoated polyimide) in LEO, $E_y \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-24}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{atom}$, which serves as the **reference baseline** in NASA-HDBK-6024.
- **Criticality (0.5 mark):** It governs the **surface recession rate** under ram-direction AO flux of $\sim 10^{14}$–$10^{15}\,\text{atoms/cm}^2\text{·s}$ in LEO, dictating protective coating requirements and mission life.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Explain the Fickian and Non-Fickian moisture absorption behaviors in polymer composites. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fickian behavior (1.5 marks):** Fickian absorption shows a **monotonic weight gain** approaching saturation $M_m$, with $M(t) \propto \sqrt{t}$ at short times and plateau at long times. Example: **untreated glass/epoxy** in 50 °C water.
- **Non-Fickian behavior (1.5 marks):** Non-Fickian absorption shows **two-stage uptake** (initial Fickian diffusion + slow relaxation) or **anomalous saturation overshoot** due to matrix relaxation, chemical aging, or leaching. Example: **epoxy/amine systems with excess unreacted epoxy groups** showing saturation overshoot, or **high-$T_g$ thermoplastics** in hot/wet conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B format: 14-mark question, internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Total marks for Part B: 14. All sub-parts must be answered.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU ESE Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**A. (a)** Define and explain **Fickian moisture diffusion** in polymer composites. Derive the one-dimensional solution of Fick's second law for a thin plate exposed on both sides. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A carbon/epoxy composite laminate of thickness $h = 4$ mm is immersed in water at $T = 60\,^\circ\text{C}$. The diffusivity is $D = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ and saturation moisture $M_m = 2.0\,\%$. Calculate:
   1. The characteristic diffusion time $\tau$. (2 marks)
   2. The moisture content after 48 hours. (3 marks)
   3. The time required to reach 90% saturation. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**A(a) — Fickian Diffusion Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Statement of the law (1 mark):**
Fick's second law of diffusion in one dimension:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

where $C$ = concentration (kg/m³), $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s), $t$ = time (s), $x$ = spatial coordinate.

**Step 2 — Assumptions for thin plate (1 mark):**
- Initial condition: $C(x, 0) = C_0$ (dry)
- Boundary conditions: $C(h/2, t) = C_s$ and $C(-h/2, t) = C_s$ (symmetric exposure)
- Constant surface concentration, isothermal conditions.

**Step 3 — Separation of variables (2 marks):**
Assume $C(x,t) = X(x) \cdot T(t)$. Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{T} \frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{D}{X} \frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} = -\lambda^2$$

This yields $T(t) = A \exp(-\lambda^2 D t)$ and $X(x) = B \cos(\lambda x) + E \sin(\lambda x)$.

**Step 4 — Apply BCs (1 mark):**
Symmetry forces $E = 0$; boundary condition $C(h/2) = C_s$ gives $\lambda_n = (2n+1)\pi/h$.

**Step 5 — Final solution and conversion to moisture content (2 marks):**

$$\frac{M(t) - M_0}{M_m - M_0} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left[ -\frac{D(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 t}{h^2} \right]$$

**A(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Characteristic time (2 marks):**

$$\tau = \frac{h^2}{D} = \frac{(4\,\text{mm})^2}{5.0 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}} = 3.2 \times 10^7\,\text{s} \approx 370.4\,\text{days}$$

**2. Moisture content at $t = 48$ h = 172,800 s (3 marks):**

Dimensionless time: $Dt/h^2 = (5.0 \times 10^{-7})(172,800)/16 = 5.4 \times 10^{-3}$

Only the $n=0$ term is significant (higher $n$ terms negligible):

$$M(48\,\text{h}) = 2.0 \left[ 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp(-5.4 \times 10^{-3}) \right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\exp(-5.4 \times 10^{-3}) &= 0.9946 \\
M &= 2.0 \left[ 1 - 0.8106 \times 0.9946 \right] \\
&= 2.0 \left[ 1 - 0.8062 \right] \\
&= 2.0 \times 0.1938 \\
&= 0.388\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**3. Time for 90% saturation (2 marks):**

For 90% saturation, $M(t)/M_m = 0.9$, so $1 - (8/\pi^2)\exp(-\pi^2 D t/h^2) = 0.9$.

$$\exp(-\pi^2 D t/h^2) = 0.1 \times \pi^2/8 = 0.1234$$

$$-\pi^2 D t / h^2 = \ln(0.1234) = -2.093$$

$$t = \frac{2.093 \times h^2}{\pi^2 D} = \frac{2.093 \times 16}{(9.8696)(5.0 \times 10^{-7})} = 6.787 \times 10^6\,\text{s} \approx 78.5\,\text{days}$$

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU ESE May 2024 Expected Pattern]

**B. (a)** Discuss the mechanisms of **atomic oxygen (AO) erosion** in Low Earth Orbit. Explain why polymer composites are particularly vulnerable and how protective coatings mitigate the damage. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** A satellite composite panel made of Kapton®-covered CFRP (effective $E_y = 0.5 \times 10^{-24}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{atom}$ with alumina coating) is exposed to LEO AO for 3 years. The ram-direction AO fluence is $F_{AO} = 2.5 \times 10^{21}\,\text{atoms/cm}^2$. Calculate:
   1. The total mass loss per cm². (2 marks)
   2. The surface recession depth. (2 marks)
   3. The expected service life if maximum allowable recession is 25 µm. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**B(a) — AO Erosion Mechanism (7 marks):**

**1. Source of AO in LEO (1 mark):**
At altitudes 200–700 km, UV photodissociation of O₂ produces atomic oxygen at densities of $10^8$–$10^9$ atoms/cm³. The orbital velocity (~7.8 km/s) gives incoming atoms ~5 eV kinetic energy.

**2. Reaction mechanism (2 marks):**
AO attacks the polymer surface via:

$$\text{Polymer} + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}, \text{CO}_2, \text{H}_2\text{O}, \text{NO}_x, \text{etc.}$$

These volatile products escape, causing **net mass and thickness loss**.

**3. Why composites are vulnerable (2 marks):**
- Polymer matrices contain abundant C–C, C–H, C–O bonds susceptible to AO attack.
- Erosion is **non-uniform** — heterogeneous surfaces develop "shark-tooth" texture.
- Erosion of the matrix exposes fibers, accelerating **fiber-matrix debonding**.

**4. Protective coating strategies (2 marks):**
- **Inorganic coatings:** Al₂O₃, SiO₂, SnO₂ (sputter-deposited, ~100 nm).
- **Fluoropolymers:** Teflon FEP (forms protective AlF₃ layer).
- **Silicone-based coatings:** SiO₂ formation under AO attack.

**B(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Mass loss per cm² (2 marks):**

Convert $E_y$ to g/atom: $E_y \times \rho = 0.5 \times 10^{-24}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{atom} \times 1.42\,\text{g/cm}^3 = 7.1 \times 10^{-25}\,\text{g/atom}$

$$\Delta m = (7.1 \times 10^{-25})(2.5 \times 10^{21}) = 1.775 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{g/cm}^2$$

**2. Surface recession depth (2 marks):**

$$\Delta z = E_y \cdot F_{AO} = (0.5 \times 10^{-24})(2.5 \times 10^{21}) = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm} = 12.5\,\mu\text{m}$$

**3. Service life for 25 µm recession (3 marks):**

Allowable fluence:

$$F_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\Delta z_{\max}}{E_y} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm}}{0.5 \times 10^{-24}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{atom}} = 5 \times 10^{21}\,\text{atoms/cm}^2$$

Annual fluence rate: $2.5 \times 10^{21}/3 = 8.33 \times 10^{20}\,\text{atoms/cm}^2/\text{year}$

Service life:

$$L = \frac{5 \times 10^{21}}{8.33 \times 10^{20}} = 6\,\text{years}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE Module 4:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $E_y$:** Students frequently mix cm³/atom with m³/atom or use $\rho$ in kg/m³ while $E_y$ is in cm³/atom → 1–2 marks lost. Always convert all quantities to a single consistent CGS or SI system.
> 2. **Confusing Fickian with Non-Fickian:** Writing "Fickian" when the problem indicates two-stage uptake → full 1.5 marks lost in Part A.
> 3. **Ignoring synergism:** Board examiners specifically deduct marks if students treat UV, AO, and thermal cycling as **independent** additively. Always state: *"These stressors act synergistically; the combined effect is greater than the linear sum."*
> 4. **Forgetting AO flux ram-direction dependence:** AO flux depends on spacecraft orientation — **ram direction** has 4× higher flux than **wake direction**. Loss of 1 mark if omitted.
> 5. **Misapplying Arrhenius model:** Writing $D = D_0 \exp(E_a/RT)$ (wrong sign) instead of $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$ → -1 mark penalty.
> 6. **Outgassing units:** Mixing TML/CVCM in fractional form vs. percentage. Standard is **percentage**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 Rapid Revision Checklist — Environmental Effects on Composites**

- ☐ **Fickian diffusion** is governed by $\partial C/\partial t = D \cdot \partial^2 C/\partial x^2$; the **thin-plate solution** is a Fourier series in exponentials.
- ☐ **$M_m$** is the equilibrium saturation moisture content; $D$ is Arrhenius-dependent: $D = D_0 \exp(-E_d/RT)$.
- ☐ **Moisture causes** plasticization, $T_g$ depression, ILSS drop, and hydrolytic scission in polyesters and polyamides.
- ☐ **CTE mismatch** ($\alpha_f \approx 0$, $\alpha_m \approx 60 \times 10^{-6}/\text{K}$) is the root cause of **thermal cycling microcracking**.
- ☐ **Crack density** saturates as $\rho_c(N) = \rho_{\infty}[1 - \exp(-\eta N)]$.
- ☐ **UV degradation** is wavelength-selective: only $\lambda < 400$ nm causes bond scission; Beer-Lambert law $I(z) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\epsilon z}$ governs depth.
- ☐ **Atomic oxygen** in LEO has ~5 eV KE; $E_y$ for Kapton® HN = $3.0 \times 10^{-24}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{atom}$ (reference).
- ☐ **AO recession depth:** $\Delta z = E_y \cdot F_{AO}$; protective coatings reduce $E_y$ by 10–100×.
- ☐ **ASTM E595 limits:** TML $\leq 1.00\%$, CVCM $\leq 0.10\%$ — must be verified for all space-grade materials.
- ☐ **Synergism** is the key concept in 2024 scheme: combined UV + thermal cycling damage $\gg$ sum of individual effects.
- ☐ **Orbital regime matters:** LEO → AO dominant; GEO → charged particles + UV; interplanetary → all stressors.
- ☐ **Strength retention** $R_f = \sigma_{\text{aged}}/\sigma_0$ is the universal metric for property loss; space qualification typically requires $R_f \geq 0.7$.
- ☐ **Hygrothermal aging** is often modeled as $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0(1 - \beta\sqrt{t})$ for short-term prediction.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Environmental Effects on Composites**
> *Mapped to KTU 2024 Scheme CO4, Bloom Levels L2–L5.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT746 - POLYMERS FOR SPACE APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Composite Characterization by Mechanical, Thermal and DMTA Techniques # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Composite Characterization** refers to the systematic experimental evaluation of the physical, mechanical, thermal, and viscoelastic properties of fibre-reinforced polymer matrix composites (FRPs) and polymer nanocomposites to establish structure–property relationships, predict in-service performance, and qualify the material for specific engineering applications (in this course, space applications). The three principal characterization pillars studied in this module are: 1. **Mechanical Characterization** — Quantifies the response of a composite to static, quasi-static, and dynamic loads (tensile, flexural, interlaminar shear, compression, impact, hardness, fatigue). 2. **Thermal Characterization** — Quantifies the response of a composite to controlled temperature programmes (TGA, DSC, TMA, DMA thermal scans, CTE). 3. **DMTA (Dynamic Mechanical Thermal Analysis)** — Quantifies the viscoelastic response of a composite by imposing a small sinusoidal oscillatory stress/strain over a temperature or frequency sweep, providing storage modulus (*E′*), loss modulus (*E″*), and the loss factor (*tan δ*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Characterization is the *bridge* between composite fabrication and end-use certification. For space applications, characterization data feeds directly into **material allowables** used by structural analysts in finite element codes (NASTRAN, ABAQUS) for satellite, launch-vehicle, and spacecraft structural design. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **tennis racket**: - **Mechanical testing** is like pulling the racket frame hard along its length (tensile), bending it (flexural), or hitting a ball with it (impact). You are asking: *"How much force can it take before it breaks or bends permanently?"* - **Thermal testing** is like leaving the racket in a hot car in summer, then in a freezer in winter. You are asking: *"At what temperature does the frame lose stiffness, decompose, or expand?"* — Critical for a satellite cycling between +120 °C (sun) and −150 °C (shadow). - **DMTA** is like flicking the racket frame gently while slowly changing the temperature, and listening to the ringing tone change. A stiff, glassy frame rings with a high pitch; a rubbery, damped frame gives a dull thud. The "tone" and "decay" tell you the **storage modulus** and **loss factor** — the *viscoelastic fingerprint* of the composite. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** Mechanical tests tell you how *strong* the composite is, thermal tests tell you how *hot or cold* it can survive, and DMTA tells you how it *behaves dynamically* as a function of temperature and frequency — its true "operating envelope." ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted The key standard metrics students must commit to memory are: - **Tensile strength (σₜ)** — measured in **MPa** (ASTM D3039 for polymer composites) - **Tensile modulus (Eₜ)** — measured in **GPa** - **Flexural strength (σ_f)** — measured in **MPa** (ASTM D7264) - **Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS, τ)** — measured in **MPa** (ASTM D2344 short-beam shear) - **Glass transition temperature (T_g)** — measured in **°C or K** - **Storage modulus E′** — measured in **Pa or MPa** - **Loss modulus E″** — measured in **Pa or MPa** - **Loss factor tan δ** — **dimensionless** - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE, α)** — measured in **ppm/°C or µm/m·K** - **Char yield (Y_c)** — wt% residue at 800 °C in N₂ (TGA) > [!TIP] > For space composites, low CTE (< 5 ppm/°C), high T_g (> 200 °C), high char yield (> 40 %), and high specific modulus are the *hallmarks* of qualification for LEO/GEO structures. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanical Characterization — Theory of Operation ### 2.1.1 Tensile Testing (ASTM D3039) A unidirectional or multidirectional composite coupon is gripped in a universal testing machine (UTM) and pulled at a constant crosshead displacement rate (typically **1–2 mm/min**). A strain gauge or extensometer records longitudinal strain. **Why it matters:** Tensile strength along the fibre direction (0°) is dominated by fibre strength, while transverse tensile strength (90°) is matrix-dominated and is typically **20–40× lower**. This anisotropy is the defining feature of composites and must be quantified. ### 2.1.2 Flexural (3-Point Bend) Testing (ASTM D7264) A simply supported beam is loaded at mid-span. The test is preferred over tensile for brittle composites because the failure mode is more controllable. ### 2.1.3 Short-Beam Shear (SBS) Test (ASTM D2344) Used to measure **Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS)**. Span-to-thickness ratio is kept low (≈ 4:1) so that interlaminar shear failure dominates over flexural failure. ILSS quantifies the *fibre–matrix interfacial bond quality* — a critical property in space laminates subjected to thermal cycling. ### 2.1.4 Impact Testing (ASTM D256 Izod / ASTM D7136 Compression After Impact) Quantifies the absorbed energy-to-failure, J/m. For composites, **Compression After Impact (CAI)** strength is the more relevant metric in aerospace — it measures residual compressive strength of a panel containing a known impact damage area. ### 2.1.5 Hardness (Barcol / Shore D / Vickers) Surface indentation hardness is used as a **quality-control tool** for cured laminates (Barcol 935 is standard for fibre-reinforced plastics per ASTM D2583). ## 2.2 Thermal Characterization — Theory of Operation ### 2.2.1 Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) A small sample (5–20 mg) is heated at a controlled rate (e.g., **10 °C/min**) in N₂ or air. Mass loss vs temperature is recorded. Outputs: - **T_d5%** — 5 % mass-loss temperature (onset of degradation) - **T_dmax** — temperature at maximum rate of mass loss (from DTG curve) - **Char yield Y_c** — residue at 800 °C (N₂) > For space composites, high char yield correlates with ablative performance (re-entry vehicles, nozzle liners). ### 2.2.2 Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) Sample and reference are heated at the same rate; the heat flow difference is recorded. Outputs: - **T_g** from step change in heat capacity (mid-point method) - **Cure enthalpy ΔH** (residual cure exotherm) — used to verify degree of cure of thermoset composites - **Melting T_m**, **crystallinity %** ### 2.2.3 Thermomechanical Analysis (TMA) Probe measures dimensional change vs temperature → gives **CTE (α)**. For a unidirectional carbon/epoxy composite, longitudinal CTE (α_L) can be **−1 to +1 ppm/°C** (because the carbon fibre is near-zero/negative CTE), while transverse CTE (α_T) is **20–30 ppm/°C**. This **CTE tailoring** is critical for space structures (e.g., CFRP-aluminium honeycomb sandwich panels matching the CTE of silicon solar cells or Invar). ## 2.3 DMTA — The Theoretical Core of This Module In DMTA, a small sinusoidal strain is applied: $$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ For a viscoelastic material, the stress response **leads** the strain by a phase angle **δ (0° < δ < 90°)**: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$ This single phase lag **δ** encodes the full viscoelastic fingerprint of the composite. The complex modulus is decomposed as: $$E^* = E' + iE''$$ where the **storage modulus E′** is the in-phase elastic (recoverable, energy-storing) component, and the **loss modulus E″** is the out-of-phase viscous (energy-dissipating) component. The **loss factor** is: $$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **α-relaxation peak** in the *tan δ* vs T curve is taken as the **dynamic T_g** of the composite. It is more sensitive to molecular mobility than DSC and is *the* preferred method for T_g determination in cured thermosets used in space structures. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Used For | |---|--------------------|------------|-------|----------| | 1 | Tensile stress | $\sigma_t = P / A$ | MPa | ASTM D3039 | | 2 | Tensile modulus | $E_t = \Delta\sigma / \Delta\varepsilon$ | GPa | Stiffness of 0° ply | | 3 | Flexural strength (3-pt bend) | $\sigma_f = 3PL / (2bh^2)$ | MPa | ASTM D7264 | | 4 | Flexural modulus | $E_f = L^3 m / (4bh^3)$ | GPa | Slope of load–deflection | | 5 | ILSS (SBS) | $\tau = 0.75 P / (bh)$ | MPa | ASTM D2344 | | 6 | Poisson's ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_t / \varepsilon_l$ | — | Transverse strain | | 7 | CTE (linear) | $\alpha = (\Delta L / L_0) / \Delta T$ | ppm/°C | TMA / dilatometry | | 8 | Rule of mixtures (tensile modulus) | $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | GPa | Upper bound (iso-strain) | | 9 | Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse) | $1/E_T = V_f / E_f + V_m / E_m$ | — | Lower bound (iso-stress) | | 10 | Storage modulus | $E' = ( \sigma_0 / \varepsilon_0 ) \cos \delta$ | MPa | DMTA in-phase | | 11 | Loss modulus | $E'' = ( \sigma_0 / \varepsilon_0 ) \sin \delta$ | MPa | DMTA out-of-phase | | 12 | Loss factor | $\tan \delta = E'' / E'$ | — | Viscoelastic damping | | 13 | Complex modulus magnitude | $\vert E^* \vert = \sqrt{E'^2 + E''^2}$ | MPa | Overall rigidity | | 14 | Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) shift | $\log a_T = -C_1 (T-T_g)/[C_2 + (T-T_g)]$ | — | Time–temperature superposition | | 15 | Arrhenius (above T_g) | $a_T = \exp[(E_a/R)(1/T - 1/T_{ref})]$ | — | Activation energy of relaxation | | 16 | Char yield | $Y_c = m_{800^\circ C} / m_{initial} \times 100$ | wt% | Ablative / thermal stability | | 17 | Crystallinity (DSC) | $\chi_c = (\Delta H_m / \Delta H_m^0) \times (1/w_{polymer}) \times 100$ | % | Semi-crystalline matrix | | 18 | Degree of cure | $\alpha_c = 1 - \Delta H_{residual} / \Delta H_{total}$ | — | DSC of thermosets | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Equations 10–15 are the *heart* of DMTA interpretation. Engineers use **time–temperature superposition (TTS)** to extrapolate short-term laboratory DMTA data to predict long-term creep/relaxation of composite structures in orbit (a 10-year LEO mission ≈ 10⁹ seconds of thermal cycling). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Storage Modulus, Loss Modulus, and Complex Modulus from Sinusoidal Loading This is the **fundamental DMTA derivation** and is high-yield for KTU ESE. ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Sinusoidal Strain Input A DMTA instrument applies a controlled sinusoidal strain to the sample: $$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ where $\varepsilon_0$ is the strain amplitude and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency in **rad/s**. ### 3.1.2 Stress Response of a Linear Viscoelastic Material For a *purely elastic* Hookean solid, stress and strain are in phase: $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t)$. For a *purely viscous* Newtonian fluid, stress is proportional to strain rate: $\sigma(t) = \eta \, d\varepsilon/dt$. A real polymer composite is *viscoelastic* — it behaves somewhere in between. The stress response is therefore a sinusoid of the *same* frequency but **leads** the strain by a phase angle $\delta$: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$ ### 3.1.3 Expand the Stress Expression Using the Trigonometric Identity Apply the identity $\sin(A + B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 [ \sin(\omega t)\cos\delta + \cos(\omega t)\sin\delta ]$$ ### 3.1.4 Separate Into In-Phase and Out-of-Phase Components Group terms in $\sin(\omega t)$ (in-phase with strain) and $\cos(\omega t)$ (90° out-of-phase with strain): $$\sigma(t) = \underbrace{\sigma_0 \cos\delta}_{\text{in-phase amplitude}} \sin(\omega t) \; + \; \underbrace{\sigma_0 \sin\delta}_{\text{out-of-phase amplitude}} \cos(\omega t)$$ Define the **storage modulus E′** as the in-phase proportionality constant: $$E' = \frac{\sigma_0 \cos\delta}{\varepsilon_0}$$ Define the **loss modulus E″** as the out-of-phase proportionality constant: $$E'' = \frac{\sigma_0 \sin\delta}{\varepsilon_0}$$ ### 3.1.5 Construct the Complex Modulus Rewrite the stress as the real part of a complex exponential. Let the *complex strain* be $\varepsilon^*(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$. The complex stress is $\sigma^*(t) = E^* \varepsilon^*(t)$. The real part yields the physical stress. Identifying terms: $$E^* = E' + iE''$$ ### 3.1.6 Derive the Three Key Engineering Quantities **(a) Magnitude of complex modulus:** $$\vert E^* \vert = \sqrt{E'^2 + E''^2} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}$$ **(b) Loss factor:** $$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{\sin\delta}{\cos\delta}$$ **(c) Phase angle:** $$\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{E''}{E'}\right)$$ > **Physical interpretation:** > - $\delta = 0°$ → purely elastic (a stiff carbon-fibre tow, well below T_g) > - $\delta = 90°$ → purely viscous (a molten thermoplastic) > - $0° < \delta < 90°$ → viscoelastic, the regime of cured thermoset composites near T_g ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rule of Mixtures for a Unidirectional Composite (Longitudinal Modulus) This is the **most-tested mechanical property** in KTU ESE. ### 3.2.1 Assumptions - Perfect fibre–matrix bonding (iso-strain, i.e. $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_c$) - Fibres aligned with the load direction (unidirectional 0° ply) - Both phases are linearly elastic - Volume fractions: $V_f + V_m = 1$ (voids neglected for first derivation) ### 3.2.2 Force Equilibrium The total load on the composite cross-section equals the sum of loads carried by fibre and matrix: $$P_c = P_f + P_m$$ Dividing by the total area $A_c$: $$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$ Recognising $A_i = V_i A_c$: $$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$ ### 3.2.3 Apply Iso-Strain Since $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$, divide both sides by $\varepsilon$: $$\frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon} V_f + \frac{\sigma_m}{\varepsilon} V_m$$ By Hooke's law, $\sigma_i/\varepsilon = E_i$. Hence: $$\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$ > **Engineering significance:** This is the **upper bound** (Voigt model). For a typical CFRP with $V_f = 0.6$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa: $$E_c = (230)(0.6) + (3.5)(0.4) = 138 + 1.4 = 139.4 \text{ GPa}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Transverse Modulus, 90° Ply) ### 3.3.1 Iso-Stress Assumption When load is applied **perpendicular** to the fibres, iso-strain is not valid; rather, the stress in fibre and matrix is the *same*: $$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$ ### 3.3.2 Strain Compatibility Total strain is the volume-weighted sum: $$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$ ### 3.3.3 Apply Hooke's Law in Each Phase $\varepsilon_i = \sigma / E_i$. Substituting: $$\frac{\sigma}{E_T} = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m$$ Cancelling $\sigma$: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}$$ > **KTU Pitfall Alert:** This is the **lower bound** (Reuss model). For the same CFRP: > $1/E_T = 0.6/230 + 0.4/3.5 = 0.00261 + 0.11429 = 0.1169$, so $E_T = 8.55$ GPa. > Note $E_T \ll E_L$ — this **anisotropy** is why space structures use **laminate stacking sequences** (e.g., quasi-isotropic [0/45/–45/90]ₙ). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time–Temperature Superposition (WLF) Shift Factor DMTA at one frequency cannot cover 10 years of orbital service. WLF lets us *shift* curves horizontally on a log-frequency axis: $$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$ For most amorphous polymers, the **universal WLF constants** are $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K (valid for $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ °C). ### 3.4.1 Derivation Outline (Free-Volume Theory) The Doolittle equation for viscosity is: $$\eta = A \exp\!\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$ where $f$ is the fractional free volume. Assuming free volume is linear in temperature: $f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$, with $\alpha_f$ the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume, and defining $C_1 = B/(2.303 f_g)$ and $C_2 = f_g / \alpha_f$, the WLF equation emerges. > **Engineering use:** To predict 30-year creep of a composite panel at 80 °C in orbit, we measure DMTA at T = 60, 70, 80, 90 °C and use WLF to *shift* all curves horizontally to build a master curve at T_ref = 80 °C spanning 10⁻⁶ to 10⁹ s — the entire mission lifetime. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Composite Characterization Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fabricated Composite Laminate] --> B[Specimen Preparation
ASTM Standards Cutting] B --> C1[Mechanical Testing] B --> C2[Thermal Testing] B --> C3[DMTA Testing] C1 --> D1[Tensile D3039] C1 --> D2[Flexural D7264] C1 --> D3[ILSS D2344] C1 --> D4[Impact D256 D7136] C1 --> E1[Strength and Modulus Database] C2 --> F1[TGA Mass Loss vs T] C2 --> F2[DSC Heat Flow vs T] C2 --> F3[TMA Dimensional Change vs T] C2 --> E2[Thermal Property Database] C3 --> G1[Storage Modulus E prime] C3 --> G2[Loss Modulus E double prime] C3 --> G3[Loss Factor tan delta] C3 --> E3[Viscoelastic Database] E1 --> H[Structure Property Correlation] E2 --> H E3 --> H H --> I[Material Allowables for Space Qualification] I --> J[FE Input to NASTRAN ABAQUS] J --> K[Satellite Launch Vehicle Spacecraft Design] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — DMTA Output Curves and Their Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DMTA Run: T sweep or f sweep] --> B[Raw Outputs] B --> C1[E prime vs T Storage Modulus] B --> C2[E double prime vs T Loss Modulus] B --> C3[tan delta vs T Loss Factor] C1 --> D1[Glass Region: E prime High GPa] C1 --> D2[Transition Region: E prime drops 3 orders] C1 --> D3[Rubbery Plateau: E prime low MPa] C3 --> E1[Alpha peak position is Tg dynamic] C3 --> E2[Peak height indicates damping] C3 --> E3[Peak width indicates heterogeneity] D2 --> F[Fibre Matrix Interphase Quality Assessment] E1 --> F E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G[Accept or Reject for Space Use] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Stress–Strain Vector Decomposition in DMTA ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sinusoidal Strain input epsilon t] --> B[Stress Response sigma t] B --> C[Decompose into components] C --> D1[In phase Component: E prime times epsilon] C --> D2[Out of phase Component: E double prime times epsilon] D1 --> E[Elastic Energy Stored per cycle] D2 --> F[Viscous Energy Dissipated per cycle] E --> G[Storage Modulus E prime] F --> H[Loss Modulus E double prime] G --> I[Ratio gives tan delta] H --> I I --> J[Viscoelastic Fingerprint of Composite] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — Space-Composite Property Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Composite Property Required] --> B{Application} B -->|Satellite Structure| C1[Low CTE, High Specific Stiffness, Low Outgassing] B -->|Re entry Thermal Shield| C2[High Char Yield, High Tg, High ILSS] B -->|Cryogenic Tank| C3[Low Tg margin, High ILSS, Microcrack resistance] C1 --> D[TMA + Tensile + DMTA Master Curve] C2 --> E[TGA + DSC + ILSS + Char Yield] C3 --> F[DMTA at cryo T + ILSS + Thermal Cycling] D --> G[Qualify for GEO LEO] E --> G F --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Mermaid is sufficient here:** The topics (characterization techniques, modulus decomposition, workflow) are *process- and data-flow* concepts. Block-level functional architecture conveys the workflow more clearly than any physical sketch would. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Define the loss factor (tan δ) in DMTA. What is its physical significance for polymer composites used in space applications?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The loss factor is defined as the ratio of the loss modulus to the storage modulus: $$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$ It is a **dimensionless** quantity representing the ratio of energy dissipated (as heat) to energy stored (elastically) per cycle of oscillation. **Physical significance for space composites (2 marks):** - The **temperature of the α-relaxation peak** in the tan δ vs T curve is taken as the **dynamic glass transition temperature (T_g)** of the composite. - The **height** of the tan δ peak quantifies the damping capacity — critical for vibration damping of spacecraft structures (e.g., solar array booms, instrument platforms). - A *narrow* tan δ peak indicates a homogeneous, well-cured matrix; a *broad* peak indicates a heterogeneous interphase or partial cure. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Distinguish between storage modulus (E′) and loss modulus (E″) in the context of a viscoelastic composite.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Storage Modulus E′ | Loss Modulus E″ | |--------|-------------------|----------------| | Definition | In-phase elastic component of complex modulus | Out-of-phase viscous component | | Formula | $E' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ | $E'' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\sin\delta$ | | Physical meaning | Energy stored reversibly per cycle | Energy dissipated as heat per cycle | | Magnitude behaviour | Drops sharply at T_g | Peaks near T_g | | Engineering use | Predicts stiffness | Predicts damping | (3 marks — 1 mark for the formula pair, 1 mark for the energy interpretation, 1 mark for the engineering use.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches, describe the **Short-Beam Shear (SBS) test (ASTM D2344)** for measuring Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS) of a polymer matrix composite. Derive the expression for ILSS and state the typical span-to-thickness ratio used. **(b) [7 marks]** A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite has the following properties: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.55$. Calculate (i) the longitudinal Young's modulus $E_L$ using the rule of mixtures, and (ii) the transverse Young's modulus $E_T$ using the inverse rule of mixtures. Comment on the large anisotropy obtained. --- **Model Solution (Question A):** **Part (a) — Short-Beam Shear Test [7 marks]** A small rectangular composite coupon of width $b$ and thickness $h$ is placed on two cylindrical supports separated by a short span $L$. A third cylindrical loading nose applies a load $P$ at mid-span from above (three-point bend configuration, see Figure). To force **interlaminar shear** failure (rather than flexural failure), the span-to-thickness ratio is kept low: $$L/h \approx 4$$ For a typical $h = 2$ mm coupon, $L = 8$ mm. The maximum apparent interlaminar shear stress is derived from classical beam theory. For a 3-point bend, the maximum shear force in the beam is $V = P/2$, and the maximum shear stress in a rectangular cross-section is: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = \frac{3(P/2)}{2bh} = \frac{3P}{4bh}$$ The empirical **ILSS formula** used in ASTM D2344 incorporates a small-correction factor, giving: $$\tau_{ILSS} = \frac{0.75 \, P}{b \, h}$$ **Significance:** ILSS is dominated by the **fibre–matrix interfacial shear strength** and matrix shear strength. It is the most sensitive mechanical test to surface treatment of fibres (sizing, silane coupling) and matrix chemistry. **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]** Given: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$. **(i) Longitudinal modulus** — Rule of mixtures: $$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$ $$E_L = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)$$ $$E_L = 39.6 + 1.575$$ $$E_L = 41.175 \text{ GPa} \approx 41.2 \text{ GPa}$$ **(ii) Transverse modulus** — Inverse rule of mixtures: $$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$ $$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5}$$ $$\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.007639 + 0.128571 = 0.136210 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$ $$E_T = \frac{1}{0.136210} = 7.341 \text{ GPa} \approx 7.34 \text{ GPa}$$ **Anisotropy ratio:** $$\frac{E_L}{E_T} = \frac{41.175}{7.341} \approx 5.61$$ **Comment [required for full marks]:** The longitudinal modulus is **5.6× higher** than the transverse modulus. This is the hallmark anisotropy of unidirectional FRPs and the reason why real space structures use **multidirectional laminates** (e.g., [0/±45/90]ₛ quasi-isotropic stacking) to obtain balanced in-plane properties. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expressions for **storage modulus (E′), loss modulus (E″), and the loss factor (tan δ)** starting from the sinusoidal strain input $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$ in a DMTA experiment. State clearly the physical meaning of the phase angle $\delta$. **(b) [7 marks]** With the aid of a labelled diagram of the DMTA output curves (E′, E″, tan δ vs temperature), explain how the **dynamic glass transition temperature (T_g)** of a polymer composite is determined. How does fibre volume fraction $V_f$ affect (i) the magnitude of E′ in the glassy region, and (ii) the height and position of the tan δ peak? --- **Model Solution (Question B):** **Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]** (See the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes — the following is the exam-ready condensed form.) **Step 1:** Apply sinusoidal strain: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$. **Step 2:** Stress in a viscoelastic material leads the strain by phase angle $\delta$: $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$. **Step 3:** Expand using $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t) + \sigma_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4:** Define the storage modulus from the in-phase amplitude: $$E' = \frac{\sigma_0 \cos\delta}{\varepsilon_0}$$ **Step 5:** Define the loss modulus from the out-of-phase amplitude: $$E'' = \frac{\sigma_0 \sin\delta}{\varepsilon_0}$$ **Step 6:** Complex modulus: $E^* = E' + iE''$. **Step 7:** Loss factor: $$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$ **Physical meaning of δ (1 mark):** $\delta$ is the phase lag between stress and strain. $\delta = 0$ for a perfectly elastic solid, $\delta = 90°$ for a perfectly viscous liquid, and $0 < \delta < 90°$ for a viscoelastic polymer/composite. **Part (b) — DMTA Output Interpretation [7 marks]** **Diagram (3 marks):** Plot three curves on the x-axis of temperature (T): - **E′ (storage modulus) vs T**: High value (~10 GPa for epoxy) in the glassy region, drops by 2–3 orders of magnitude through the glass transition, then levels off in the rubbery region. - **E″ (loss modulus) vs T**: Shows a peak at the glass transition (the α-relaxation). - **tan δ vs T**: Also shows a peak; the temperature at the *peak* of tan δ is taken as the **dynamic T_g**. **Determination of dynamic T_g (2 marks):** The dynamic T_g is the temperature of the **tan δ peak maximum** (or alternatively, the onset of the drop in E′, or the E″ peak — depending on the convention used in the lab). The tan δ method gives a slightly higher T_g than DSC because DMTA is more sensitive to small-scale molecular motions. **Effect of $V_f$ (2 marks):** - (i) **E′ in the glassy region:** Increases roughly linearly with $V_f$ (rule of mixtures). More fibre → stiffer composite. - (ii) **tan δ peak:** The peak *height decreases* and the peak *broadens* as $V_f$ increases (because the fibre dilutes the mobile matrix volume). The peak position may shift to slightly higher temperature (fibre-matrix constraint suppresses polymer chain mobility, raising apparent T_g). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE — DMTA & Composite Characterization:** > > 1. **Confusing dynamic T_g with DSC T_g.** They differ by 10–30 °C. State explicitly which method is used. The *tan δ* peak T_g is *higher* than the DSC mid-point T_g. > 2. **Forgetting units.** E′ and E″ in DMTA are in **Pa or MPa**, NOT GPa (typical glassy polymer E′ is 1–10 GPa, but always state the unit). > 3. **Using |x| inside a markdown table** — breaks the table layout in the answer script. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$. > 4. **Mixing up rule of mixtures (longitudinal) and inverse rule of mixtures (transverse).** A common slip: applying iso-strain to the transverse direction. Remember: **iso-strain → longitudinal; iso-stress → transverse.** > 5. **Skipping the assumption list** in derivation questions. KTU examiners *expect* to see "assuming perfect bonding, linear elasticity, no voids" at the top of any rule-of-mixtures derivation. > 6. **Drawing DMTA curves without labels** on both axes AND without units. Always label: E′ (MPa), T (°C), and show the three regions: glassy, transition, rubbery. > 7. **Writing "tan delta" instead of $\tan\delta$** in the math section. Use proper LaTeX in derivations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Module 4 Topic: Composite Characterization** - **Mechanical Tests (and their ASTM standards):** - Tensile → **ASTM D3039** → $\sigma_t = P/A$, $E_t = \Delta\sigma/\Delta\varepsilon$ - Flexural (3-pt bend) → **ASTM D7264** → $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bh^2)$ - ILSS (Short-Beam Shear) → **ASTM D2344** → $\tau = 0.75P/(bh)$, with $L/h \approx 4$ - Impact Izod → **ASTM D256**; Compression After Impact → **ASTM D7136** - Hardness (Barcol) → **ASTM D2583** (QC tool) - **Thermal Tests:** - **TGA** → mass loss vs T; key outputs: $T_{d5\%}$, $T_{dmax}$, **char yield $Y_c$** at 800 °C in N₂ - **DSC** → heat flow vs T; outputs: $T_g$ (step in $C_p$), $\Delta H$ cure, $T_m$, $\chi_c$ - **TMA** → dimensional change vs T; output: **CTE $\alpha$** (in ppm/°C) - **DMTA — the star of this module:** - Input: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$ - Output stress leads strain by phase $\delta$ - $E' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ (storage, elastic) - $E'' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\sin\delta$ (loss, viscous) - $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ (dimensionless, peaks at dynamic $T_g$) - $E^* = E' + iE''$, $\vert E^* \vert = \sqrt{E'^2 + E''^2}$ - **Composite Micromechanics:** - $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (rule of mixtures, **iso-strain**, longitudinal) - $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (inverse rule, **iso-stress**, transverse) - $V_f + V_m = 1$ (no voids assumed) - **Time–Temperature Superposition (TTS):** - WLF: $\log a_T = -C_1(T-T_g)/[C_2 + (T-T_g)]$ - Universal constants: $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K - Used to extrapolate short DMTA scans to years of service life - **Space-Specific Property Targets:** - $T_g > 200$ °C (cyanate ester, polyimide matrices) - CTE < 5 ppm/°C (matched to Si solar cells / Invar) - Char yield > 40 wt% (ablative re-entry shields) - High specific stiffness $E/\rho$ (CFRP > 100 GPa/(g/cm³)) - Low outgassing (TML < 1 %, CVCM < 0.1 %, per **ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C**) - High ILSS (resists microcracking from −150 °C to +150 °C thermal cycling) - **Top 5 Formulas to Memorize (cold, in sleep):** 1. $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ 2. $E' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ 3. $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ 4. $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bh^2)$ 5. $\tau_{ILSS} = 0.75P/(bh)$ - **Viva-Voce Quick-Fire Q&A:** - *Q:* Why is the SBS test span-to-thickness ratio ≈ 4? - *A:* To ensure failure occurs by interlaminar shear, not by flexure. - *Q:* Why does the tan δ peak shift to higher T with higher $V_f$? - *A:* Fibres constrain polymer chain mobility, raising the apparent $T_g$. - *Q:* Why is dynamic T_g > DSC T_g? - *A:* DMTA detects smaller-amplitude motions at lower frequency → transition appears earlier; the tan δ peak corresponds to a higher T for the same molecular mobility criterion. - *Q:* What does a high char yield indicate? - *A:* A high carbonaceous residue after pyrolysis — good ablative and thermal-oxidative stability, vital for re-entry shields and nozzle liners. ---
